I LOVE my fitbit. In fact I may be obsessed. My only issue is the app tends to be buggy after updates and I end up having to restart everything. I also wish there was a way to link it to MyFitnessPal or some of the other tracking apps.

You know what you're getting here - it's an implementation of Chess. What you might not know is if you try launching the app without an internet connection, it will refuse to launch, so for example this app is useless if you wish to kill time on a plane or in a car without a mobile data plan.

If you're fed up with the overly complex and expensive copy protection and licensing scheme that Microsoft Office uses, this is a usable alternative. It installed easily on my Windows 10 PC. The basic word processing features all work fine. Importing Microsoft documents usually works fine, though more complex documents can be slow. WordPerfect is "compatible" with Microsoft Word, but that means it can import and write the Microsoft file formats. Individual commands and even command naming is often different, so you may have a learning curve to use this product.

The Adobe PDF importing features are better than the freeware PDF apps and generally works well for simpler PDF documents, but it can be hit-and-miss for more complex documents. I wish they had included the PDF features in their home product, since it is really not up to professional performance levels.

This product is shipped on a DVD, but the quick start guide in the box says you can download it from their website if your computer does not have a DVD drive. You will need the activation code that is included in the box. You do not need to give up your email address or any other personal information like you do with Microsoft Office. However, the first thing the program does after you install it is to connect to their server and do something (that they do not explain) so you do need an internet connection to run this program.

If you're casually tracking a couple of stocks, this might be the app for you. More than a handful of stocks and this display gets cluttered very quickly. The quotes are not time stamped and may be very out of date (like 15 minutes old) so this app has limited value to day traders. Many popular exchange-traded-funds and market indexes are not supported by the app. Google Finance is always free and has a more useful display, as well as support for widgets.

This app is extremely effective and powerful, especially considering I got it free.

This app has the ability to record any activities you wish, by any category or subcategory you choose to designate, and you can also type in an explanatory memo for any individual entry.

When you create categories, the app allows you to color code each category and assign an icon, which is visually very helpful.

This app can generate reports for any categories you choose, for any range of dates you desire. These reports can be exported to yourself or others, whoever you designate, via email, in either HTML or CSV (Excel) format.

I've used this app on my iPhone 5c for almost three years. I am what one might call a "power user," as I have used it consistently, recording essentially the whole day's activities, every day of those years. Because of this I've accumulated a huge amount of data in the database file within the program. The more one uses a program like this, the more important the ability to backup one's data becomes.

With this app, you can backup your data via several means: (1) synchronize with the developer's website, (2) do a routine backup of the entire contents of your smart phone, or (3) create a backup within the program which you can then copy off the phone into particular folder on your computer that you designate yourself.

I have had reason, several times, to contact the developer with questions about this app, and he has responded quite rapidly, often within hours, and he has been very friendly and helpful.

All in all, this is a terrific app!

I downloaded this app to my Kindle and I keep an Icon on my Home screen, available for use when I'm interested in seeing what the weather is like where my family lives, in different areas of the Country.  It was free and for that price and the weather in the background of my use of tablet, it is to my liking.  I only spent a minimal amount of time setting it up for about 10 different areas of the country and have removed those I don't want to look at, like New York.  The HD part did not play any role in my decision to download this app, but it is pretty good graphics and the content is useful.  I use several different Weather sources on the different Computers, tablets and phone I have and each has something to offer, but at this point I still prefer having the ability to call up maps with active content occasionally, such as temp maps, active radar and Storm and Lightning tracking available on other weather apps on my Computer.  I spend much more time at my computer, than on my Kindle Fire Tablet or my smart phone.  Preference is a personal thing and relative to how you use an app and how you have developed the proper use of it.

I haven't even had a chance to start using this, and it has already started begging for more money. I installed it on my computer right after I bought it. It found my bank but couldn't - for some unknown reason please try again later - download my data. Basically told me tough luck: export a csv from the bank and then try to import it. Well, great. I figured I'd get back to it when I had an extra day or so to figure it out.

Now, Quicken is sending me notices that my subscription is about to expire. WHAT subscription? It's been less than 60 days on what is supposed to be a 14 month product, and I did not sign up for ANYthing.

I stopped using Quickbooks years ago because it was little better than ransomware. I had hoped Quicken would be better. Apparently it's just as bad. If it had worked as expected, it would have been worth the money and not a penny more, It didn't, and it already has it's hand out. I wish I had never bought this.

I used this program for several years in a specialized school.  The first year, I didn't tell any of my team teachers about the software.  I approached it like an experiment.  Within a few weeks, teachers began to ask me what I was doing differently.  They saw a marked difference in my students' focus and retention.  I only used it a few minutes (10-15 minutes maximum) two or three times a week.  The students varied the type of activity daily as instructed.  It is not for entertainment and should be used in short segments.  I highly recommend this program for building retention, focus, and auditory skills.

I've used TurboTax for decades to keep the CPA honest and more specifically to understand what the CPA tells me. My taxes are complex because the regs and laws are like hyper-complex as I have to consolidate `S' corp entities.

So my CPA sent the final package to me this week and I compare it to the TurboTax 2011 that I've scratch-papered while the accountants work away. A couple entities don't use Intuit based accounting. I have to manually transpose the #'s to TurboTax. For the QuickBook accounted entities, it's like magic to import into TurboTax.

2011 was a complex year as I plumbed the `loss' side of the tax code. I have a good mental image of the code as it applies to `profits'. I've never had a loss to account for. I expected to find a difference in the `quality' of TurboTax's underpinnings for the increased number of forms that accompany `loss' reporting (it costs CPA $ to account for a `loss' ... indeed an irony if there ever was)

The score between TurboTax and the high $$$ CPA? To $mall to mention. TurboTax adroitly managed the correct new forms. If I didn't worry about IRS and state biz audits and wanting someone sitting beside me, I'd not pay the outrageous CPA fee.

The folks at TuboTax are masters of discovering tiny ways to improve the product. The TurboTax price goes down year over year while the IRS complexity goes up. You can learn a lot using TurboTax yourself. Such a deal! ... 5-stars.

update 3/20 ... I don't get the download problem reviewers are citing. This particular product version I'm reviewing is the hard disk and not the download version. I just checked again. There can't be any confusion. Amazon says only 10 left at this moment. Is Amazon combining reviews?

I use the Kindle for PC quite often and it does work. However, many features still haven't been implemented in the PC reader that are standard on the "Cloud" readers. (iPad, Kindle fire, etc.)

Two examples are "Kindle in Motion" and "Collections". Kindle in Motion is a kitschy feature with pictures on pages that move. Not really needed for reading, but could be nice depending on the book.

Collections, however become critical when your library gets into the 100's of books. Right now, you cannot sync Collections from or to the PC reader from any other reader. WHY???

The other minor annoyance is that the Kindle for PC does NOT flag you when there is a newer version available. There isn't even a manual option to do so. This is the redheaded stepchild reader...it's available only so they can say kindle is across all platforms.

This app is supposed to integrate with ancestry.com and other genealogy data bases world-wide. I've been using ancestry.com for most of my research and before buying liked the idea that it this app integrated and worked with ancestry.

The Good:
--This app does a reasonably good job of accurately importing a GEDCOM file from ancestry.com
--The price is reasonable
--It is easy to start using it straight away
--Easily incorporates pictures to associate with family members
--Narratives are easy to add

The Bad:
--When this app attempts to link to your ancestry.com account it sends ancestry to a page or pages that no longer exist on ancestry's site. If I log into ancestry first things seem to work together and I'm still after a couple of months unable to verify this for sure.
--On both my Windows 7 computers the app hangs intermittently causing me to sometimes lose work
--The best view is the one showing your direct ancestors. A family view either does not exist or is so well hidden I haven't been able to find it. This makes pruning the tree impossible
--It can create some reasonably good looking reports, although multiple picture content is a kluge and report customization is very, very limited.
--It does not automatically sync with anything you do on ancestry. You end up creating a GEDCOM on ancestry and importing it into this app. This means everything you've done like adding pictures and stories in this app need to be done over in the new GEDCOM download from ancestry

The Ugly:
It is fair to preface this section by noting that most of what's ugly is not really the fault of this app. If I do think something in the app is ugly in and of itself I'll preface the point with ** instead of the -- I usually use.
--GEDCOM files contain the bare bones of your family tree. When you import them from a source like ancestry you get the tree and none of the pictures you've put into your ancestry work. Neither do your stories from ancestry come over. This is due to the structure of the GEDCOM file and not this app.
--When you import a GEDCOM into Family Tree Heritage Platinum 8, you will have to re-add any pictures, stories and other media content that may have been part of a tree created in ancestry or other on-line apps.
**This app does not, or cannot integrate two versions of a tree and it is possible for you to end up with more than one if you've juggled multiple GEDCOMs
**The ability to merge duplicates in a tree is touted as a selling point for this app. So far, even after using Help and experimenting I cannot get it to work properly. It still seems to leave the duplicate information

How This App Stacks Up Against Others:
Honestly I have not tried every app in this category. I've tried a few and am currently testing another. They are all mostly the same and none of them really sparkle unless tightly and properly integrated with ancestry or another on-line platform. The major difference among apps in this category is price and not functionality.

In spite of any of the foregoing, this app is a reasonably good buy in its category. If you plan to use it as a way to keep the on-line platforms from sucking on you wallet every month this app or others in the category can do that for you.

Use an on-line app to create your tree, go as far back as you can, don't add picture, story and other media content on-line as it will not come through an export with any of those items in tact. Use the on-line platforms to build as complete a skeleton as you wish and once you've got what you wanted create a GEDCOM, export it from ancestry or other on-line platform and import this into Family Tree Heritage Platinum 8 then add your media content, produce your reports and so on. Just remember to get everything you need done on the on-line platform before you import and start adding media content. Otherwise you get to start over a lot, really a lot.

Final Note For Those Of Us Hooked On This Tree Building Stuff:
Once you've got the basic skeleton and as much information about your near and distant relatives it is likely you will find more and better, even more extensive background information, stories and pictures of relatives, their wills, and so on by using the Wikipedia stuff that comes up on a well written Google search. I found I got better information from Wikipedia the further back I worked than I did from ancestry. I am not saying it's not on ancestry; I just didn't have any luck finding it to the depth I did with a good Wikipedia article.

I have used Turbo Tax for several years, it just does not get any easier than this for taxes. You can follow the guided program when entering information or chose what to work on. Following the guided program steps you through the entire return, advises you as to what you are entering, and calculates out the taxes across different scenarios for most people. There is also the ability to import different forms like W2's, Investment statements and such. There is also a state tax version included with this package which automatically uses your entered information to complete your state return. And as someone with income earned over several states the ability to buy state programs to file these correctly is wonderful.
Turbo Tax will also import earlier versions of Turbo Tax from prior years to assist in filling out your information, and provides you a snap shot of your prior years information in comparison to this years entries, this helps key you to any items you may have missed. It also provides you a indication of your audit probability based on your entries. The great thing about Turbo Tax is the elimination of stupid errors like math errors, missing information like SSN, and such which will trigger audits.
About the only issue I have is Turbo Tax is always modifying their program with updates, but that could be due to changes in tax law.........

Office 2007 is good. It is pretty slick.

But the real story is also the story of my life:

After years of using an antique Office 2000 and then trying to make do with Sun OpenOffice (close, but no cigar, especially translating PowerPoint briefings), I bit and bought not one but two copies of Office Home and Student 2007 because each was under $80.

Not a month later, after, as I said, years of procrastination, my Company announced they would be moving the entire enterprise to Office 2007 and that employees were eligible to purchase home-use copies for...wait for it...$20.

As Dr. Smith said so long ago, "Oh, the pain...."

I wish these guys would really be straightforward with you and not charge you extra for every little thing, like filing, but I'm too lazy and unmotivated to fill out a paper form (probably because I've been using this for so long).  Even so, most tax advisors won't actually save you money, so why not just use a machine.  And I'm guessing - just guessing - that you're less likely to be audited if you're just trying to get it done with software rather than risking mistakes - intentional or otherwise - if you do it by hand.

I wanted this Easy CD & DVD Burning software to edit and arrange songs, then burn them on CDs that can be played anywhere (CD-Rs). I've been using it for 2-3 months now - editing and burning music CDs is one of my hobbies and this is adequate for me. I'm guessing that this is not a product that professional DJs would use - it's probably too basic tor them.
I had an older version which started crashing, so I bought this version. Be aware that if you saved 'projects' on any older versions, they do not work well on new versions, so you will have to start over with your source music files (this was told to me by the Corel/Roxio technical support I had to contact because of a crashing problem on this newer software version with previous versions saved projects on older software version).
Anyway, this newer version works well with new projects. Occasionally, there is a music file that may be corrupted and causes problems, but I can work around that. I like the editing part ("Sound Editor") of this software to allow my creativity to kick in. You can select the entire song or segments ("clips") of the song to enhance, add or remove parts, or do various other things with it, then re-save it ("export") and add it to a project in "Music Disc Creator" to become part of your new CD you create. The "Music Disc Creator" part also has basic editing tools, that are good for minor tweaks, such as fading, volume control, Bass, treble adjustments, etc.
Before getting this, I downloaded the trial versions of "Acid Music Studio 10.0" and "Dexster Audio Editor 4.2". I did not like them because they were too complex for me. Maybe because I was used to the simplicity of Roxio product, I decided to stay with Roxio.
I have not used the Video or Photo portions of this software yet.

I bought this because I wanted the disk encryption feature that is not available with the usual home version. But now Microsoft's implementation wants one of those new systems with a chip or something to store the key. As this is an older system, I would have to use a USB drive to hold the key. Sheesh. Even LINUX has encrypted disks for free and with no hassle. Also note the privacy implications of the questions you are asked in setup. If you try to keep your real name and identifying details to a minimum on the internet, Windows takes all that stuff and then labels you with this actual data. Goodbye privacy. When you set up your system, use a fake name, age, etc and set up a new anonymous email that you use only for logging into windows. Otherwise, installation was more or less quick, easy, and foolproof, and windows 8 beats Vista (except for that silly live pane stuff you have to click through). Also, I've figured out how to summon the "charms" (new and dumber terminology), so now things work, but nothing is obvious in windows 8. It's like an adventure game, trying to figure the magic words.

this one died regularly when I tried to use it.  I did all the things they recommended to keep it running but no success.  I finally just cancelled the purchase . Too bad.  A good idea, a bad implementation

If you bought a Mac anytime recently, you should already have a version of iLife as most Macs come with it. This includes iPhoto for weak photo editing and gallery creation (like Google's Picasa), GarageBand for music production, iWeb for posting your media/life to the web, iMovie for video editing and iDVD for burning to a DVD.

Most of these are decent apps. GarageBand is well considered, and if you want to do video podcasts, iMovie is okay. It's not as powerful as Final Cut (Pro/Express), but pretty good. iDVD is not a great DVD writer, but it does the job. Here are some of the limitations in iLife 09:

iWeb doesn't let you edit the page theme (template) HTML in any meaningful way, and you can't create new templates.

iMovie does not have any fancy effects. Previous versions did; they were removed in 08.

iMovie does not integrate well to iDVD - you must "Share" media between them, which can take 4x real time on a current-version MacBook laptop.

iDVD cannot set arbitrary chapter markers. (You do that in iMovie.) To set Titles, you must import the clips separately. But iMovie can't "Share to iDVD" more than one clip at a time so you have to do non-standard stuff for that.

(This means that if you want to put video onto DVD, e.g. from your camera or digitized from your VCR, you must import it into iMovie - takes a long time and transcodes - do the editing, chapter markers, etc., - and then export to iDVD which again takes a long time and does more quality-reducing transcoding, create the menus and structure, and then burn it - another set of transcoding. Very time consuming and lots of transcoding.)

Most of these programs support "Themes", but the Themes aren't easily editable.

iDVD does not support BluRay.

In fairness, the primary video alternatives are programs like Roxio's Toast and "DVD Creator", neither of which support real editing or manual chapter markers at all and have less impressive menu structures. But both of those do support BluRay.

So the real question isn't whether iLife is useful, but whether an upgrade from iLife 08 (or whatever version you have) to iLife 09 is worth the cost.

It is a great package for doing accounting and tracking expenditure. There are a few quirks and procedures that are difficult to find and/or are not available where it would be nice if it had a better help section or manual.

In March 2007 I tried three leading a/v-firewall suites on the same XP system image: Norton Internet Security 2007, ZoneAlarm Internet Security, and Kaspersky Internet Security 6.0.

I chose Kaspersky, have loved it ever since, and recently upgraded (free) to version 7.0 - an even better product.

About my comparative evaluation in March 2007
---------------------------------------------
I was using NIS 2004 (annually renewed, daily updated, fearful to move to a later version due to strongly negative customer reviews). Yet I could not keep it forever, as it lacked malware and root kit support (which I augmented from other sources). Plus (another motivation for upgrade): I grew weary of yet another month of Symantec's "Critical!" failure alarms that announced "in 4 weeks you will expire" - a tremendously bad design choice because you tend to overlook !!RED!! general alarms for several weeks' time, and you could easily overlook a legitimate security problem if one were to occur. (All that, just for Symantec marketing purposes.)

So I needed a vanilla PC platform, stripped of my previous A/V-firewall suite, to make the testing fair. Uninstalling NIS 2004 was a huge ordeal requiring several days of trial and error. (It truly looked like Symantec deliberately designed this to be so daunting that the faint of heart would give up, and stay with Symantec.) Add/Remove Programs doesn't remove the product nor its services, and while there were two tiers of Symantec tech support uninstall tools, the first is hugely ineffective and actually leaves the Symantec service still installed and running - yet the second tool actually strips out much of the operating system, including required support for Virtual PC, MSXML, and BITS (reportedly the solution is to reapply XP's SP2 plus all subsequent hardware and software patches). Sadly, Symantec corporate's "NoNav" removal tool is unavailable for the NIS product line. Ultimately I followed this published third-party tip: use Add/Remove Programs, reboot, then perform a detailed search of the drive and registry for things named "Symantec" and "Norton" - and delete them by hand. I removed about 70 registry references; fortunately I'm experienced with XP's registry and can recognize when to remove a 'value' versus a 'rule.'

Evaluation experiences:

Norton Internet Security 2007

++ Pro:
* 1. Flashy, fun, highly automated, set and forget it.
* 2. Highly rated by reviewers.

-- Con:
* 1. I had become a master at configuring NIS 2004 firewall exception rules, yet I remained completely stumped how to customize NIS 2007 for websites I trust but which NIS 2007 restricts. (Still don't know if such customization is actually available. It must be.)
* 2. Twice the executable dropped (failed). Symantec said they fixed this error, but only for paying customers, and they wouldn't distribute the fix to trial-download customers. This left me with no possible way to evaluate whether the fix actually worked before buying the product. How dumb. Unacceptable. Dead ended, I moved on.

ZoneAlarm Internet Security

++ Pro:
* 1. Great GUI, nice integration of features, great logging features.
* 2. Highly rated by reviewers.

-- Con:
* 1. Unstable operation. Installed twice on top of the same PC image, it behaved differently each time: sometimes it would leave Windows Firewall enabled, sometimes disabled. Sometimes logging would work, sometimes not. That sort of thing.
* 2. A known corruption in the ZIS database halts event logging, but even when logging worked, log detail was so underreported that I could not gauge product effectiveness.
* 3. Managing and overriding firewall restrictions on trusted websites was ponderous, requiring multiple tiers of screens for each and every website, including the requirement to perform a visual search against all previously customized websites plus all websites visited during this session - which sadly you cannot sort by timestamp nor by the parent portion of the website name, only by prefix - so good luck finding your website in that growing list!
* 4. Days into use of the product, when the learning mode switched as predicted to restricted mode, PC operations became seriously bogged down, and stayed that way.
* 5. The VSMON.exe engine (the main executable of the product) repeatedly failed, stopping the service with a popup memory error alarm. This may or may not drop the firewall protection - I couldn't tell.
* 6. Scan time is slow.

Kaspersky Internet Security

++ Pro:
* 1. This product is geared for geeks and gear-heads who want and expect a lot of initial interaction to teach the firewall the rules for operation. And it really performs and excels in that capacity.
* 2. Yet there is also a "typical" setup mode which I did not try, myself - for less technical users. I can't evaluate it, not having tried this mode.
* 3. Detailed logs - exhaustive, reliable, searchable by field, selectable between major versus all events, exportable to other formats.
* 4. Adding websites to the trusted list is a simple, convenient operation.
* 5. Your custom configuration settngs can then be exported and later imported again.
* 6. Scan status is a piece of art compared to the competition: Kaspersky shows you the scan percentage completed on the task bar icon, and if you open the scan it shows you the start time and the predicted completion time.
* 7. If you're not afraid of understanding firewall and antivirus/antispyware product operation, this product is a dream.
* 8. Flashy, fun, highly automated: configure it, and as it matures with use, you just set and forget it.
* 9. Web support and web forums are the best, most helpful among these three publishers. Plus the Web forum posts aren't filled with frustrated, tear-my-hair-out complaints (a good sign).
* 10. Very stable operation. Not seeing bugs! (That's unique among the three.)
* 11. Highly rated by reviewers.

-- Con:
* 1. Price is highest of the three.
* 2. I'm seeing some sluggishness with some of my web use at times. I'll keep watching this. Generally though, among these three products, Kaspersky runs neck and neck with, or slightly behind, Norton Internet Security. And Kaspersky blows the socks off ZoneAlarm.

Conclusion:
Hands down, I was absolutely thrilled with Kaspersky's operation, low resource utilization, terrific logging, extreme ease of customization, and high regard in the industry in terms of its reportedly high level of effectiveness. By day two I was sold - and I ordered it and took advantage of Amazon's rebate to reduce the cost.

Update:
The above tests pertained to the KIS 6.0 version. Version 7.0 only improves on product features (adding Root Kit scans, a more attractive GUI, and a bit more customization). Highly recommended.

Perfect? No. I experienced one false positive alert that cost me some investigation time; that's not unique to any publisher, in my experience. I just have to disclose it here.

Bottom line: solid, stable, responsive, customizable, and a pleasure to use.
Can't figure out how to get it to work.  No documentation to speak of.

UPDATE Feb 2018: A recent software update changed something so I can no longer Import videos. As that's critical to make my very-basic product review videos (no menus, FX, etc.), this made the product useless for me. I was also less impressed with the program when I tried to do more than the very basic functions.

I received the disc version. You still need an internet connection to download all the updates, etc. for the program, plus the User Guide and training videos are only available online. This worked fine for me, but why even bother with the disc if you're still so dependent on downloading from the internet? I have an older, Windows 7 computer that meets the minimum requirements of the software. I'm intending to use the software for some product reviews, youtube-type videos, and maybe some editing of family phone-camera videos. It looks like this software can very easily do anything I want. The software started up with a demo project so you can see what goes where and what can be done with the program. After simply looking this demo project over (no training), I was able to do a new project where I imported a tablet-camera short video, shorten the video to desired clip instead of the whole length, and export it in the size and format I wished. It was quick and pretty simple to figure out. I'll be going through the User Guide next, and I'll update relatively soon with how more complex projects + training turn out.

I like it.  There are enough ad supported "free" puzzles to provide some variety and sufficient choices of pieces for play, including the option to rotate pieces for added difficulty.  I do wish to state that I am playing this android app on windows devices using "Bluestacks" which is available for download on the internet. Bluestacks does take some "figuring out" to enable the implementation of game downloads from the Google and Amazon game/app stores and later loading of the games -- but the effort is worth-while.

I got this flashlight ap and at first I didn't notice anything. i chose the light I wanted and used it. Then I noticed that after a few seconds of nothing unusual on the screen you start to see ads running at the bottom. As time goes by, the ads increase. When I bought the ap, I didn't pay attention to what access it might need because I thought "What things could this ap really need." I can't believe how many ads were running on my phone that I would not want to be running. I just want a flashlight. I don't want to be used as somewhere to run someone's ads. I don't want someone to be making money off of me every time I use it. It's a nice little scheme, but I won't be a part of it.

I'm sure most people will never notice the ads due to the fact that they run as you are facing it away from yourself. Beware. Don't just not use it and install another one. Uninstall it. If you don't, it will be tracking you and more and gaining money or points for you just owning it still. It has use of your personal information and more things that it should not need access to. There are free flashlights with no hidden agenda. Good luck.

WebPlus X4 makes great claims, but fails to live up to them.

If you are making an informational web site, it will do a fine job once you master the unusual structural methods. I previously built a web site from scratch with only a basic HTML editor so expected quickly to master WebPlus. Not so. Previous experience with HTML is neither necessary nor helpful. Documentation is poor, as is typical of most software, so one struggles to figure out how to make the web site work. Fortunately there is fairly good support on the Serif web site in its forums.

I bought WebPlus x4 specifically to construct a website on which merchandise would be sold. I selected it because it claimed "Using e-commerce to sell online has never been easier" and boasted that it "teamed up with the top shopping cart providers . . . ." What I found, however, is that, when used with its free shopping carts, it is totally inadequate for anyone who has a limited supply of items, and thus must integrate inventory controls to prevent a single item from being twice purchased. It may be adequate if one is selling items available in unlimited quantities, such as downloadable software or goods purchased from a drop shipper. It could be tied to a shopping cart other than one of the free ones with which it readily links, or a really good web site developer could build a cart to go with it, but you would be spending a lot in addition to the cost of WebPlus to get the e-commerce capability.

It was after struggling at great length with WebPlus X4 on the basic web site design that I discovered its inadequacies. In searching for a low-cost solution I found that there are several free, or near free, shopping cart programs that are far better suited for selling online. I selected zencart due to the many templates available to customize its appearance. While not for the timid or technically challenged, I have found that zencart does, for free, nearly everything that WebPlus can do, plus can actually handle online sales of goods.

So if you are putting together a web site to give information, consider WebPlus X4. But if you are planning to sell goods from your web site, either go for another software package or plan on spending a lot on supplemental software.

I first started using Corel Draw back when I purchased a license of Corel Draw 2. The last full version that I bought and used was Corel Draw 8, not counting the home and student license of X5 I bought (but never really used) during one of Amazon's Gold Box Deal sales. I got away from using it at that point when I got assigned to teach a digital graphics & animation class where they were using Adobe's Photoshop 5 and Illustrator 10. I eventually bought myself a license of Adobe Creative Suite 4 Design Premium to use at home. And have since upgraded to their Creative Suite 5.5 - Design Premium. But now that Adobe has chosen to eliminate physical updates in favor for digital downloads (and maybe even going to an online application) I'm looking to go back to using Corel Draw. Nearly everything I need to do can be done with this new version of Photo-Paint and Corel Draw X7. You can even set the programs GUIs to mimic Photoshop's and Illustrator's.

The one thing that I have not liked about this new package - is that the content libraries are all on-line now. I found them to be a bit clunky to navigate and somewhat confusing as to what the three different badges were suppose to represent. I couldn't find any reference to one of the badges in any of the online documentation. Not really a big deal for me, as I hardly ever use the stock clip-art, and I still have all the stuff that came with my previous versions of the software.

The PDF manual that you can download is adequate for getting up to speed on the software - but I will be purchasing the CorelDRAW X7 The Official Guide (if and when it becomes available).

I like the new versions of the software, with maybe the exception of the Website Creator package. First off, it doesn't come on the installation disc - you have to download it off the internet. And it doesn't install with the software packages. But I think the main reason I didn't have a positive feeling about it, is when you open it you are presented a matrix of squares much like the new Windows 8 GUI. That really turned me off. Again, not that big a deal as I don't really do a lot of web page creating these days - and when I do, I like to code things by hand using plain HTML. Maybe later I can play with the Website Creator software and see if I can get past the look of the opening screen.

Over all I would say Corel's done an good job with this new release. I'd recommend it.

You can find lots of information with it.  You can do some interesting math with it.  Taking a group of data from it and analyzing it?  Not so much - at least not easily.  Documentation is lacking other than for very simple examples.  Lots of promise, but it's got a long way to go.  I'll try it again in a year.

I bought Office '11 for Mac hoping to use Outlook for my enterprise email accounts with a couple of clients. Unfortunately, this Outlook is not backwardly compatible with older versions of Exchange Server before ES 2007, SP1. My clients haven't upgraded and so it is totally useless for me. I now am back using Entourage for exchange email. The new outlook doesn't suport syncing with Ical, but my experiences with syncing Entourage across Ical and some other applications was disastrous. I had so many problems with Ical, Itunes and Entourage that Apple recommended that I do a clean reinstall of the OS and my apps. I traced the problem to Entourage appointments made through exchange meeting invitations trying to sync to Ical. I turned off the sync function and haven't had any problems since. I have to manually enter calendar entries into Ical. I can sync my Entourage calendar created with one exchange account on the Iphone, but that calendar won't sync to Ical. But I can live with that. I need to use Ical because I sync it with Daylite and Billings to track billable time and invoice client, and there's nothing that will do that with Office, unlike the PC world where programs like Act can give you customer relationship capabilities.

Word 2008 was very buggy for me when trying to edit highly tableized documents. Often I ran into crashes and issues with editing. Word 2011 seems to work better with tables so far. I still run into problems with clients who say that they can't open the Mac based Word documents on their PC.s But I can usually work around that, by saving the files in the .doc format.

I like the opening screens that allow me to access recently opened documents from a desktop like environment. I did like the toolbox in the older version of excel. But the tabbed functions across the top of word allow me to more rapidly access functions without going through the drop down menus, so that's a plus.

Except for the outlook issues, Office 2011 is an improvement and worth the price of the new software.
Use for tracking my spending for retirement and is fast and easy, after get some data entered.

I purchased Pinnacle's Dazzle DVC90 and Studio Plus 10. I am using a 6 month old computer with the latest XP updates, Intel Core 2 2.14 GHz processor, 2 GB memory, and a raid drive.

It took me 2 days of installs, uninstalls, updates, etc. to even get the capture to work. Plus I got lucky and discovered that my final problem was the volume control on the source camcorder. Turn the volume off.

As a computer professional and as someone who has authored installations with Installshield I had some advatanges. I could see all of the problems being created by Pinnacles lousy install programmers. There uninstalls basicly do almost no cleanup. I had to run Registry cleaners and even Microsoft's Windows Installer Cleanup Utility repeatedly. I had to search for and delete dozens of Pinnacle files. I ran there registry deletes, my registry cleanups, there multiple patches, etc., etc. Over and over again.

I did finally capture video and edit video. Life was looking up.

Yet on my fourth day I have yet to burn a DVD. Hangs. Does it even finish rendering? Can't tell! No software feedback. No action log. And once it hangs you have to re-boot. No idea on this yet. But each time I try takes over an hour and uses up about 30 GB of disk for one 3.99 GB DVD write. And where is the documentation describing the various temp files and what can be deleted after a failed DVD burn?

Enough. I could go on and on. Why should I say more? If you are not convinced to look elsewhere ...

Follow-up: I've been using this software since March 2015 to chop up videos of some motorcycle trips and generally just goofing around with my AS100 and AS200. I learned the software has a whole lot more capabilities than I'm able to understand or take advantage of. In my mind I kind of know what I want but translating that to a final result using this Movie Studio has become a challenge for me. I'm enjoying every moment of it. I may only be using this software at or just above the level that free software is capable of but I'm constantly trying new things out. The end result is often gimmicky but that's just me.

I like the way I can select an exact frame then synchronize the lyrics of a song or some funny sound effect to that point. I like how the suite provides additional editing programs (sound, music and a DVD assembler?) that seem to have plenty of stand-alone capabilities. They act a little more like separate programs (think Word, Excel, Powerpoint) rather than a single integrated program (old MS Works). This means the transition between programs isn't always the smoothest but also I think it means each program has more control over its function.

I'm a little confused by all the different video formats available. I never know what is the right one to select. Often I have to do multiple renders. I uploaded one video for an Amazon review I did on the AS100 skeleton frame. It looked perfect on my computer but very pixelated when I see it on Amazon. I'm thinking if this program was geared a little more towards casual users there might be some wizards included for common projects. You can render for Youtube or Facebook but it looks like the software must upload the actual video to these services. I'd prefer it saved to disk in an appropriate format and I'll upload when I've reviewed it (I pay for each byte on my Internet service, must be VERY careful with video - second tries cost money). A series of wizards for project workflows might actually help with the education process.

I'm not so happy with the community support. It suffers the same problem as Microsoft's support or any big company "community" support. It is often difficult to explain the problem. Sony official responses are often canned responses from people reading from a script. User responses are often condescending or misunderstand the original point. This is typical and I don't let it affect my judgement of the product.

I do find a series of tutorials on YouTube quite helpful. These are titled "The Full Guide for Sony Movie Studio 13" produced by a user named "bai" and really do a fine job of introducing the basics plus. The narrator's accent is a little distracting but she is clear and the content is helpful.

I've not found any book (Dummies-style) that might be helpful. I'll keep up that search.

When you register the program you get a couple of free downloads. One will not run properly on my laptop. This is the NewBlue Titler EX. It says I don't have a capable video card. This is a MSI GT70-2OD. Plenty capable. The problem with the laptop is the high-end video card only kicks in when needed. Apparently the software checks before the better video card is running and fails to load because of that. I've been able to trick it a few times but find that it is a pain and the add-on isn't that useful for me anyway. The other addon, HitFilm provides some fun video effects to play with and isn't a pain to run.

So - after some use I still recommend Sony Movie Studio 13 Suite. It does the basic editing any program does but has tons of headroom to add new features to my videos as I learn more. The price for the suite seems very reasonable to me.

----

Really pleased with the whole suite of software. I only work on videos to torture my friends and family and this gives me a whole lot of tools to play with. I have no training or experience working with videos but Movie Studio makes things rather intuitive. I've been able to add interesting (to me) titles, sound effects, music tracks, fades, and even cool things with pan and crop. Now I think I want to learn more.

I downloaded the trial version and after a short time decided to purchase. Because the trial version is fully functional I ordered the disk version of the program. I always prefer to have disks available and didn't miss out on using the program immediately because of the trial.

Lots to learn but lots of fun too.

I think it's important for anyone considering buying this program to know that this program hasn't been updated since 1995. I have been trying to use this program in conjunction with Illustrator 10 and I've had a myriad of problems. I have found it very difficult to import EPS files created in Illustrator 10. "Cut and paste" (the recommended method for importing vector data) doesn't seem to work at all. After hours of experimentation I finally figured out how to import an EPS created in Illustrator 10 (save the file as an Illustrator 5.0 document with no preview image, as a postscript version 2, and without any thumbnail) but then I couldn't get it to export a properly working font. Every time I use the font and then try to save the file in Illustrator or generate a PDF from InDesign I get a warning that states that the font can't be embedded due to be protected, even though the font permissions are set to give full access to any user. I researched the problem and found that there is a Microsoft extension module that can be installed to permit additional control over font permissions, and it apparently has solved this problem for a few people running Windows ME, but I cannot get it to work on XP. I am a huge fan of Macromedia's Flash, and I am deeply disappointed to see such a crappy product being sold for $349! Macromedia very recently lowered the price to around $300, but it's still far too much to pay for a ten-year-old program that fails to deliver on its intended purpose. I would strongly encourage anyone considering this program to look elsewhere for a product to meet their need. This product is too outdated to be of much use to modern designers.

So glad something like this has finally come along!  I have tried numerous products over the years with no success.  I have even had my VHS transferred by professional companies.  Quality has been lacking.  The tape that was almost priceless to me kept coming back with the look of wobbly tracking lines, color and sound distortion, etc.  I have AT LAST been able to transfer this 'precious' video!  Thanks so much from a Grandma that is now VERY happy!!!
No issues so far. I have ripped several CDs. Once you set up the settings it's pretty easy to use. I used it for awhile free before I decided it would be worth the updates and documentation.

Free vs paid: at first glance, there are not many differences. But as you start using it you find more and more.
Stability. Corel has not once crashed. Sometimes the free ones do, losing your work. Very frustrating
Support. If you need help, Corel is there. The free apps? Sometimes, usually not though.
Polished finish. Most of the free ones that I have used feel slapped together, there are gaps in paths and logic.
Advertising. When I am working, I do not want to accidentally click on an advert that will launch an Angry Birds advert interrupting what I am doing!
Quality and options for output. Corel is far ahead when it comes to formats and quality.

All the above tells you that you need Corel.
And I have not even started on the quality of the tools, the ease of use, the documentation. If you are a keen amateur or a professional of any grade, you need the Corel solution.

I purchased this somewhat circumspectly - fully expecting to have to return it due to implementation issues. But I can say this has changed our lives. We have 5 children at home, and a home office, and at anytime there can be 20+ devices connected to our network. Trying to control Internet access for children was almost impossible and the idea of putting apps on every device and then managing those in decentralized way was completely unviable.

This product does that in a smooth and efficient way. It connects to our Cox access point via a bridge connection to the existing modem/router. I have seen no drop in network performance and the WiFi coverage is excellent in a large two story home. It was a very smooth set up.

I can now instantly turn off and on access for each child, set daily limits, and set content filters - all remotely and even when not home.

All in all it was worth every dollar and has made a huge difference to our family.
I was the guy at neighborhood parties who was clueless about home values. Not any more.  Im not buying or selling, but just tracking values.  It's nice to know what's going on.

I was very gratified with how easily the product transferred video from my 8-mm camera to my computer. I could see the video on my monitor and set-up was very easy.
I would say there are two downsides to the product:

(1) The documentation - e.g. it does not explain the file types that are created in the process, nor does it explain well what it means to save a "project". It would have been very helpful to see an overview of the file types that are created as you go from video tape to file on the computer to file types ready for burning to a DVD. I still haven't figured out how to burn the DVD-type files to a DVD by retrieving the DVD-files created somewhere on my computer.

(2)The editor provided is somewhat limited in scope, and is very labor-intensive when you want to insert DVD "chapters". There is no time counter, so you have to basically replay the whole tape to find the sections where you want to insert chapters. The program file-naming conventions are confusing, especially when you build up multiple files. And make SURE the program "sees" your DVD burner before you try to burn a DVD.

However, having said all this, the product does the job - just be prepared to spend quite a few hours of trial-and-error before you figure out all the nuances (e.g. I got "burned" one time because I did not realize that the program EITHER writes to a DVD OR creates a set of DVD files on your hard disk. NOT BOTH AT THE SAME TIME, as you might have expected.)
It became evident that they had shorted the editor in order to encourage you to spend another $60 to get what is presumably an easier-to-use editor. However, once I figured out how to slice out worthless sections of the video, I got a nice set of clean segments (I was editing old videos of my son's wrestling matches). There are some cute "transitions" you can enter between tape segments - but note that these transitions are NOT the same as inserting "Chapters". There was no explanation of how to best relate Transitions to inserted Chapters.

No way to view camera photos and videos on web.
No Google integration, you have to go to IFTTT route for google assistant/home arm/disarm commands.
No way to schedule individual cameras

No proper REST API documentation to access camera stream, so you cannot create a 3rd pray app of web interface for to overcome the shortcomings of the product team.

If you have everything Amazon/Alexa in your home it should be a good pick. But if you have Amazon Ring and Amazon blink systems, just be aware both are incompatible with each other, so you must have to have multiple apps.
Last but not least, the delay to get video from camera is up to 20 seconds to start the live stream, this actually kills the 2 way audio communication, so very bad performance.
Video quality is acceptable.

This app is a legitimate lifesaver.  I had been looking for a good budgeting app for some time, and this has helped me beyond measure in tracking and sticking to my budget.  I highly recommend it.  I love it so much I upgraded to Pro to get rid of the Ads and enable recurring transactions.  One of the best apps I have used that helps with my real life.
Poor implementation. Some ridiculous changes that make it difficult to use. Poor service from support group.

I'm beginning to make my own MP3 audio guides for travel and my granddaughter suddenly developed an interest in creating her own rap lyrics. For the life of me, I could not make the Windows 7 Home Premium voice recorder software work (no output.) As a non-expert user, NCH 'saved my sanity.' I did the free trial download from this Australian company's web site and carefully experimented with my output settings in Windows and it works very well. I hopped right on purchasing a licensed version (plus mailed CD.) I also purchased NCH's Recordpad the same way. With just the downloads, I was ready to go. Downloading was not a problem, even for me. The CD backup came in good time (about 7 days.) Just as soon as I purchased the software, I got technical support and response was prompt and helpful (via e-mail.)

Open a file in Recordpad, press record (the red sun-like button on the toolbar)and you will be prompted for a filename. Press the red button again and you are recording. Do whatever and when you finish for the first time select the option that lets you finish the file and exit. Otherwise, you will continually be prompted that the file is in use when you try to do something with it. So much for Recordpad: if you want to add to the file or to clean it up, you need Wavepad. All the record pad controls are available there and the files created in Recordpad can be found in the open file tab. I find that I spend most of my time in Wavepad, inserting content and editing out dead space or poorly read content. Wavepad also works on other audio material to remove hiss, clicks and background sounds, if you use the utility (see sidebar menu)for that purpose. I quickly adapted to Wavepad's oscilloscope green sound wave screen, where you will be doing your work. Navigation backward and forward through the file can be speedily accomplished in several ways so it pays to experiment a bit and let intuition be your guide.

As with most modern software, documentation is sparse. Half the time I tried the help tab, I found myself in Windows help. (I'm not a 'power user.') I did find help topics eventually that pointed me in the right direction. Semi-pro sound editors or advanced beginners with experience elsewhere will likely do better than I did.

My audio files downloaded readily to my MP3 player using the 'send to' file option in Windows. I would buy this program again!

I have several Nuance products and though I know Omnipage 18 is getting a little old, I decided to revisit it when OCR'ing some documents. I am going to clear up a mystery here: what is Omnipage for.

If you use Google drive, Adobe Acrobat, Nuance PDF Creator or Power PDF, those packages will scan and OCR a documents for you; no problem. You can then copy and paste text and images from those documents, search them, or whatever.

Omipage is finicky about OCR and requires alot of editing, the user interface is confusing, etc., but it does excel at one thing: finding discrete portions of a document or image and grouping them together so you can extract the text. For instance, Omnipage is great for scanning a magazine artricle or recipe that has multiple columns or formatting. It will lump the text from a colum together, instead of lumping it with the column next to it. I have had this software for some time and still have no use for it, but having finally experimented some, I think that is where is "shines".

If you just need to scan documents and convert them to Word or some other format, you are better off with another package.

I give it three stars because of its limited use, confusing GUI, terms ("workflow" being the main one), and menus. However, at less than 50 bucks and given its focus, is rises to 3 stars from where I would have ranked it (2). 1 star, for me, is something that simply doesn't work or crashed my computer. 2 is "works" but not well, and not for me. 3, it works and I can see the value, and the price is right, but not for me. 4, I use it alot and because I do I have some suggestions for making it better but it gets the job done winningly. 5, perfect.

This is a solid 3.

Although I am computer literate (VMS, Windows), Unix and Linux are new environments for me.  The default installation from Suse boots quite slowly, and a newbie would have some difficulty improving that.  However, consistent software installation is facilitated very admirably via the YaSt tool.  Although the useful documentation seems mainly to be on line (who would not prefer print?), it is sufficiently automated (via KDE desktop tools) and sufficiently documented to introduce any new person in a kind and gentle way.  The distribution is extremely thorough, and it includes a very large quantity of very high quality software.  It seems clear to me that GNU is the path to computing enlightenment, and SuSe is a very good way to get on that path.  There seems to be no effective limit to the utility of the system you receive.  All congratulations and thanks to the GNU people and also to the SuSe people.

I first ordered this version in error but decided to keep it.  I ordered the correct version "Professional" and it worked so well I figured I'd be using this one soon enough.  This is for an in-place upgrade on the same PC.  My only wish is that PCMover could be exported to an external drive then imported to the same PC or another PC.  That way you don't have to be concerned about versions.  The Professional is fantastic.  Moved all my data files and all programs I selected.  Even moved programs it said it couldn't.  I selected anyway and was successful.

I tested this app in my home area, which I know very well, and which has a high concentration of folks seeking organic and healthy food restaurants, stores and accommodations. App listed several stores of one large, general, supermarket chain which carries some organic products. It did not list several stores of other similar supermarket chains which have better offerings. It listed several health food stores of, again, a chain enterprise but missed several medium and larger independent stores. Their listing of smoke free motels and healthy restaurants was similarly limited in nature. The Hudson River Valley is the home of the world class culinary college, the CIA (Culinary Institute of America) and I know of dozens of organic and healthy food restaurants in the area, a few of which were listed. On the app, it asked for user input and info and offered a link, which did not work on my phone (Motorola Bravo). Their web site had similar listings and also solicited input. This link worked fine and I was able to send them what I knew. Perhaps it will be useful when traveling but, I suspect, other sources of information might be superior. Better than all the other similar app that are I could find (Jan 2014), which are totally useless.

[...]

Wearing a Fit bit or any type of tracking item will not make you healthy. What they do is provide the motivation to keep you moving. This in itself is what most of us need. Love that it tells you when you have sat to long. Nice to have a watch again too. Price is average for this device. Provides multiple ways to track activities. Sleep patterns are interesting. I would like to say more , but it is still new to me.

UPDATE ON WHY I DOWNGRADED TO ONE STAR:: The EXPORT CSV FILE feature creates a spreadsheet with the following format for the DATE COLUMN: "Fri Sep 02 20:21:42 EDT 2011". Can the developer change this to a NORMAL format that accounting programs recognize, like mm-dd-yyyy???? Presently, all you can do is "open" the csv file it creates, and it will open in Excel, all unformatted and everything, with the following headers on the first line: category, description, value, and date. Note that the VALUE column is a SHARED column between expenses and incomes. This causes a huge problem when you try to "import" this csv in a program like, let's say, Easy Cash Manager for the PC. FIRST, there is NO PROGRAM that will recognize "Tue Aug 11 20:21:42 EDT 2011" as a DATE!!! It just DOESN'T in the world of finance get recognized and CANNOT be imported. The programs STOP YOU when importing it by saying what format do you want? i.e. mm-dd-yyyy or dd-mm-yyyy. There is NO CHOICE for day of week abbreviated, month spelled out abbreviated, the TIME to the exact second, then EDT or CST, etc., and then finally the YEAR at the end of this long string. Really, if the developer really wants us to have the ability to save the day of the week and the time of day and what part of the county we live in as data to export, put them in separate columns like notes... but not in the DATE field! It's just not being recognized when you IMPORT the csv into DESKTOP PROGRAMS. And secondly... this is what happens when the income and expenses SHARE a column: the income for a hundred would be 100, and if it's an expense in the program then it looks like -100 in the column. SO, now the desktop program STOPS ME on EVERY SINGLE LINE OF DATA that it's trying to import and says "Is [CATEGORY] an income or an expense?" as a pop up as it's trying to import them one by one into a desktop program that has the capability of having incomes and expenses share columns or not, your choice when you set it up. BUT, now let's say you open a spreadsheet in Excel with JUST this apps data. You have to add a SUM formula to the last cell in the value column, which will recognize the minus sign in front of a digit as a negative.... but it's all in one column and will be VERY difficult to take the money columns and do anything else with them on the spreadsheet (let's say, each column adds up the negatives, let's you create a new column to add up all your expenses to get a total... or vice versa, all the incomes add up to a figure you can easily recognize as all your incomes for a certain period. It's just now limited in what you can do with that CSV file, because now Easy Cash Manager can't even import this SIMPLE CSV FILE THAT HAS FOUR BASIC CELLS IN IT because of the DATE FORMAT BEING SO WEIRD AND CONTAINING EXTRANEOUS DATA. The developer HAS TO FIX THIS if they want the csv file to actually IMPORT INTO OTHER PROGRAMS.

OLD REVIEW WHEN I UPGRADED FROM TWO TO FOUR STARS: I'm upgrading my review to 4 stars ONLY because the app is NOW showing a menu that has the export function showing!!! I had NO menu that wd open when I was in account mode previously (and I pressed it goodness knows how many times in desperation trying to find this apps export feature! I have downloaded the csv file into Excel..... its basic, just has category, description, value and date as labels in the first row. His wd be a good app if u find a decent MS Excel template for checking accts, etc, and import it in. It is VERY basic, just so u know. And there is nothing u can do with the expense report feature that Im aware of other than view all the withdrawals (???)... or does it selct categories that it considers "expenses" and create the report from those? I dont have but a couple of transactions in my accts yet to be able to make that determination yet. I cant imagine that if I create a "minus" category called "refunds" or "loan" that it shd be included in an Expense report. But since u cant export or print any reports, this feature has serious limited value. Its a good app, and with improvements such as functioning in landscape mode and running total column, and maybe even customizable reports to include only transactions u check off, it wd easily go to 5 stars (especially if able to export a qif Quicken file!!!) For that feature, people would definitely pay for this app!!
Great app for tracking our numerous thunderstorms. Quick and reliable.

Turbo tax takes ALL the hassle out of doing taxes. It is excellent for finding all your deduction s and credits . Helps with preventing possible audits by finding and reviewing all concerns. I've used turbo tax for 15 yrs and haven't had any problems  with my tax returns . I love turbo tax !!  ( I was a tax preparer for 15 yrs . )
Item came unexpectly and no tracking information but I did receive the item.

I haven't gotten too deep into the app as I purchased it today (free).  However, I would LOVE to see this app created with an account.  So my husband can log in and add his purchases with the app and I can as well.  Then we could see everything.  Rather than me backtracking and asking questions as to his purchases.  Let me know when this is available.  I think it would add a lot of value to the app!

MD installs and runs on Windows 10 without a hitch. As a Quicken refugee, I exported my files from Quicken to QIF format then imported that into MD - no problem. I reconciled with my bank statement perfectly (can't say the same for Quicken!). Online help is thorough and useful. The MD interface takes some getting used to. I'm still not sure what the"confirm transaction" window is all about on the right side, or how to get rid of it, but these things take time.

I don't link anything from my local computer to my bank. I am extremely paranoid about my money so I use "double entry." I enter expenses and payments into my secure bank-online account and instruct my bank to pay the bills from there (including recurring amounts). Then I enter the same transaction into my checkbook register (MD) because I need to know actual cash-on-hand exclusive of scheduled transactions that the bank takes care of. Later I will attempt to share the MD datafile with my wife's Mac by using OneDrive or Dropbox, or perhaps simply by marking the data file "shared" on our home network. That's a separate science project.

I'm hopeful that MD reporting for budget and tax purposes is easy -- it looks to be, but this is just a preliminary report on kicking the Quicken habit successfully.

I have used Quicken for many years.  I also use Quickbooks.  So, I am a pretty sophisticated user.  I won't quibble about the Quicken interface.  My real issue is that I use the program mainly for tracking investments, including stock options (puts and calls).  After all these years, I have many defined securities including many, many options.  Suddenly, the program will not let me add a new security; to do so I must rename an old security with the security edit feature.  Quicken online support won't answer even the question of whether or not there is a limit on the number of securities that you can have -- they refer you to the $1.95 per minute support service.  Also, the program was touted as importing option prices.  It doesn't.  As an attempt to "patch" the security name problem which I suspect is an undocumented limit on the number allowed (with a step that may or may not work, but will apparently cost me the ability to track financial history), I tried to use the "new year" feature to try to cut down the file size.  But the program refuses to copy my file or make the conversion.  I am now going to try Microsoft Money to see if that will work.  I am generally anti-Microsoft, so this move pains me, but Quicken apparently no longer serves my needs.
Like this app for tracking. Easy to get results. USPS keeps their tracking up to date.

So helpful for creating good habits and disincentivizing bad behaviors for my child with ADHD. I can set up any challenges, goals, tracking of bad behaviors we need to work on. My kid loves earning points to build his avatar and "buy" items. No money is needed to play this app.; child exchanges the points they earn with good behaviors! Great concept and it works. Parents should remember to turn off chat and other peer to peer features if being used by a child. (Adults use this app as well.)

I had a really bad experience 365 that caused me to return the tablet I'd bought along with the Office 365 subscription. Let me explain.

First I'll repeat my Microsoft mantra. I hope it will become a meme for the masses. "Mr. Softee doesn't care."

Ok. So I bought one of the those nice Surface Tablets. I travel and do a lot of presentations. The lightness and seeming functionality of the Surface made it a perfect solution for an older traveler who is tight of lugging a 10 pound boat anchor thru customs. Not so fast.

I began the commissioning process of the Surface and ran into a train wreck installing 365. 365 is poorly engineered for the Surface. In a final part of the activation you have to push a button on the screen that simply will not fit onto the Surface. I struggled with this for a couple of hours. After perusing Mister Softee's FAQ's (which is all the support you get) I deleted the original installation and began reinstalling. An entry into Cloud based software hell. What happened was that the Cloud monitor detected two installations on my machine (and I had bought a single user, single time for one machine) Full stop, shut down the engines, do not pass go.

My choice at this point was to wait on the phone for a couple of hours to talk to a nice computer expert reading a script in India, or....give up, return all for credit while I could in the grace period of the store where I made the purchase...and that's what I did. I have fought the good fight against Mr. Softee in my younger days and I just dont have the energy anymore.

So, i am still lugging my boat anchor HP through customs and thinking Apple.

but I find this app very useful because it lets you make your own cards. I make self motivation sets for both short and long range goals. Repetition, visualization, writing down and saying aloud are all recommended techniques for goal implementation and this app helps with all of them.

This could be a much better app with just a few updates; it works ok but ....
It's really annoying that you can only view app in portrait mode, and when you click on articles it switches to landscape.
When entering foods for diet tracker it doesn't give you the option to check mark favorites, you have to go through several steps to select each.
I do like that I don't have to see annoying ads which are on many other tracking apps.
What's with the bodies?? You came view body views but I have no idea how this can help me.
The exercises are really good but they don't stream very well when there is poor connection. And, for the yoga exercises the background music gets really annoying.
The daily health news articles are interesting but as stated before wish the main app was in landscape mode like articles.
Personally, I'll keep using for now but if no updates soon will start the search for another app.

I have multiple maintenance medications and keeping track of everything is sometimes daunting.  This app has a great reminder function and I like the tracking of how many tablets I have and refills.  I recommend it.

I'm a photojournalist on the Gulf Coast. We don't fool around during hurricane season down here. This app shows a variety of active radar, satellite and tracking map loops with weather reports that look great on a Kindle Fire. It is winter now and things are nice and quiet, but I like "Track-It Deluxe" so much I coughed up $1.75 to eliminate the ads. Heck, this is way cheaper than a single Starbucks! It also includes a minimal emergency checklist (food, water, batteries, medicine, etc.) for first-timers.

This app lets you select which region you want to view: Pacific, Atlantic, Gulf of Mexico, Caribbean--N.O.A.A. and G.O.E.S. maps, outlook and active storm information. A bargain!

Also take a look at "Hurricane Hound." It has a simpler interface and great visuals.
