I have been using Family Tree Maker for many years. I upgraded this year because they were no longer going to support the edition I had, too old. Every year something is added to try and make it easier to organize your family tree. Sometimes it helps and sometimes it doesn't. Although I was looking forward to syncing with the new edition, after some of the horror stories I have read in reviews, I didn't even try that so cannot comment. Hopefully the bugs will get worked out by the next edition.

Other than that, I do feel this is a good product. Probably the thing I like best is the fact it is linked to Ancestry.com. It will help you search the internet for your family (although I don't think this edition is quite as easy to use for this purpose as some of the older editions. It is more cumbersome to use and with fewer results.) It will let you link documents, including their pictures, to your tree with a simple click of your mouse. These are filed in the media section of FTM and can also be linked to others.

One of the most important things you can do in genealogy is document your sources. Citing sources is somewhat (emphasizing somewhat) easier with this edition although it does not document in the same form as that required of professional genealogists. A warning here....do NOT take what you find in trees as true unless there are proper citations. Over and over I have found errors such as trees with children born before their parents, as one example, that are picked up and carried on and on in different people's trees without question. Use common sense, and in order to be sure, you need to view copies of original documents. You may be able to get clues that lead you in the proper direction, but always verify. Fortunately, copies of many of those documents can be obtained through Ancestry.com and brought right into your program.

In spite of it's shortcomings, I would recommend FTM to help provide a well organized and documented tree if used properly.
great idea, implementation is still a bit awkward.

I have been using TurboTax for 25 years. Over the years it has become much simpler to use and navigate. The person using TurboTax must have some knowledge of Tax regulations and accounting. I highly recommend the software!

I found this tedious and boring. It took about 5 minutes of incessant tapping and reading to get to the first puzzle, and then another 5 minutes to get to the next. All the dialog has to be read before you can move on.

I found this constant tapping to get anything done to much to be fun.

The concept was good, but the implementation I feel needs work.

The second to last level challenge cannot be done by the rules offered. Not cool....I really loved this game....And I can give a rip who's tracking me. If the challenge cannot be solved by the rules....it's not a game....it's a code breaker....not fair....was learning and enjoying up to that point...

I am upgrading to this product from PaintShop Pro X6, which I have had for about a year. In the older version you are constantly bombarded with ads for the new version, so I decided to try it out. Other than the new splash screen there doesn't seem to be a lot that has itchanged under the hood though. They seem to have fixed some of the bugs that X6 had. You would think that bug fixes would have been included in online updates for the product though.

PaintShop Pro is a good value for the price, but it has some negatives that are really, really annoying. First of all, you need an internet connection to register the product. This is so they can spam you with advertisements for upgrades and other Corel products. You can TRY unsubscribing or opting-out when you first do the install, but in my experience it is impossible to get off of Corel's mailing list once you get on it. I would recommend using a "disposable" e-mail address for registration.

Similarly, even though you "own" the product you will get hit up with pop-up advertisements from the company as long as it is installed on your computer. Again, you can TRY shutting this "feature" off, but it won't do you any good. The advertisements will continue until you delete every last Corel product from your computer, since they are all full of this annoying adware. Corel is notorious for this and it is incredibly annoying.

If you have the X6 version of the software I can't really see any new features that make it worth the upgrade price. Unfortunately, the older version of the software is not automatically deleted when you install the newer version so you will need to delete that manually if you do. They each take up quite a bit of disk space and will pop-up twice as many ads, so you will really want to delete any older versions on your computer.

If you have a much, much older version of PaintShop Pro it may be worth it to upgrade, especially if you do not have a version with the instant photo fix option. You can get many of the same features with a lot less memory consumption and ads by using the open source program The Gimp. Both programs have extensive learning curves though and PaintShop Pro does have better support and documentation. PaintShop Pro is still a good value option compared to more pricey fares like PhotoShop. The latter has an almost infinite number of capabilities compared to this software though.

I had previously written a review on this item, but it seems to have disappeared. I'll do my best to recall my previous review.

In a nutshell, Corel's WordPerfect OfficeX7 is an attempt to create a (more or less) drop-in replacement for certain parts of Microsoft Office. In particular, you will find "cloned" versions of MS Word (a word processor), MS Excel (a spreadsheet program) and MS PowerPoint (a presentation program). Obviously, this is not meant to be a full-blown replacement, as it lacks its own versions of MS Outlook, Access and some proprietary extensions that you can only get from Microsoft.

The results are a bit mixed. For those of you who are casual users of Office, there is no steep re-learning curve to speak of. If you are a "power user" who has memorized many of the keyboard shortcuts, menu trees and other items that let you whiz through Microsoft's products like a pro, it can be much more difficult to get used to.

For the most part, this product lets you open most files created in Word, Excel and PowerPoint without any problems. You can then edit or modify them and save in a variety of different formats, including most traditional formats used by Microsoft (i.e. .doc, .xls). If you are creating a document from scratch, you should be able to save it in the same variety of formats, so that someone using MS Office can open it without knowing that it was created by WordPerfect. This seems to work about 98% of the time and when it doesn't, it is not always the fault of WordPerfect to be fair.

Compared to the "real" Office, Corel's product fares pretty well for the most part. It's biggest drawbacks seem to be lack of documentation, lack of support, some occasional software instability and the few instances where compatibility of documents does not always work exactly as it should. For the "average" user, these trade-offs are probably worth the considerable price difference. In corporate settings or other situations where you might actually need professional support, MS Office is clearly the safer bet.

The main reason I give this product only 3 stars (other than the limitations outlined above) is due to the fact that individual users can get what is essentially the same suite of programs that are included in WordPerfect Office X7 for free elsewhere. OpenOffice (Google it) is an open source version of MS Office that includes all of the same programs and features as Corel's version. Due to the fact that it is open source software, it is distributed for free though. I have used this product for a number of years and if anything, I find it to be more stable and better supported than Corel's version. I would offer a list of reasons to buy Corel's version vs the free version, but I honestly can't think of any.

Having not used Paint Shop Pro since it's earliest shareware days, I was quite impressed with how far this product has come. If you are looking for an exact replacement of Photoshop, this isn't it. However, it has most of the features that non-professionals want and need for graphics editing in addition to a lot of features that few people will ever use. Paint Shop Pro is not the most intuitive program on earth, but I have yet to see any powerful graphics editing software that is. It certainly is easier to learn than Photoshop and costs hundreds of dollars less. It is even a greater bargain if you buy it bundled in the Corel Photo Video Suite X6.

The Corel Video Suite is a great video editing package that seems aimed at the home/hobbyist user. It may not be as powerful as some of the very expensive professional video editing software on the market, but it fills a niche that many of us fall into. The one caveat I will mention here is that you really need a powerful computer with great graphics to get the most out of this program. There are very few (if any) laptop computers that would fall into this category. I would recommend a minimum quad core CPU with 12 GB of RAM and a very recent graphics card with at least 2GB of onboard memory. For hard drive usage, a SSD or minimum 7200 RPM drive should work, although for large amounts of storage and speed I would recommend using a combination of a SSD for the operating system files and a secondary RAID array for long-term storage of working video files. This app is CPU, memory and graphics intensive, as one would expect with any software of this type. The results though can turn home movies into professional-looking short films and has too many features to even begin to mention.

On the whole, the bundled software is not only a great value, but a great product for getting professional-looking graphics and video edited on a home PC. My install was on a copy of Windows 7, which I had no problems with. Keep in mind, if you choose the 64-bit install to take full advantage of your hardware's speed, there will still be a number of 32 bit drivers installed. As long as you have an adequate amount of disk space, this shouldn't be a problem though. It's partly the inclusion of both sets of drivers and other files that allow the program to run as stable as it does. Compared to most software running on Windows, especially ones that use up a LOT of resources, the Corel Photo Video Suite X6 is an extremely stable and mature product. With all of the free and demo software being produced these days, this is one of the few that I don't feel cheated spending money on.

About the only downside I feel worth mentioning with this product is the lack of physical documentation (i.e. a manual) or extensive online support that is available with some other products (especially Photoshop). Although Corel offers paid support for some of its products, it is usually much easier finding solutions to problems by searching for them online. Since this software suite does not have nearly the number of users that Photoshop has, nor the online support facilities of a company like Adobe, it may be more difficult to find answers to your questions than one would like. This would be a major sticking point if it were aimed at professional graphics artists or video producers, but that's not the market niche that Corel is aiming for, which is one of the things that makes this graphics/video suite as affordable as it is. All in all, I'd say that the support issue is worth docking the product a single star, but it is still easily a 4 Star product and well worth the money.

I received a copy of [[ASIN:B009066OXC Creator NXT (2013) [Download]]] (as a CD in a box from Amazon Vine) and installed it on my laptop. I was interested to try editing some videos for the first time.

I have to say that Creator NXT has most, if not all, the functionality I need to modify images and videos.

My biggest problem is that NXT is hard to use, and documentation and support are poor. I'm not one to read manuals before using a new application - if it isn't intuitive, then it's badly written. When the manuals are poor, I become quite frustrated.

The question, then, is whether Creator NXT is cost-effective when compared to similar products from Adobe, Sony, Corel, CyberLink, and the rest. Adobe, well, you'll drop some coin there for a great professional-grade product. As for consumer-grade packages, you should check the reviews here on Amazon. Some of the packages are very well reviewed and should be worth the investment.

Also, I'd advise to poke at the photo editing and video editing categories at cnet.com. Some of the free products there have quite impressive reviews.

This is a good row counter. I haven't delved into some of the other project tracking features, since I use Ravelry for that. Fairly aggressive ads on my android phone, but I suppose that is the price of a free app.

I bought Manga Studio because I've been drawing a comic for almost two years and saw some of the tools available in this program that would save me time. Had a few initial problems, like not being able to get the program's installer to come up and my Intuos4 not tracking correctly. (No matter what sort of line I made the line that came out would go straight down. Had to uninstall my tablet and reinstall it, works fine now!)

This program has a pretty high learning curve, so I don't know if it's really that "newbie-friendly." My usual method of pushing buttons at random to see what did what wasn't working at first. Thankfully there are a great many helpful tutorials out there on YouTube and the rest of the web, so when I've gotten stuck I've always been able to find the information I'm searching for. The tools I was most interested in to save me time on my own comic (the perspective rulers and the panel cutter tool) had wonderful tutorials up on YouTube that got me started pretty quickly so I could then begin playing around with the program as a whole.

There are some things I don't like about the program. One, I can't seem to get my touchring on my Intuos to work with it, so I have to manually select the zoom and rotate tools to navigate the canvas. Maybe there's a fix I just haven't found yet. Also, I can't do what I usually do in Photoshop while inking, which is having a copy of my canvas open on my second monitor to check progress without having to zoom out. I can still open a copy of the file and have it zoomed out on the same screen, but considering that I can take my toolbars over to the second monitor I don't know why I wouldn't be able to take the image over there too.

However, the amount of TIME it's going to save me on my own comic is insane. The ruler tools are just what I've been needing. I love the look and feel of the pencil tools and the pens. I can't wait to get in to the next chapter of my online comic and really get in to the nitty gritty of Manga Studio! It's going to save me so much time and make my comic that much better for it!

Cons: Truly bad support, documentation of error codes is completely non-existent, etc. They won't help you and you won't be able to help yourself.

This product has been absolutely dreadful for us even though we're using the newest build released just yesterday (5/22/2006).

The backups of files we select seems straightforward and the process tells us the backup was successful, but then when we go to restore from it we get one error message after another (both when trying to back up to DVDs, and then to the hard drive itself [just in case the DVDs was the issue]). Either way, we still have all kinds of error messages and unreliable backup of data using this product.

Oh, and if you need technical support what you get is you send an e-mail and in A FEW DAYS OR SO IF YOU'RE LUCKY someone responds with a canned answer that's useless and has little to do with the problem you've had to describe to them since no one will talk to you on the phone. (If you want to talk to a human you have to pay $29.99, and that's right after you just paid them for the product that never worked once.)

This is truly the worst PC product I've ever had the misfortune of ATTEMPTING to use in over 20 years of doing this, and the company has an attitude problem -- they want your money but they don't want to hear from you (or help you) after they've got it.

For some people the product seems to work, but for those for whom it doesn't, you will find as I do that this company will be little to no help to you at all.

UPDATE:

My problem with this software turned out to be the length limitation on file paths, and since all the backup files are in a tree branch structure of files, some folders plus subfolders plus file names plus the stuff the program adds makes the name too long for the program to handle. I had to figure this out for myself over two full days (add that to the price of the product!) because Acronis doesn't tell you that (perhaps by including a box of tips in the backup section) and doesn't provide a list of error codes and what they might indicate as the problem. Tech support is non-existent unless you want to pay $29.99 per call, and all of this that I went through was completely avoidable. In fact, I got a hunch the file name length might be the issue from using someone else's product which just plain SAID SO. If Acronis isn't going to help the user the should provide the means for the user to help himself, but they don't, and even when they add features like the DVD burning feature they don't update the downloadable or web site information about it -- it just shows up as a bullet point on a list of features at the time of this writing. You may have no problems with this, but if you do you are on your own. Personally I would look somewhere else for your backup needs just in case something goes wrong and you need answers. Good luck!

I'd love to tell you what I think of this program, but I can't get it installed. The documentation is good, and it appears to have great features, but I don't know for sure. I'll update this review if I manage to get it to work.

TurboTax is a great product and walks you through every step you need to take to get your taxes done properly, including keeping a running tally of what you owe (or if you're lucky, what you're getting back!) The software is detailed, comprehensive and cheaper than using a tax preparation person. (It also doesn't require an appointment and can be used late at night or early in the morning. You can save your work and come back to it as needed!)

The trick of it is to have your documentation and information ready and organized. While the software will tell you what you need, it can't import or always find everything out there. It's a great program, but it's only as good as your record keeping.

Quicken is an amazing money management software.  It has helped me keep my finances organized for over 5 years.  Up until this year I have used the Windows version of this software.  Recently, I became a true believer in Mac and started switching my entire workflow over to the OS X.  I find most of the software to be superior on the mac.  NOT THIS SOFTWARE!  Compared to the windows Quicken software, this is basically useless!

I think virtually all of these tax programs are going to give you the same results, so you really should just go by price. I have been using TaxAct, but they really increased their fees this year, so I went looking around for another program. This was a good Alexa Deal and the fact that you can have a 10% bonus added if you choose to have a portion of your tax refund issued on an Amazon gift card, makes it essentially free or a money-maker. The 10% bonus is on gifts cards in $100 increments, with a minimum of $100.

You can do five Federal returns for free on this and while H&R Block push their Premium version for self-employed, this one easily handled my Schedule C. Although it says you can import your prior years' taxes, I couldn't get my online TaxAct to work with this. Not a deal breaker, but since this version sits on your computer, I'm thinking it can be exported to any program next year meaning one can go with the best deal again.

I've used CheckPoint's ZoneAlame Extreme Security since they started it, with great success. At renewal time I consulted with the ZoneAlarn "Helpless" support (sorry but they deserve the slur) several times to ensure that it would work with my systems. Then I "foolishly" as it turned out, ordered the Datalock option. Attempts to install Datlosk were a disaster. It trashed my boot record and and I could do nothing. Ater many more discussions with the "Helpless" support, I finally got someone who had a clue what they were doing and sent me a file to remove the Datalock and restore the boot record. So, after about ten hours of frustration and work, the machine would finally boot again, but would not go into sleep mode at all. It was either power down, or leave it run all the time. Eventually, I determined that I add to restore the entire Win 7 system to three months prior image, which put everything back to where I could operate. The ZoneAlarm "Helpless" support finally admitted that Datalock does not support one terabyte drives (I have two installed). Now it would have been nice if they said that in the beginning.

The strange thing is that my employer uses CheckPoint Datalock Enterprise service and it works just fine for all the 23,000 company laptops.

Catuion: install Datalock at your own risk.......:-(
The tracking really works and can be used to pinpoint the computer's location.  Other features are very useful.  Good price for three licenses.

I would give this ZERO stars if I could! The reason it exists is supposed to be to help users avoid the 100+ types of tracking by both keeping the Opt-out for sites and yet when the app tried to install thankfully my Blackberry warned me that the app wanted FAR more personal information from me than the web trackers it was supposed to protect me from! This app wanted the ability to run in the background even when when I wasn't using internet. It also wanted exact GPS location, the unique ID for my cell and my phone number, to know when I was on the phone, to be able to examine the external storage in my SD card and more besides! So even if the app blocked any tracking the makers of this app would have more information about me down to always knowing where I am, whether I'm on the phone and more. I never allow apps to have access to any of the things this app wants, so why on earth should I start now?

Frankly I was disgusted at the sleazy example where they pretend to be doing something good for the user but actually are trying to put something over on them. I had high hopes since on my PC the Alliance has a tool that lets you put opt out cookies for most (NOT all) of the 100+ alliance companies. Better than nothing, but this pitiful excuse of a rip-off is far WORSE THAN USELESS!

I'd give this a ZERO if I could! I wanted to be able to read books I OWN as well as borrow from either library or Amazon. This app wants wants to access:
Your current & saved locations (rough & precise location from GPS & network),
Device Identifying information (allows this app to access device identifiers such as serial number and PIN), Phone (allows the app to identify when you're on a call & to access identifiers such as your phone number and Blackberry ID username)
Shared files (allows app to access your pictures, music, documents and other files stored on your device, at a remote storage provider, on a media card or in the cloud and system gives app rights to modify, read or delete contents of your SD card)

Contacts (it can read your contacts regardless of confidentiality or sensitivity AND modify them!!)

NONE of these things are things that a book reading app needs in order to run and it's not reasonable to give this access to ANY app that you don't want tracking your every move, and making it impossible to be anonymous anywhere or anyplace. This app can collect all this information and (as the Amazon "privacy" policy says) be combined with all of the other personal data they have like your purchases, credit cards and spending habits, what you browse on the Amazon site, your full name & physical address etc to be a very marketable commodity to sell.

NONE of my contacts gave me permission to sell their personal information so I could use an app! Sleazy behavior, and I expected better from Amazon.

Most "free" apps get paid by showing us ads. If an app is free I understand that they make money either by showing me ads or getting click fees when I visit sites. That's the deal and I'm aware in advance there are ads so I either use app or delete the app if it becomes annoying. What I WON'T accept is having my privacy given away.

No app should be allowed to sell YOU! I never let an app have access to ANY of the things this app asks for. The only reason they get away with it is that so many people are too lazy to read the terms and blindly accept or glance at it and decide they want the app enough to get it in spite of the terms! Even worse in my opinion is the issue where SO many apps want access to your contacts. None of the people on your contact list gave you permission to sell THEIR name, birthday, address, or even more private info you might have stored at all, much less so you can get an app you want!

Spotify actually now says that if you use the app you certify that you HAVE permission for them to data mine all of your friends & family! Would you be cool with someone selling all of your info to every data marketing company it wanted to??
See "Spotify’s New Privacy Policy Doesn't Include Much Privacy"
[...]

Far too many people are sheep. Just blindly hitting "yes" without ever bothering to read the notice of what app wants. So a huge and ever growing number of apps want more and more data about you so they can sell it to marketing firms to make it easier for them to convince you to buy their goods and services. Another part of this issue is lazy app writers. Rather than writing code just for app and asking device permission for each thing they would like to get it's easier to just want total access to every part of your device. That way if they ever want to change what data they want the app won't have to ask for permission to do new things.
Not to sound alarmist, apps have been caught turning on microphone or camera without waking device.

In fact, Google+ requires ability to directly call phone numbers, find AND USE accounts on your device including accounts created by other apps, read Google service configuration, read, edit & SEND text messages, read your contacts AND modify them, activity recognition, add or modify calendar events AND send email to guests WITHOUT OWNER'S KNOWLEDGE, read calendar events plus confidential information, read your own contact card, allows the app to read all calendar events stored on your phone including those of friends or co-workers.
This may allow the app to save or share your calendar activity regardless of confidentiality or sensitivity. Read your web bookmarks & browsing history, set an alarm, ADD, REMOVE or USE ACCOUNTS & delete passwords, read contact data in Google accounts, download files without notification or letting user choose download method (WiFi, Cell connection, etc), access bluetooth settings, retrieve list of running apps, adjust wallpaper size & set different wallpaper, change your audio settings, read sync settings, read sync statistics, toggle sync on & off, MODIFY SYSTEM SETTINGS, read home settings and shortcuts, INSTALL OR REMOVE shortcuts
Creepy, but they can claim they were given permission...

Very cool. I mostly installed it to play with the API. Identifies a lot of things, reads barcodes and finds them on amazon. Not fully understanding how the recognition itself works (beyond that it clearly parses distinguishing features into something searchable, then has amazing amounts of training on amazons side. Unfortunately, it's kind of a solution looking for a problem - I'm not quite sure what actual use to draw from this admittedly impressive system. I'm sure it'll be something sooner or later. I'm hoping it'll get more of a shot than the head tracking tech in the Fire Phone, which is another way awesome thing, but which isn't possible to use outside that one hardware platform, which is to say it's a massively impressive framework used by almost no one.

I've only played with software like this before, and needing to plan some major home building projects decided to find something a bit more professional and take the leap. Overall it's been quite satisfying to use. It's generally very intuitive, even for a beginner to CAD. My main frustration lies in their "automated" roofs and the way you use invisible walls to define areas - even if you just want to put tile on part of the floor. You also can't design your bathroom tile projects in great detail - and setting tile size and design can be daunting at first. The library is extensive but is weirdly incomplete - you can find a composter but not a triangular window. You can find every exotic piece of furniture but not a sump pump or well pressure tank.

I love the way it measures everything(though that took me a half day to figure out) and the images are very photo realistic if you take the time to set the details (fabric, wall coverings, etc.). I like that you can import your own backgrounds. Problems I've had were the terrain is extremely difficult to use, and so is the "automatic" roof. Perhaps that's my lack of building knowledge, but being a beginner that has only used the program a week, I'll point that out. Building rooms has been fun but I still don't understand the floor to ceiling relationships in the dialog boxes - to a non-builder/architect they make no sense to me and should be more intuitive.

The support forums are phenominal - someone has almost always asked the question before and I can usually find an answer. The difficult part is each version of home designer has different limitations and it's hard to know if yours does what you want until you've searched for a long time. It does do pretty cool things like curved walls, pass thrus, electrical layout but they neglect HVC & plumbing layouts for some reason (we'd like to be able to do that to show the HVC guys what we have in mind). It has an excessive number of some things and almost nothing of others in the library. There are additional libraries you can download, but they too are piecemeal. Also the search capability in the library is strange - it seems you can only search for simple words like "door" but the results will bring up doors and additional odd things like colors, fireplaces, stoves. If you see something in the library you like, write down your search because you'll never find it again otherwise!

It's also hard to tell what things are controlled globally and what are controlled individually - you can eventually figure that out by expermenting. I would love it if you could just make a wall taller without messing up the whole thing. It's also good for a few laughs when you realize you have cabinets floating in rooms you don't recall putting there. I think there's a bug because I'll set the ceilings in some rooms and later they will just disappear and have to be set again.

Overall this is an excellent program and well worth the extra $100 up from the lesser version. It lets you combine 2 projects (though the terrain again is a nightmare). It does all but a few things I wanted (like moving the roof peak in the auto generated roof) and gave me a good representation of what our renovations and builds will look like when done. Overall, in spite of my complaints, it's very easy to use if you take the time to play with it and experiment.
By far the best achievement tracking app.

I started using Quicken with version 1.0, back in 19--. Naturally, that was on a PC. I stayed with it for about 10 years as it got better and better. Then Intuit started collecting personal information on its customers and that was it for me. I switched to Microsoft Money, which I had helped to develop as a usability tester. Once I got used to the differences in Money, it did everything I wanted (except it never handled my investment accounts very well).

I stayed with Money even after switching to a Mac because I could run Windows on my iMac. However, when Microsoft announced that it was dumping Money and would not support it after about another year, I found iBank.

Contrary to what another reviewer here reported, I had no problem importing my Money files after exporting them as QIFs. Note that I did not import any of my investment files as they weren't accurate in Money anyway. I track them online at the broker's Web site, so I don't care if they're in iBank. They didn't do me much good in Money either.

iBank does all of the basic stuff like maintaining checkbooks, savings accounts, credit card accounts, asset accounts, etc., and allows the creation and monitoring of multiple budgets. That's all I want from my banking software, so I give iBank four stars. I would give it five, but I'm saving room for a few refinements, which I'm sure IGG Software will provide soon.

Once you establish the discipline to enter all your receipts into Quicken, you have ONE place for viewing, reporting and analyzing all of your spending and savings!!  Isnt that what you want from a tracking sotware?!?!?!

Loving DDPY! I began with the web version just before Christmas 2017 and decided to download the app to my Kindle so I could work out in different rooms and bring it when I travel. I prefer the app over the web version because it includes the Custom Workout tracking option, and the ability to connect your Heart Rate monitor and see your numbers in real time on the screen. Now I just need to save up to buy the HRM!

I do prefer entering my measurements on the web version with a keyboard tho. I don't do well with sliders, but that's not a workout function so totally easy to schedule doing without interfering with my workout.

Whether you're starting with BedFlex lying down or progressing thru Chair Force to Standing, this is a terrific app and well worth the download. The cost of the monthly subscription is incredibly reasonable compared to other workouts on the market.

I received a fit bit for a Christmas gift,  through fitbit tracking I have become aware of my eating, sleeping, and exercise, I was pleasantly surprised, the sleep tracking, is a basic tracker, ( which I really like)  set up was fairly easy, and i can check in on my cell daily for progress

I hesitate to give Prime Photos one star, but I do so in frustration, because right now it's useless to me.

It's shocking that after months of trying to find a simple way to organize my photos, I discover it's still the case that you have to organize every photo manually into manually-created albums.

Try this on for size:

1) Export your photos from another application (say Apple Photos or iPhoto), and you'll find that most of them give you the option to take your existing albums and export them as folders. Check.

2) Import your photos into Prime Photos. Those folders are recognized by Clouddrive, but completely ignored by Prime Photos itself. Amazon confirms that you need to create each album manually. Huh?

Not everyone wants albums, and not everyone wants to keep their old hierarchy. Granted. Make it a preference at import. "Would you like to create albums from your existing folders?" Done. Prime Photos would instantly be tremendously useful to anyone who needs their photos organized rather than in a jumble organized solely by date.

I was so excited to try the Plug-In Suite on my iMac (24-inch 3.06GHz Intel Core 2 Duo) - by all the documentation, it should work fine.  Well it kinda did.  I really only got as far as to test Phototools, which worked well in Photoshop CS4 except for a bug that made transparent blocks on your images after applying the presets.  The FAQs on the vendor website provided a work-around for that.  Fair enough.  From Lightroom 2, however, i was not able to access the presets as I should have been.  More solutions in the FAQ and Forums, but none that fixed the problem for me.  A few emails with tech support yielded the suggestion that I run Disk Warror and repair permissions and try again.  I did, no joy. So, back it goes to OnOne (30 day return policy) and perhaps I will give it a try in a year or so once they work out the kinks.  I had really high hopes for this one.

Been using Turbo Tax for 10+ years, and is full of all current critical issues. With the downloading of current changes, provides confidence all will be correct. A good feature is bringing in last year's tax report, posting all previous years data makes it easier to do the current year.
Is very simple to E-file. has a great ability to bring up forms and IRS regulations.

Took a little while to develop "comfort" with the product, due to questions, concerns associated with the product.  Perhaps a better set of documentation can be made available to assist the novice.  Overall it helped me with my PDF needs.

Too expensive, and if you are like me live in one state, work in another (state line) then you have to buy another state, they want you to buy audit protection, it's just too damn expensive. It costs more than going and having your taxes done, which was kinda the point.

Very expensive and you have to pay for all the extra crap like adding states, audit protection, etc. They get more and more expensive each year. It's more expensive than going somewhere having it done anymore, which was kinda the point of doing it yourself to start with.... lower the friggin price. Also, Amazon must have been over stocked with CD/DVD versions because they were always priced less than the download which IMO was stupid.

This app was great help with tracking several meds & PT sessions over the course of about 3 weeks. It allowed for changes over time in timing intervals and dosage. I guess the free app doesn't allow for cumulative summaries. That was the only thing I would add so changes could have been documented for my doctor.

The product is what it is. But, Microsoft has made tracking the download link all but impossible to find. After 15 minutes, I found a support number. I feared that I would be connected with an Indian call center, but was pleasantly surprised ... the Central American agent was very pleasant and helpful.

Still, my beef with Microsoft is the ridiculous difficulty associated with their software. Had my wife been able to get by with OpenOffice or LibreOffice, I would have gladly avoided the hassle and $90 expense.

This is a completely commercial nonsense AI. It doesn't understand clear requests, it cannot find information about 90% of the requests it understands and in the other 50-70% of the time, it sends you to amazon market instead of looking it up on the web.

There is no way to force it to use the web ONLY, because the AI doesn't understand anything you tell it. There are no setting to make it use the web that are clear, if there are any.

So you are basically stuck with an AI that literally is malware-like obedient to marketing Amazon products. Here is the best part, if Alexa can't do something, it is because YOU do not having enough "upgrades" (AKA selling you more amazon junk) to allow it to understand you.

The only reason why this is even a legal AI, knowing that it is the epitome of a product created to exploit it's users, is because amazon is an American mega corporation. What this means is Amazon is an international entity not restricted by borders and in bed with the American government when it falls within the borders, therefore making it immune to regulations.
It is not compatibily with Quickbooks enterprise

Within these review pages, the pros and cons of the new Windows 8 functionality have already been discussed over and over again. So I'm not going to go there and instead my review will focus more on the colossal paradigm shift that Microsoft is undertaking with this release.

Quite frankly... it makes no sense. Microsoft is trying to get cool and cater to the mobile computing crowd (you've seen them, they're the ones always running around telling everyone how neat it is that their new cell phone is "just like a home computer"). More importantly, Microsoft is aiming for a seamless transition of the user experience back and forth and in between home and mobile (and Xbox) platforms. The end result is somewhat of a hybrid operating system that is clunky, unintuitive, and spells trouble for productivity to the home or office professional PC user. The initial screen is called Metro and it looks just like the screen on your touch screen Windows phone or tablet. It is designed to emulate the user interaction experience one would have with their mobile device. But for those sitting at a work station behind a keyboard and mouse, a 21 inch screen, surrounded by peripherals (apparently, Microsoft has determined that this is becoming an obsolete scenario) it will be an unwelcomed nuisance and an actual step backwards in operating system efficiency and simplicity.

I guess no one at Microsoft was brave enough during development to raise their hand, stand up, and say, "Excuse me but there is no such thing as a desktop PC that is used for mobile computing... and there never will be." If someone had, maybe it would have dawned on Microsoft that perhaps the home or office desktop user does not (and should not) interact with their desktop in the same manner as their mobile device.

If Ford Motor Co were to see that all of a sudden boating is becoming very popular, would they decide that their new cars will have outboard motors? Operating systems are supposed to evolve. This means becoming more powerful while at the same time becoming simpler, more streamlined, more intuitive and self explanatory. Rather than evolve and build upon accepted and proven paradigms, windows 8 is gambling on a completely new paradigm. We'll see what happens. Whatever does happen, there is no doubt that this release is a stepping away by Microsoft from the core customer base that built the company: the home and office PC user.

Riddled with defects, nothing works.
I received a new HP envy laptop quad core i7 with windows 8.1.
At first I was impressed to see that I had a normal desktop like windows xp and windows 7. I was also intrigued by the apps and the new start screen. Not a bad idea!
The problem is that I have spent a week trying to solve problems with windows 8.1. and looking through support documents because nothing works.
The apps stop working or do not load. The mouse freezes after sleep.
The laptop is so slow that it resembles a really old computer full of malaware. Takes about 5 minutes to boot.
Chrome takes 3 minutes to load, and so on and so forth.
These are some of the examples I have been desperately trying to fix.
Some of us have to work for a living, we cannot spend our time wading through Microsoft support documentation to make a bad product work.
I'm mortified.
Worked great for estimating shipping costs, locating stores and tracking packages. Since my Kindle Fire isn't "attached" to a printer not much use for printing shipping labels etc.

As a multiple year user of TurboTax Deluxe (for the free state download), I have always used the disc based version in case I needed to later install the program due to audit or other needs. Nice to have the disc rather than hope the download is available for a 5 year old version. Probably being paranoid, but my hand appears to be forced towards download version only after my new computer was build without an internal; DVD drive. Fortunately you can still get cheap external USB DVD drives, fortunately before I wasted money on one I noticed on the TT Disc based page here on Amazon they say you also get a free download with the disc. I figured it was worth a try- I could always buy the USB DVD drive later.

Fortunately there was no need. The download went perfectly. Unlike some earlier purchaser's reviews- by mid-January the download, installation and activation of the program went without a hitch. Additionally, I have done two program updates and two tax form updates without issues as well. The import of the previous years filing and personal information was smooth, but it normally is going from TT to TT each year. Download of the state return form is easy- you just need to be sure they are final if you have a pokey state.

The one major annoyance for me, which has carried over from the last several "Deluxe" version- it comes with 5 Federal eFilings and one State return-- but NOT ONE STATE EFILING. You have to pay extra for this in a "Deluxe" version. Which means more hassle and expense- all things I bought the deluxe version to avoid. Other than that, and the weird thing of telling the IRS what I owe when they already know it, no issues.

Easy install from the DVD, but a bit of a pain to make it find last year's tax file- that should happen automatically I think.

But the software changes are mostly for the better in this edition, and I had no issues with my Federal or State filing. The Virginia download went smoothly, and there was not any issue with my E-filing of the Federal or State return. The fact they are charging $14 on top of the deluxe edition price for the state e-file is annoying and knocks off a star for the stupid game both H&R Block and Intuit (makers of TurboTax) are playing on this. Your "Deluxe" package should include this or make one that does. I just want to pay once, and I don't need all the useless (to me or most filers frankly) Premium package.

Its cheaper than Turbo Tax and works just fine, and the audit backup is a nice feature TurboTax lacks. Recommended.
Of course I love it, otherwise I would never buy it. Have used it since 1989. I sttod up to two audits by IRS and saved me money.

Average. - Below is my rambling about the service, issues, and experiences that I'm not going to spend the time organizing or editing. It is however how I feel when I think about Daily Burn, and that may shed light on DB for you and provide information that can help the company become better, similar to what Machintosh/Apple accomplished, in that, they created products that enhanced and became apart of your life; I'm referring to the PC and then Smartphone. It's a foundation for a product that could easily be more ingrained in your daily life, if they did a better job.

exercise, diet/nutrition, event calendar (races, crossfit, and other events nearby that you could do), group support forum, notifications e.g. for those in an office - time to stand up at work, stretch, go to the bathroom, along with a health tid bit on something easy to be healthier at that moment, based on cellphone location (home, work, store, outside, car) , just to name a few that came off of the top of my head in a couple seconds.

Arrive Home - walk around the block before you walk inside (for beginners), if you excersize for 15 minutes now studies show....
Store - Low Carb or high protein veggie recipe, studies show almonds....
Work - stretch and twist to your right, then left
Car - relax your shoulders
Outside/ weekend - Crossfit is having an event @ "location" - 3pm, Rcae fore the cure is tomorrow walk a 5k before shopping

The web browser version hinders everyday life rather than enhancing it:
Poor web browser functions/options, clunky, not easy to browse for certain options (cardio vs building vs stretching vs repair). This could be good for beginners that need to repair and start out easily using the core 1 video, but once you have progressed to intermediate and want to build vs doing cardio you have to spend a lot of time reading the Info on the video to see where to start next. It seems like they tried to make their own website from scratch or something vs using silverlight/ etc to provide you with a complete browser setting. One example of how it hinders everyday life and does not fit "into" it is, my wife cannot come downstairs and minimize the video so that she can surf the web/news on our projector/TV while I listen to the workout which I have done a few times a could listen to and follow along. All the issues I have are not found with Hulu, Amazon prime, Netflix or youtube. I even posted a video to show all of them working vs DB. They gave me a discount due to my frustration and bc of that I will support them a little longer until I get frustrated enough to find a better option (one option I was leaning towards was a yearly subscription for $99 that had more videos). I will say I have lost 50 lbs, but mostly diet related, although I like my favorite Daily burn instructors enough to stay for now.

Android hopefully to the rescue:

Update #2 android casting: I spoke to soon, the cursory single casting and minimal fast forwarding was crushed when doing the core yoga and after about 40 minutes I needed to get to work; then the troubles, after 20 minutes of trying I could not fast forward reliable enough to get to the last 10 minutes and finish the cool down. Next step unsubscribe :( I think their servers just can't handle it, it's like the old days of fast forwarding through a video or loading a huge playlist in windows media player on a 1998 computer. Maybe Briohny will have some of her old videos I can purchase somewhere.

Update #1 to android: The android app on my cell works great and I can cast the videos to my projector using Chromecast so this solves the bad website issue for me. The blurry video seen with some of the workouts was solved by changing to android/Casting.

On my android I have more function to fast forward past stuff I have no interest in doing with my limited time before work, or that is not tough enough for me in certain videos. Some videos just let you skip to the next "set" of exercises of the workout. Now that I have a Chromecast I will try to cast a workout from my cell to the TV to avoid the junk web browser (but surfing the web would then be limited to their devices if my wife wanted to.The quality of the videos was good, but doing core animal (new to me) today was super blurry. I use a cell app for diet and tracking vs their system, but I haven't spent time learning about and don't feel like paying extra. I continue to pay, but I have seen other options for me when I chose to start DB. Months later I have almost spent my time looking into them, and have thought about it a half dozen times. To be honest the lack of being able to shorten videos on the browser made me avoid using the service bc I did not want to spend the first 15 minutes of my 30 minutes before work/shower doing intro stuff. So, in reality now that I think about it, the website actually made me avoid doing their workouts. Now that I have Chomecast I will see if it will work better for me. If not I will be using a different service, or buy some videos.

Considering all the money they make from the 2.5 million subscribers @ $10 minimum, you think they would do a better job and have more videos. Their instructors especially Briohny are great.Even the workouts are great; when I have the full allotted time to do them. Maybe they will stop trying to make excuses, and just make a better service.

Anyway, hope you enjoyed my rambling :)

Disappointing at best.
Okay, no interview. But 2 semesters of Income Tax Accounting in paralegal courses gets me through that.

TWO MAJOR issues slowing down filing!!
1. Parent no insurance; child has insurance through other parent. All well and good. The software says it's posting the $95 tax penalty and all is well and good. UNTIL you get to the final REVIEW!! Now it's "wait for the next update" (Feb. 12th) for a form. WTH? Over $5K sitting there and I have to wait??

2. Indiana. Renter's Deduction. All well and good until you try to put in the required address of the landlord. Fourteen character spaces only!! So, once you put in five street numbers, space, S (for south), space, you have used up eight!! Try to put any medium to long street name in there!!
In this case, "Bloomington St." No really acceptable abbreviation without Indiana either rejecting ($600+) or auditing.
A 15 minute wait at CSR online chat led to another 20 minutes with a fellow that had "to check" several times. The final answer: He forwarded the issue and it "may be fixed" in the next update. Again, Feb. 12th.

In the mean time, the Indiana AG went on TV Friday saying file now to avoid fraudulent claims against your tax account. Oh, great!!

TurboTax, with their sales down this year because of their software changing antics, now says they will return to their old system next year Woo-hoo!!

7PM EST- Update:

Plowing through all the same info in OLT.com let me file state only. Free. The upside is it allowed filing Indiana only without trying to re-file Fed, which never would have worked. So, a longer solution workwise, but faster refund.

***UPDATE** 13Feb2015--
With all of the promise of "escalation" of the Indiana problem, and fix next update (2/12/15), it didn't happen. When I opened the software to allow it to get updates (just curious now), the Indiana updates section in the box says, "NOTHING PLANNED." That's enough for me! ZERO STARS if I could. I got an e-mail to call them to "discuss the problem."

TURBO TAX update!! TurboTax Deluxe now does a MAJOR UPDATE. It brings back the interview and forms they took away. If you need one of the old forms they moved to Premier, it updates to Premier FREE. If you bought Premier and now would not have needed it with the old Deluxe, you get a refund.

FINALLY, someone listens to the user base. It looks as though withholding the mighty $$$ from a company gets their attention!!

I am pretty impressed with Paintshop after trying its demo, vs Elements and Lightroom.

It is very cost effective and has plenty of functionality and is relatively easy to learn. Many external filters work with Paintshop as well.

The problem with Adobe is that they have 3 solutions that dont work all that well:
1) Photoshop - cost a fortune, very hard to use. Overkill for most.
2) Lightroom - more of a WORKFLOW tool than an actual editor. Lacks a lot of functionality.
3) Elements - photoshop lite. Lacks a decent amount of functionality that PSP has.

The good thing about elements is that you can eventually upgrade to photoshop which is teh gold standard. However, I felt that I would never want to do that and PSP does everything I can imagine. I just liked it a lot more than Elements and felt it was more powerful with more features.

I really like being able to download Filters and Plugins. Havent found a lot of free ones that were useful simply because PSP does so much by default! But I did find several that were worthwhile with very specialized effects (like adding a twinkle/star effect at bright points in a picture).

-----
Lets discuss the addons.
* Having "royalty-free" images is worthless for 99% of people. This is a non feature.

* The Facial software seemed promising and its theory is sound. But it just doesn't work well at all...only usable for very limited adjustments. Amazingly enough out of all its presets...it doesnt have one for SMILING! Like, WTF else are you going to do with a photo of someone?!? Theres like 20 ways to make them frown and look pissed off and stupid looking, but Confident is the only preset for actual smiling. Doing it manually doesn't work very well either.

* The Nik Color Efex 3 is very nice though. It is a Filter program. A filter is similar to the Instant Effects in PSP, except that you have MUCH more control over it. So lets say you want a glamor glow effect. With Efex3 you can dial in exactly how much you want, how much fuzz, how much warmth, etc.
They also serve as a reminder of the various creative effects you have at your disposal. Just seeing Graduated Neutral Density available for example reminds me that it might be really handy for this outdoor picture I took where the sky is really bright. Or Bleach Bypass might make a cool effect for this portrait.
Note that this version of Efex 3 is SPECIFICALLY MADE FOR PSP! You CAN NOT use Efex 4 upgrade or their HDR or other software as it is not standalone and not PSP compatible.

* Finally, I recommend registering by going to Help, Corel Guide, and giving em an email address. Click DO MORE STUFF tab and there are a bunch of freebies you can download and install, including another (really odd) filter assortment, more brush styles, all kinds of stuff.

Considering that the Nik Software cannot be used without the Ultimate version, and other filters are usually pretty expensive I would call the Ultimate edition's extras well worth the extra 10 or 20 bucks.. I think you will find Efex3 worth it.

There is nothing easy or automatic about language acquisition and retention, particularly for an adult. Sure, some people are better at it than others, but for most of us, it involves hard work and continuous. Back in school, I studied Spanish, and I've taken one refresher class, but many years (okay, a couple of decades!) have passed, and once again, I find myself extremely rusty with disuse. Enter, the chance to review Rosetta Stone's Spanish language program, which I decided would be a great way for me to brush up on my extremely rusty Spanish as well as an opportunity to compare this online system to classical and immersion programs I've tried in the past. I'm a former educator, as well, so I have a lot of familiarity with learning modalities and educational materials, and I have to say, for an online program, Rosetta Stone is very well designed.

First, I unlocked and registered the program on my Macbook Air. It requires the use of Adobe, and you'll want to use a headset with earphones in order to access the program's feedback on pronunciation. Or you can use a smartphone--I've been using my iPhone 8 Plus because I hate wearing headphones and so prefer to work off my phone. Both methods, however, work quite well with few audio glitches. I've tried deliberately butchering pronunciations in ways beginners would to get it to steer me and give positive feedback.

The program is immersive, using pictures with Spanish only in an intuitive way to gradually build vocabulary, grammar, and pronunciation skills. You're continuously tested on modules and have the opportunity to go back and repeat lessons to raise your score if you'd like. You may also schedule live help if needed, which I have not yet tested in my first several hours of work through the initial units.

I love that each lesson gives an estimated time (usually ten minutes) but you can work at your own pace. Each subsequent lesson continually touches back on earlier vocabulary to force you to keep using it. Visual and auditory learning are used, as you're asked to read, look at pictures, listen, and respond appropriately. Sometimes, you're challenged to make an intuitive leap using the process of elimination to introduce a new concept or vocabulary word. No instructions are given in English, but you'll find you won't need them.

The only thing Rosetta Stone is missing is the social element providing by a really good immersion type class, which is very reinforcing, but it also won't allow you to hide behind your smarter classmates or slack your way through concepts you don't understand. As an introduction to a language or a way to refresh your knowledge, I would definitely recommend the program. I like it well enough that I'm considering purchasing the Italian version, as I've been wanting an introduction to that language.

Do I believe you can become fluent in a short time using Rosetta Stone alone or that it's a complete replacement for a well-designed class with a good, live teacher? No, but as long as you don't go in with unrealistic expectations, it's a very good place to start and a worthy way to learn at your own pace.

Great software for tracking your finances. Downfall is you have to keep up with it and to get the best results you need to down load your data from other companies, but it is still a good peice of software.

I've used this product every year for several years now.  It makes tax preparation so simple.  It guides you through each step by asking simple questions and has help buttons you can click throughout the process if you have questions.  It was so easy to use.  Once I had all my tax information available, I prepared my own taxes in just over an hour.  I also love that they have audit support and guarantee the largest return.  Very happy with H&R block - saves me from having to hire an accountant!

Turbo Tax may seem like an expense that you might get for free on some websites, but the nice things are that:
it is easy to use,
it is always consistent,
loads consistent updates
it imports from the previous year all the carry-overs, and
good tech support.

Sadly, Congress keeps making minor changes even after the New Year, but Turbo Tax jumps on the changes and installs updates very quickly. The Deluxe version allows for a free submission of the Federal Tax Return and a minor fee to submit (online) the tax return for your home state. This is the 12th year I have used it and it always finds any errors, it helps me make adjustments, it walks me through the best deductions, and it shows the audit risk for my tax return. With Turbo Tax I have never been audited—accuracy is great!.

Easy to use. I advise getting the CD-ROM disc in case you have to print a tax return from many years past, this allows you to reload any basic software and then Turbo Tax will install the updates, even it is has been 3-7 years later.
