A mixed bag. Good mix of sounds and options...but that makes it a big download. Doesn't require WiFi...but has network permissions in the code. No tracking or adware that I could find...but doesn't exit cleanly.

Pro:
- Lots of sounds to choose from.
- You can mix up to three sounds and set the volume independently to get just the mix you want.
- Save your favorite sound mixes.
- Works without WiFi/data so you can use it when you go camping.
- No adware or tracking code that I could find. (If you see someone talking about this having Millenial adware, they don't understand the difference between a code snippet being present and the code actually being active and able to run. Yes, there is code there...probably for an ad-supported version...but it's not called by any of the functions, so it's not active.)

Con:
- BIG download. Some of the sound files are rather large, and there are a lot of them, so the total download size is over 70 MB. That's a lot of space if you have a tablet without expandable memory (8 GB version of the Kindle Fire, for example).
- You can't use your own sounds or mp3 files to fall asleep to (at least I couldn't find a way to do it...that would be a nice option to have).
- Doesn't exit cleanly. I (and this is just personal preference) don't like apps that keep running in the background when I tell them to exit.
- Has network permissions even though it doesn't require a network connection. (Maybe the developer has a good reason for this permission and would explain it, but I don't see why it's needed.)

For me, there's just too much going on with the permissions and failure to exit to put it on my phone, but I will keep it on my tablet. As the FAOTD, it's a good app for a tablet. I would pay the $1.49 asking price if all the permissions were fixed (or explained better) and there was a clear exit process that didn't leave things running in the background.

This is a rather good, unique implementation of a Gin Rummy game. The game play is good. Drawing and discarding are smooth and the touch is accurate for pulling cards out of your hand. The one knock I have to make against the game play is that you don't get to decide what combinations to make. If you are holding a group of cards that could end up being two runs or two sets (say, 6H-6S-7S-8C-8H) and you pull a card that completes a run, it automatically puts that run together...you don't get to decide to keep it just sitting in your hand waiting to see if you complete the second run or maybe two sets. That is actually a pretty BIG problem in the play of the game and costs 1 star.

The theme is definitely designed to appeal to the "steampunk" fans, of which there seem to be many. Lots of monocles, gears, etc. The music is supposed to sound like an "old timey" player piano or silent movie background, but to be perfectly honest, I just found it distracting.
The game play is slowed a bit by all the animation, and I couldn't find a way to turn that off. If anyone has found a way to change the level of animation effects, I'd really like to know where that setting can be found. Thanks.

There are more permissions than there should be...and the author has chosen to include Flurry analytics code. But the biggest gripe I have in this area is the "tell your friends on facebook/twitter" buttons along with the "more of our games" buttons being front-and-center. This is a paid app, there shouldn't be any ads...not even for the author's other games...on the main menu page. If you want to hide something like that in the settings or information menu, fine...but not right up front on a paid app. This is what cost the second star on the rating.

There are no ad networks, the app will run with the WiFi off, and when you exit via the back arrow, it doesn't leave anything resident in memory or running in the background.

As the Free App of the Day, it's definitely a good deal. But with the up-front "share me" stuff, ads for the author's other games, and the big issue with the game play, I would not recommend spending $2.99 on this game. $0.99 or even $1.49...probably, but definitely not $2.99.

SHAME ON YOU Amazon for offering this privacy-violating, misleading, horrible app as the FAOTD. You should put an "Out of Order" sign on the FAOTD page rather than offer stuff like this!

1. The list of permissions is not a lie. This thing really will try to take control of your CAMERA! Why does an alarm clock need to be able to take my picture?
GPS? Networks?
What's it going to do, take my picture to make sure I'm asleep, then send my GPS location to some burglars? (Yes, I'm sure that's not the case...but come on! When an app is asking for this many ridiculous permissions, you have to wonder why.)

2. This IS NOT the full version. "Ad-Free" seems to be a new trick to get people to buy stuff that still has In-app selling. Fine, there are no ads...but you don't get things like holiday reminders unless you pay more money.

3. The interface is a complete nightmare. Good luck trying to figure out exactly what you can and cannot do (see the next item for more on this). It is not well-designed as far as navigation, and it is not well-drawn for display. It looks clunky, nothing pleasing to the eye at all here.

4. If there's a way to get it to play a song from your on-board storage as your alarm, I can't find it. It keeps sounding the default notification tone no matter what settings I change.

I'm going to go make sure there are no remnants of this nightmare app still floating around on my tablet.
Do yourself a favor...do not even download this piece of garbage.

*******UPDATE**********
Someone claiming to be the author of the software and his friends have been going through a lot of these reviews and leaving rude, fallacious comments.
This is something written by the author in a comment to me:
"As you know about coding, the backups are stored into SQlite database on external storage (SD Card). Screenshots that you take when you want to save results from stopwatch are saved as image on SD card too. Without read/write permission this will never work."
So now we can confirm that this author knows nothing about Android programming. (Or maybe he just thinks we can't do backups for save screenshots on a Kindle Fire HD or any other device that doesn't have a place for an SD card?)

I have a hardware monitor on my devices, and while sometimes you can chalk up bad permissions to bad coding...when the app tries to access the camera and take a clandestine picture WHEN FIRST STARTING THE APP...there's nothing to do but call that an attempt to breach your privacy.

The author and his friends can vote our reviews down and leave all the snarky comments they want. The fact is, this app is a privacy nightmare.

But even if not for the privacy issues...there are a lot of free apps out there that do a better job than this one even claims to be capable of doing, look better, and have better documentation.

I am a long time user of Quicken and upgrade every few years. I had some technical problems with my PC that affected my existing version of Quicken so I decided to do a 2017 upgrade. As usual, I always read the reviews first when making a purchase and particularly the low rated reviews. I was surprised at the negativity of so many reviews on the 2017 version so decided to wait. However, the technical problems with my PC were serious enough to force me to risk downloading this version.

Installation of Quicken Deluxe 2017 was an easy task. All went smoothly and my existing files were transferred to the 2017 version. I don't use all the features that are available but the basic tracking of my accounts, which is my core requirement, is working flawlessly. All's well that ends well and this installation was a piece of cake.

I'm sure it is a fine app, but why does it need network socket permission? Ads? Tracking?

Perhaps the author could answer that. thanks.

I use Quicken 2016 for Windows on Windows 10. I mainly use Quicken to keep track of my checking account register. Making entries for tracking income and expenses keeps me aware of how much money I have available at any time. The One Step Update option downloads the latest bank information. I can keep a few months ahead in the Quicken Register and see what's coming and what's going.

I have always used a software program for the purpose I stated above. I started with Microsoft Money in 1985 on DOS 2.0.

I love it Windows 7 installed on 3 separate computers in my home for a reasonable price.

It seems MicroSoft is finally living up to their promise of cheaper software with the implementation of activation.

If you are running Windows XP, or Windows Vista, you should buy this immediately and move forward. I delayed for years, almost completely skipped Vista, except for a couple cases. What a mistake Vista was. Both computers that had Vista work much better now with Windows 7 installed.

Windows 8 is a whole different beast that hasn't worked itself out yet. Give it time, but if your running Vista upgrade to 7 while you wait.

I initially doubted any app for logging data for my bike would be adequate enough for my needs, but this app has surprised me.

You can log all purchases into separate "divisions/files" for Fuel, parts, services, and "other" expenses (insurance, helmets, jackets, registration, etc.)
The app keeps up with your tank-to-tank MPG, as well as MPG over time or life-time. The app also reminds you of service intervals, based on mileage or elapsed time (you set the interval).
This app has already helped me keep up with expenses and track costs like I only could have dreamed.
The app also allows for tracking multiple bikes at the same time, tracking their expenses, MPG, and service intervals independently, while allowing you to view them independent, grouped, or summarized. There is also a nice "graph" view to show change over time for all tracking provided by the app. This thing is very well made/designed... even for a free app.

Honestly, I may upgrade to the pro version, only so I can also scan in my receipts and transfer data between my phone and tablet to make things that much easier to keep up with.

UPDATE:

I upgraded to PRO, and really do like the sync between devices (tablet/phones), so I can make updates on fuel purchases "real-time" from my phone and up-link the data to the server and view it on my tablet later. The sync has only hiccuped once and didn't transfer a fuel purchase, but I'm not complaining... Oh, and I've also begun to use this APP to track expenses and fuel purchases for my other vehicles too... car/truck... its not JUST for motorcycles!

First of all, this DOES NOT link to your bank info! It is NOT AN ONLINE BANKING APP. It is an electronic register - period. It DOES NOT think for you - it only does MATH and remind you of auto-transactions!

I've been using the Free version for over 6-months, and I love it. It makes tracking my expenses (checks, debit-card, auto-pay, and auto-deposits) so much easier, but it does take some effort to force yourself to use it at first. You have to update your info and make sure your auto-deductions, auto-payroll, etc. are correct and make adjustments (reconcile) as needed, just like balancing your check-book the old-fashioned way. Password protection is nice, as well as back-up to SD card, so your info is secure and up-to-date.

There is no access to the data from other sources, like your computer. This is a good thing, since your data is not being transmitted anywhere... it remains on your phone, only (despite what the paranoid masses say).

For those of you upgrading to the paid version from the free one, follow these steps so you won't lose any data, and it is easily transferred to the paid version:

Open Free version, select Menu > More Options > Backup
It will create a back-up point for today
Close Free version
Install Paid version (if you haven't already done so)
Open Paid version
Select Menu > More Options > Restore
Long-click on the restore point labeled for today's date
Select Restore
Your data will now be in the Paid version... you can now delete the Free one.

The Paid version has a few more features than the free version (and no ads), but takes up 5x the space on your phone... so if you don't mind the ads, and need to preserve space on your phone... stick to the free version.

Now to address the dissenting reviewers (especially those that have no idea what this App does or why)...

Anyone that doesn't keep track of their own money (checks, debit card, auto-pay, and auto-deposits) and reconcile the bank's statement every month is asking for the beating they'll get. I don't trust any bank as far as I could throw it. Keeping track of every penny myself has helped me save money over the years in bank "errors"...

This App, and many like it, are just a replacement for the old-style pen and paper register... make your entry here, forget the pen, and forget the inevitable mistake when trying to do basic math... The app does the math FOR you.

Never had a problem so far.  I like the fact that they have a disclaimer that says they stand by you if you ever get audited.  The installation was easy, it retrieved my records pretty easily and I barely had to scan or copy much of anything.  Of course, everybody's tax situation is different, but Turbotax seems to adjust pretty easy enough.  I even ran my taxes through twice just to be sure and it was consistent.

I have used Turbo Tax for several years and it always works well for me.  They ask questions and don't miss anything in terms of following the IRS regulations.  It's very handy to download the software and automatically input your data from last year to save you many keystrokes.

Have just installed and captured some 20 minutes of video from my old Canon HV20 camcorder which used miniDV tape and has FireWire connector. The FireWire cable needs to be plugged in to the interface box firmly as I initially did not have it pushed in all the way and it couldn't sense any signal. The box was able to detect the Canon HV20 with no problem.

I used to have desktop with FireWire and used it to capture directly from the Canon HV20. That desktop had i7 Quadcore 3GHz CPU running Windows 7 Ultimate, but it occasionally had problem with not detecting the Canon and required reboot. I no longer had that desktop, so have been looking for a way to import from the Canon.

There was some pop-up dialogue about script error, but I just ignored it and continue. I did not encounter any other issue.

The Pinnacle software was able to detect scene change and separate the individual scene into separate clips (shown in the capture window). So it would be easy to edit and get rid of scenes you don't want.

Once I stopped the capturing, there were 1 video file, 2 WAV files in the destination folder. The video did not have sound, which I'm guessing the capturing split everything separately for editing. I guess if I exported everything, then I would have a complete movie.

I will do a more complete capturing tonight of a whole tape (I used 83-minute MiniDV tape) and will update should there be any other issue beside the script error thing. But so far so good..

I currently use the 2010 version, but I didn't buy it. Why? Because I bought the 2011 version to update 2007, but the 2011 version makes mistakes in calculating a budget, so Intuit's solution was to "allow" me to revert to a 2010 version, which they supplied. They said they'd contact me when the problem was fixed so I could go back to the version I paid for, but they never contacted me; now I know why. They moved the faulty 2011 engine to 2012! If I had paid for this 2012 version, I'd be returning it.

It's tough to trust Intuit with budget and money tracking. I have a 2010 version that calculates properly so I will stick with that even though there are other problems I've had to work around. I hope that someday Intuit will change management and once again create new and innovative products that function well. Here is what I found so far:

Pros
*Automatically updated via Internet during installation from CD.
*Kept my interface and report customizations.
*Runs faster than 2010.
*Lots of great tools and features that do function properly.
*60-day money back guarantee.

Cons
*Installer required uninstalling 2010 version. Makes it tough to compare two versions or revert to previous version.
*Automatic update took 15 minutes on a 2.8 GHz computer with broadband connection. Hmmm!
*My old file had to be converted to the new format. Good thing I kept a backup.
*Requires Windows. PC version has multiple problems but Mac version sucks banshee farts.
*Budgeting tool still has calculation problems that originated in the 2011 version. If a money program cannot calculate accurately, what good is it?
*Transaction download issues still abound. Intuit usually blames their server, the bank's server, or recommends deleting the connection and re-establishing it (a lengthy, somewhat complex process).
*Still has trouble dealing with time and suggests restarting the program to fix it.
*Auto-fill is still a nightmare.
*Support is still lame. They may answer the phone better (sometimes) but the solutions they provide do not fix the problem, they tell you how to work around the problem. They have been doing this for years!!!

UPDATE Jan 2013

Intuit has just notified me that they will no longer support 2010 as of April 2013. No more downloading transactions. No more customer "support." Not that support was ever really there, but no more downloading transactions? They already have numerous problems with downloading so I must download a file from my bank and import it into Quicken, but two of my accounts work OK. So they are pushing me from a version that calculates properly to one that doesn't. And I've heard nothing good about the 2013 version.

Poor service and support never ends with Intuit, so expect this to happen in a couple of years for the 2012 version, so I dropped the rating to one star.

Time to explore other options. Lots of them out there.

I had a very negative experience with this product. On the surface, it seems like it could work well for many people which I'm sure is the case, however, not if you bank with Bank of America which is very common for many people. Firstly, I set it up to do a "feed" so it would automatically import transactions which is the whole reason I bought it in the first place. I worked on it for half a day with error messages before I found out you need to call Bank of America to flip a switch to allow quickbooks access, and there is a $15 monthly charge from your bank (This is not mentioned anywhere as it is a BOA fee).  I then come to find out there is a 250 transaction maximum feed import. Then you have to do it manually by uploading statements. Unfortunately, Bank of America doesn't export these transactions in a Quickbooks compatible format, you have to change it to a different form, and THEN Quickbooks doesn't recognize my bank code and it won't import. After scouring the internet I find many users with this same exact issue who have said they needed to actually change the bank code in the import so it shows up as a different bank that is compatible, most often Chase, but then they have a duplicate account with the wrong bank name. For nearly $200 and banking with such a common bank, I expected no trouble, and no extra costs. I am trying to find a way to return this product.

I have a love/hate relationship with Microsoft. On the one hand, it's obvious the company has really just taken disparate products and mashed them together. On the other, this becomes less of a problem with each version that comes out as Microsoft continues to improve on getting them all to work together seamlessly. After my stint as a tech writer for Lotus Notes I can speak when confidence when I say that although I hate most of Office, I hate Microsoft's competitors so much more. We got this for my wife, who works on a Mac.

Of the programs included, we primarily use Word and Excel -- she more than I since she handles the finances -- and because I frequently create presentations for my speaking engagements, PowerPoint. Never used OneNote. Ever.

As a student version, this edition doesn't include Access or Visio, which is fine with me. I used Access probably 10 years ago and gave up trying to create a database. I didn't realize Visio was still around, having used it once back in the 90s before Microsoft brought it into the collective. Microsoft Office's biggest competitor has always been itself: I use Excel to track projects instead of Project and PowerPoint instead of Visio in both cases.

So what's new with this version? Probably the biggest improvement is in collaboration. Word and PowerPoint introduce co-authoring, which is basically typing on the same document, even in real time. A lot of this stuff are innovations that have been the de facto nature of working on the web for some time -- look kids, you can see who has access to a document (thanks Google docs!). There's also cross-platform collaboration (a necessity if you're going to introduce co-authoring) and a more natural help file integration.

Perhaps because I've been working in an office at a Fortune Five company for 15+ years, the thing I'm most excited about is the new PowerPoint templates: treemap (which displays patterns), waterfall (trends moving both up and down), Pareto (useful for quality control in tracking to a data set), histogram (data frequency), box and whisker (uh, displays outliers but I don't have much use for it), and sunburst (a circle displaying hierarchical data). It's the little things.

If you're new to Office, this isn't necessarily going to convince you of how awesome it is. Instead, Microsoft is doing its due diligence to ensure that Office is up to the standards web users have come to expect. And that's not a bad thing.
Very simple to use, and helpful options for rapid swing tracking.

This is basically a very good program, and certainly helped me find a lot of hints for my family tree. Some people here reported difficulty logging in and activating the free 6-month membership thst comes with this premium edition. I had no such problems, although you do need a credit card and will be automatically billed for a renewal if you don't cancel before the trial period expires. Once you get logged in, there will be a mandatory update activated, which, they say, fixes some bugs.

I am very glad this edition comes with a printed manual, because I found some of the features confusing, and the online help info is not always easy to find. For example, if a person on your tree was married more than once, you CAN add both spouses and list all the descendants together, but the instructions to do that are not immediately obvious. And I would really prefer to have links within the program open in a new window, so I could BOTH look at my tree diagram AND search info at the same time. More than once I have made mistakes because I could not visualize how someone did or did not fit on my tree. (I think better in visual diagrams than in lists.) And I still have not figured out how to merge two similar people on the tree once I have added them. The "find duplicate people" feature does NOT always find possible duplicates, even ones with very similar dates and names.

Also, if you want to move tree files from one computer to another, you can't just import a Family Tree Maker file, even though that is at the top of the export options list. You must first export as a GEDCOM file (at the bottom of the options list) and THEN import that -- something I found through trial and error. It could be that there are ways to do these things more smoothly and I'm just new to the program and not "getting it." But I don't think so, because others who are longtime uses of FTM have also complained that the software interface is confusing and clunky. This is a powerful program I find useful, but I do wish the instructions were clearer and more complete -- for that, I'm docking it a star.

PCsync 3.0 works great -- as advertised. However, installation isn't quite as easy as advertised (is it ever?).
Here's what happened as I connected two laptops running Windows 2000: After intially installing the software, I found that the two computers would not communicate with each other through the serial port. I went through all of the troubleshooting actions suggested in the documentation and came up empty.
Out of desperation, I uninstalled the software from one of the machines and tried to do what PCsync refers to as a "remote install." By doing this, I realized that the machines were unable to access the COM1 ports through the serial cables. The COM1 port (on both machines) was busy because of the Palm Desktop HotSync program running in the system tray (bottom right of Windows). I closed Palm on both computers and PCsync instantly worked.
One would think that such a simple program conflict would be detected by the program (our outlined in the troubleshooting documentation), but it wasn't.

Love this app for tracking my steps per day. It seems to be very accurate and helps me meet my personal fitness goals.

Ack! Another annoying sudoku app. The snowflakes are so damn annoying, twirling around from the bottom of the screen to their cells. Kids might find this fun but I find it distracting. Another distraction I could do without is the numbers spinning whenever certain events occur: all of a given number have been entered; a row or column is complete; one of the nine blocks is complete. The last annoyance is that it won't let you make mistakes. I don't need a nanny who won't even let me fall down!
Three stars because it works, but it has time wasting annoying distractions.

Another review after downloading this app again: I decided I had not been fair. There was no help for this app so I had been using it with what I assumed were required settings. I decided to reload it and play with it again. Lo and behold, there are some settings you can control...not a lot, but some. I turned off the sound. I turned off the snowflake animation. I turned on allowing me (the user) to enter errors. Then I played it again.

Darn, you know, I missed the snowflakes? Really! So I turned them back on and horror of horrors they are there again but they are no longer doing their twirling around thing. They just float up to the cell and, poof, disappear. I really miss the original twirling snowflakes. Why did I mess with them? Why, oh, why?

Since this game does not have a restart option or a fill in all the pencil marks auto-magically option, when you do enter errors, it takes forever to get rid of them. To erase a wrong number, you need to enter the same wrong number into the square again. It took me a while to figure that out. Took me a while to fix the game I was playing and actually solve it after entering a couple of wrong numbers.

So I have decided to keep the game loaded on my kindle fire even though it really is a 3 star implementation.
General features were generally ok. But this app is more interested in finding out who you are, where you are, and tracking what you are doing. I passed in this one.

CyberLink PhotoDirector 9 Ultra is everything the non-professional photo editor needs at a sweet price.

CON
1) It's not intuitive. This isn't a program you can just "plug and play." It took me *time* to learn.

PROS
1) Has the ability to import and edit both still and stills captured from films.
2) Ultra version gives one the ability to edit 360 degree/panorama shots.
3) Ultra version gives you one year access to the Cyberlink Cloud.
4) Layers editing.
5) Create Mode allows you to use your photos to create slideshows and new photos.
6) Truly a great program for non-professionals to find their visually creative niche.
7) Best and most versatile photo editing software for the money, hands down.
8) Useful for home, work and school.
9) For both Windows and Mac O/S.

In my opinion - and from personal experience - PhotoDirector 9 Ultra is everything the non-professional needs and then some!

For anyone who has ever wanted to give animation a shot, but been overwhelmed by the complexity of the subject or put off by the price tag of high end software, there are more affordable and user friendly options available. One such option is Anime Studio 6 which, despite the name, is easily suitable for animating any artistic style and not just Japanese anime. The program does have a bit of a learning curve, but anyone who knows their way around a computer shouldn't have too much trouble getting started.

As soon as the program loads there is immediately an option to open the introductory tutorial, which means no one has to search around for the tutorial files. The included tutorial is a PDF document, instead of a video or an interactive program, but it is fairly in-depth and includes a lot of images showing where everything is located. Apart from the tutorial there is also a separate PDF user manual, which is much more extensive in its coverage of what any given option does.

The software is meant for both Mac and Windows, so the Mac style buttons and layout may be a little strange at first for Windows users, but the simplicity of the design becomes apparent before too long. After playing around for a bit and reading the tutorial it does become more intuitive and natural to change various settings and move objects around. The layout follows a logical order, with the left side of the screen for creating or modifying new objects, the right side for changing colors and effects, and the bottom end for animation timelines. There is a handy "Reset all Tools" feature included to automatically return everything to the default values if a user can't remember what settings were changed on a selected drawing tool.

The program lets you either draw your own images from scratch or import your own art from another file. The images are then manipulated and moved around, rather than having each scene of the animation re-drawn. To move images, a custom bone structure is inserted into each character or object so it can move based on where it's "limbs" are. Bones aren't limited to just limbs and can be used in other ways, such as if there was a character with cape that needed to move separately from the arms or legs.

Adding different effects to characters is a simple process that only takes a few clicks. The array of effects available covers most situations for any type of scene, offering background glows, motions blurs, and more. The shadow option makes it a snap to change the direction and color of shadows.

As far as improvements from the previous edition, the biggest draw is that Anime Studio 6 includes a built-in lip synching feature for any animations using audio. Previously the images had to be exported to another program, then go through the lip syncing process, and then be moved back.

Overall this software is an excellent place to start for anyone who wants to create a cartoon and put it online. Anime Studio 6 isn't the end of the line, however. Anyone wanting to get serious with animating should probably also invest in a USB drawing tablet for hand drawing objects, as well as a USB microphone for recording audio.

I have extensive experience with desktop publishing software and I have found Page Plus X7 to be extremely easy to use (it is very intuitive); and, it is quite powerful in its overall capability.  The drag and drop photo insertion and in program photo editing is especially fast and useful for creating projects quickly and professionally.  Of special interest is the ability to publish a project as an e-book that can be exported to tablet format.

I have been using TurboTax Deluxe to file my taxes for many years now. It is always easy to use with accurate calculations. This year's edition is no different. I like how they keep the look and feel the same each year so that you can easily get started working on your taxes without being distracted by interface changes.

I filed my taxes a few days ago, a few days before the IRS starts accepting them. TurboTax will transmit them once the IRS is ready to begin. The entire time, from installing through working through taxes both federal and state including filing, was 2 hours. I had all of my documentation organized ahead of time though.

TurboTax Deluxe includes federal e-filing for up to 5 returns. It allows you to download 1 state product for free, once you get through your federal return, but you need to pay for state e-filing. If you need more than one state, I think they just charge you for the additional states but I have never needed more than 1 state.

Inside the case there is a card with a code that allows you to install (either from the disc or via download) to up to 5 computers.

All of the tax forms were up to date with 2020 rules, such as the charitable deduction even if not itemizing. At the end it also asked about amounts of received stimulus checks to verify if you received correct amount. It would have handled requesting if incorrect amount had been received. So if you had issues with your stimulus payment then this can help resolve that too while you are doing your taxes.

As always, it integrates well with other Intuit tools such as Its Deductible which makes entering charitable contributions easy.

Overall this is a great product for filing your taxes yourself.

I've been using TurboTax to do my taxes for a very long time. It is easy to use and enables easily and accurately doing taxes. I have specifically been using Deluxe plus State. This year's edition is no different in quality from years past.

One new feature this year is that the install can be done direct from the disk (as you'd expect from the disk edition) or via download. Both install methods use an activation code that comes in the package. This is the only thing I don't like about this year's. I buy the disk version because I don't want to mess with codes and downloads. I understand need for activation code for download. But why is there an activation code to install from the disk?

Deluxe plus State includes federal e-filing. Although it includes the cost of 1 state product, which downloads for free when needed, there is a charge to e-file state returns. This has always been the case. If you want to print and file state through the mail, then there is no additional charge for that.

If you itemize then one very nice feature is its integration with Intuit's free website ItsDeductible which enables tracking charitable donations all year long including estimating donation values for item donations. When you get to charitable donations it has you log into ItsDeductible and it downloads your data.
It's an expensive program but it has a lot of features for tracking time and billing.

This video takes you through a step-by-step tutorial to save time and trouble.  The learning curve has been simplified and minimized to take your audio editing to a professional level.  The instructor is a subject matter expert that takes the time to explain even simplistic steps to becoming self confident in audio editing. As other reviews noted, when you order EZ Stamp you can expect a phone call to verify your order. In my case, I wasn't clear about whether to order the new 64-bit edition or the older one. Marios called and explained that the new 64-bit software would run on 32-bit Windows. So I took his advice and ordered the new 64-bit edition. Now, after working with it for a few hours, I haven't encountered anything in it that doesn't work exactly as advertised. It is easy to learn and use, and I am now enjoying creating a digital version of my stamp collection. For the first time, thanks to EZ Stamp, I even have an idea what my collection is worth.

My order was sent by mail, as I requested, and took about 10 days from Canada to US. That is well within the time frame specified by Amazon.

One thing I want to pass along in case it helps: I did some work in the demo version of EZ Stamp, and then backed it up according to directions in the EZ Stamp documentation (a complete backup requires making copies of particular files). When my licensed copy arrived, I uninstalled the demo and installed the licensed copy. Oops. Before uninstalling the demo, I should have used the "Export to 64-bit" menu option, to prepare my collection for transfer from the demo software to the licensed version. The EZ Stamp documentation is pretty clear about that, but I approached it as a backup/restore thing, which I also found in the documentation. Net result is that I couldn't import my backup because it wasn't exported to 64-bit.

Fortunately, my backup included the directory where I stored my scanned images--for each stamp, I had looked up the Scott number on the web (thank you, eBay), and then used my scanner and scanner software to make and save a jpg image of the stamp. I saved the scanned image with its Scott number in the file name. Having the Scott and the stamp image made quick work of recovering from my mistaken assumption about backing up instead of exporting to 64-bit. So beware, if you are making the move from the 30-day demo to the licensed version, that you will have an easier time than I did if you simply Export to 64-bit before moving to the licensed version.

I would recommend the 64-bit EZ Stamp software to collectors wanting the digital benefits now available to stamp collectors. The software's user interface is clean and easy to learn, and it provides the computer-searchable Scott catalog (this alone is a great leap forward). I expect to expand my stamp collecting activities now that I have the versatility of a collection in a searchable/sortable database. This is an amazing way to take stamp collecting to the next level. Good luck and have fun!

ATIH 2012 Plus - I have been using Acronis products for many years. Experience has been with XP, Vista and Win 7 (standard, professional, home, premium & Ult.) I have used it successfully on desktops, laptops, IDE, SATA drives, SSD's, RAID arrays and with many different controllers. Consumer based operating systems are supported. Server based operating systems require Acronis Enterprise Applications.

Acronis True Image is a powerful back up utility that can be used two ways. You are able to run the program from a bootable CD or by installing it on your PC. The bootable CD is handy for performing on the fly back ups manually.

If installed, Acronis allows back up tasks to be automated. Tasks can be scheduled and will run automatically. Each release has become more robust from a feature standpoint. The majority of these features work very well, but like all software Acronis is not without it's faults. When manipulating data, things can go wrong, and sometimes they do.

Data if it's truly important should be backed up to two or more media sources. No one should ever find themselves in an unrecoverable situation. Anyone who doesn't do this is not taking the necessary precautions to safeguard their data. There is simply no excuse for this.

Acronis has never left me in a situation that I couldn't recover from. At worse, I've had to restore and start over, when things haven't turned out as expected.

Acronis allows you to back up to just about any media you like. Full network, FTP and cloud is also supported. The validation feature is great and is helpful by allowing you to confirm that a created image is error free.

Whether you are installing Acronis or building bootable media, it is very important to download the latest build of the version you've purchased. These are available free from the Acronis website. Big disks are also not a problem. This latest version supports disks greater than 2.2TB and GPT disk structure.

The Plus version adds support for dynamic disks, WinPE Boot Environment, and provides the ability to restore to dissimilar hardware (Universal Restore). Most will not use this last feature.

Using Acronis is said to be as easy as 1,2,3. I do not agree with this statement. You will not receive the full benefits of this product until you have spent some time using it, READING the manual and learning it's nuances. Some of its features are invaluable. Some you will never use. In the end however, it's a great product. One I recommend to everyone I know.

This is a nice if unspectacular upgrade. All three programs have been improved. It isn't the have-to-have change that iWork 08 was, but I'm happy with it. All three programs include more of Apple's slick templates, integrate with each other better and sport useful new features.

The biggest changes are in Numbers, which makes sense as it's the newest member of the suite. Apple claims 90 new functions. No longer being a power spreadsheet user I don't need all of them but the much improved charting is appreciated. Numbers also integrates with Pages for page merge, so you can use non-Address Book contact lists. That was an obvious omission in the previous version and is honestly useful. Also Numbers charts and tables inserted in Pages and Keynote will change if the spreadsheet is changed. Again an obvious omission in the last version that's been addressed. Don't move your files, though or you'll lose the links. As before, Numbers is an easy to use and attractive program for normal people. It isn't a replacement for Excel for power spreadsheet users. Thank goodness I don't have to do that kind of work any more.

Keynote has some stunning new transitions and effects that extend the sheer elegance of its presentations even farther beyond Powerpoint. If you do a lot of presentation work, Keynote is worth the price of the suite all by itself. And there are some new templates that reflect Apple's excellence in presentation.

Choosing templates in all three programs is now a bit easier with the improved template chooser.

Pages is the program that I use daily and it has a couple of new features that I appreciate. The full screen mode, that blacks out background distractions is welcome. That's been around in a number of programs before and I've gotten used to using it. The Pages implementation is a bit different, but done with Apple's typical elegance. The outlining feature introduced here is brilliantly implemented. It's the killer feature for me in the suite. I appreciate all the others but use this often.

There are some other new Pages features like the ability to work include formulas and integrate with Endnotes that I haven't used yet. This is a step in the right direction. Hopefully Apple will also include endnote compatibility Bookends, which I do use in an update. Pages still lacks some of the high end features of Word and probably always will. On the other hand it is more pleasant to work with and stable. Compatibility between Windows Office files and iWork seems to be as solid as it is between the Windows and Mac versions. Since Microsoft dropped macro support on the latest version of Office for Mac, I've switched totally to iWork and haven't looked back. This version of iWork just makes me happier with that decision.

I wanted to post a quick review on this because I have read mixed reviews while researching and looking at previous year's versions. First of all, I received this product about 1/2 hour ago, I am new to Hallmark software, didn't read any instructions, and have a 5yr old's birthday tomorrow so I figured this would be a good test.

1.) Install: The install was quick and the program auto-updated itself once. I am a circa 2013 PC using Windows 8.1 64-bit so it's a relatively new system. One word of caution is that the CD will spin for several minutes without any "install bar" movement. At first this was concerning. Once the bar started moving, the install took about 8 minutes. Keep in mind there are thousands of likely encrypted images so I thought he install was relatively painless.

2.) Creating a Card: I needed a unicorn or rainbow pony (this is a 5yr old girl!) and was happy to find one in about 30 seconds. Interface is very intuitive. I was able to add her name to the front of the card and import a photo into a custom frame for the inside cover of the card. The Hallmark text was great and I didn't mess with that.

3.) I printed to Avery 3265 folding card stock (from Amazon by the way) and the software had a custom setting for that exact product. The software recognized my Cannon PRO-100 (also from Amazon) and had to run two easy test prints to make sure the software understood how the printer loads - very clever. I loaded and printed the card in about 2 minutes.

Overall, I am very happy. The card looks awesome - very professional - and took less than 10 minutes. It took me longer to write this review. Installation, creation, and printing went very smooth. Hopefully this review will assist others. After reading prior year reviews, I was nervous but the software has exceeded my expectations. I plan to print Christmas cards this weekend so wish me luck!

Yes, you can edit uncomplicated videos with this package. In my limited testing, the program was stable.

Workflow is not much different than any of the other products out there.

Magix acquired this product line from Sony and I get the impression that they were in a big hurry to get the rebranded product into the marketplace. Feature enhancements from the last Sony version are pretty minor. More importantly, competitors like Cyberlink and Adobe offer more features for the same price.

Documentation is poor. I haven’t dealt with Magix support in ages, but it used to be okay. Not great, but okay.

Personally, I will continue using Cyberlink Director as my go-to video editor.

Jerry

Although I received the boxed version, I installed the downloaded version for lack of a working optical drive.

Downloading the installation package was a long process, mostly because Magix doesn’t clearly explain that when their server stops responding – which it often does – you can trigger a resumption of the download.

The download comes with an integrated installer that will install all the components. It doesn’t seem to include the add-in stuff seen in recent Magix installers.

But the installer does do something that in my opinion is unforgiveable: a restart in the course of the installation. No warning: it just Restarts. It presumes nothing else is open and that the Magix installer owns the machine. I am tempted to give the product a one-star review solely because of this.

That said, I find the documentation inadequate. There is a 91 page quickstart manual that omits many features, leaving you with the online help. The online manual is parse. For example, here is the text for Panning And Cropping Video Events: “Use the Pan/Crop plug-in to crop, zoom, rotate, or pan the selected video event. You can also use panning and cropping to add motion to video events created with still images.” There ae links to other sections, but many of them follow the same pattern.

NewBlueFX brings up its own App Manager application which demands a product key for Vegas, but won’t accept it. There is no explanation of why a product key for Vegas, which is not a NewBlueFX product, is required.

Smartsound Sonicfire Pro 6 does not seem to have been installed. I question the value of this program in any event, since I believe it is primarily a music loop editor, which I have little use or interest in.

In all, the Vegas Movie Studio Suite seems cheapened from previous incarnations.

As for editing, in terms of features, my first blush impression is that Vegas has not kept up with competitors such as Cyberlink Director and HitFilm and others.

Vegas itself has no operational features that set it apart from its competitors.

So the bottom-line: would I use it on a day-to-day basis when I have several other choices available?

No. It’s usable, but not a standout.

Jerry

Magix is a European software publisher and has been around for a long time. They publish a range of audio and video products as well as others.

I’ve been using their video and music products for years. On the whole, the products perform well and are typically updated annually. Some annual versions have been better than others, particularly in terms of stability. In fact, with Movie Edit Pro, the stability issue became so severe a few years ago, I pretty much switched to Cyberlink PowerDirector.

Movie Edit Pro 2016 Plus, so far in limited testing, is stable on Windows 10, but it’s still early in the game.

In terms of features, there is great competition among the entries on this run of video editing which includes Adobe Premiere Elements, Cyberlink PowerDirector, Corel VideoStudio and a very interesting upstart, TrakAxPC. Each entry is competent and can satisfy the needs of the casual to fairly advanced videomaker.

This edition of Movie Edit Pro comes with a couple of very useful extras, ProDAD’s Mercalli v2 image stabilization, which is very effective and the New Blue Titler, which is a pretty good package. Many action cam features have been added and if you have a GoPro or similar action cam, this will more than satisfy your needs. There is also limited support for 360 degree editing, where you can import 360 footage but not export it as 360. This is a feature with zero interest to me.

You can create up to 99 multimedia tracks, sync multiple cameras (which works well and is very handy), do limited color grading of your video, work with ChromaKey and a lot more. Object tracking has been vastly improved, so if you want to obscure someone’s face as they walk or run, it is much easier now. The keyframing feature has been substantially upgraded and is now on a par with that in Cyberlink PowerDirector.

Don’t be lulled into believing that there isn’t a learning curve with Movie Edit Pro. There is and it is fairly seep once you leave the very basics behind.

I strongly urge that you download the 30 day free trial and, in particular, the 399 page manual. At the very least, do browse through the manual to learn what features are available in the program.

Movie Edit Pro, by the way, can be used to produce some very sophisticated slide shows.

Overall, the feature list is long and the program has the potential of being very useful. It is a bit of pain to install: I had to provide my product code at least four times and go online three times after installation for additional downloads, permissions and registration.

Movie Edit Pro’s Use Interface is not particularly intuitive and you my initially find it confusing and intimidating. Don’t try to bull your way through it: watch the online tutorials, read the manual or both. Take your time. In about half an hour or so, you’ll pick up the basics.

Overall, I give this program a four out of five. I’d like a more intuitive interface. As I remarked earlier, I’ve had stability issues in the past and am a little nervous about the future. Time will tell.

Jerry

When it comes to tax, I am a complete idiot. I've been slowly learning but the progress is very slow. I bought this, thinking it is a simple "Yes or No" deal.

I was wrong. I often encountered a question to which I had no definite answer. I moved from NY to MA in the middle of the year, which made the situation a LOT more confusing.

NY, however, acted 100% according to my prediction; the accountant's calculation result indicated I was eligible for approx $1,000 return from NYC but instead they stole approx $370; a classic move of the most rotten city in the world.

If you're not good at boring documentation, forget this or TT and just go to your tax professional. It saves time, frustrations and concerns. Trying to save mere $50 or so by using a software like this is not worth it.

I've been using Turbotax for quite a few years, having switched from TaxCut when Kiplinger went to a completely online help system that used MS Internet Explorer exclusively, effectively cutting out Macintosh users.  I've had no issues with Turbotax; generally easy to use, updates returns starting with last year's information, etc.  Audit risk function is nice.  I've not used the state add on, since my state has an extremely simple tax form, that residents can file via the web for free.

Fire support not really. Poor documentation and not intuitive to use. Save to bricks will share progress with other accounts but not able to backup for self or share account with progress across multiple devices.

As a long time user, I have once again been forced to purchase this software. I cannot call it an upgrade, because there is really nothing of value in each new version to justify calling it an upgrade. Each new version carries some visual or cosmetic changes, but nothing of significance. The forced purchase is simply to continue the online update of banking and investment accounts. If you don't purchase the software, these online updates are turned off. If these updates are turned off, this software has little value for tracking your investments. Therefore, once you begin using this software to track your banking or investments, you are trapped into buying upgrades. For that reason, I would advise anyone who is contemplating an initial purchase of this software, to look elsewhere. Otherwise, you will be trapped just like the rest of us who are waiting for a competitor to show up with a better product that allows a transfer of our data out of Quicken.

UPDATE 27 March 2014: One more thing..... my online One Step Update with Wells Fargo is now broken. I simply want to retrieve any transactions from my checking accounts. This was working perfectly BEFORE the update to Quicken 2014. Now it is broken and in order to get the account updated I must login to my checking account and initiate the creation of a download file. Then I must go back to Quicken and upload this file. What used to be a seamless and simple process now takes a number of steps. This is not an upgrade. This is a downgrade.

UPDATE 5 April 2014: It turns out there are alternatives that handle the downloading of transactions. I have been looking at an app called iBank and it offer several ways to automatically download data your banking and investment data. It also allows for importing of your Quicken data. Check it out.[...]

UPDATE 5 May 2014: I've noticed that this version randomly double enters values into different accounts when I run the transaction download. Over the course of a week, it repeated entries from some unrelated checking account into a CD from another bank. Completely random entries. As a result, until I noticed this, I was led to believe I had over $5000 additional in this CD account. This is a very bad problem. If an employee made this serious of an error, they would be fired.

UPDATE 24 May 2014: As noted by numerous other users, the default spacing for most of the register fields is totally worthless. It does not matter if you use the normal or larger fonts. In either case most columns are unreadable until you manually expand them. And of course the program makes no attempt to save your settings, so each time you run the software you are fiddling with column spacings just so you can read them. Very, very sloppy programming. Intuit must be scraping the bottom of the barrel for their development talent because this product is not ready for general release.

UPDATE 18 June 2014: More bugs... Software now sporadically disconnects the online link with my bank account. As a result, I can't update my checking/banking accounts until I go through a lengthy reset process. Another interesting bug is how the software randomly inserts bogus numbers into hidden accounts. These hidden numbers show up in the net worth calculation. This is getting downright scary! I am losing trust in this software.

Our organization recently purchased ACT 2007 Premium for Workgroups ST edition (which is the top model). ACT 2007 is buggy, has terrible performance, practically non-existent Tech Support, and Zero technical documentation. The performance is so bad that if you ask someone on the phone support line about it their response is "Yes it will be slower than ACT 6" that's not made up folks, that happened to me!  If you are on the IT staff and tasked with making this software work don't expect to find any good documentation on ACT, particularly if you are looking for technical details because you won't find any. Not on Google and certainly not on Sage Software's website. Sure there's a knowledge base but great care is taken to make sure that any behind-the-scenes details are left out. And certainly don't be impressed by any talk of ACT 2007 running on a SQL database because it is a proprietary named instance of SQL and you can't manage it unless you want to pay $1000 (yes that's in addition to the cost of purchasing ACT and any licenses you may need) so that you can have the SA password. And please, PLEASE for the love of all that is holy don't try to share your ACT database across a WAN or you WILL have nightmares about the experience. If you are just one person considering ACT as a personal contact management solution... it might be an acceptable solution. If you are considering this for multiple users then consider something else because ACT will not work for you unless you are willing to shell out the additional $$ to get consultants to set it up.

As with all software, the user interface and its workflow has its quirks. Some of it you may find counter-intuitive and not very helpful.

The State edition is less evolved than the Federal edition. It expects more things of the user to know. It is almost seamlessly integrated into the Federal edition of the software. If you have a fairly straight-forward tax situation and you already know what you are supposed to do tax-wise, then this is a convinient alternative to pen/paper and a lot cheaper than going to a specialist when you send in all the rebates.

As with all software, the user interface and its workflow has its quirks. Some of it you may find counter-intuitive and not very helpful.

But if you have a fairly straight-forward tax situation and you already know what you are supposed to do tax-wise, then this is a convinient alternative to pen/paper and a lot cheaper than going to a specialist when you send in all the rebates.
Why oh why didn't I get Quicken a long time ago????  Great program for tracking expenses and keeping organized.  Love all the reports you can print to really get into the number.

I have tried a couple of different software packages from Corel that claimed to convert between Office file formats and PDF. Neither of those was effective; therefore, I was still in search for an effective solution. So I decided to give PDF Converter Professional 8.0 a try. It has plenty of features beyond converting files, but those are expensive window dressing if the conversion isn't effective.

So, how does it stack up? Unfortunately, it didn't fare much better than the Corel software I've used in the past. After installation it integrates with Office to provide the capability to create PDF files. It does a reasonable job preserving formatting in the Office to PDF conversion. However, the converse is almost an utter failure. I tried numerous PDFs that were sourced from Word and Excel, and I used PDF Converter Professional to import them back into their origin format. The Word documents lost font integrity and bullet points. However, the bigger issue came with the Excel file. It was six pages with color formatted header and title lines. The resulting Excel file had no color, and each page from the PDF was placed in a separate sheet. The result was useless. I realize that the issues could likely be with Office, but Nuance claims that their software provides the most accurate PDF conversion. None of the documents I sampled were overly complex, but the PDF to Office conversion was an abject failure. My search continues, and I have to advise against this software package if preserving format when going from PDF back to Office is a priority.

I love taking pictures with my digital camera and enjoy sharing them with family and friends. Paintshop ProX5 allows me to organize and edit my pictures as well as to share them with family and friends.

It is important for me to have an easy to use photo editing software. This software is easy enough for someone like me to use but has features that an advanced user would enjoy.

There are three tabs at the top of the screen that help a person use the software. The tabs are manage, adjust and edit.

The manage tab deals with organizing photos, identifying them and rating them. The adjust tab allows you to use a lot of different features to adjust the sharpness, tone, brightness and contrast of a picture. The edit tab allows you to edit photos in more ways than I care to list. There is an adjust option in the edit tab that allows a person to use many of the features in the adjust tab.

I have yet to use the adjust and edit features completely but I could see a professional being able to do very complicated tasks. The help menu is great and there are ways to do quick fixes which allow the software to fix up a picture for you. I have had this software for a couple of months and am still learning all that I can do.

Paintshop ProX5 has helped me to better organize my photos. More importantly, I have been able to make okay pictures into really nice ones.

This is the best version of Norton Internet Security yet. It loads easier than ever, runs better than ever, has some great new features, finds more spyware and offers great technical support. I highly recommend it.

Loading:

I have been using Norton products for many years (starting with anti-virus). Early on Norton was a hassle to load because the previous year's version had to be removed before the new version could be installed and the process was not very easy and often had to be done by searching for files manually. Things got better over time and now loading is simple. I installed the disk, it removed my version of Norton Internet Security 2009 and then installed version 2010. Nothing could be simpler.

Running:

I have an older computer with only 512 MB of RAM. Some newer programs don't work on it and can only be used on another computer which has double the ram. This version of Norton Internet Security runs better than last year's version on the computer with 512 MB of RAM. It has a performance feature which does make my computer run better and a performance screen which shows the CPU usage of the computer itself and that of Norton.

New Features:

My favorite new feature on this product occurs when I am downloading something. I downloaded a coupon printer program so I could print a Disney Pixar coupon. When I attempted to download the program Norton scanned the program and a screen popped up saying the program was safe, it had been available for about a certain number of months and that a certain number of Norton users had already downloaded the program (I don't remember how many months or users but Norton listed exact numbers). Knowing that others with Norton had downloaded the program without a problem made me feel more secure.

I also like the new Norton screen. It is much easier to use all the Norton Features this year as compared to last year.

Spyware:

Last years version of Norton would pick up 1 tracking cookie each time I ran a scan. I always run one or two other spyware programs every so often and they would fine a lot of spyware that Norton did not find. Now when I run a Norton scan it will pick up approximately 30 or so tracking cookies/spyware threats and remove them. I still run other spyware programs that will pick up a few things Norton missed but there are not nearly as many as in past years.

Technical Support:

Technical support continues to improve. I am okay with computers but I am not great, so I often call technical support before loading a new program to make sure I am loading the program correctly and to make sure my computer has enough RAM etc., for the program to run correctly. Some technical support people seem to be reading off a screen and know very little. This is not currently the case with Norton.

Norton Technical Support was great when I called. I was not pleased with it a few years ago but it has gotten much better. As mentioned Norton loaded without a problem but the tech stayed on the line and then went over a few of the new Norton features. He knew what he was doing.

Conclusion:

This is the best version of Norton Internet Security yet. It has been great seeing Norton software continually improve. Norton seems to be listening to customer concerns and actually cares about creating a user-friendly product. This is a superb product.

Unlike the previous vendor's cookbook I used, this software delivers a sense of intuitive features and initial trust. Where this software may be a bit overwhelming at first, I STRONGLY suggest you download the manual and just read it - once thru it, you should have a good understanding of how to use the features. The software delivers, and does great things to simplify your life in that you don't have to sit and type each and every recipe out - I have scanned, downloaded, pinned, and even taken photos thru the IPAD to upload about 300 of my recipes that I have held onto for many, many years. The ability to capture and move blocks of text into the recipe software is OUTSTANDING - could not have done this without that feature. I was absolutely dreading the task of typing each of them.... On another note, we did purchase the MasterCook interface, it worked like a charm - just make sure to export an then import into Cook'n.

One thing to note - Where the cloud idea is really fantastic, giving the ability to interface between my PC, Ipad, and Iphone, please know that there are some buggy features, that will need to be worked out. We have experienced a few problems with it, but Customer Service, has been very supportive and responsive to our emails. Don't rename cookbooks once established on the base system - the cloud hates it - so think out your organization of cookbooks. Fear not though, the software does do a back up on the system of record, without relying solely on the cloud - think of the cloud interface as an interface tool where you can check recipes on your iphone while at the store for instance. We do back up frequently and the backup is actually easy to find!!!

In the end, this software is well worth the price, if you are really interested in capturing recipes from the web, such as Pinterest, blogs, or other established cooking sites. In owning this software for just over a week, I could not be happier with the intuitive features, good design, and ease of just doing the simple things, let alone the more advanced internet interfacing. I want to thank DVO Enterprises for their vision - cookbook software that FINALLY delivers - hands down.

On Black Friday 2013, I got this game as the FAOTD (Free App Of The Day). I downloaded a previous Fire Maple Game, "The Lost City", as the FAOTD exactly 1 year ago, but I never played it... as I was playing other games on my phone at the time. I eventually forgot about it after I upgraded my phone and forgot to reinstall the app. I came back to playing both games just a few days ago.

Simply put, this game is a point & touch graphical puzzle game. If any of you played Myst or 7th Guest back in the 90s on PC, this is pretty much the same type of game. I have played other types of puzzle games on my phone but have often deleted them due to a lack of interest because of "hidden" objects that make the puzzle games extremely frustrating.

The thing I like about Fire Maple Games' products is that the puzzles are really straightforward if you think hard enough. There is a a lot of backtracking but the hint system is there to help you in case you get stuck. I had to use it a few times in this game and after the solution was provided to me, I felt so stupid.

Graphically, the game is very well done with nice animations, sound effects, and music.

In my opinion, "The Lost City" was much harder than "The Adventures of Mosaika" due to the numerous backtracking you had to do, but it was still fun nevertheless.

Very fun game. So fun in fact, I'm considering buying their original game, [[ASIN:B004FOA84A The Secret of Grisly Manor]].

I got "The Lost City" for free as the FAOTD (free app of the day) exactly 1 year ago, but I never played it... as I was playing other games on my phone at the time. I eventually forgot about it after I upgraded my phone and forgot to reinstall the app. I came back to the game shortly after completing [[ASIN:B00FGFVMGA The Adventures of Mosaika]], another FAOTD on Nov 29th.
