Quicken has made my life much easier in tracking balancing and budgeting for my family daily and monthly expenses.  Balance monthly expenses with banking is outstanding.  It has also help me track and set goals for future retirement income

I've used a number of products over the years, including an envelope based system that worked well for years. However, I found myself too much on autopilot, losing touch with my real budget needs. YNAB has helped me to get a better grasp on my real budget needs. The ipod touch/iphone App has saved my wife and I lots of grief over lost receipts, long intervals between getting receipts entered, etc. which constantly led to overspending. We just bought the App a few weeks ago and for the first time in a long time, it looks like we will stay within our grocery budget!!

The forums are very active. You post something on there and within no time, multiple people are helping. Very newbie friendly people too. Documentation and the training videos (not to mention the free webinars) have been hugely helpful. I've watch some of the videos multiple times to make sure I fully understood the process and the rules.

Product is very active in development, which means new features and quick bug fixes. Both the owner and the developer are active in the forums so they have a constant feel for how people are using their software. Very hometown feel but yet big city results with the product. Well refined and only getting better. If you're looking to really get a hold of your spending and budgeting, there's a bit of a learning curve but with all of the resources they provide, you can't go wrong.

It is to do your taxes so what is there that you can truly like about it.  It has worked for me for over 10 years with various situations, various complexity levels, with varrying levels of frustration.  I get specific.  If it says this "and" that and I was looking for this "or" that, I will break out in a sweat.  Over the years they have gotten better but I always have one or another tricky  problem in which I feel I am taking an educated guess.  In any case, it does work but watch out because they try to sell you a few things at the end including audit support.  If you were sure of your product, would I need audit support at an extra expense if I put everything in properly and honestly, which your audit support can't guarantee.  In any case, I still use it every year and with proper prep like gathering bills and receipts, sorting them, totaling them. etc.  ahead of time, I get my taxes done pretty quickly.  Many don't need it if you are getting paid by 1 employer who takes out Federal and State taxes and you have no major expenses or deductions.

Hulu Plus at a mere  $7.99/mo is great! Exactly what I needed, which is odd because I did NOT subscribe to Hulu or Hulu plus through Amazon. I upgraded on the Hulu site, and do not appreciate Amazon tracking my off site purchases.

This is yet another year that TurboTax delivers on quality and their promise of empowering the average joe to do your own taxes! This version offered yet a few more innovations and improvements. Nothing real notable, but at least they aren't getting complacent and letting this staple item just go stale. Loved the results I got this year, and (assuming I never get audited for crazy issues), I'll keep buying this! I highly recommend!
The fact that they include the audit defense in the price, along with e-filing, is what sold me on the product.

I use this to help in auditing wireless signals for my home and office.  I've also used it to help customers resolve their signal issues in their homes.  This is by farmost one of the best android apps for any IT admin.

If I could give zero stars I would. In fact, I would give negative stars. Whether it's the Alexa app or a Fire tablet, Amazon is always spying on us, intruding into our lives uninvited. The only reason I have the Alexa app is because the Echo supposedly requires it, although I have rarely been able to get the app to do anything it's supposed to do. Instead, I either have to call tech support (another nightmare needing an entirely separate review), or I have to try to do it with a computer (which, by the way, can't be just any computer; it has to have a WiFi connection that is also used by the Echo). I couldn't even install the Alexa app from Amazon. I had to go to Google. And I have to reinstall it every time I need to use it. And with every reinstall comes Big Brother: all of my notification settings are changed so that I'm notified of EVERYTHING on my phone. The Fire tablet has the same problems. I'll buy a different tablet next time because it took me three hours on the phone with tech support to get the notifications stopped on the Fire. And Amazon forces all of their preferred apps on to the home screen whether you want them or not. I'm delighted to see the big tech companies like Amazon under investigation for intruding into peoples lives. Regulations with an iron fist cant come soon enough.

I downloaded this from Kingsoft, but it is the same thing so here is my story-
1) Bought a new Windows 8.1 laptop after Microsoft finished off my XP Pro.
2) Discovered MS Office is a 30-day trial, then rent or buy at an outrageous price
3) Downloaded WPS 2014 FREE to check it out
4) It's fantastic! I've been using MS Office professionally and at home for thirty years and still running 2003/7, later versions gradually got too busy for my taste. WPS does everything I need and more, and much more user friendly. Some esoteric things may be missing but nothing very important.
The files seem fully compatible and save and open in both formats. MS Office users, we are free at last! I really do recommend everyone to at least try this.
Good product, does the job quickly and correctly. I've used this for years and never been audited.  But wait until February 1, when the price is cheapest.

The gameplay is excellent. All animations are quick & smooth. Definitely the best card based game/app I've ever played. However, I feel that Aces Gin Rummy Pro is really lacking in not tracking any kind of statistics. It doesn't even show a simple won-loss record! Capturing other data like average points per game, average points per hand, average opponent points per game & hand, etc., would be really cool!

For the first time in many years, I didn't use Turbo Tax last year to do a return. Oh what a mistake. Never again. I usually let cpas take care of my personal return. But I often help my children do their far less complicated forms and this has been a lifesaver. Last year I thought I'd just do an online program that was free. Ouch. Just avoided an audit and had to call in some help to get it cleaned up post-submission. I've never, never, never had that problem with TurboTax.

This free version is not perfect as it happens with many other free and/or paid-for versions; however, I gave it five stars for the benefits received throughout the years. It has detected viruses my security program has not and it deletes a great amount of tracking cookies. Also, it does not interfere with my current anti-virus program and does not disrupt my system.
It was an okay tax program.  It was a little expensive but cheaper than paying someone.  I got my refund and it does not look as if I am going to be audited.

The app had stopped working. and for a while it was no longer available. But the app was recently updated (March 2018) and seems to be working. I am tracking my daugther's trip to Europe right now. It notified me of a track change, which my daughter confirmed had happened. It has the weather at the arrival airport (but not the time zone). It does show current in flight status such as airspeed and altitude, and plane type. The map can't be enlarged, but it does show the approximate position. .

Permissions: nothing horribly intrusive.

Battery/memory: app does not exit when using Kindle Fire back button.

The concept of tracking items on various game servers ("realms") is nothing new, so it is the concept execution which matters most.

I wish the controls were more intuitive/better documented. However, the app does have a very clean user interface. I use The Undermine Journal ([...]) so I don't have a use for WoW Wish List. I could see myself using it though if I owned a smart phone with a data plan.

I have used MalwareByte's Anti-Malware program on my desktop and laptop PCs for many years and have never been disappointed with its quality and effectiveness. Naturally, I was ecstatic when I discovered there is now an app for mobile and other devices.

The app's user interface is clean and simple. Very nice.

It has what a person normally expects from this type of program - scan for unpleasant things, set up frequency of updates, set a schedule for automatic scans - but I was pleasantly surprised to see functions for auditing security and privacy, managing apps (I particularly appreciate the inclusion of the ability to task-kill apps that like to run in the background or shut down messily), and that it smartly detects what your device does or does not need it to do (no duplication of effort, exactly as described in the app's details). Of special note: The app has real-time protection! For free! (This feature requires a one-time purchase of a license in the desktop version.)

I strongly recommend people browse through the screenshots on the MBAM app homepage so they can see what I am describing.

Thank you for a great, trustworthy app, MalwareBytes. You've helped me catch and clean out viruses, trojans, and adware for years; and I am very pleased to have this protection extended to my Kindle Fire.

This is a poor substitute for the Google App that was removed from the Kindle Fire's Android implementation.  I am stationed in Germany with the Army.  Every time I try to search for something in my town over here, MQ automatically appends the postal code and then interprets it as Burbank, California.  Even if it says Germany or Deutschland in the search.  This app couldn't find it's way out of a paper bag.  Getting so tired of things i paid for like Google operating system and text-to speech being gimped by Amazon.

I downloaded this for my teen for period tracking. Unbeknownst to me, there is a chatroom within the app with multiple rooms. My teen was chatting with quite a few other people in there who encouraged starving yourself as well as express and encourage suicidal ideation. Do not let your teen use this to track their period. We are still dealing with the side effects of this.

I've heard this game called Boxes or Dots.

You draw a line between two dots, if you make a box, you have to draw another line. You claim boxes you've made, person who had claimed the most area when all the dots are connected wins.

This app let's you have four players - any mix of human and AI (different levels too). You can play straight boxes or other shapes.

It's really a perfect implementation of this childhood game.

I bought Moho from the official website. I bought some tutorials off of Udemy. I’m going to probably spend at least a few months wrapping my head around the best workflow for this software. Don’t be fooled by amateur works. This software is capable of amazing works. You just have to devote time to learning it.

Anime Studio Pro is pricey compared to Debut. However if you want to create frame by frame Animation. Anime Studio Pro 11 finally has this feature. Smithmicro has many videos that mostly cater to this version of Anime Studio 11. Most of the documentation is geared towards the Pro version. I only bought the Debut version because it was cheaper and I wanted to learn Anime Studio's original selling point it's bone feature. Many Anime Studio users are thrilled by the frame by frame feature. I however am more of a Toon Boom enthusiast. I have Harmony Essentials for traditional animation. I recommend this version over the Debut version of Anime Studio 11 because you will find more tutorials for the bone feature and a webinar for the frame by frame feature. I have seen many animations made using Anime Studio that are nice looking but I have yet to create anything that fluid and lively with Anime Studio yet. Most people who make great animation with Anime Studio are far and few between or foreigners.
If you have a previous version of Anime Studio Pro you can get Anime Studio Pro 11 for a bargain at Smithmicro. I reccommend only buying Anime Studio from Amazon if you do not own versions 7-10 of Anime Studio Pro.

I like this version but I have a few complaints. The features that were in previous version of Manga Studio are absent. I notice I cannot create a project with more than one page like I could in version 3. If you want a project with more than one page and be able to print a story you need the EX version. Other than that I love Manga Studio 5. It is perfect for a hobbyist like me and at a price I actually find reasonable. EX version is better but you'll pay more for it. I recommend EX if money is no object or you happen to be a professional comic artist.
I also like the painting features. I don't find the 3D characters and environments useful at all. But I guess they're good if you like to create comics with 3D figures. I just don't know if it is possible to import your own 3D models and characters in this version of Manga Studio 5. I'm sure you can in the EX version though.

I do find this software to be very good since I'm familiar with how to use Adobe Illustrator's 3D extrude ,revolve and Bevel effects. This software is good but version 9 is available in english too bad it's not being sold on Amazon.com.

Shade 8.5 is pretty much a steep learning curve but Autodesk Maya and 3Ds Max are way harder and so are Carrara 6 Pro and Carrara 7.
This software is pretty good for under $1,000 dollars though I think this software would be worth the price their asking for if it came with video tutorials and detailed documentation.
I've recieved this software as a gift from my father and I think I have a basic understanding. I've this software for at least a week and I think I'm getting somewhere in the modeling aspect of this software.
3D software regardless of it's vendor is very difficult to learn so you better have patience and hunt for online video tutorials if you don't feel like reading manuals.
It does an excellent job of tracking your steps and the distance you have walked.  It keeps me accountable.

So, turns out - thanks to an IRS audit - that this software doesn't know how to correctly handle federal deductions of mortgage interest. Yay. Just what I wanted, a huge extra tax bill with late payment penalties. Thanks H&R Block.

Specifically, it doesn't understand that there's a tight limit on how much mortgage interest you can deduct, based on the amount of your mortgage (e.g. $750k if you're filing singly). It just blindly deducts _all_ the mortgage interest. The IRS very adamantly do not like that. There are costly penalties. The IRS couldn't care less that this software was to blame, not the user.

Oh, and their 'guarantees' about correctness and all that jazz - basically empty words. There's no way at all to reach anyone with relevant authority short of a very convoluted and onerous process involving snail mail - the instructions for which are buried so deep on H&R Block's website that it took me days to find them. It's clearly designed to actively discourage you from actually holding them to their promises. Plus at the end of the day all they'll even offer is to cover the cost of the penalties, which may be the least of your concerns vs dealing with an audit and having to make unexpected, huge extra tax payments.

OK, sat down to do our taxes, only to discover, having made some progress, that it said that the Fed. Tax people were still working on how to handle the "1099R" forms.  In other words, it was not complete as I expected.  So, anyway, I proceeded and then went back to check on the internal menu, if I could update it?  Yes, I could and promptly did so.  OK, I proceded on and everything looked great.  I sent the tax forms on line,  3/1/2022.  But not having it to automatically do the normal update, "Just-IN-Case," decided to purchase the "Audit Protection Plan." The IRS has at least accepted the  e-Tax forms.  I've used Turbo-Tax for a bunch of years.

I am an intermediate spanish student with a score of 3/3 on the Defense Language profieciency test (DLPT V). I am using Rosetta Stone to retain my proficiency pay.

Rosetta Stone version 3 is the best product on the market for drilling spanish. It makes doing spanish drills almost seem like a game. My teenage daughter studied nine hours on rosetta stone - including four hours at one stretch - a personal record of studying anything for her!

Version 3 is vastly improved over version two. I would recommend upgrading to anyone with verson 2. Version three has better progress tracking. Version 2 was a top competitor amoung other language products on the market -version three makes Rosetta Stone the best by far.

I do not recommend this product as a stand alone solution to learning a new language. After a student has completed a spanish 101 / 102 course, or in conjunction with, this product will take the student to new levels of proficiency. By itself you will be confused of why you are learning items by rote.

As a high intermediate spanish student, I am learning, or relearning, new items with every lesson, even in level 1.

The only con with the product is the marketing of the product as the method to learn a language is like a child learns. This is done through repetition, correction, and visually. This is deceptive as children have higher capacities than adults to learn new languages. As adults we need explanations and rules that relate to our language to allow us to take an already ingrained skill set and apply it to a new lanquage. If you are a visual learner, this product may work for you as a stand alone program. If you learn best with written instructions, you definetly need an introductory spanish course.

I've been using LastPass for years. I use it on all my PCs and on my smartphone. I've never had a single problem. Does what it says, and at a buck a month, the premium version is worth every penny and more!

People call cloud password managers "unsafe" but that's only because they don't understand how they work. NONE of your passwords are actually stored in the cloud where someone can read them if the service gets hacked. LastPass only stores the mathematical mish-mash that results when your passwords are combined with your LastPass passphrase. So even if someone stormed LastPass's headquarters and took the servers, they'd still need your password to decrypt the information. When you use LastPass, the decryption takes place on YOUR computer (or phone) so EVEN THEN, the password is STILL never revealed to LastPass.

If you're using the same password(s) on multiple sites, you're going to get bitten eventually. With LastPass, not only can you use a unique password per website, but you can store and use unique USERNAMES per website, making tracking you down more difficult while keeping you safe.

Software makes you uninstall all other competing products. Attempts to install itself in every facet of your machine, and then slows the thing down.

Promised rebate. Denied rebate even though I submitted ALL required documentation. No email support. No humans at 1-800 number.

Don't Do THIS!

I like Quicken. It seems that I have to get the new version of Quicken every year as Intuit stops supporting older versions. If there are great new improvements, I don't use them for tracking my home income and expenses. The minor differences that I see just change  the way I do basic tracking. Sometimes the categories seem to change to their default category with new versions. I would be sure to check the online balances vs. Quicken every month and every new version update. Old discrepancies are difficult if not impossible to find if you let it go too long.

This is what you want, well now that XP, and before that Windows 2000 went away.  How does Microsoft make me want the previous version of their software with each new release?  And now, like Star Trek movies of the last century, they only make a winner every other time!?!!  Tough times for the help-desk team until someone else takes over the enterprise desktop.  Well, I don't get paid to ponder the software industry, I'm getting all the Win 7 I can until Win 9 Service Pack 2 arrives.

Love this software. Coverted from another program. No looking back. Easy to organize. Uses records/cards -- so easier to import/export. Great search feature. Syncing with multiple devices & platforms is much easier.

Update 2: Turbo Tax Deluxe with state file just gets worse and worse. After having to settle for filing my Federal return by mail (see Update 1 below), I went back through to make sure there were no more software updates to download / install, and went through my entire return again. I didn't change any entries. Turbo Tax runs an error check before you complete your return. It said "congratulations, there are no errors in your return." I followed all of the prompts to file by mail, and when I clicked on "print my returns," Turbo Tax popped up an message saying there were errors on my return. This is literally 20 seconds after the error check said there were no errors on my return, and I haven't changed a thing. The entry that Turbo Tax said contained the error was the child care expense (see below). So I went back to the stinking return, and sure enough Turbo Tax has changed the expenses I entered YET AGAIN. This time I deleted them, re-entered them, and Turbo Tax showed the correct amount until I clicked "continue" and went to the summary for deductions. Guess what? Turbo Tax once again changed the total. And when I went through the error check, it said there were no errors. But when I wanted to print the forms, it said there was an error with the childcare expenses! It said the total amount did not match the amount I had entered. I have tried fixing this error at least a dozen times. I'm going to ask Amazon for a refund, because there's no way I'm going to file my state or Federal return now.

Update 1: I am revising my original review down from 2 stars to 1 star. I will definitely NOT buy Turbo Tax again next year. The e-filing doesn't work, and you have no choice but to print and file your Federal return by mail. I tried to transmit my return, and got an error message saying that I couldn't e-file because there was a product update that I had to download first. I downloaded the update, went through the entire e-file steps again, transmitted my return, and . . . got an error saying my return could not be transmitted because there was an update that I needed to download, and that if the problem persists, to file by mail instead.

I have used Turbo Tax for 3 years because I was tired of paying a professional tax preparer an arm and a leg. I have been generally happy with Turbo Tax, until this year. Turbo Tax 2013 Deluxe edition for Mac has a LOT of bugs this year.

Examples:

When it runs the error check, it identifies entries that don't match. I have repeatedly gone back and forth from the entry that it says doesn't match, and guess what? The entry I put in is correct, but the number Turbo Tax shows for that field is different. I even cleared out the field and re-entered it, and the error check still showed and error and a different number. After fixing it three times, the error check didn't show that field as having an error. How on earth Turbo Tax reads from one field and then changes the entry I have no idea, but it happened several times.

I also entered some deductions, and Turbo Tax changed them when I looked at the summary for the deductions section. Specifically, it changes one of the medical expense deductions on the 1040, and it also changed a child care expense entry. And it does that repeatedly, even after I click on "edit" and correct it back to what I entered. This is not to be confused with the deduction not matching the expense. Turbo Tax is changing the expense I enter.

Also in the error checking, Turbo Tax displays a cryptic error referencing a field that contains an error. There's no description about what that field is, or where to correct it. I had two of those.

Turbo Tax imports all of the data from the Federal return into the state return. You'd think that it could do that accurately since it's just reading the data you enter into the fields in the state form. Wrong. For some reason, it changes the AGI. It displays an error telling you the AGI on the state form doesn't match the AGI on the Federal form, and if you manually correct it, it just asks you to confirm the amount and then it accepts it.

When e-filing the Federal form, you have to either enter your IRS PIN # or last year's AGI. The link to get your IRS PIN doesn't work. Or more appropriately, the link works, but when you enter the information into the IRS website, it returns an error, too. If you choose the AGI method, guess what? Turbo Tax Deluxe 2013 imports the AGI from last year's return (remember, I filed last year's and the year before, so these files are on my computer). But guess what? Turbo Tax 2013 imports the WRONG AGI. I have no idea where it's coming up with the number. I pulled out the paper copy of my 2012 return that I printed straight out of Turbo Tax, and sure enough the number that TT 2013 is importing is wrong. So I manually re-entered the correct amount, and TT 2013 accepted it.

If you click on the help section related to these errors, it gives you basically unhelpful information saying that you should check your entries, and if you're still getting an error, there "might" be glitches in the software, and that you should report them to Intuit. They provide you a link to report the problems, but you can't actually report any problems unless you create a user account to post to their forums. I'm personally not going to waste my time to create a login in order to provide free software QA services. Either make it easy for us to report bugs, or give us a credit for identifying ones you can duplicate.

Hidden Fees:

I'm very disappointed about the very misleading description Intuit provides. The product says that it includes one state return (you can pay less for a different version of the software that is Federal only). If you want to e-file your state return, there is a $19.99 e-file fee for each state return. Sure enough, in the fine print, there's a little note that tells you e-filing your state return "might" also incur additional fees. There's no fee to file a paper copy, so just know that even if you take advantage of the free Federal e-file, you will probably want to print a copy of your state return and spend a few cents to mail it in.

I DEFINITELY won't buy TurboTax next year.
The software was clutch, the licensing utility could use better documentation.
I couldn't download this software because of some remnant of McAfee Enterprise hidden away on my computer. searches for this remnant came up blank rendering the software useless.

Since I am untainted by using earlier versions, I can only judge by what the product is now.
Being a manualphobe I jump in and start using the product and for me, at least, it was fairly intitutive. I had it a week and already input a couple hundred members of my family tree.
How the search works is kinda strange. Sometimes when I find a relative in a census and find the parents attached in the document and I go to merge the data, no parent information is gathered. OK fine, I will write it down then go look for the parents. Sometimes it re-finds the document an sometimes it doesn't.

I like that I can attach pictures to relatives. I raided my mom's scrapbooks for old photos to scan in. I hate thinking of these people just vanishing. There was a picture of my great-great grandma and grandpa's gravestone that grandma had taken years ago. I didn't know where it was located but it clearly showed the names and dates. By back tracking up grandma's data I found her people had come from Virginia, and that was where I found the grave site - pretty cool.
I get annoyed at it when it seems to freeze up, but if you wait patiently (aka play freecell for a little bit), it eventually settles down (on all but 1 occasion). It does not seem to loose data when this happens. I worry about overloading the thing and back it up every time I get done updating it.

All in all I would purchase again.

Word of warning however - using this software is addictive. Every time you have a spare few minutes you are on it and time gets away and it is midnight on a work night. A sympathetic spouse is a must.

This takes some of the pain away from tax season. It asks and you answer, doesn't get much easier. Make sure you have your numbers in front of you so you can get it all done at once and not have to skip around. Last year I got audited using TurboTax, but I think it's because I adopted and they weren't seeing the receipts (that I did indeed send to them). But whatever, worked out.

I've been using Quicken 2004 since I got my Mac laptop three years ago. It's not great but what other choice do we have? I've been intentionally not upgrading based on all of the negative reviews since what I've got does what I need it to do and I'm used to its problems by now.

Today I got a prompt when I launched Quicken saying that I will no longer be able to download my transactions from banks, credit card companies, etc. Intuit will not support it or allow it -- unless I "upgrade" to Quicken 2007 for the low low price of 50 frickin' dollars! That's an upgrade price? (All "online services" for Quicken 2004 will be discontinued starting April 1, 2007, including getting stock quotes and portfolio tracking.)

Oh how I wish there were a hungry young software firm that made a basic, home financial program for the Mac that would download transactions. Anybody know of any? As soon as one comes out, I'm switching. And, Intuit, if you're listening, I'm never buying TurboTax again. Your competitors are getting my money for the bad-faith way you treat your customers. Forcing your product to be basically unusable after 3 years is unconscionable.

I used this to do last year's taxes when my CPA could not be contacted and the deadline neared.  It is simple and takes you step by step through each question. There are links to click to read more about certain fields and questions. It really is and has always been the easiest and only way to file your own taxes without worrying that you made an error or missed something that could cost you money! This takes the guesswork out of it and gives you a reliable tax prep that also includes a review at the end to tell you how likely you are to get an audit with the information entered. I highly recommend to those who need to do their taxes but don't have the finances or need for a professional CPA.

10 years of using Turbo Tax no audits, no real hassles. Makes my life easy. Their "Next Year Tax Planner" works perfectly. The last 4 years I owed ZERO. My entire family uses Turbo Tax and are very happy with the results as well.

Have to add the price they charge for an additional state besides the free one included in the package is too high.

I have been making home videos for years. I used VideoStudio 7, 9, X1, X2 plus, X3 Plus, X4,X5 and now the latest X6. I also tried digital video softwares from Pinnacle, Roxio, Adobe Premiere. However, Corel software beats them for stability and easy of use. The Adobe Premiere is a great software, but it's overwhelming and overkill for most of us. In general, Corel's VideoStudio is less frustrating than any other softwares.

From product line point of view, Corel VideoStudio has all the basics, not a lot of bells and whistles that are hard to learn. The basic functions are easy to use and intuitive. You use the timeline to place video clips, then add transitions, background music, titles. After you are done, it can be easily exported to YouTube, FaceBook, Flicker...etc. Or you can burn your own DVD, or Blu-Ray disks.

By the way, editing HD videos requires a very powerful PC system, this is especially true if you are dealing with the highly compressed AVCHD videos. My system configuration is: Core i7 processor, 12 GB ram, Window 7 64-bit, 1GB video card, two SATA-2 drives. I have no problem to advance AVCHD videos frame by frame for precise editing, or to fast forward AVCHD videos for quick preview...etc. Also, the rendering time is fast. During rendering, my CPU usage can go up to 90%. If you see frame drop, out-of-sync between audio and video, color bleaching, software is slow to response to mouse clicking, video editing process is not smooth...etc, before blaming VideoStudio software, you may want to check your hardware system first.

Here is one trick I learned from professional Video Magazine. While running the VideoStudio with you main hard drive (like C-drive), you should put all video files (both the video input files and video output files) in a separate hard drive. Doing so can ensure a better system performance. With my system, if I put all the video files in the C-drive where the main program resides, from time to time, the VideoStudio program will still hang (not response to your mouse) for couple of second (not crash). This hanging problem goes away once I put videos files at a separate hard drive (not the C-drive).

So far, I have done couple home videos and created some AVCHD disks. I am very happy with the latest VideoStudio X6. Especially the newly added subtitling support.

Though I have long experience with VideoStudio, I would definitely recommend this software to beginners. It comes with so many pre-packaged automated processes that beginners can use them easily.

This software is essential for any corporate/transactional attorney who needs to quickly and accurately review and compare changes in negotiated documents; and to manage versions of documents.  The software works quickly and flawlessly, even though I am running it on a relatively old machine.  The interface is also very intuitive--I haven't really had to refer to any of the documentation.  Highly recommended.

I am just past the installation and printing each application's documentation phase (there are 5 equivalent to MSXcel, MSWord, AutoCad, MSPowerpoint, MSAccess, and a sixth, Formula Editor Guide) and have converted one Excel document to the LibreOffice's spreadsheet application.
The applications' documentation is available in PDF-wow! It's a lot to print out, but what a resource--rare these days unless you find it in print from an alternate resource. A lot to sink your teeth into (and a learning curve adjusting to the differences in the tabs and icons, but not a problem for those familiar with Microsoft's Office programs). The documentation seems very thorough and the install presented no problems.
I had a couple of questions before I got started and received a reply within 24 hours--no more Microsoft frustration. There is no user limit and you can share the program. And no cost to upgrade!
I have used this daily for several years. Works great for tracking calories and activities. If you're concerned with fitness, this is a must.
I was looking for an app comparable to the myUSPS app available for iOS devices. Not quite the same, but minus the auto tracking nature of the iOS version, it works.

Tax regulations seem to change every year. TurboTax keeps up with the changes better than I can. One thing TurboTax does that its competitors do not do as well is to download 1099 data from financial institutions.

Sometimes I think that tax programs such as TurboTax have become too complex by using the question and answer format. But when I go back to the basic IRS forms I quickly find myself willing to proceed with the TurboTax questionaires without knowing the underlying details of the tax code. I fill in the computer forms and transmit them to the IRS electronically.

I can attest to the ease of using TurboTax. I am at the mercy of their beta-testers and the IRS to validate the accuracy.

I intend to use TurboTax again for my 2010 return!

I'll get my one real complaint out of the way first. I usually use HR Block's Online option and it's worked well for several years. Using the desktop software this time I thought would be seamless for for some strange reason it isn't. The only want to import is to first go online and export a PDF and import that to the software. Being able to log in and be done just seems... obvious. There were a few details about or kids that didn't import either but that wasn't too bad. In any case from there on out it is pretty much the exact same experience as online. They divide the process up into different categories like base pay, credits, deductions and so on and ask in each if you have any of a number of tax situations. If you check yes you get the relevant questions that reference the correct boxes on the forms. They even helpfully ask questions about common mistakes. There was one time where the answers one two different questions had it ask me if I was sure about that and I double checked: sure enough I had missed something. I was amused at one point that they mentioned bundling tax deductible things from multiple years into one which is something I hadn't even thought of.

I was only confused at one point where it was asking me to review some possible mistakes at the end and every time I reviewed them it would take me back to the same screen. When I stopped to look it did tell me I could just close the window if everything was OK but it just wasn't intuitive like the rest of the experience. Also remember when getting to the point where you enter your checking account info it won't tell you but when you submit it gives you a whole 'nother screen to enter it again to confirm. If you've already gone to put your check book away before clicking you're going to be making another trip.

As much as I would like a simpler tax code that didn't require software like this for the time being it doesn't get much easier than this. Filed on Thursday, refund approved on Sunday. Should be deposited by Wednesday. Can't argue with results.

I use Lightroom all the time but I refuse to pay for a monthly/yearly subscription for software so I haven't upgraded in years and that means Photoshop is out of the question too. Fortunately Cyberlink has come along and crammed a good portion of the functionality I want together into one tool. It could use some polish but it gives me the tools I need to quickly process my photos while having that little extra oomph for more complicated changes.

Complaints first: I wouldn't call this beginner friendly. Not so much a problem for me but the interface feels a little busy and clunky. If you play with it a while yo start to see the flow they want you to follow and where everything is placed. There are also some bugs that that crashed it altogether; nothing that makes it unusable but it has crashed on me a couple times from actions that I could almost tell where I confused it. Cyberlink needs to improve their testing a little bit. Lastly the nag screen to buy add-ons every time you start the app needs to go. Fast.

When starting to process a large batch import is easy and straightforward allowing you to both import a folder or individual images and in each case gives you an in-app window for immediately applying presets and weeding out a few imports. You also get a tethered mode and the ability to import form a video which are neat options. From here on out you move from standard adjustments->their fancy adjustment tools->full on photoshop-esqe editing. There is also a slideshow mode tucked in there and hey, I'm not complaining but it feels a little out of place in a workflow. I've been able to get everything I need done for adjusting exposure and picture angle type things.

What I really like about this software is the "other" tools it includes. The first thing that got my attention is it's 360 photo editing. I shoot a lot on an Insta360 One X and while their software is nice you can really fine tune things in here. Being able to fix horizons and alignment is wonderful. I get a little carried away with HDR and panoramas and being able to merge these in a single workflow tool alone makes me sold on PhotoDirector. Panos stitch effortlessly; even out of order collections. HDR sometimes takes some decision making but I've enjoyed the results and included a couple on my review.

Their different smart selection/clone/remove tools are a a nice addition but results are a little more mixed. You need to experiment with each and sometimes the results are very anti-smart. The magnetic lasso as well as the smart brush selection work a little better and help when you're going heavier edits.

I won't try to say this software is a polished product; it is a little rough around the edges and if you already use Adobe products there is no reason to switch. What I think PhotoDirector is right now is an affordable yet powerful alternative to Adobes offerings. If a friend asked me what I'd recommend right now for everyday processing I would not fail to mention this.

There's a lot going on in this software; enough that you've got a lot of reading if you want to learn it all. In most cases I use basic functions for things like photo slide shows or throwing different views of a wedding together. I have found several things that are just plain fun to use though. The chromakey effect allows for blue/green/any-color-you-want screen which any good software should have. I like motion the tracking feature too for applying effects to a specific item in a shot and have it follow the item. Effects, filters, and sample media are plenty and the software puts the ability to even to some video capture front and center. I'm not sure I agree entirely with its placement when I feel like the software should open right to the storyboard. Good news though, you are very free to do form the software to your needs and setting it to open right to the storyboard is a cinch. It's received several updates over the months I've had it which says to me the developers spent the needed time to fix issues and even added the ability to work with 360 videos. In the end you also get lots of options for burning images or discs and I'm impressed that they even include blu-ray as an option.

This review is for the free version. Very nicely done implementation of backgammon, with fast and elegant interface, nice user controls, and good documentation. I agree with some of the other reviews that the computer opponent seems to follow a flawed strategy with its end game in that it doesn't try to get its pieces off the board as soon as possible. Also it seems slow to double when the game is winning and quick to accept doubles when I'm winning. All in all, it's just challenging enough for me to make it interesting at level 5.

As for the random dice generator, I don't agree that the computer cheats, but it doesn't seem to have a true uniform distribution (where each of the six sides of each die is equally likely). In one of my first dozen games it gave me the same roll three turns in a row, and it does seem to roll an awful lot of doubles.

This is one incredible program! The best way to describe it is that it's an entire art studio (plus a photo editor and web publisher) on a disc. From chalk and crayon to paint and even water color (!) this program handles it all. I've been amazed with the high quality images I've been able to create. The dregee of subtlty I'm able to achieve when blending and smearing is incredible. I've already posted some things on the web and have just foud out that the program also lets you create basic animation! These animations can be created in the frame rate of your choosing and be exported as Quicktime,AVI (windows only) or GIFs for posting on the web. That's taking things to the next level!
Professional and serious artists will get the most out of this software since it follows many of the same rules that apply to using actual paints and papers (watercolors run and require 'drying' time). But other users should be able to pick up the basic techniques and be able to create some terrific art. The interface with OS X is easy on the eyes and very responsive. However, you will want to use a tablet and stylus instead of the mouse to maximize your results. If your looking for the best painting program available (on the best OS around), well your search has ended. The possibilities are as limitless as your imagination.

I bought a copy of 9.0 locally because I've always used McAfee and online updating is a bad joke with my connection. The first version, whatever it was, came preinstalled on my Gateway and came with no print documentation. Next up I bought a copy of 6.0 and got the print stuff, no real problems if a guy would just read directions I guess. I updated that one online but made mistakes (their system wasn't clear enough for me and the download took too much time)and decided to go and buy software this time based on the reputation I'd heard McAfee had.

I was pleased to read, on the box, that just about any PC out there could run this thing but I was dismayed to find next to no printed material in the box. The box itself almost has more info than the sorry little scrap of paper inside. Quick Start Guide they all it and that's about all you get, a quick run through on preparing your PC and installation of the product. I'm not very computer literate and this thing seems to be written for Windows XP (I'm using '98). Any real operations information seems to be contained on the CD-ROM (so you'd better not throw that away), I'm not entirely sure I can figure out how to open it up- I want it in print. Another thing I don't like is that the software seems to be autogeared to search and update on it's own- not one word in print anywhere about that.

Next time I may just see about using somebody else's software.

...I purchased this in mid-March. Let's face it, buying tax software early invites a frustrating experience. There are last minute tax changes and bugs that will need ironed out. If you HAVE to have your taxes submitted in January, go to H&R Block.
Otherwise, TurboTax continues to be a rock-solid choice.
I have a complex return, with capital gains, mulitple income sources including K-1s, lots of optional schedules to fill out and a lot of deductions. TurboTax handled it all without a single glitch, as usual.
The "reminder" feature that lets you know when you have an income source or deduction from a previous year that you didn't take this year is a real life-saver. Not only can you catch a deduction that otherwise would have been forgotten (where DID you put that Salvation Army receipt?) but forgetting that stock dividend can trigger an audit.
The E-File is a great feature and worked like a charm, as did the state download and submission.
TurboTax has made tax time a minor burden where it used to be a multiple day consuming nightmare.
...it is worth every penny, and it is deductible if you itemize!
Also, if you are the kind of person who waits until the last minute, TurboTax is the ONLY way to go. Within a couple of hours your tax return will be ready to submit!

I had been using Weather Channel's app for awhile, but after its last update, I was not happy as it went from user-friendly to being very difficult to operate.  Enter WeatherBug...a complete 180!  This app is very easy to set up and use.  You can tell it to turn off "location-based" tracking if you are not comfortable with it being on (this will not turn off the "location-based" function on your kindle fire hd though).  You can program different locations and it will let you scroll through them when you want to see weather at one of your other locations...this comes in really handy if you are planning to travel.  It is very in-depth in its information, too, once you figure out how to access this information, like humidity, heat index, pressure, sunrise/sunset times, etc.  Very worth having...you'll be happy with this weather app.  :)

Listen, I am NOT a fan of Corel, check my reviews. I gave 1 star to [[ASIN:B005HTIWF4 WinDVD Pro 11]], and I gave 1 star to [[ASIN:B002J9V9BO PaintShop Photo Pro X3]], and I gave 2 stars to [[ASIN:B001VGZLQ0 Digital Studio 2010]]. You can say with confidence that I am NOT A FAN.

So, if anything, my bias runs AGAINST Corel, not for them. So, imagine my surprise when I installed Pinnacle Studio 17, and it wasn't a complete train-wreck. I mean, don't get me wrong, I wouldn't choose Pinnacle Studio as my go-to video editing program, but it would certainly do for me in a pinch.

I am a LONG TIME user of Adobe's Premiere Elements application for video editing, and I used that one suite for a long time (dating all the back to the 3.0 release), until Adobe ruined it. I am now using CyberLink's PowerDirector as my main video editing application, and I am pretty happy with it.

I use video editing software for very specific purposes:
1.) to cut together video for reviews
and
2.) to edit together nice DVDs/Blu-Rays of family home videos to give out to friends and family, that include chapter breaks, and title screens, etc.
Those are the ONLY two purposes that I use video editing software for. So, those are the only two sets of conditions I am going to review. A professional using the application might have more to say about various features, but those are outside of the scope of my usage or knowledge.

[Note, I tested this application on a Windows 7 64-bit computer with a 8-Core i7 Intel processor running at 2.30GHz, with 16GB of RAM.]

So, with my background established, it should be clear that video editing software is old-hat for me. One application looks and functions pretty much as well as any other application for me. I can pretty quickly and easily figure out how to preview the video, cut and edit the video, and export the video. They are all laid out basically the same. So, I had no problem getting around in Pinnacle Studio 17. However, if Pinnacle Studio 17 was my VERY FIRST video editing application, I think I would have been in trouble. It only comes with a little folded pamphlet to get you started, and the instructions are pretty anemic. In contrast, all of the editions of Adobe's Premiere Elements came with full-fledged books with manuals in them, to get you started, for all of the editions I bought, before I gave up on Premiere Elements. And CyberLink's PowerDirector has tutorials on their website, and FULL documentation included WITH their application. Pinnacle Studio has documentation as well, but it is only on their website, and that sucks.

Additionally, CyberLink's PowerDirector is a native 64-bit application, and it takes full advantage of my hardware. In contrast, Corel's Pinnacle Studio 17 is a 32-bit application, and it doesn't take full advantage of my hardware.

First things first, though, TURN OFF the "Watchfolders" (go to the Setup Menu > Control Panel > Watchfolders and click the "Remove Folder" button for all of the entries in there, then apply the changes) -- Corel is continuing their INSANE tradition of all of their other media applications of trying to be your media manager as well, by constantly monitoring your media locations in "my documents" and your "documents library" for videos, music, photos, etc. The end result of that, is that the stupid thing will CHUG when you first launch it, as it tries to index your ENTIRE HARD-DRIVE'S worth of media in the locations it is automatically monitoring. With that nonsense turned off, the monitoring agent will still be running, and eating up some of your RAM, but at least it won't be causing your system to freeze.

Another annoyance of Pinnacle Studio 17, is that it installs a DRM watchdog application, NeroCheck, on your system without asking you (unless you actually read their 50 page user agreement in their term of use at install), that will run constantly on your system. (I find it hilarious that clueless companies like Corel are still trying to use DRM as a means to control copying their software, these people have no concept of reality. Newsflash guys, it doesn't work, has never worked, and all you are doing is aggravating your paying users, while the pirates continue pirating. Always-Online is the only way to slow/stop the copying, but that has its own issues we won't get into.)

But, all of that being said, the key question is, can I get it to do what I need it to do? And the answer is, yes! Once the "Watchfolders" are turned off, the application is surprisingly useable, and pretty darn responsive. You can import your videos into a project (without having to actually copy the files), and you can preview them VERY smoothly. Editing them was no problem for me, and exporting them was a breeze. I did a trial edit, compile, and export to AVI, MOV and FLV format, at various settings, bit-rates, sizes, and custom settings, and the exported videos all came out very nicely. I then did a trial edit, compile and export to DVD with chapter breaks and a title screen, etc. and it came out nicely as well.

Also, I will say, and tip my hat to the Corel developers, that of all 3 video editing products I have ever owned or used, the compile and render process for Pinnacle Studio 17 was INCREDIBLY fast! Even faster than CyberLink's PowerDirector, which is saying something.

So, this application has the usual baggage from Corel, dragging it down, and I WOULD NOT classify it as a good entry-level editor for someone new to these kinds of applications. But, it did the job well in all of my tests once I turned off its most annoying "features" and I have seen and used far worse.

3.5 stars (rounded down to 3 stars, since Amazon doesn't do partial stars. It is better than 3 stars, but it isn't good enough to rate 4 stars, so I have to officially rate it 3 stars, but my 3.5 star rating stands unofficially.)

I am not going to go into great detail about this product. It is garbage. I got it a while ago, and tested it, and due to that test, I actually had to re-install my operating system just to get it off my hard drive (I took the opportunity to upgrade some things while doing so, so it wasn't a total waste of time).

In short, the software is garbage. If you want computer security software, download MalwareBytes, or AVG, or PC Tools Threatfire. All of those are free, and work just fine.

This piece of garbage, which you have to pay for, not only doesn't work, but it crashed twice while I was installing it, and the installation takes FOREVER! (well over 20 minutes -- which is worse than MS Office) It refuses to install unless you uninstall all competing products. The forced restart after it uninstalls the competing products hung, and I eventually had to hard-power-off the system. Then the install hung at 27% on the task named, "copying files and preparing framework" -- which eventually crashed the computer again, and I had to do another hard restart. And, it REFUSED to uninstall. It kept claiming I needed to be an administrator (which I was), and then quitting-- see uploaded screen shots. There is only one account on the machine it was tested on (a 3.2GHz, dual core processor, with 2GB of RAM, running Windows XP SP3) and that is the administrator account.

I have uploaded screen shots of all of its bad behavior and questionable license agreement (check out clauses 19 & 20, which give them a right to come and audit your use of their software -- creepy), and also a picture of my screen after the system blue-screen crashed and died when I was trying to install it.

Needless to say, I didn't get around to much testing in the way of detecting viruses, etc. Most virus scanning software is generally useless for zero-day threats, and for everything else, pretty much ALL of the virus scanning products work to catch. So, one is as good as the other.

1 star. I would give it zero stars, but I am not allowed.

[Curriculum Vitae: Bachelor of Science, Computer Science with a Minor in Security and Forensics. 12 years professional field work in computer programming, and dealing with all aspects of computer security]

This PDF product more or less works, but that is about the best I can say about it.

I have the full professional version of Adobe Acrobat 9, and it has worked perfectly in concert with MS Word as well as a stand-alone product. It does all of the things that this product by Nuance does, but the exception is, Adobe's products have always worked for me. Luckily, I got most of my Adobe products through my college with a student discount, so I have yet to have to pay retail for them -- but they ARE worth the money.

PDF Converter Pro 6.0 is NOT worth the money. First of all, it crashed upon attempting to install it. Normally, I choose the "custom" option when I install something. But this time I was in a hurry, so I left everything in the default modes. After it crashed I tried the custom option, and chose to not let it "integrate" itself into my existing setup. That seems to have done the trick and it installed.

Frankly, the menus are confusing, the buttons are jumbled, and the PDF import and edit function doesn't generally work. When it does work, it only works on totally unsecured PDFs, and it tends to not like PDFs created with the most current version of Adobe Acrobat. It does tend to convert Word documents into PDFs without much trouble though.

If you are concerned about price, Open Office's suite of office software is equal in dependency to the Microsoft Office suite, and free. And all of the Open Office software has the capability to export to PDF, and it does it for free. You can even password protect it, and limit what others can do with it, just like Adobe's full version. What is CAN'T do, is what Adobe and Nuance's software can do, which is OPEN and edit PDF documents. But, if you are solely concerned with authoring PDFs, and price is your main concern, go with Open Office. It can even open Microsoft Office files (not 100% accurately, but good enough for most cases). If price isn't your main concern, then you might as well go with the full version of Adobe's Acrobat. Don't settle for the middle ground, you get what you pay for. And in this case, if you pay for Nuance's product, what you get is buggy software.

This could be a truly phenomenal app. COULD be. It's got a ton of little quirks to figure out though. One thing it absolutely needs is the ability to edit the scorecard after the game is over. There isn't always time during the game to go back and make corrections and still score the next play properly. For this reason, I'd recommend still using a pencil and scorebook to score the game and then just transfer all of the scorecard marks into the app later. This does involve some extra time, but it makes it less frustrating while watching the game.

The other thing that REALLY needs to be improved is the syncing across multiple devices. It is a very archaic import/export system to sync devices and you never really are 100% sure you are updating the right data. It'd be REALLY nice to have actual files with time/date stamps on them so you can know for sure what you're uploading/importing. It'd be really nice that instead of having to register a million different device ID's to an account that you could just use your email address to sign in and it handles things autonomously. It really should sync up like the Kindle is able to across multiple computers, phones, tablets, readers.

I do really like the ability to upload a complete team roster using a csv file. That makes adding players to the system pretty easy. The ability to purchase MLB rosters is pretty neat too if you want to use it to score MLB games you're watching. I hate having to buy the rosters, but that is MLB and not iScore. Once you get the hang of what you're doing it's a pretty intuitive app to use and scoring intricate plays is fairly easy after some practice. It's pretty overwhelming the first few times you're trying to do it but just get over that and you'll be buzzing in no time. I use it mainly for scoring my kids little league games and sharing the stats with the manager and the other coaches. We all love looking at the stats because they lead to some surprising findings and help us shuffle the lineups around to be even more successful. It's also just fun to see stats (that may not be complete or are just skewed because the sample size isn't there for a proper stat) that you'd never think of looking at. You really can grab pretty much any stat you want from this app if you set it up properly. The options are there to get as advanced as you'd like or keep it as simple as you like.

Being part of the Amazon Vine program means that I get to try out a lot of Anti-virus and security products. I probably try a new one every few months. I even started keeping a log so I know what is on what system. Over the years I've found most of them do a pretty good job, and there are reasons to like most of them. However I've started to notice the only brand I tend to keep is McAfee: after a while I uninstall the Norton, Trend Micro, and Zone Alarm (or whatever) and go back to McAfee. In some way the others end up failing me: a virus gets through and I can't get good support, they crash another program, or I get just tired of dealing with their interface.

After kicking the tires on this one for a while I think it's also here to stay. First off McAfee consistently rates at or near the top of the Consumer Reports and PC Magazine tests. You'll also find McAfee is pretty much the main choice of most major companies for Enterprise Class computing. But while it's great that "experts" love it, if a home user can configure it correctly, what good is it? And to me that is where McAfee really shines. You can access a lot of features if you like to tweak them, but they make the basics pretty simple. If you just want it to work, you can make a few simple choices at setup and you're done. If you like to customize things at a very low level, you can do that too.

To be clear, until recently, I didn't always think they had the best interface, but it was a good one that handled a wide range of users. But with the 2011 version, I think they've finally stepped up to the top of the pack in terms of interface. They redesigned the home screen, something that usually bugs me when I'm used to the old GUI. In this case they made it far easier for novice users. I've already purchased this for several tech impaired family members and already I'm getting fewer calls for help.

I've also had good luck with McAfee support. Or more particularly, after I found I couldn't trouble shoot a malware problem on my Dad's computer, I had him call McAfee, which remotely trouble shot the problem on his system and removed the malware for him. Most companies require me to play email tag, but with McAfee my dad was able to make one phone call and get his computer fixed while he read the newspaper.

And for me that really seals the deal. Just about anything you will buy will do the job, but eventually something is going to slip through. Once that happens I want the company with the resources to step up and fix the problem.

Microsoft want us all to buy a monthly subscription but it knows we don't want that so they offer all sorts of alternatives--including those for families--that end up becoming a nightmare. Oh sure, the first time you clean install something it works, but after that it becomes a tarpit of redirection, complexity and obtuse procedures. At this point I'm going to use google docs. I have to import (and export at times) to process documents, but google docs does a pretty good job.
Used for years and haven't gotten audited yet, so I guess it works. I always wonder if the Deluxe Version is really all that I need.

I make about 10 videos a year for informal corporate use, usually as a way to effectively communicate the outcome and learned lessons of technology prototypes.

In addition, I attended broadcasting school in my youth and spent a short time as a corporate cable TV reporter.

In order to deliver a quality video, basic editing skills are the most important, which any NLE can do - make sure you have plenty of coverage, keep your cuts very short, just a few seconds or so, and tell the story with the video. Know how and when to cut audio over video, and keep your voiceover short, concise, and easy to understand.

Although most packages come with dozens of transitions and title types, you really only need about 4 of them - most of the others look unprofessional and are distracting.

Less is more.

Given that, PowerDirector is a decent, Pinnacle-like offering at a reasonable price for users who require its advanced features over an iMovie or Movie Maker for Windows.

I should say, however, that I prefer the built-in video editing packages with Mac and Windows, iMovie and Movie Maker. They do the basics very well, are free, and you never have to worry about support or licensing issues with multiple machines, or punching in 2-digit license codes, or going to work and having to produce a video with Movie Maker, while you are used to using a different package at home.

Pros: Installed on Windows 10 for me just fine. I did order the Disc version as it was cheaper than the download.
Cons: Tells me I need Quicken 2016 or later to import my data and I have Quicken 2017. TurboTax FAQ says this was fixed in a 12/8/2017 update and on 12/14/2017 it still did not work for me (I'm current on all my updates it says). Home Energy Credits and Education Expenses "Will Be Ready Soon" and it says the IRS has not done their part yet.
I'm cautiously optimistic that I'll be able to do my taxes without too much pain this year. My college kids don't use turbotax (or quicken) for their taxes (or financial tracking) as I've encouraged them to find other tools/sites for each, which they've done. I still use turbotax (and quicken) but I no longer recommend them as I am no longer confident nor comfortable doing so.

As a user of Quicken since 1990 (give or take a year) I've had a love/hate affair with this product. The older it and I get the more it tends to the hate side of the equation. I use to write articles about how much I loved Quicken and how I didn't understand how anyone can do a good job of managing their finances without it. I still sometimes mention it, but now it is rare.

With that said, I have been on occasion pleasantly surprised by Quicken (now using 2014 ... by Intuit coercion). Recently I just figured out that the "Bills" display, when configured as I needed it, solved a tracking problem I had done manually for years. It reminded me that I need to periodically explore Quicken's capabilities to see if they've added something that would help me. In another example, I had an investment split into two companies, one of which was then taken private. With the help of the Quicken community I was able to reflect the event reasonably in Quicken.

The brilliant part of Quicken, for me, is the ability to download all my transactions directly into my accounts. This allows Quicken to be my daily dashboard to my finances. The second best part of quicken is the ability to take all the data I've accumulated and roll it into a decent long term financial projection. This is my long term outlook and answers the question "how am I doing for the long term?" My financial plan goes out to my 100th birthday.

Reports are still brain dead. It should be easy to just click on my electrical bill, for example, and have Quicken give me a trend chart (line or bar) showing my costs over the last 12 months (or a period I pick). This answers the question of how much I spend and is it going up or down. Being able to superimpose a moving average would be great also. Being able to quickly export the data so I could graph it would be a 2nd choice. I said quickly. Export is also brain dead.

[Update 2/4/2015] I just got around to budgeting for the new year, using Quicken. I applaud the desire to make budgeting automatic, but one size does not fit all and Quicken 2014 budgeting is braindead. Instead of an option to take last years painstakingly handcrafted budget and copying it to this year, it has some algorithm that appears to take some of last years and some of this years and then projects whatever is this year forward as if what I spent in January represents all of the rest of the year. I am now expected to individually edit 12 x 74 (all my categories) or 888 entries. I know of no financially savvy/educated person that budgets or recommends budgeting this way. I could fix this monstrosity a month at a time (as Quicken allows me to propagate a number to all 12 months of a category) if I could at least display last years budget/actuals while I work on this years, which I can't seem to do ([Update] I can do it as a separate report outside of the budgeting module). It worked fairly well in the past and they then implemented this atrocity. Why? I'd love to have seen the committee meeting that decided on this solution. Removed one star.

[Update 2/5/2015] To copy last year's averages to use as my starting budget: 77 categories x 10 keystrokes+number: over 1001 clicks/keystrokes to do what one button did in previous versions of Quicken. This does not include my numerous misclicks/mistypes while entering data and of course I may have typed something wrong which I'll hopefully catch later. "Quick"en is now "Slow+ErrorProne"en.

Also it is long overdue for Quicken to allow add-ins by 3rd parties to leverage all that data.

I really wish there was a competitor but so far there does not appear to be one. Until then I just grimace from year to year and hope it doesn't become a disaster because I rely upon Quicken daily.

As a professional software developer who spent many years maintaining a cross-platform drawing application, I can appreciate the huge amount of effort that results in such a smooth and feature filled software package. Back in the day, we didn't have the need for terms like design language, typography, or workflow. Thankfully, we no longer live back in the day, and we have nice, pleasant, easy to use apps like this.

I tend to think, this is optimized for simple, common workflow such as quick color correction, or removing common face imperfections. It can get lucky and do things like remove bags under the eyes nearly automatically. If you get lucky, but it's likely you'll have to get more manual.

My kids enjoy making grotesque enhancements of their parents faces. It is easy to give people creepy huge button eyes or other distortions.

Printing is more complicated than it should be.

Performance is quite good on Windows 10 on a 2012 i5 with 16GB RAM, so I can't say the minimum usable hardware, but it isn't extravagant.

Good job.

I've spent my professional life as a software developer writing cross-platform applications with an aim at making my Mac products look and feel like native Mac applications. This requires skill, discipline, and expense, and sometimes my employers didn't appreciate the added value, but my users have. And this application doesn't even try. Its GUI is generic lowest common denominator all the way, in the ugly, coarse, heavy handed skin style all too typical of cross-platform frameworks which don't have the time for subtle. Depressing, really.

And this is a shame on two levels. One is that Smith-Micro has put a lot of resources into this package, and crammed a lot of functionality in, and it is unfortunate that they didn't go the extra mile in writing an application I actually want to see on my screen. And two, is that once you know what you are doing, it isn't all that hard to put a thin layer of Apple's Cocoa framework between your users and your cross-platform core, and you get so much extra stuff for free, things like rich text editing in dialogs, document handling, window handling, rich GUI editing tools, that in the end they might well have spent more time delivering this mess than what they would have spent doing it right. And if they ever want a 64-bit version, they will likely be in for a world of pain.

Anyway, I'm giving this 3 stars because the product does work, albeit inelegantly, and they spent some time on putting together tutorials to get started. It seems like you will end up buying a lot of add-on bits to get the custom animation you actually want, so I can't even give it plaudits for cheapness.

This app works on my Kindle, but not my android. I love the Fitbit for tracking my steps, my sleep, and for the reminders to get me to move throughout the day. I love when my Fotbit celebrates when I hot 10,000 steps. I feel like dancing. Woo-Hoo!
The challenges are fun and fun to do with friends. I wish they would add some more solo and group challenges then just NYC and Yosemite.
Great App.

I've been using Turbotax for my own returns and my son's and his wife's since my regular tax man died about a dozen years ago.  Turbotax has not let me down yet.  Always get a nice refund (well my son has) and haven't had any audit issues.  I haven't used their tech support so I can't comment on that.
I've been using TurboTax for 10 years.  Haven't been audited yet.  Also use it for my son's taxes.  Same story.

I recently was installing this product and had an issue. I contacted Rosetta and they sent the following response:

Dear X,
Thank you for providing documentation of your purchase. Unfortunately, the person who sold the program to you is not an authorized reseller of Rosetta Stone. Since your product was resold in violation of the license agreement, we will not be able to provide you with a new Activation ID.

We sincerely appreciate you taking the time to report this unauthorized sale of Rosetta Stone. It is unfortunate when potential customers unknowingly purchase products and the license has been compromised.

We appreciate any information you can provide regarding the illegitimate resale of Rosetta Stone products. The information you offer is vital to our operations as we strive to protect our client base from unlicensed, outdated, and otherwise unserviceable products carrying our company name. We ask that you please take the time to file a claim with our Legal Department by navigating to: [...] and selecting the link: "Report Piracy/Internet Fraud".

If you have any further questions, please feel free to contact us.
