I've just installed Dragon Dictate today and begun to use it, but a couple of years ago I had to use the Windows-based Dragon Naturally Speaking suite for some time due to an injury that left me unable to type, so I do have previous experience and a basis for comparison.

Installation, compared to Windows, was a breeze. Training was, too--rather than the pages and pages of arcane and boring text that I had to read for the Windows version (and that never seemed to quite do the job, no matter how much further training I did), there were maybe 20 sentences of training for the Mac version before it suggested that I was good to go and after that very brief training period, accuracy today (producing about 20 pages of text) was 99.99%. I had about two "typos" in twenty pages--that's fabulous by any measure.

The Mac version is also able to dictate into just about any Mac application--including Scrivener, which is a major plus for me. I didn't notice any slowness dictating into Word or Scrivener as others have experienced, though I do have 8GB of memory (upgraded from the original 4GB on my 2010 MacBook Pro) so perhaps some are experiencing memory use issues.

More interestingly, I'm somewhat taken aback by the claims that the editing commands are different from the Naturally Speaking editing commands. Granted, it's been a couple of years since I used the Windows-based product and I was not a hardcore, long-term user, but I didn't need to reference any of the documentation for Dragon Dictate 2.0; the cursor movement, selection, and editing commands that I was able to dig up from memory all seemed to work fine. Based on others' criticisms, there must be some differences, but it may be that some of them are in lesser-used features (or, at least, features that I never used).

I'm giving Dragon Dictate 2.0 for Mac five stars because (a) the recognition accuracy is much higher than I remember in Windows, (b) the training required to get there was much shorter, (c) the price is lower, (d) the product is fast, fast, fast, and can keep up with my natural speech for pages at a time, and (e) I can dictate into the Mac applications that I use most.

That's good enough for me.

I have used Turbo Tax for about ten years. Never a problem with it. We do not have a state tax so I can not comment on that. Federal works great, does all the step by step calculations, checks them, tells you where you might stand in chances of being audited and many other featurs. Never any complaints about Turbo Tax from me!

We had originally wanted Rosetta Stone for our daughter who has the desire to take French classes in high school and be a chef in Paris some day. However, she has a learning disability and we felt like adding French would be a real struggle for her. She carries a heavy load at school on a regular basis with just the required classes. She was heart broken that her other friends would be moving forward with a foreign language with out her.

With this software package now in our house she has the ability to learn at her own pace with out the stress of being graded in class and peer pressure to do well. We figured while we had one child learning French we would add our son to the lessons. Together they work on a daily basis learning French.

It has turned into a game for them and I often find them laughing together while they learn.

For a child with a processing disorder, in both visual and auditory areas, the pictures, voice and practice sessions make this not feel like learning. They are both learning and challenged in areas that they don't excel in, yet love the success they get with every session.

This is also helping us, as a family prepare for the mission field where French is the official language for the countries we will work in.
Use it every year. Also use Its Deductible online throughout the year tracking donations which Turbo Tax then uploads. I find the program easy and efficient.
Poor connectivity to other devices, low grade answers to the questions that it does have an answer for. Immature implementation of the technology. Would not recommend others to purchase at this time.

How I loathe this program. If there is such a thing as rude software, this is it! If you are, as many older Americans are, a bond investor, Quicken has taken software which enabled bond tracking pretty well in the 2007 version and made it virtually useless. You much input each end of month bond price manually with many, many more clicks required than in the past. Worse, the software downloads and inserts into your bond price history, stock prices, having nothing to do with your bonds, i.e. incorrect data which you must catch and correct.

Furthermore, this software is designed active investors who buy and sell stock based on price. NO ONE SHOULD BE DOING THIS! Scientific research has proven that even professionals cannot beat the market. You may have a lucky patch, but overall, no one comes out ahead of the market. Just buy total market mutual funds and read the first few chapters of The Four Pillars of Investing by Bernstein to understand why. Buy and hold. Buy and hold!

And, if you hold bonds and have a choice, do not buy this software.

I love this!  I  am working on several manuscripts, so  I create a separate notebook for each of the manuscripts, and I use each tab as a notepad for each work.  In truth, this app can take the place of several apps; you can use one notebook as a notepad, another as a journal, and another as a recipe book, etc.  I can see where this app would be great for a student, where he or she could actually create books for each subject.  Press the  "?"  symbol and read the directions that teach you how to create a new notebook. You can export and import to other devices.  I love the fact that it doesn't try to force me to go to website and create another account and "sync"  as other products do.  I can't believe that it is FREE!

I had very high hopes for this software, as I wanted to take advantage of it for real estate purposes. Since I am also new to the product, I was also curious about the interface, in that I didn't have any expectations based on previous versions, unlike others that have posted here.

Suffice it to say that any hopes I had were destroyed almost instantly. This is one of the worst user interfaces I've seen in quite a while, certainly in a major product from a major publisher (and I'm a software development exec). Virtually everything is non-standard and few things work as expected: clicks and double clicks, right/left button functionality, drag & drop, highlighting & selecting, map navigation, context menus, zooming, export & import, etc. The iconography is just awful, awful - reminiscent of the software of a decade or more ago. I also found the functionality outside of routing and route planning (both of which are excellent) to be more restrictive than hoped, with particular disappointment in the territory functionality, territory definitions being too limited for my uses. Finally, the map graphics themselves just don't make the grade. While readable, they don't display any real quality until quite closely zoomed, looking quite sketchy to that point.

The high point of the software is the routing functionality, as mentioned above. It is powerful, flexible, and has many options I have not seen elsewhere, and which are far beyond anything available in Google Maps, Mapquest, or other web-based mapping software. If this is your primary interest or need, this software is for you, even with the issues mentioned above.

Still, had I to break it down, I'd give MapPoint 2009 5 stars for routing, 4 for Microsoft software integration (Outlook, Excel, Access, etc.), and 1 for everything else. Couple that with its rather steep price and I really can't give it more than 2 stars overall. Very disappointing.

I purchased my first copy of Autocad Lite for Windows 95. I've been using the free upgrade to AutoCad Lite 97 for about 13 years now. I wasn't too shocked by the user interface differences and I was very pleased to find that I could popululate the sides of my screen with old style classic icons. So while I may not know everything there is to know about the new product I can import files drawn in Autocad Lite 97, I can create files and drawings with my autocad lite 97 knowledge and I can save files as AutoCad R12/LT2 dxf files which are readable by MacroMedia Freehand (yes I'm an old guy). So I still have the same capability of producing high quality and accurate cad drawings and then export them to tools that are good at creating artwork and graphics. So I've been pretty happy with the transition. I had been thinking about purchasing the software for a while and I decided that with the rebate it might be a good time to take the leap. I went to the autodesk website to get one of their mail in rebate forms. You can't use the Amazon Part Number. Use the model number instead 057C1-A8A111-1001. It works fines. I've already sent in the rebate. I've been really happy with Windows 7 pro. I probably could have gotten the Autocad Lite 97 to work on Win7 but I wanted to have the 64 bit version instead. The software installed without any problems. I'm running it on a T3500 Dell Precision Workstation.

If you've never use Autocad Lite. You may not like the way that it constructs drawings. But once you get use to it you'll wonder how you lived without it. It really is a superior cad system. Unfortunately, it also has a superior price.

I've used Quicken for years, not sure who now owns Intuit but its fundamentally the same flawed database product, only priced as a more expensive subscription. I have been downloading bank and investment data from their respective financial institutions for years. I gave up on using Quicken to track my bank because after the initial download it never, ever matches the numbers the bank (Chase) posts on-line. Quicken cannot add and subtract--never rely on its numbers without checking-- and it is not worth the time it takes to track down the errors, your paper check register is likely to be more accurate. I still use Quicken for "tracking" investments but you need to see that as a limited resolution snapshot and not a penny-accurate, let alone dollar accurate, view of what will be on your broker's web site. As my subscription is about to expire I am looking for a different semi-automatic way to track investments, couldn't be any less accurate than Quicken, but will probably be forced back to Quicken from lack of choice and bad habits.

Long term Windows Quicken users are familiar with all of the chronic issues in those versions but Quicken has long been the only game in town. Over the last couple of years even I will admit there have been significant improvements to the stability and reliability of the Windows version, and I have spent too many hours with telephone tech support over the years.

Quicken for Mac had not been upgraded in years and was not very useful. As I use both Windows and OSX with Quicken running in Windows on OSX under Parallels, and poorly at that, I decided to give the 2017 Mac version a tax deductible trial.

Quicken for Mac 2017 is not as full featured as Windows versions and has its own interface challenges. But it is indeed usable for my personal banking and investment needs when I am traveling with my Apple notebook without access to a secure Windows machine. It gives me the basic info I need.

My wish would be to have the same interface and file formats on both OSX and Windows versions but that would make life too easy. All office type products and Photoshop have enabled this for years so there is no reason Quicken can not be made the same in Windows and OSX. It should be.

For OSX users looking for a basic financial tracking program I can recommend this version of Quicken. It has its own learning curve and is not as full featured as it should be for the cost. I find it better than the other OSX financial programs I have used, which admittedly is a low standard, but hopefully whoever owns Intuit now will see fit to enable more features comparable to what is in Windows versions.

This review is for the Kindle Fire/Android version of Serverauditor. I have the same app for iOS and I'd give that version four stars. This version, not so many. I've run into a number of bugs but the two worst are the soft keyboard not showing when it should and a lack of access to meta keys when you need them. That renders the app useless for it's intended purpose. Since I've used this on other platforms, I know what the software is capable of doing. That makes this version even more annoying that it would be otherwise.
This is such a well behaved implementation which has given me hours of enjoyment.

I think this is a very solid pair of software (for video + photo editing) - especially for the price.  I wanted to note a couple things that I read on other reviews.  One is a problem with installation.  Make sure you do the install as "administrator" on your machine.  My install for premiere would keep giving me an error - until I read on another review - to right click and choose install "as administrator" - PROBLEM SOLVED!  The other thing I read is that someone was very disappointed that this was an online service, instead of the actual software.  NOT TRUE!  Though adobe does have an online service for a whole menu of software bundles (and singles) that you can pay a monthly price for (usually around $20 - depends what you choose) - this is the actual, stand alone, install on your machine DVD.  And that's another point - I originally ordered this, then realized that it was in a DVD format - and quickly cancelled it.  Then I found another version that is an electronic download, which is what I got.  I've already made a couple videos in Premiere and it's been very stable - no crashing.  I do have a machine with 32 GB RAM (this is very important to have enough RAM for video editing!) plus a dual core 3.20 GHz processor.  Even though the video editor is considered a very entry-level video editor (not a professional version) - I feel like it is plenty powerful to do many effects - including making key frames in your timeline and applying all kinds of motion paths, fades, etc. etc. etc.  The final thing I like about Premiere elements is it gives you a whole lot of export options for your video AND you can make custom adjustments to your video export (size of the video, bitrate, all kinds of other video and audio settings).

After downloading numerous apps I finally found one that is useful. I really find it useful that you can reconcile with the bank statement. This feature is only on a very few apps that I have seen and I believe it is very important. Overall I find this a wonderful  app for tracking your money.

I decided to try this software since it was free. I did not expect much since I have also used the very expensive version from MS. This works basically like the other software, and in fact time lines made in the other version can be imported and likewise time lines made using this software can be exported and they work like a charm.

I think there is quite a bit of confusion regarding Corel's bitmap software, since many people are comparing PaintShop Pro to Photoshop. Corel's professional graphics editor and Photoshop competitor is actually Corel Photo-Paint, which is part of the CorelDRAW Graphics Suite.

Corel Paint Shop Pro has an emphasis on digital photo-editing and is thus quite Lightroom like with RAW support but with the added ability to use brushes to directly edit photos, similar to Photoshop Elements. There's a saying that there's no bad products...just bad prices, and I think that is the rule of the day when considering Corel's PaintShop Pro x6. PaintShop Pro isn't Lightroom or Photoshop Elements...but it offers similar functionality in many areas and a highly discounted price. It's worth noting that Corel makes it pretty easy for users to learn their product by including several tutorials and guides which are actually genuinely useful since they explain the design-language of the software eg. workflow and how to do specific tasks.

Functionally speaking, I believe that with Corel, the enlightened user can create just about anything their Adobe-using counterpart can, though they will trade blows when it comes to ease of implementation and polish. Corel is marketed as an inexpensive alternative to Adobe, and it isn't quite aimed at demanding professionals. this is not to say you cannot get professional quality output...you certainly can. Overall, I think Corel PaintShop Pro offers a very impressive value.

Notes:
1) I own CorelDRAW graphics suite X5 and Lightroom 5, and the combination of these is more than sufficient for my present needs. I was basically curious about Paint Shop Pro which is why I chose to review it.

Too bad Amazon won't let me rate this product at less than 1 star, because it doesn't even deserve that.

Like many other Mac OS Lion users who have been abandoned by Quicken/Intuit, I've been looking for another finance app. I was using demo versions of iBank, See Finance, and MoneyDance side by side for comparisons. The jury is still out on the other two, but MoneyDance is now in my Trash Can.

Import of previous data from Quicken was a disaster. I was in the habit of saving new Quicken Data files every year, so I have multiple data files. Even just working with my current data from 2011, the result was utter chaos. MoneyDance imported transactions without carrying over the opening balances, so all my accounts were screwed up and I had to correct the opening balances manually. Then I discovered that vast numbers of transactions were being duplicated because they were transfers from one account to another (for example, paying my mortgage involved a transfer of funds from "Checking" to "Mortgage".) My account balances were hopelessly screwed up.

I deleted all my data and started over, this time I exported each account from Quicken individually. So I had separate QIF files for checking, savings, mortgage, etc....

Still had to correct all the opening balances by hand. And since "checking" was the first account I started with, each subsequent account that I imported screwed up my transactions & balances even more. So I had to delete "Checking" AGAIN, create it again, and then re-import all my transactions and correct the opening balance again.

It was then that I realized that NONE of my old Quicken categories had been carried over into MoneyDance. I now have thousands of transactions in multiple accounts with no categories for any of them. I am not about to go back and manually enter all that info. So therefore the Reporting features are worthless to me.

And oh, by the way, all the old accounts that I deleted mysteriously kept reappearing. I deleted them again, and they reappeared again. With the result that I had multiple accounts named "checking", etc. And I accidentally imported a year's worth of transactions into the wrong one, so had to start all over again.

This is just ridiculous. Doesn't anyone test this software before releasing it to the public? SeeFinance imported all my data from Quicken correctly the FIRST time, with all associated categories and opening balances. iBank didn't... I had to enter the opening balances manually again, but at least it handled my categories correctly and old deleted accounts stayed gone.

My advice to all Mac users looking to transfer data from Quicken: stay the heck away from MoneyDance.

This is good for tracking your weight loss, goals, and BMI. My only problem was I couldn't find a way to make changes to figures that I had earlier entered incorrectly. So I had to delete the whole thing and then go back and download it again, and reenter my correct information. Otherwise, it is easy and straightforward.

Good concept, but full of the same problems I've seen with every app from this developer - poor tracking caused me to lose my tracing at random points through the pattern, and the game froze up at the end of the level, making the continue button totally unresponsive.  Maybe it's my phone, but I see these problems all the time from these guys, meaning their apps are pretty much useless to me.  Hope you have better luck, because it at least seems like it would have been an interesting game.

Oddly enough, I just started the 100 pushups program after seeing this app in the store, but I wasn't willing to pay for it since you can get information on the program and documents to chart your progress for free on the Internet. But since the app was free today I thought I'd give it a shot. I does have the basic structure of the program mapped out, with a handy automatic timer built in for your rest periods, and a graph to chart your performance. I could do this on paper, certainly, but it will be more convenient to have it on the phone - particularly at the end of the workout when my arms are rubber and it's a chore just getting off the floor, much less writing a bunch of stuff down.

The obvious drawback here is that there's no explanation of what you're doing. There's no summary of the program, and there's no indication that the last set of each workout is meant to be done to exhaustion - the number listed for the last set is the minimum number you're supposed to do. I don't know yet if the app takes into account the performance tests you're supposed to take at the end of weeks 2, 4 and 5, but I would assume that it does; if it doesn't then there's really no point. Even so, while the app seems handy for tracking your progress, you're still going to have to spend a little time online to figure out what you're supposed to do.

[[ASIN:B002RS8F64 TurboTax Premier Federal + State + Federal efile 2009]]

I have used TurboTax for years and find it just as good, for me, as using an accountant. It guides you through step by step and gives explanations if you need them. I particularly like the feature that transfers your finanicial data from the finanicial institutions, and the feature that figures how much of your pension and/or social security is taxable. It also assists in deciding if taking the standard deduction or itemizing is best for your situation.

It finds all the deductions you are entitled to and informs of your audit risk. This year both my Federal and State forms were rejected and Turbo Tax guided me through to fix them and resubmit free.

For years I was afraid to do my own taxes for fear of screwing up & getting audited! (Nobody wants that!) But many people said it was easy because of step-by-step instructions, so I decided to give it a go. This is the first time in about 10 years that I'm getting money back & I'm not making any more or less money, nor do I have more deductions. Bottom line, I've been paying some jerkoff big $$ only to send the IRS a thousand dollars or more every year! So glad I tried this.

I'm a video hobbyist, and I think Sony Vegas Movie Studio HD Platinum 11 is a great solution for my level of need.

I do not know every feature and setting of the Vegas 11 yet, but after a couple of months, I'm ready to review.

First, the Bad:
- Vegas crashes, not too often but too much. (All video programs do, in my experience.)
- Title making is not hard, but the ready-made options are very weak compared to iMovie.
- Most transitions and effects are in the "I'd never use this" variety.
- There's a lot of setup/export options available with little guidance, and I think it is confusing for someone who just wants to upload a good quality video to YouTube. (Since web uploads is what most people will do, why not include a specific suggestion?)
- Multiple layered effects had some unpredictable results.
- Steadying the video is a crap shoot -- iMovie gives a sliding scale of the applied effect, whereas Vegas is to cropped and take-it-or-leave-it, it seems.
- Choppy preview when effects are added.
- Masks and addative video are a bit confusing but seem to work.

The Good:
- Vegas imports AVHCD .MTS files from my Canon camcorder with no pre-translation required. (THANK YOU!)
- No pre-rendering of files before editing.
- Multiple video & audio tracks.
- Easy dragging and fade/crossfade.
- Good audio effects and adjustments.
- Good set of starter video effects.
- Video/audio effects were easy to apply
- "Green-screen/Blue-screen" chroma-key works with a lot of slider tests.
- Text/Titles certainly work good enough to add a "bug" in the bottom right and overlay a title card.
- Good potential to combine multiple videos, motion graphics, and text/titles for expanded effects/styles.
- Very easy to synch audio to video or vice-versa.
- Includes some good soundtrack music, plus a modest selection of sound effects, too.
- Can control speed of playback.

The end video output seems like very good quality. (After uploading and exporting a .MOV file to YouTube, it appeared darker and lower in quality, though.) A LOT of time and experimentation will be necessary. Editing can do a lot of things, but you still need good quality images and sound to begin with.

The audio editing program included did not seem to use multiple tracks, so I was not really impressed with it -- too basic. I did not try the DVD-making software yet. The iZotrope voice remover is just a gimmicky promise, it partially works, but of course it takes out some of the music or leaves too much of the voice. The tutorial videos are very basic.

I may have purchased Movie

I've used TurboTax for so many years that I can't even remember when the last time I didn't!  I used to use H&R Block!  What a joke!  They messed my taxes up so bad that every year I used them, I got audited due to H & R Blocks mistakes!  TurboTax is a great product with outstanding support!

I have this on my Galaxy Note 4 and it is great. I especially like it because it gives me the ability to use my GPS tracking app when doing my walk or Bike ride and still listen to music without haveing to carry a second device.

Have no fear of doing your taxes correctly or how to fill out the right form.  Intuit makes it so USER-friendly.  You have the option to explore the tax forms yourself, but if you use the guide, be prepared, it will walk you through EVERYTHING.  You can't fail.  It asks you questions and then at the end it goes through everything to final check it.  Then it gives a rating to assess the level of risk that you have of being selected for an audit.  If you are at risk, it is going to tell you.  Being that I had all of my previous tax returns from the last 5 years to check it against, I am confident that their final review and assessment is accurate.  I didn't need to use this program because I know how to do my own taxes, but I tested this because I wanted to pass it on to my father and mother who always pay for their tax returns to be prepared by those centers that charge about $200-$250!!  In addition, TurboTax provides you a pdf copy of your return for your file.  When it was ready to submit, it tells you to click "submit" and it is uploaded into the IRS website directly and you receive immediate status tracking via email.

I bought the deluxe because of dividends from stocks and the state comes with it.  I don't have difficult taxes.  Make sure to read the description of the different types of Turbotax  available before buying.  The program walks you through step by step, checks for mistakes and allows efiling.  I haven't been audited (knock on wood) so I recommend this.  Very easy and actually kind of fun.  There is also a support site that can help with questions.  It also downloads w-2's, dividends and other tax forms.  I recommend updating your version every time you use it to keep up with any changes.

I've been using Turbo Tax for a decade, so I'm obviously satisfied with the quality of the product. It works great and I've never found a error or missed opportunity to lower my tax bill. Occasionally it will add a form used in past years, even though you answer that it isn't needed - this shows up on its final audit process as an incomplete form and requires deleting or zero entries in the blanks... Other than that, I feel the premier version should include one free state e-file.

So I'm reviewing this without really being an expert in the field. Let me note that I received this in exchange for an impartial review.

Ok, so if you're into editing there are so many choices. Let's say you want to take a bunch of pictures and make some videos for the holidays. Let's say you want to make a "Best Of" video with your child's performances on the piano or best hits at Football or LAX.

Today, products are varied as many phones, the 360 cameras from Samsung, countless cameras and everything else lets you stream directly to YouTube, Periscope, Facebook or any other social media channels. This has some hidden gems for those of you in the video game community.

In any case, you're generally presented with three options. The first is the very well known company. They've been around forever. They have a cloud version and you can pay monthly for each of their products in a suite. The next is a company well known for making very high-quality software at reasonable prices. If you decide to make content for companies like Pluralsight (the biggest vendor for online training content) they'll have suggestions that include the first two. And then you have everything else. In the everything else bucket. as you ask around, the Snobs will all tell you to use the Creative Cloud and spend a zillion dollars on books and learning. Pragmatists will tell you to go with item B b/c it's easy to use. And among the everything else crowd, this has come up more than anything so far now that I've been asking.

Ok, with the Creative cloud, unless you have hours (or a big training budget) to the point of being days, forget it. It's a career unto itself and a profitable one. You'll make great stuff, the trip to get there will be hard and you'll have a career more than a hobby. People can argue all they want and it generally centers around (there's more expensive stuff) but the prosumer market is dominated by option 1. Option 2 is something you can be up and running within an evening. A few hours a night for a week and you'll be good enough to make decent enough training videos to get accepted with many of the training companies.

Using this, I installed it, and off I went. I have a few machines, mostly Macintosh and Linux, but I have a high-end gamer laptop with a powerful video card for a laptop and I bought a video card for my desktop that is pricier than a lot of upper-end laptops are. So that seemed the perfect machine. 8GB of Video Ram, 36BG of RAM, a ton of hard drive space, cameras that were integrated specifically for tools like this, what could go wrong.

The installation was pretty easy, I accepted the defaults. I followed along someone on youtube b/c I don't know enough about videos to truly get the distinctions but they seemed to know what they were doing and it seemed to work. If you're like me, you may want to make quick, easy, content heavy (as opposed to special effect heavy ) youtube vids or training materials and you'll want to do a good job. You're willing to invest maybe 40 hours before you can put together something decent, no more than 8 to at least be able to put something together and past that 8 hours you want to be learning refinements and how to make things fancy.

So here are the big selling points according to Amazon:
*Movies in Minutes with Express Projects
*Introducing: 360º Video Stabilization
*Intelligent Color Correction
*Extreme Editing at the Action Camera Center
*True 360 View Designer
*Express Color Grading with LUTs
*Video Collage Designer
*Smoother Previews, Faster Exporting
*Premium Plug-ins and Templates

Movies in minutes are the biggest. Yes, it's true. You can do it. This level is an ultra beginner and the more you invest in getting it right first, the easier the rest becomes. As you get better, this moves on to adding cool and interest effects to make your videos interesting. Note that the 360 style cameras that are popular play really nice with this.

360 video stabilization. Stabilization has turned into something that just blows my mind. I'm old. When I was a kid my parents bought me a Pentax 35mm camera and I went crazy with filters, a motor drive and the like. Stabilization was something unheard of and it was the photographer that made the difference. Well this really does what it says and yes, hardware will have a big effect too, but it's not too hard to more or less inject this afterward unlike a lot in the video world. I find this feature amazing, in both how easy it is and how good it is.

Intelligent color correction is another feature. IDK, this seems underwhelming TBH. I've been used to phone cams doing it long enough the whole idea of having to engage in it and go through it to smooth and even if in a longer video didn't sit well with me. I'll say it's easy to use and it works. Even if in reality I didn't need a whole lot of help with it or have much need for it.

"Extreme Editing at the Action Camera Center". Well, the Action Camera Center does a lot and is really intuitive. Where the line is for Extreme editing eludes me, but I'll just say the Camera Center is a nice touch, I like it and so I'll give it a thumbs up.

True 360 View Designer - Yep, and as I've been using more and more advanced cameras that support this I appreciate it more. Yes, this is a great feature. Yes, it works well. Yes, even lower end cameras and video recorders have it and yes, you'll want to take advantage of it. When you do, you won't find this lacking unless you're on a whole different level than I can even imagine being in anywhere in the near future.

Express Color Grading with LUTs - Someone cares, probably a lot. I'm either too ignorant to understand why this is such a big deal or too ignorant to do it right. Either way, all I was left with was a feeling of "Meh" but it's probably b/c I'm doing it wrong.

Video Collage Designer - Yes, yes and more yes. So there are those YouTube and Periscope folks and there are those that are planning on making collages of their kids grow up over the years or the holidays each year. These used to be terrible and you'd cringe when someone brought them out, the best you could hope for was getting a DropBox or OneDrive link so you could <strikethrough>not really watch</strikethrough> pay very close attention gratefully for the opportunity. I'll just say, I made three in one day. I asked for criticism including from my teenager. She spent more time telling me what to include and leave out than trying to get out of watching it or making excuses on why she couldn't help. That tells you all you need to know. And she asked if I could get her a license of If I could get her a remote account b/c she wanted to build a few for homecoming last year, her pledge sister, and their pledge class and the like. It was a big hit. And it was easy to use. Doing it to where we get fancy can't be far away.

I don't have a lot in the way of comparison points, but it was fast, and by fast I mean, it never took so long to run through we found ourselves drifting off or making plans to do something else. Maybe the video card saved the day. Maybe it didn't. Either way, yes, this worked, it worked good enough and compared to the others we're used it, all was well. But again, I suspect the card and RAM at least had a little to do with it.

Premium Plug-ins and Templates - All I can say is it looks like there's a lot. They look easy to find and add. Past that, I can't say much, sorry. It seemed legit, seemed easy, we could find what we wanted. My daughter was better at it than I was by a long stretch, but to the best of my knowledge, it does what it says it' does.

So where does that leave us? Well, the cloud is fairly reasonable monthly but adds up fast. And you don't end up needing or using most of the items unless you're a whiz or plan on spending a lot of time learning the family. That second one I mentioned works great, it's more money, you do get a trial product but it clearly says "Made with Trial Version " until you get the license which is fine with me but I'm just warning you.

This is cheaper, it's easy to use, wasn't resource intensive, ran on the laptop (even though it was a gaming laptop) and thrived with the desktop. I'd love to update in a week or two with some links to some of the videos I make. I race cars at Road Atlanta and do some sports like Skydiving and a few other things that might lend themselves well to video editing. More importantly, as a Data Scientist, teaching people how to get into data science and teaching customers what I did for future reference and maintenance, teaching others the methodology for peer review, making internal (company based training vids instead of training docs) and the like seem like the sweet spot.

Just hopping on youtube as one metric, I saw over 20k different vids on this, so it's clearly used, used heavily and there's plenty of both free and production or for purchase training guides available.

I like it. I'm very glad they offered it. Unless something really awful happens as a surprise, my update will probably only sign the praises louder. Right now, especially at this price, it's a winner

I'm just going to go right ahead and say it... I don't really understand how the interface is supposed to work. Apparently you can only switch between workspaces if you've saved your document first, or whatever you've got loaded goes away? Or is it the workspace itself you're supposed to save? Needless to say, this is a bit of a problem for this review.

That said, this is still a pretty good program once you figure out exactly what's going on. The interface is similar to Photoshop Elements, with Manage, Adjust, and Edit workspaces that you can (somehow) switch between. As it happens, I generally prefer to use GIMP, and PaintShop compares pretty favorably to that too. There's plenty of tools; anything you need to do can probably be done here; in Adjust mode, you get a pile of filters and tweaks (iPhoto-style), while in Edit mode you get a full photo-paint interface, with a huge selection of filters and Photoshop-style layers. Basically, it's pretty much everything you might be looking for. As for the ancillary programs, I haven't really looked at them.

This is a pretty versatile program, and quite a bargain to boot. However, getting up and running in it is likely to take a while, stumbling through menus and preference panes to figure out exactly what's going on before you get your workflow going. This isn't a huge liability, but it's definitely a pain if you're, say, a reviewer with a limited period of time to learn your way through. I'll stick with GIMP, but if you're patient enough to figure it out you can't really go wrong with this.

A Top Tier NLE, but not for beginners.

I teach video production professionally, and I use a variety of NLEs. I teach both FCPX and iMovie. We also have Adobe CS, however we use that primarily for print and digital publications, and rarely for video production. I have used Premiere, but not extensively. For personal use, I've used AVID (Pinnacle) and Sony Vegas. Out of all of these, I prefer Vegas for a variety of reasons, which I'll summarize here. Mostly, I'll be comparing Vegas to FCPX, as I have less experience with Adobe and Pinnacle is simply garbage (sorry AVID).

Despite the claims of proponents of each product, they all have failings. Even Mac's beloved FCPX will crash from time to time. I've used so many different machines - from out of the box Macs to Top end PC's built exclusively for rendering video. The bottom line is, no platform is perfect. Overcoming foibles is part of the job. I run Vegas pro on a top end PC constructed for video editing. I suspect that Vegas will demand a high end hardware configuration, but I really don't know how if fares on an out of the box machine. I do find it crashes occasionally (more so when I use velocity envelopes on high frame rate clips). I also have a terrible time with the NewBlue titler. I had similar problems with the titler using Vegas Movie Studio - and despite the multitude of fixes avaiiable, I've learned to render NewBlue independently of the project I use it in. In any case, I've done a couple of big projects with Vegas Pro, and have not lost any content. From time to time I'll have to re-start the program, but it auto-saves nicely.

Vegas accepts a wide variety of formats smoothly, which is key for my productions. I use several cameras, each with very different codecs. From a Hero3 recording 2.7k at 24fps or 960p at 120fps to a DSLR recording .mov at 24fps, Sony Vegas manages them just fine. I prefer Canon cameras, but also use Sony and some other randoms. I recently completed a project that used a multitude of cameras, including these cheesy "game trap" motion sensing cameras used to film animals in wilderness settings. No problems stitching them all together. (FCPX did also handle the same footage, but my MacBook Pro had difficulty recognizing several of the cameras making importing a nightmare). I do wish the file management system in Vegas was better. Those of us who use FCPX or iMovie do enjoy the scrubbing of thumbnails. Certainly not a deal breaker, but I do wish Sony would adopt that feature or come up with a quick way to scrub clips.

The actual editing is where Vegas wins. I'll summarize by saying this: Vegas affords greater creative freedom at the expense of being more mathematically and technically cumbersome. In other words, if you don't have a calculator and pencil & pad sitting next to your computer right now, you might not be Vegas type. I find that I frequently have to calculate aspect ratios, frame rates, etc. I'll confess that I am a nerdy math type, so this is not a barrier for me. I also enjoy figuring out things like frame interpolation and drop downs. If this sort of thing does not interest you, consider buying a Mac and go the FCPX route, where these sorts of things are done automatically - albeit in a way that you might not like. Vegas uses timelines in a very linear manner, and you have to get comfortable linking clips from different timelines using groups or the ctrl-L feature (I forget the name now). In other words, Vegas will let you manage the content in your timelines in a Non-Linear way - it's just hard to figure out and get good at. Again, FCPX is better at this at first, but in the end you'll have more control with Vegas.

Vegas also gives you great control at animating your corrections/video effects/pans etc. It takes a while to get good at this - especially when you move sequences around your timeline - but it can be done, and the possibilities are pretty cool. Again, this is easier in some ways with FCPX, but Final Cut is way more limiting (I should say: I have not found a way to make FCPX do the things I want).

While Vegas does not have features like auto multi-cam editing (FCPX wins here), it does offer features like smooth velocity envelopes and more powerful color correcting (again, it's hard to learn, but very powerful). I've yet to exhaust the timelines (at least 20). It makes BluRays easily, and gives you lots of control without having to resort to secondary products (like compressor - or the jigsaw world of Adobe). Example: When I film a concert or play using several cameras and a digital recorder, I use FCPX to do the edit. FCPX will also allow brainless camera syncing and for easier color correcting to get the cameras to match. When I make a "surf movie" of my family vacation at the beach, I'll use Sony Vegas to stitch the different formats together (GoPro and others), and the color correcting features of Vegas do a better job of letting me get the rich contrast of a day at the beach just right.

This review is starting to ramble, and I'm not sure I need to say more. At the price offered by Amazon for the suite (under $250), Vegas is a steal for semi-pro type editors. If you're new to video editing or don't want to spend days learning ticky-tacky technical detalis it might not be for you. For those of us who like creative control, enjoy technical problem solving and have the time and patience to learn all the obscure little techniques, Vegas is a great product.

Right now I've been using a demo copy of Vegas Studio 11. I'm evaluating it for a middle school film editing class. Currently we use iMovie, but we may be moving to a PC lab, and I am not pleased with the Adobe Premiere software that we have now. I have been using Pinnacle for years for my personal movie editing.

Anyway, I'll start by saying how amazed I am at the professional features and smooth workflow of Vegas Studio 11. It's really not that different than the professinal version (Vegas Pro 11) - just lacking the high end format support (2k and 4k for example) and a 64 bit version and some layout features.

I'm writing this "pre-" review, in part because I have been wildly misled by some of the reviews floating out there about Sony Vegas Studio. Recently I read a review on PCMagazine, in which the author grossly misrepresents this software. I couldn't disagree more with the sentiment of that review. Other reviews (CNet) have also been disparaging of Sony, and I simply don't agree with general senitment regarding this software. I teach film editing (yes at an 8th grade level, still it's something), and I've used a variety of platforms and programs over the years. Here's my take:

Vegas Studio 11, is clearly a consumer version of Vegas Pro 11, with several pro level features disabled. Lacking in "one-touch" solutions, it is often mischaracterized as lacking functionallity. Quite the opposite is true. This program offers creative control that is simply non-existant in many of it's peers. This true depth of control is however, complex (you need more than one click for most things). The learning curve for a novice is predictably steep. I would not recommend this for casual editors looking to throw together a youtube video or vacation dvd. I would also not recommend this for first time videographers (start with iMovie or MovieMaker 11). For the moderate enthusiast, who can do without 64 bit or 4k support, and who does not worship in the cult of Apple, this is hands down the best product I've seen. Followers of Apple will likely find that Final Cut remains a better product. As to the technical elements (rendering, preview speed/quality, hardware support), I have been amazed. For those running 64 bit machines (like me - i7 with 24GB of ram), try the pro version (which supports 64 bit) and compare the improvement in speed. It's really not that much better - if you stick to standard formats like FullHD. I suspect the real, benefit of 64 bit will come when you render to 2 or 4k formats, or 3D FullHD (dual stream). I actually did some tests with Vegas Pro 11 in 64bit, and found that it rarely tapped into more than about 5GB of ram. Both programs, however, used all 8 processing threads of my i7 at over 95% - which does make me worried about overheating my CPU during long renders. I remain unsure how to dial it back if this becomes a problem. In any case the render time was fantastic (nearly 1:1 time for FullHD with 4 picture-in-picture tracks from my Canon g10). The program supports up to 10 video tracks, as well as a bunch more audio and text tracks. Output options are good, albeit confusing. If you are uncomfortable with the nightmare of video file choices out there, this program will not help. Wikipedia might.

4.5 out of 5 stars. Rounded up because the price is amazing. If you're interested, download the trial from SonyCreative first and test drive for yourself.

bottom line: Best "pro-sumer" product. Bad for beginners - great for those of us wanna-be's on a budget. I anticipate it will also be a great tool for teaching introductory film editing using the PC platform.

UPDATE: After a year of editing with Vegas, I have really developed into a Sony Vegas fan. Last fall, my employer purchased me a new Macbook Pro with Final Cut (FCPX). I teach video production in a mac based school and use FCPX exclusively for my professional work (I have no choice). Final Cut is wonderful, but when I do personal video editing, I always choose Vegas. It's much more my style. It allows much more specific control. I'm sure this next comment will insult Apple fans (I consider myself platform independent), Vegas is good for people who are creative and GOOD at math. Final Cut is good for people who are creative and BAD at math. I say this, because so many of the tools in Vegas require a mathematical understanding of the art (literally, I keep a calculator handy for things like frame rate conversions, aspect ratios, timing, clip length, etc). FCPX is much less numerical, but it also gets really sloppy. It compromises by doing cool things automatically, but then it seems to me that FCPX videos all tend to look the same because of this automation. I guess, I don't want to get into any more apple vs. pc debates. I'll just say, I really like Vegas. I don't recommend it for casual use or for the mathematically inept (sorry if this sounds mean).

A top notch NLE all the way.

OH yeah, one more thing - There are some key features that are disabled in the the "studio" version that might make "pro" upgrade worth it. One in particular is speed envelopes (pro only). There are also some limitations on rendering (can't make that perfect AVCHD file for vimeo).

After loading and running the INTUIT TurboTax Deluxe Fed Efile & State on my home PC last night, I curiously couldn't load my ATT/Yahoo homepage as I had several times earlier in the day. All I can load is Google Chrome - which I hadn't used in over a year. Oh, I can link to it - and get my mail - eventually. I cannot physically change my home page back to ATT, either. And - access through Google Chrome is via MSN - and I get an ad for 'no cost' TurboTax emblazoned on my screen. Well, I did give them 'permission' to make changes to my hard drive to load their software.

That cost thing is the main reason for the low score. They advertise no cost for some of their services - and sold me a product for $50 that included the state return. When I went to e-file the state return, I was hit with a bill for $25 that had to be paid by a CC before they would file the state with a bank listed for transfer of the refund. And - if you want them 'by your side' in an audit, you have to pre-pay that 'insurance' by submitting another $45.

I usually claim the standard deductions, but I had some questions re some possible deductions. They used the standard deductions. I'll go back to my tried and proven manual way next year - it has worked well for me for half a century! Now I know... be forewarned! In it's defense - it did 'work' - we'll see when the e-refunds arrive!

This is the tool I have used almost exclusively for the past 15 years. The only difference this year was my switching to Mac OS and hence ordering Mac version of the software. From interface point of view, I did not feel much delta between the Windows version and the Mac. Both smooth and easy to use. The Mac version was easily installed. I was also able to import my last year's taxes done on the Windows version to the Mac with a few easy steps. So, no issue with the cross platform migration.
As for the tool itself, in overall, I have seen it evolving over the years. On the surface the software is very user friendly and takes the user through the process while keeping complex calculations under the hood. What has worried me over the past couple of years (especially this year) is some assumptions and short-cuts the tool takes in filling out the forms with user's little or no knowledge. Last year, I found out IRS had fixed my schedule C through my lender when a copy of submitted form was compared against IRS records (fortunately, there was no impact to the bottom line). This year, I came to know (purely by accident) that software dropped some of my qualified expenses without notifying me. Unfortunately, did not have time to get help with it so I ended up not claiming a few hundred dollars! So, seems that all the customization and effort to make the tool more useful is taking toll on the accuracy of the end result. Yes, Turbo Tax guarantees its accuracy, bla bla... the above observations have convinced my wife and I to perhaps have a professional tax preparer go over our last couple of years tax filings; just to put our minds at ease.

I am very familiar with Photoshop and somewhat familiar with Adobe Illustrator. A recent change in adobe policy made it impossible to update or use my old version and I don’t wish to pay a monthly subscription for something I hardly ever use. So I found myself unable to even open many AI files and welcomed the idea of having a program that could open them and, hopefully, even edit them.

There were several features that attracted me to try this product:

1. The possibility that it would replace Illustrator for the few uses I gave it—namely changing color fields in vector images, saving the changed file in PDF, then reopening the pdf file in photoshop and proceeding with it. This worked fairly smoothly but with a slight learning curve and took much longer. Still, my primary wish was met.

2. The idea of a font manager. Here I was disappointed. From what I gather, the font manager option is for the full program. I do like that, when fonts display in the font-drop down menu, they show the font name in that font instead of the adobe method of just showing the word “sample” in the drop down. The corel method is preferable. It wasn’t clear in the description if the program came with additional fonts. It doesn’t. It also isn’t able to locate fonts installed in the new location that Microsoft has been putting them in since an update sometime in April 2019 (user>app data>local>Microsoft>windows>fonts). Fonts in the old default location (windows>fonts) do appear.

3. The idea of easily turning a photo into a painting. I’m not finding this to be any easier than in Photoshop even though there is a dedicated program for it. I can’t find how to work in layers (no layers palette or information on how to display it—I have to work on the background layer, never a good idea. The search function of the help box doesn’t come up with anything even though it indicates that it respects some masks (which would imply layers at some level). It’s the opposite of streamlined, which is what one would expect from a program dedicated to just one function.

4. Converting bitmap images to vector objects and curiosity as to, exactly, what Powertrace might be. There are many google results having to do with Powertrace. Unfortunately, it’s the same tutorial over and over and always referencing another version, not the Home and Student Edition. Since this wasn’t that big a curiosity for me, I haven’t pursued it further.

On the whole, I’m a bit disappointed. The Home and Student version seems to be just one big commercial for the full version and a venue for making purchases of assets that, frankly, could well have been included in the purchase price.

This is a list of things that I find annoying:

--There are constant links and pop ups urging you to get the full version.
--It comes with very little content of its own. It contains no new fonts, no clipart (well, it does come with a grand total of 4 clip art images like a bunch of red peppers).
--Everywhere you turn you come up with a window to purchase content. I haven’t come across any free downloadable content.
--While it does make it easy to locate and open assets elsewhere on your computer, many first time users might not already have those assets. The Corel folder it creates in your documents folder is, initially, empty.
--Some of your existing content can be opened and others must be imported. No idea why.
--The included templates are uninspired.
--The tutorials sometimes run, sometimes not.
--The tool palette, on the left, shows the tool icons but, sometimes, certain common tools just aren’t there (like the brush which I’ve used and now can’t locate).
--The hints section to the right of the workspace is awkward and sometimes assumes that you’re more familiar with the program than one would initially be.
--Everything seems to take more steps and more dialog boxes than really necessary. (Ex: when opening an existing AI document you’re asked if you want it as text or curves—huh? I want the whole document as it would open in Illustrator. Apparently, the right answer is ‘text’ which makes no sense for a graphic.)
--The program does not presume to be either a full-bodied Photoshop nor a full-bodied Illustrator and I don't expect it to. But the point is that this program is SUPPOSED to be easier, more straightforward and intuitive. It’s aimed at the non-professional home user or a newbie student. Yet I keep finding that the workflow is less smooth even if, in the end, the outcome might turn out be similar.

I'm ill, in pain, and often tense. Sleeping well has always been a problem but never more so than now. I've found that having someone with a nice soothing voice read a much loved book to me is very soothing and relaxing. I fall asleep and spend a restful night without needing sleep medications. It's also relaxing during the day. I like that like with Kindle fire on I can get a preview--more like an audition, really, to see if the voice, diction, speed, and tone are consistent with your expectations.

For relaxation purposes, listening is much more effective than reading for some reason. I speculate that several factors combine: outside noise is blocked; the earphones concentrate your attention and they are so close to your brain that other distractions don't interfere; and you can focus without interruption as the text being read sets a pace and you don't. In my case, where I'm specifically looking for the pleasant and familiar, it creates a comfortable environment in which my mind doesn't wander to what is hurting since I'm in another world.

I waited to complete the entire course before writing this review and, in no particular order, these are my observations.

I should tell you that my web skills are limited. I used Front Page for years and have created several very elementary Dreamweaver sites for family members. I'm in the process of trying to work more professionally (cleaner, less, and better code) and more creatively (a less stilted and more unique and appealing design). This course doesn't purport to be a web design course, it assumes that you already have a basic idea for a (relatively simple) website and it's going to teach you how to implement it. Don't expect inspiration.

POSITIVE:
* Level: absolute beginner to intermediate.
* It is entirely DVD-based. The booklet (106 pages including index) that comes with the DVD just provides additional random information; it is in no way connected to the content or the sequence of the course.
* The scope of the information presented is all that is needed for basic competence in the implementation of a simple Dreamweaver CS5 website. You do become familiar with the DW interface; the workflow; best practices; web and DW terminology; and basic website structure and creation.
* The sequence and progression is logical and sequential. There is some reiteration of basic principles as the course progresses to clarify how something previously explained applies in a new situation.
* The interface is very well thought out. You can go backwards or forwards easily. It has constant access to a table of contents that allows you to search (by both topic and subtopics) in case you want to navigate to a completely different topic. It allows you to bookmark (with long notes-to-self) on topics of interest you may want to keep coming back to. The interface coloring and design are neutral and un-intrusive. The screen always retains focus and the surrounding options melt away.
* The course has the secondary purpose of preparing for the Adobe Certified Expert (ACE) test. Each main section has a practice test at the end.
* Another asset is that the video transcript is captioned under the viewing screen (optional). This is particularly helpful if you're in in a public place where you can't turn on the sound.
* All explanations of hard-to-grasp concepts (ex: the box model) are clearly demonstrated with visual examples. As a matter of fact, this is the clearest explanation I have ever seen of the box model.
* I did find the booklet section on Fireworks instructive, since I have never used that program in the past. In fact, the entire booklet is very instructive and useful--it's just totally unrelated to the course on DVD.

NOT SO POSITIVE:
* Coverage of other concepts, such as content placement and positioning, are a little more basic and less detailed than I would have liked. I can't recall (or locate now) any reference to floats, whereas AP divs and frames (both older concepts) are well covered.
* The narrator has a pleasant voice and clear diction, but is somewhat monotone and unexciting.
* There is no "search" function or "index." This makes it impossible to look up a term or a topic that is not specifically listed in the table of contents. I would have thought that the transcript text might have been searchable, but it isn't.
* For my learning style, I would have preferred that the booklet function as support material for the video so I could follow along with text that directly reinforces the visual demonstration (and is easier to go back to or use as reference material.)
* The bookmarks that you create when taking the course on your laptop are saved to that computer. If you want to change to your desktop, the bookmarks, obviously, are not there. So as a "personal index" these bookmarks have their limits.

On the whole, I do recommend this course. I wish I had taken it earlier, because it laid down the groundwork better than an online course that I had just taken. However, I think that, for a person who is learning-as-you-go (not just learning general theory but actually trying to apply it to a real website) it is essential to have reference material on Dreamweaver CS5, some sort of guidance on design principles, and a solid HTML and CSS anthology--all in well-indexed books.

Video and online courses teach you very well how to build their sample websites, but when you are stumped on YOUR website, you need to be able to supplement the general principles they taught you with a more on-point reference.

I've been using software for tax preparation for years. For the last few years (through 2012) I used an H&R Block product (Tax Cut) and although I was generally happy with it, last year I tried the on-line version of TurboTax. That experience was positive enough that I was happy to give the packaged version of TurboTax a try this year.

Although I need to wait for some additional documentation from my bank and employer before I can finalize my 2013 taxes, my experience with TurboTax this year has been even better than last year. The interface is clean and the process is intuitive. TurboTax imported my tax returns, and all my personal information, from last year's return. And when my employer makes my W-2 available for the year, TurboTax will import that as well.

In my opinion, far too many people pay others to do their taxes despite having no extraordinary circumstances. For most of us, products like TurboTax save money and make the tax-return creation and filing process much simpler.

I enjoy taking photos with my Sony camera, but I'm an amateur and have never learned an editing program. As a result, I've captured beautiful moments, but my pictures are imperfect and need organization and correction before I can share them with family and friends. My pictures taken on my smartphones over the years especially need help. Many pictures seemed hopeless due to overexposure, blemishes and other issues, but I've been truly amazed at how photos that are dull, overexposed and lacking in many ways can be transformed with Corel AfterShot Pro 3 into beautiful images to frame and share. Although AfterShot Pro 3 seems perfect for batch editing by Pros, I'm using it for for editing individual photos and am really pleased with it so far.

AfterShot Pro 3 is feature-rich and offers more than I may ever use, but I appreciate that there's still room to grow once I master the basics. A Pro won't have issues, but the program wasn't completely intuitive at first given that it's my first editing program. But in under two weeks, it's become quite intuitive with practice along with tutorials and the manual. I've gone from not knowing how to even import my photos to learning enough to vastly improve photo quality and complete my finished pictures with a watermark. The online tutorials offer some help, but the 230-page manual on the Corel site is indispensable. I downloaded it to iBooks on my iPad mini and keep it beside me for reference. It's made a world of difference between being frustrated and really catching on to this program.

At this time, a 30-day free trial of this program is available from Corel. That seems like a great way to decide if it's for you. But let me note a few things that stand out about this software. AfterShot Pro 3 is fast and runs perfectly on my iMac- it hasn't crashed or acted glitchy. There are no subscription fees. I can take liberties editing and experiment on my photos because the originals are never altered when working in AfterShot Pro 3. I can open photos from folders on my computer, but better yet I've learned to import my photos into my AfterShot Pro catalog so I can just click on the catalog and see all my pictures in a vertical thumbnail panel view. I simply click on the photo I want to edit, and it enlarges to the center screen so I can flag it and edit away.

The simple editing features in the lower portion of the screen are so easy to use for cropping, whitening, red eye and blemish corrections, etc. The right-hand tabs (Standard, Color, Tone, Detail, Metadata, Watermark, etc.) allow me to apply more sophisticated changes and alterations to correct color, exposure and so forth. Within those tabs, easy adjustments can be made. For example, in "Basic Adjustments” under the "Standard" tab, I can check "auto level" or “perfectly clear” for automatic adjustments that make huge quality improvements to my photos. Under the "Detail" tab, I can apply changes to sharpen my photos, etc. "Watermarking" is a great feature with obvious value for Pros, but it's also fun for me to add my own mark (text or image). Since I'm still learning the program, I add my watermark when I'm done editing as an easy way to distinguish my finished photos. A watermark preset can be created to save time, and there are many other capabilities.

I've learned enough in just a short time to see a real benefit in my photos. I'm sure I'll be calling customer service again (I've called and received helpful support) because I still hit minor obstacles. It sounds so basic, but I was having difficulty figuring out how to export my finished photos into useable jpeg files. With the manual at hand, I managed to achieve what I want by pressing control and command and dragging pictures to another file, but there's probably a better way so I'll seek support after I save up a list of questions. With more practice, AfterShot Pro 3 will clearly get easier, but I do feel I've worked through the program enough in just under two weeks to recognize it's a very good value for all it has to offer, and I definitely recommend it.
Way too labor intensive. All I wanted to do was integrate with Rally and export xml to create beautiful charts and reports.  Doesn't import xml at least as far as I can tell.
I have been using turbo tax for the last 12 years but I may not use them this year because I have been audited once again and a CPA said that they look for the words self-prepared.

I have been using this software for many years with few if any problems. This year is a glaring exception.
As at least one other person has mentioned, there is a bug that prevents importing info from Charles Schwab with no projected date for resolution. I contacted H&RB tech support. Their "solution" was for me to enter the data manually. What's the point of tax software if you have to enter numerous financial transactions manually? The other tantalizing option given by tech support over a chat was to upload the data. There is no documentation in the software on how to do this. On 2 separate occasions, just as the tech support person was supposedly about to tell me how to upload this info, the chat spontaneously terminated. As I write this, I have been trying for the last hour to invoke a new chat. All I get is a blank Internet Explorer page with a spinning wheel. Much as it irks me, I will probably have to switch to TurboTax and go through the pain of learning how to use a new package. H&R Block, get your act together.

I purchased a new computer and decided to upgrade some of my more frequently-used applications to the newest version. In the case of Roxio Creator Suite, that was a mistake. I had been using Roxio 7 and many versions prior to that - even back to the Adaptec days.

The user interface is cumbersome and non-intuitive. Many of the design decisions were just plain stupid. A simple DVD copy takes up most of the screen real estate, just to display the progress in 48 point Helvetica. Hey guys, there are some of us who can run more that one program at a time.

...and boy is it slow. It slogged along on my Intel Duo Core with 4G of RAM and a 7200RPM drive. How can someone write code this bad?

The documentation should be an embarrassment. I was deciding what to install. One of the features was "MediaShare". There was zero documentation in the install or on the website. I still don't know what it was.

The worst problem for me is that I started getting BSOD (Windows crashes) after installing. There is something that doesn't get along with my brand new hardware.

From the packaging, it appears that Roxio was purchased by Sonic. It looks like they dumped products from both product lines and slapped a launcher in front of it instead of really designing a suite of multimedia products.

I removed the code and am installing Roxio 7. I just wish I could get my money back...

Bought this because it was advertised as supporting caldav.  However, it doesn't sync with my caldav server.  No errors, no nothing - just doesn't work.  Documentation is minimal, user interface can be generously described as a train wreck.  Might be OK if you use their service, but otherwise a waste of money.

After downloading the trial version, suggest you do the same, I also downloaded Sony Movie 13. As you are aware these companies have stopped including operating manuals. I remember DOS 6 as big as the bible. CyberLink I felt is superior to Sony for ease of editing. I have some Mini DV tapes that I duplicated into digital via Roxio, I didn't care to be a Hollywood producer or cinematographer just home videos that are important to just my family and I. I went ahead and edited a 45 minute DV and wanted to burn it so I could see what the end product after editing and titles would look like. The program got to 20% authorizing and just stuck there. It took 10 minutes to get to 20 and another 10 went by and didn't budge, so I tried it two more times and same results. So being something less then a rocket scientist, I figured Sony may have a lock until you purchase, I can understand that, but could not find and documentation. I sent a note to Amazon customer service, to see if they know, no response. While looking at other editing software I saw Adobe Premier Elements, so I downloaded their free trial. When editing there is a strip that runs across the entire video, so if you were to make a copy that would be on the video, like the old Copy stamps we put across the face of wedding photos when digital first came in. So I am OK with that I understand. I played with Adobe Premier Elements 13 and loved it. I have used Photoshop 7 in the past so I understand what many of the icons are for. Also I feel the desktop is superior to any I've seen. It does cost $15 more then these programs, but over a few years, what is that. I don't want to spend all my hours editing, I rather take the pictures and Videos right the first time. I guess that's because I am out of the film era where we had to take quality all the time, now digital just shoot a lot and edit on the back end, what a waste of time. Have fun on your quest, and thanks to all who put their 2 cents in, I know it helps me in making a decision.
