text: Suzanne Foster worked for over two decades for Victor Kemp, ostensibly as his chief of security in a legitimate company but more importantly as one of the contract killers for his illegitimate enterprises. She didn't expect to fall in love and has every  reason to expect Victor would take her choice of a life partner poorly.  He does. She continues to work for him despite some personal bumps. The story focuses on her attempt at retirement and to live a "normal" life.  "The Former Assassin" twists continuously and while every clue is there and each twist is believable, it still feels surprising enough to be supremely satisfying. Although there are plenty of thriller aspects to the story itself and connected with the roles of those in government intelligence, in human trafficking, who carry out calculated and gruesome murders and quietly "necessary" murders, the book also reads a bit like a Cosy Thriller and it works.  Stern entertains throughout in what appears to be her first novel.  It reads like the work of an experienced best selling writer, face paced and entertaining with great characters of dubious morality.

This book is a shining example of how a mediocre person can rise to the pinnacle  of success.  Being at the right place at the right time and doing exactly what is  required has earned Mr. Brown accolades, awards, praise, and financial success from an extensive network of well-placed supporters.  As a Dallas resident myself, it's encouraging to read about our former police chief from such a personal and gloriously self-promoting perspective.  Reading it has been so satisfying that it almost allays my dismay that no official account, footage, or investigation reports of the July 2016 event were included.  Maybe someday.  The Robot chapter was interesting, and I understand his reasoning to blow up the cornered and contained suspect rather than continue the standoff a couple more hours.  Only a seasoned and experienced cop like Mr. Brown would have the confidence and intuition to know the suspect was lying about all the bombs he'd planted all over downtown, and telling the truth that he acted alone and had no accomplices, so no need to take him in alive and vigorously interrogate to confirm the veracity of those statements.  The story of Mr. Brown's rise to prominence is all the more amazing when reflecting on the many challenges he faced as Police Chief.  From all five police associations calling for his resignation, hundreds of officers retiring early, the sharp increase in violent crime in Dallas, a police force understaffed both then and today.  But what a moving personal story of Mr. Brown's success and keen street-smarts demonstrated when he wisely and deftly averted all of those issues when he announced his decision to retire.  When the choice must be made between the honorable path of public service or the path of promoted private citizen, Mr. Brown shows us by example, through a combination of insight, determination, raw grit, and particularly key supporters, it is possible to do both.  There are so many rich lessons to be learned from this wonderfully marketed book.

text: well written... excellent historical references throughout every chapter! I love Viking/ Scandinavian history, literature and films!!

First of all, I must note that this book dealing with children's emotions was published by DK, a publishing company rightly noted for its superb travel books. I love the clear, wonderful colors used in the book, and the DK logo attracted me and set up my expectations. I was not disappointed.

That said, this is not a book you should simply give your child to read and expect him or her to understand it. The way to approach this book is together with your child, in small increments, reading together and discussing various concepts along the way. As I read this book, I was imagining reading it with a child and discussing it together, even acting out various imaginative scenarios. As a retired teacher, I could imagine using this book in elementary school "circle meetings," where kids discuss with an adult how to deal with some of the feelings they are having.

This book deals with positive emotions, like happiness and joy, but more to the point, it covers difficult feelings, such as anger, fear, and sadness. I liked the fact that the book explains what is going on in our bodies as we experience these feelings, and it also gives kids an expanded vocabulary for expressing what they are experiencing. For example, instead of "I feel bad," or "I feel mad," children are given words like "embarrassed," "jealous," "guilty," "stressed," and many more. There is a wonderful section on bullying, which is approached from the POV of both the bully and the target of bullying. The book contains all sorts of suggestions for what to do when you experience these emotions. I only wish this sort of thing had been around when many adults were children. It seems to me that so many of our present day problems may have been forestalled if people had had a chance when they were kids to express and deal with their negative emotions.

I give this book my highest recommendation for every parent, and every teacher as well. The book itself is most appropriate for pre-school and elementary school, but even high school teachers and parents of teens could benefit from it--for themselves, not for the kids, who would be insulted by a "kiddie book" but who may desperately need the lessons it teaches.

text:
Anyone who thinks that all high schools are created equal, or that it doesn't much matter where your child goes to school or who his or her teachers are, will be quickly disabused of that myth upon reading this wonderful book. In its pages, the reader will meet a dedicated teacher and his amazing students, all of whom have embarked upon professional-level explorations into various scientific projects, and have entered and won the most prestigious science competitions in the country.

Though the book is long, the reader will be rewarded by getting to know Andy Bramante, the teacher who guides his remarkable kids, not only through advanced inquiries into scientific problems and questions, but through the typical problems of teenage life in 21st century America. You will also love the kids, who are both exceptional and typical. And you will wish that all our kids had teachers like Mr. Bramante and opportunities to leave behind the usual classroom science experiments and follow their passions, their dreams. What these kids accomplish is mind-blowing.

I recommend this book to everyone who cares about education today, and who longs for an alternative to lockstep schooling. "The Class" shows that, while not easy in the least, it can be done.

text:
"The Latecomers" is an interesting if overlong family saga tracing the interrelationships of two young women of roughly the same age but far different circumstances, their ancestors, and their descendants. Bridey Molloy comes to the United States from Ireland in 1908, beginning her journey with her fiancé, Thom Flynn, who dies on the ship. Only 16, pregnant, penniless, and completely on her own, Bridey's experience is more or less typical of that of many young Irish immigrants. Parallel to Bridey's story is that of Sarah Stanton Hollingworth, a young woman who is Bridey's opposite in almost every way. Sarah's family is Old Money, and they live on a family estate, Hollingwood. Bridey's and Sarah's paths cross and intertwine repeatedly through this novel, which ends in 2018 with their descendants in both Ireland and the United States.

This novel does a fairly good job of presenting the Irish immigrant experience, though Bridey fares much better than the families in Frank McCourt's "Angela's Ashes," another remarkable and far more graphic account. As I was reading, it occurred to me that this book may be targeted at a YA audience, as the grimmest and most brutal facts of immigrant life are glossed over. Bridey suffers, yes, but she fares better than most of her contemporaries of similar station. Sarah, too, is more caring and compassionate, at least until the middle of the novel, and more accepting of immigrants than most young women of her class.

The two families are full of secrets, and these secrets drive the plot. The secrets range from the trivial to the profound, and some are not revealed until generations have passed and the originators of the mysteries are long dead and buried. Their children and grandchildren are left to pick up the pieces and try to put them together. The effects of these secrets determine the lives and destinies of nearly everyone involved.

One major problem I had with this book is that to find it credible, the reader has to believe in an amazing number of coincidences. I wish I could give examples, but to go into much detail would reveal some plot twists that the reader should discover for him or herself. However, trust me when I say that these coincidences are just too far-fetched to be credible, and they continue to the very end. I selected this book to review because I enjoy family sagas and I like stories of the immigrant experience in America, but I wish the author had handled the plot development with fewer astonishing and unbelievable coincidences.

Still, I did enjoy this book, and if you enjoy family sagas and mysteries and can suspend your incredulity as to how and why some events occur, you will enjoy it too.

text:
I was drawn to this novel because in our family we have three wonderful adopted children (now young adults), all from Russia. I was interested to see how the author would handle the topic of international adoption in a fictional setting.

The novel is set in an upper middle class suburban community in the Northeast and revolves around four families. The narrator, Nicole, is married to Jay and has two young sons. The action begins when Gene and Paige, one of the couples in their circle of friends, announce that they are planning to adopt a child from Russia. They travel to Russia with their young son, Cameron, to pick up four-year-old Winifred (Winnie).

From the start it is obvious to the reader that this adoption was a terrible idea. Paige is far too controlling, the kind of person who thinks family life should look like a Hallmark movie, and she will do anything to make her vision a reality. Certainly Winnie has her problems--who of us does not?--but Paige's incessant demands on her and on the rest of the family are too much for these relationships to bear. Nothing good comes of this, and the novel ends tragically.

I was disappointed by this novel for several reasons. First of all, I did not like any of the characters. The husbands/fathers are one-dimensional at best, and the mothers are often selfish, self-centered, and downright mean. They want to create an idyllic life, failing to see that such a life does not exist. They are woefully inadequate as parents, and more than once I wanted to shake them. When they are not controlling their children, they are over-indulging them. Nicole cannot even manage a baking session with her seven year old without completely decompensating. Paige is downright abusive to Winnie, the most sympathetic character in the book.

Second, several plot lines were begun and then simply dropped. There is a pretty important subplot concerning Nicole's relationship with her adult sister, who is an alcoholic and possibly a drug addict, and her mother, who cannot carry on a civilized conversation without screaming and slamming down the phone. Nicole has several interactions with both her mother and sister over the course of the book. It is obvious that she wants to create a life that is the exact opposite of those of her mother and sister, but there is no resolution to this plot line at the end; it is simply never mentioned again.

Finally, the style of writing is annoying to this reader. Serling uses sentence fragments. A lot. For effect. Knowing she is doing it. Hoping it will work. Failing. (See how annoying this is?) A few sentence fragments are one thing, but this book's primary style is the sentence fragment. There are literally hundreds, and perhaps thousands of sentence fragments in this book. No, I am not exaggerating. Sadly, the author does show a lot of insight into some of her characters. The content of the sentence fragments is fine. Even when she is doing exactly the wrong thing, Nicole sees what she is doing. She feels remorse and shows insight, but the way she expresses herself is distracting. I think the author may have wanted to create a stream-of-consciousness effect, but for me at least, the style simply does not work.

Even after all that, I did not hate this book. It has some interesting parts and partially developed characters. But I cannot recommend it.

This little commentary/guide has been very helpful to our social justice study group as we consider Laudato Si'.  The summaries of almost all sections are written in more user-friendly language (not that the encyclical itself is not, but the summaries are more concise), and the study questions are thought provoking.  We always run our of time before we run out of questions.  I also like the supplementary material, especially the Canticle of Nature but St. Francis.  Highly recommended for groups wanting to begin a good discussion of this important encyclical.

"All the Time in the World" is a debut coming-of-age story of Charlotte, a gifted young composer whose work has been plagiarized by one of her professors. This plagiarism immobilizes Charlotte professionally, and she all but stops composing music altogether. As she tries to come to terms with what has happened, she takes a job as a nanny for an upscale Manhattan couple, looking after Matt and George, their two preschool sons. She finds that she has a gift for relating to small children and becomes increasingly immersed in the lives of her employers, Gretchen and Scotty. The first third of the book is taken up almost completely with day to day events in the lives of the children, their parents, their preschool teachers, and their little friends. This part gives a good, sometimes ironic and amused glimpse into the world of wealthy Manhattanites and the lives they live, their preoccupation with the correct organic food, the way they relate to their children, their spouses, and the help, and more.

Then tragedy strikes, and the remainder of the book deals largely with how each character deals with what has happened. Part of this is masterful, especially as it shows the reactions of little Matt and George, as well as many of the remaining characters. At this point another character is introduced, Patrick, the brother of Scotty, and it is here that the book falters, in my opinion. In the midst of all the tragedy, a kind of romance blossoms, and to me it was just not convincing. However, I will give the book credit for not having a predictable, fairy tale, "Sound of Music" ending without the Nazis and the nuns.

This book deals with time in a very interesting way. It shifts between the past, present, and future, with the tragedy as the focal point and everything else recounted as "before" and "after." I like this sort of time structure when it is skillfully done, and it is done quite well here. Events that relate one to another are juxtaposed, and it is easy to tell what is past and what is present because the timing of each event is given at the beginning of the chapter. However, I know from members of my book club that some readers really hate this structure, so if you are one of those readers, be forewarned. In addition, the book is divided into five major sections, each with the name of a main character. Something pivotal happens to each character in his or her designated section, though all the other characters are dealt with in each section as well.

The title should be mentioned, as I think in at least some ways it is ironic. The author seems to suggest that we often live our lives as if we had all the time in the world, but this is an illusion. Therefore, if you have a talent, use it, develop it; if you love someone, say it, show it; if you have an issue with someone, air it, resolve it; for you decidedly do not have all the time in the world.

I liked this book very much. It is not deathless prose, and it will make no one's list of classic works. But it is well written, easy to read, interesting, and deals with the emotional consequences of tragedy in a realistic and mostly unsentimental way. The weakest part was the quasi-romance, which I grew bored with, but I thought the parts dealing with the little children were remarkably realistic. I recommend this book for readers who enjoy family dramas and coming-of-age stories.

text:
"Don't You Cry" is a psychological mystery narrated by two young persons. Quinn is a young woman in her early twenties living in Chicago whose roommate has gone missing. Alex is a teenager whose mother abandoned him when he was only five and whose father is an alcoholic; he works in a diner in Michigan. The story is narrated in alternate chapters by these two characters, and for most of the book it is impossible to see the connection between them. Both narrators, for different reasons, are unreliable, and this makes some of their conclusions and judgments erroneous. The two strands come together at the end of the novel, and mystery fans will like all the twists and turns, all the false clues and misleading observations.

I, however, was very frustrated by this book. It was neither the subject nor the structure that troubled me; indeed, I really like the dual narrator device when it is skillfully done. What annoyed me repeatedly was the writing, which was not uniformly terrible, but which had frequent moments that gave me pause as a reader.

First among these was the author's frequent use of the pathetic fallacy, or the attribution of human emotions to nature. In one scene, the sea comes on shore, grabbing at the ankles of a person standing near it, inviting her into the deep. In another, the "deranged November air" dislodges a tin can from a trash barrel. This sort of thing occurs all too often for my taste.

Second is the tone. I realize that the author is trying to convey the affect of young people, but the tone of both narrators is smart aleck-y and flippant, a "too cool for school" attitude. Both narrators are irreverent and cynical, and if you were given a chapter without knowing whose point of view it was, you would be justified is guessing either one. There should be some differences between a college-educated young career woman in the city and a disaffected and somewhat lost adolescent boy, but with respect to tone, there are none.

Finally, the author makes frequent use of metaphors that are imprecise and distracting. For example, when Quinn is trying to find her missing roommate's cell phone by calling her number, she describes it thus: "I . . . continue to search, tracking the subdued ringtone as Hansel and Gretel tracked bread crumbs through the deep, dark, woods." Alex describes the waitresses who use their break time to smoke cigarettes this way: ". . . the two of them teeter-tottering all day on their rotating smoke breaks." Both of these metaphors are ridiculous if you think about them in any depth, and these are only two examples. I love these use of effective metaphor, but most of the metaphors in this book were ineffective and annoying. (If you are interested in an author whose metaphors are superb, read Amy Parker's new book, "Beasts and Children.")

So here is my advice for anyone who is considering buying this book. Read some of the positive reviews, of which there are many on the Amazon site. It may be that what detracted from my reading of this book would not bother you at all. Then make your decision.

text:
My husband and I just purchased our iPhones about a year ago, and we love them. However, we realize that we do not use a great deal of the phone's capability; in fact, we probably use only about 1% of what we could use. In addition, the iPhone 6 does not come with a written user's manual, and I wish it did.

We ordered both iPhone 6s Portable Genius and the Teach Yourself Visually manual for the iPhone 6. While I have to give a slight edge to the Teach Yourself Visually book, the Portable Genius is excellent, too. It does not have as many colored screen shots, but it does have detailed illustrations and step by step directions for any operation you can imagine. Actually, the two manuals complement each other nicely. The Portable Genius has more text, so if that suits your learning style, go with this book. Some of the areas covered in this manual include an overview of the audio features of the phone and an extensive chapter on the camera and all its features. (By the way, if you have not yet purchased an iPhone, you will be blown away by the potential of the camera. It is, by far, the best camera I have ever owned,) You will also learn about the Cloud, and I freely confess that I did not really understand the Cloud until I read this book. The chapter on fixing your iPhone is invaluable. Truly, I am glad we have both books. I give the Portable Genius my highest recommendation.

text:
I have loved Elizabeth Strout's writing ever since I read--and reread--"Olive Kitteridge," so I leapt at the chance to review her new book, "My Name is Lucy Barton." This latest of her books is shorter than the previous two, and I think it is more personal as well, though I have no way of knowing how much of the action, if any, comes from real life.

As the book opens, Lucy Barton, a successful writer, is in the hospital with a mysterious complication of surgery that is never fully explained. She remains in the hospital for nine weeks, largely isolated from the outside world--her husband hates hospitals and rarely visits, and her two daughters are too young to come on their own. Suddenly, Lucy's mother, whom she has not seen in several years, shows up and stays with her in the hospital for five days. During this time, the action alternates between the present and Lucy's past, her childhood and young adulthood, and the reader gradually comes to an understanding of both Lucy and her mother. The two reminisce about various events and persons from the past, and in particular, they discuss the marriages of various friends and relatives. As the book develops, the reader sees that all this talk of other people's marriages is a way for both Lucy and her mother to avoid the elephant in the room, their own marriages and relationships.

Lucy's mother's visit is not all sunshine and roses. In chapters alternating between past and present, we see the truly terrifying past that Lucy has overcome, and her mother's limited awareness of the damage she has done to her daughters and son. Lucy's brother and sister are mentioned in small but important glimpses into the past, and the reader sees the childhood of poverty and abuse that is her heritage. Despite all this, however, Lucy loves her mother and desperately wants her approval. She slowly comes to a realization of who she is--the time in the hospital could be compared to an extended retreat, without the religious element--and we see the results of her epiphany in the last part of the book.

I loved the way this book was written. There is very little action; most of what happens is conversation and memory and conversation about memory. The point of the book is the development of a self, an identity, an awareness of who you really are--thus the title: "My Name is Lucy Barton." The book reads very quickly--it has fewer than 200 pages--but it will stay with you long after you have read it. I recommend this book for anyone who enjoys contemporary literary women's fiction.

This is a charming little ABC book for preschoolers.  It is subtitled "A Natural Alphabet," and I found that it was true to that in many ways.  First, the animals and other items named in the book are somewhat unexpected.  For example, E is for egret, and Y is for yellow-bellied sapsucker.  I can imagine kids having fun with the language of the book.  Second, the illustrations are lovely.  They have the look and feel of woodcuts, though actually they are paper art.  Finally, the colors are subtle and beautiful.  There are grays, yellows, blues, browns, pinks--but nothing bright or garish.  (If you want bright colors and cute animals doing human things like washing dishes or cooking, this is not the book for you.)  This book would be a lovely and inexpensive shower gift.  Highly recommended.
product: Professional Trade Compliance Certification Study Guide

text:
"All Dogs Go to Kevin," a clear reference to "All Dogs Go to Heaven," is a delightful book that will be loved and appreciated by anyone who loves dogs and has ever had a dog share his or her life. Jessica Vogelsang, the author, details her own coming of age and her relationships with the three dogs she has owned: Taffy, Emmett, and Kekoa. She begins the journey in middle school and takes us on an emotional journey through high school, college, veterinary school, and marriage.

The book is engaging and very easy to read; even kids in upper elementary grades would be able to manage it. However, the part I found most compelling was the role that dogs play in the lives of, as the author puts it in her dedication, "the misfits, the miscreants, the misunderstood, the freaks and geeks and socially inept, and the dogs who love them." It is clear from the outset that the author sees herself as one of these "misfits" who found salvation in the love of a dog. She write with grace and compassion of her relationship with both animals and people, and she clearly does not write off anyone, no matter what issues they may have.

Some reviewers have compared the book favorably to James Herriot's "All Creatures Great and Small." I do not share this view. I found Herriot's books far more charming and compelling and have read each one several times. This book is just not in that category. However, that is not meant to dismiss the many virtues of this book. Almost any lover of dogs would enjoy and identify with the characters in the book, especially the author. I recommend it highly, and if you are not expecting Herriot, you will enjoy this book exactly for what it is.

As a retired English teacher, I am always looking for interesting and amusing ways to teach language concepts. The title of this book, "My Teacher Is an Idiom," suggests that it will introduce young children to the concept of idioms, and indeed it does. It describes what an idiom is, gives many examples, and even investigates the idea that different languages have different idiomatic expressions.

Where it falls down, however, is in the "interesting and amusing" category. Perhaps second graders will be captivated by the borderline cruel Patrick and his hapless victim Richard, but that may be too much to hope for. The third main character, Sophie, a transfer student from France, is better, but only somewhat. The two boys run afoul of school rules and are required to do a presentation about good manners. If this does not sound particularly interesting or amusing to you, that is because it is not. I have a feeling kids will be bored with this book and set it aside about a third of the way through.

That said, it is easy to read, written on a very basic reading level. I cannot in good faith recommend it, but if you decide to get it, it will teach your child about idioms. There are, however, far better ways to accomplish this.
I

text:
"The Year She Left Us" by Kathryn Ma is the story of a multi-generational Chinese American family told from multiple points of view. At the center of the story is the youngest member, Ari (short for Ariadne), a very young adult who is trying to come to terms with having been abandoned as an infant on the steps of a Chinese department store and adopted by a single parent, Charlie (short for Charlotte), a Chinese American public defender with more than a touch of social worker in her. The other major narrative voices are those of Charlie's sister, Les (short for Lesley), a lawyer/judge, and their mother (and Ari's grandmother), Gran. Each of these women is complex and interesting, each strong in her own ways.

As the book opens, Ari has graduated from high school and is on her way to her grandmother's alma mater, Bryn Mawr. She decides to visit China with her closest friend, A.J., who is also a Chinese adoptee, to see the orphanage she was adopted from and to determine whether her birth parents ever regretted their decision to give her up and tried to retrieve her. The reader realizes that this is futile, but it is important to Ari, who is suffering from many of the issues that beset adopted children, especially those of abandonment and the fantasy of "what might have been." In China, Ari suffers what can only be called a breakdown and does something irrevocable to herself, a highly symbolic act that I will not reveal.

Meanwhile, at home in San Francisco, Charlie is living in major denial that anything is wrong with Ari. She wants her to come home in time to get ready for college; she has no clue as to Ari's true state of mind. Charlie has a secret of her own, one that is not revealed until midway through the book, but it has a major impact on all the characters. Les's importance as a character increases as the book proceeds; despite being sisters, Les and Charlie are very different personalities with diametrically opposed approaches to life. Gran has the strongest personality of all. She and her sister left China after World War II, leaving their entire family behind to deal with the horrors of the Communist regime. Gran has more secrets than anyone else, and some are revealed only in the final chapters of the book when she herself returns to China for a visit.

Most reviewers would classify this book as a coming of age story, and indeed it is, but it is also a family saga, a cautionary tale about the corrosive power of the past and its secrets, and the dangers of not dealing with reality. Frustrating as all of these characters are, they are also endearing; the reader cannot help but feel affection and sympathy for each one of them. The writing is superb; if I have any issues, it would be with the pacing of the book about a third of the way through, when it seems just a bit sluggish. The second half, however, is riveting; it was hard to put the book down.

I know personally some young adult adoptees, and the issues Ari is dealing with seem authentic and well defined. I hope to share this book with my young adoptee friends, as well as with their parents and others who are trying to help them deal with their issues of abandonment and identity. I give this book my highest recommendation.

I ordered this book because my husband is a railroad enthusiast, and I knew that he would be able to help me review it honestly. In addition, the is a DK book, and for those unfamiliar with DK travel books, they are a delight. Although other books may provide more text, DK books offer a wealth of photos, maps, and drawings.

This book, "The Iron Road" by Christian Wolmar, has exceptional drawings, photos, maps, and graphics, but in addition, it provides a superb and detailed text. The book is divided into five sections, each dealing with an aspect of railroads around the world in chronological order. Many books have examined the engineering of railroads, but this one also deals with the human aspect of railroad construction, especially the intrigue and greed that form the backdrop of many railroads. Most readers, unless they are well acquainted with railroad history, are unaware of the human cost of rail expansion, not only in the United States but around the world. Wolmar recounts this human cost with compassion and insight, never over-dramatizing it, but making sure the reader does not dismiss it.

In Australia, for example, rather than standardize the track width, 22 track widths were used, a case study in how not to create a railroad network. Why did this happen? Every rail baron wanted to be first to get his piece of the railroad action. This greed precluded cooperation and set Australia's rail system back decades.

The transformative effect of railroads on society cannot be overestimated. In addition to travel, industry, and commerce, the impact of railroads on war, especially the American Civil War and in Europe, World War II, is covered in detail. The role of rail transport in the mass deportation of entire populations to prison and extermination camps in Germany, France, Poland, and China is recounted. Railroads in Asia and Russia are significant, too. This book reads more like an adventure story than a history; indeed, if history were taught this way in schools, many more of us would know it and learn from it.

I recommend this highly readable book to anyone who is looking for a good general history of railroads around the world. An expert will likely know most of what is in this book, but the general reader will not. This is a perfect book for the general reader.

text:
Count me among those who were immediately involved in E. Lockhart's "We Were Liars." Readers both young and old will be captivated by the characters and the superb writing, which combines everyday life with a hint of magical realism.

As other reviewers have noted, the plot revolves around the Sinclair family. When you imagine this family, think of a cross between the Kennedy clan and a Ralph Lauren print ad in an upscale magazine. This is an old money family, with more than its share of secrets, prejudices, rituals, and customs. The patriarch of the family owns an island off the coast of Massachusetts, to which his three daughters and their families come each summer for a golden idyll in a magical place. At least, this is the way it is supposed to be, but that is a lie, and it is just the beginning of lies.

Now think of King Lear, and you will have an idea of the sort of father this patriarch is. The daughters are not exact parallels to Lear's daughters, but there are enough resemblances to keep the reader interested. The focus of the book is on the next generation, the children of the three daughters (and their friend, a contrast both in culture and social status).

I hope no one reviewing this book has revealed the plot in too much detail. You will have to trust me when I say you will not see the final plot twist coming, though when you re-read the book (as you will feel compelled to do), you will see all manner of foreshadowing and hints. This book is an examination of the lies we tell ourselves and each other to live harmoniously on the surface of our world, and the price those lies exact. I am recommending this book to all my reader friends, and I will be sharing it with my nieces, who I am confident will love it.

A final note on style: You will occasionally encounter a disconcerting narration that seems at first literal, but is in fact magical realism. For example, when Cady Sinclair Eastman, the 15-year-old narrator of the book, is recounting her parents' divorce, she describes her father packing his things into his Mercedes. Then you read this: "Then he pulled out a handgun and shot me in the chest. I was standing on the lawn and I fell. The bullet hole opened wide and my heart rolled out of my rib cage and down into a flower bed. Blood gushed rhythmically from my open wound,
then from my eyes,
my ears,
my mouth.
It tasted like salt and failure. The bright red shame of being unloved soaked the grass in front of our house, the bricks of the path, the steps to the porch. My heart spasmed among the peonies like a trout.
Mummy snapped. She said to get hold of myself."

My first thought upon reading this was that Cady's father had lost control and actually shot her. The mother's comment makes clear, however, that this passage is a poetic description of how devastated Cady was at her parents' separation. This sort of thing happens throughout the book, and I came to enjoy the poetic imagery. Just don't take these passages literally.

I hope you enjoy this book as much as I did. Highly recommended.

I was confident that National Geographic would do a stellar job in presenting a unique baby book, and I was not disappointed. The photography alone makes this book a treasure, in itself well worth the price; the accompanying quotations and poems are of highest quality as well. Everything you would expect to find in a traditional baby book, you will find here, but without the pastel colors and cute drawings. I loved this book and am looking for an opportunity to give it to parents of a new baby.

Though I think almost anyone would love this book, parents who enjoy writing and reflecting will appreciate it the most,. There are opportunities to reflect on the baby's future as well as on what was going on in the world when he or she was born, and to reflect on many other events in the baby's life, and what these events might mean. For example, one page has a space for parents to write about what they learned from their child. Anyone who has a child knows that they teach us much about life, love, and what really matters.

Parents should think of this book as a gift for their child, to be given when the child asks about his early years and may perhaps be starting a family of his or her own. Well filled out, it will be a testament to parental love, and who would not appreciate that? Highly recommended, without reservation.

text: I absolutely loved this picture book.  With starkly lovely illustrations and a minimum of words, the author takes us through the first year in the life of a baby robin, and by analogy, the growing up and launching of a child.  As I "read" this book, I marveled at the ways a parent or teacher could engage a child in its various aspects.  For example, in the depiction of the parent birds feeding the chick, you could talk to your child about how human parents feed their babies, what foods your child used to like/hate, and how gross it would be if human mommies fed their babies as birds do.  In another picture, the baby is learning to fly, and a cat lies in wait.  Not to fear, however:  the parent birds are there to protect their chick.  I see this as a gentle way to get across the idea that parents always want to protect their children and keep them safe, and are aware of dangers that the child may be oblivious to.  Even the endpapers of the book, huge robin's eggs, could be used for a little elementary math.  I also loved the way the seasons were portrayed in the book, with lovely illustrations that invite the reader to look at nature in a fresh way.  It seems to me that this is a book that invites a parent and child to engage with one another on so many levels.  Mostly, this book is about family bonding and love.  I will be giving this book at the next baby shower or christening I go to, and I give it my highest recommendation.
product: Professional Trade Compliance Certification Study Guide

text: In a recent purge of our bookshelves, my husband, a retired math teacher, donated his copy of this book to our local library.  Of course, six months later he wanted it back, and we were fortunate to find this copy on Amazon.  It has no bells and whistles, no colorful pictures or cutesy approaches, just clear, direct explanations of all aspects of analytic geometry.  He is tutoring students in high school geometry, and although much of the material in this text is beyond the scope of most high school curricula, he is finding it helpful to review some of the principles in this book.  This text is a classic, and it is particularly good in its approach to matrices and trigonometric functions.  It emphasizes the use of matrices in solving equations.  Though this text was written nearly sixty years ago, it is still relevant.  The examples for each concept are clear and the practice problems are excellent, and answers are given for the odd numbered problems.  The section on solid analytic geometry is extensive.  I highly recommend this text to anyone desiring a thorough understanding of analytic geometry.
product: Professional Trade Compliance Certification Study Guide

text:
"No Book but the World" is a a very thoughtful, philosophical novel that is on its surface a family saga/crime story, but is in reality a meditation on the limits of human freedom and the existence of free will. The main characters, all adults, are brother and sister Ava and Fred, and their childhood friends Kitty and Dennis (who by the time of the novel is married to Ava). The principal narrator is Ava, who has always felt a combination of responsibility and resentment for Fred, who is impaired in some way that is never fully defined. Ava and Fred's parents were followers of the philosophy of Jean Jacques Rousseau, especially with respect to his views on education. They founded a free school based on Rousseau's principles, and Ava and Fred were brought up there in relative isolation from the real world. This is important because of the interplay of imagination, freedom, and reality that is a constant motif in the book. Kitty and Dennis are brought up much more conventionally, but they remain close friends.

As the book opens, Ava is trying to find out what is going on with her brother, who has been accused of a murder. There are long sequences of memory and description, and if the reader is looking for a fast-paced, plot-driven narrative, he or she will be very frustrated. There is little dialogue, and what dialogue exists is only a springboard to more memories and philosophical musings. The book is divided into five sections, with Fred, Kitty, and Dennis each narrating one, and Ava bookending them with the first and last. The reader learns about the unusual relationship between the parents and the children, and all the linkages between the past and present. The events of the present, with all their legal ramifications, are also explored.

The theme of this book is most aptly summed up in the frontpiece, a quotation from "Promenade" by Maria Irene Fornes: "I know everything. Half of it I really know. The rest I make up. The rest I make up." This quotation gets to the heart of the book: What do we really know? How accurate are our memories? How do we frame the story of our life so that it makes sense? How much of what we think we know is really wishful thinking or imagination? All of these questions are explored in great detail through the narrative.

This book is not for everyone. It is extraordinarily well-written, but the long ruminative passages will be off-putting to some. However, if you know what to expect and you are interested in a reflection on memory, imagination, and freedom set within an unusual family, you will find this book ultimately rewarding.

"Doing Harm" is Kelly Parsons first novel, and it is the best novel I have read since "Defending Jacob."

Steve Mitchell is a chief resident in surgery and is the supervisor of a group of medical students at Boston University Hospital, a teaching hospital associated with BU. Steve, who is in charge of making these students into doctors, is married to Sally, a stay-at-home mom who used to be a high-ranking assistant to the head of Human Resources at the hospital, where the two met.

Sally and Steve go to Dr. Collier's annual cocktail party. Collier is Steve's boss and chairman of Steve's department. Sally wants Steve to become a member of the faculty of the medical school, which would be an enormous advancement for him. She hints at this to Dr. Collier, with whom Steve is scheduled to meet in the next week. At the meeting, it seems that Steve will get his promotion, but then things become a little crazy at the hospital.

Two of Steve's new students are Luis, a very serious and conscientious student, and Gigi (which stands for Golden Girl), a very bright student who is perhaps a little too smart for her own good. After Steve is promised the promotion, very bad things begin to happen. Medicine is not given in the proper dosage, patients go into comas, and some do not ever come out. All of these events are blamed on Steve and his students, and soon, even Steve's favorite mentor will not allow Steve near him in the operating room.

Does Steve get to the bottom of these mysterious events? Is he destroyed mentally and professionally in the process? Read this superb novel and find out.

This is a very well written novel. The characters were very realistic, and it is easy to see that the author has first-hand experience in a hospital. If I could, I would give this novel ten stars.

text:
This is the latest of several "Teach Yourself Visually" books that my husband and I have ordered to help us use our electronic devices more fully. Like the others, "iPhoto for iPad" does not disappoint. In my opinion, these books are all you will ever need to help you use your devices more effectively.

First of all, the book assumes nothing more than that you have an iPad and the you want to work with your photos on it. The chapters are made to order for those of us for whom technology is decidedly not "second nature." They begin at the most elementary level imaginable--looking at your photos and experimenting with different ways of viewing them. From there, you learn how to import your photos and use the Cloud. There is a great chapter on organizing your pictures into albums and editing your photos with special effects. You learn to "photoshop" your pictures and use color and filters. There is a chapter on creating advanced photo effects that I am not ready for just yet, but I look forward to learning about sharing photos and making journals of our travels. We do not have an iPhone, but if you do, you will enjoy the chapter on iPhoto and the iPhone. It is important to note that you do not have to cover all these areas if you are not interested. The book is set up in such as way that browsing and selective learning are easy.

Second, I love that this book has photographs of exactly what you see on your own screen. Not diagrams or sketches, but real photographs. These photos accompany easy-to-follow, step-by-step instructions. They take nothing for granted, which I appreciate a great deal.

Third, I like the use of white space. The pages of this book are not cluttered up with "stuff" on every inch of space.

Finally, I really enjoy the sidebar "Question and Answer" sections that accompany almost every step, and I found the index complete and easy to use.

In short, if you are mystified about how to integrate your photos with your iPad (or even your laptop computer), this is the book for you. I cannot imagine that it would not be helpful to just about everyone. It has my highest recommendation.

I ordered this book because my husband and I are thinking about setting up a living trust, and I was interested in seeing whether or not we could do this without a lawyer. This book is extremely clear and well-written; it does not assume any legal knowledge other than general information, so it is easily accessible to the lay person. It seems to me that this book covers every conceivable question one could have about trusts, from basic questions like why a trust is a good idea and why people set them up, to complicated questions about selling property that is in a trust, revoking a trust, or leaving property to minor children. Each chapter has many concrete real-life examples of each major point the author is making. I especially liked this aspect of the book as it broke up the expository parts with clarifying examples. I was also impressed that with the purchase of this book, you can go online and download, free, any legal documents you may need in order to set up your trust. You also have access to the NOLO website, where you can find out about any changes in the laws regarding trusts.

Now, the ultimate question: Would I set up my own trust after reading this book? To be honest, I am not sure that I would, but that is more about the sort of cautious person I am than about the quality of this book. In my opinion, even if you do end up going to a lawyer to set up your trust, if you read this book you will be far more informed that you would be without having read it. You will be a better consumer, and you will understand far more of what your lawyer is telling you. I have learned so much about trusts from this book, even though I have not read every single chapter. I do recommend this book to any lay person who is interested in setting up a trust. I do not think you will ever regret your purchase.
product: Professional Trade Compliance Certification Study Guide

text:
I have never, even in my wildest dreams, imagined myself a member of the preppy group. So you can imagine my surprise to find that I was already familiar with many of the recipes in "The Preppy Cookbook," and that a few of them were identical to those my mother used to make when we were kids. I ordered this cookbook for review because I am a "reader" of cookbooks, not one who consults them only for recipes, and I knew that this one would fit that category. I was not disappointed. The interesting and occasionally tongue-in-cheek commentary that accompanies each recipe is fun to read.

"The Preppy Cookbook" is divided into several sections: The Formative Years (comfort food you will likely recall from childhood); Summer & the Living is Easy, The Sporting Life (snacks and drinks for after golf, etc.), The Grand Tour (recipes from other countries), Brunch as a Verb, The Cocktail Party (appetizers and more drinks), Luncheons and Showers, and Home for the Holidays. Each section has a lighthearted introduction to the sort of recipes you will find within, and the photos are good, though they are not in color, and not every recipe is photographed. The cookbook ends with a section devoted to stocking your kitchen and pantry with staples that will be useful in creating these recipes.

I liked this cookbook more than I thought I would. The recipes are, for the most part, very easy to put together and do not use exotic ingredients. I do question some of the directions; for example, for the recipe for flank steak, I recommend marinating your steak for at least eight hours before you grill it. And some recipes are simply traditional favorites given new names--for example, the ubiquitous cheesy potato casserole is called "Hangover Hash Browns." There is something for everyone in this cookbook. Even people who like to can and preserve food will find the "Branston Pickle" recipe intriguing. And for you traditionalists from the mid 20th century, the recipe for creamed chipped beef is identical to my mom's, though she often served it on English muffins or toast.

This is not an all-purpose cookbook like Mark Bittman's "How to Cook Anything," but it has its place. A college student or young person just starting to cook could do much worse than this book; its simple and easy-to-follow recipes would be a godsend to a new cook or frazzled mom. I enjoyed it a great deal, and am happy to recommend it.

text:
I write here as one who has never read a book by Allison Leotta, but "Speak of the Devil" has made me want to read everything she has written. The main character in "Speak of the Devil" is Anna Curtis, a sex crimes prosecutor in Washington DC, who has just become engaged to Jack Bailey, chief of the DC homicide unit. Jack has a six-year-old daughter, Olivia, and his late wife, Nina Flores, had been killed four years earlier by one of the street gangs of Washington DC. Most investigators believe that that the crime was committed by MS-13, a notoriously violent and ruthless DC gang. The leader of this gang is Diablo, accurately named "the Devil." Diablo's skin is completely covered in tattooed hieroglyphs. His nose is two sunken holes; he has long black hair and two fleshy horns protruding from his forehead. Indeed, he is aptly named.

Diablo and his gang appear throughout the book as Anna Curtis and Jack Flores attempt to solve various gruesome crimes occurring in the DC area. There are many twists and turns, and a huge red herring that comes as a total surprise to the reader in the last third of the book. I really enjoyed this book and recommend it to anyone who likes true-life crime fiction. The author's experience as a sex-crimes prosecutor for 12 years in Washington DC is evident on every page, and she is a superb writer. If I could give the book more than five stars, I would. Highly recommended.

text:
"Chocolate for Two" is the fourth book in the Waverly Bryson series by Maria Mumane. I have read only this one, so take that into consideration when assessing my review.

As tis book opens, our heroine, Waverly, Is recovering from having been almost literally left at the altar by a cad who appeared on her doorstep two weeks before the wedding and proclaimed he did not love her. A popular young woman with many love interests, Waverly is a well regarded talk show host who also owns her own very successful business, Waverly's Honey Shop. Despite all this, her self esteem is shaky until she meets Jake McIntyre, a wealthy scion of an upper class family.

Naturally, they fall in love. We seem to be headed for a story book ending, but wait--we need some drama. Enter Jake's parents, specifically his mother, who is controlling and insufferable, in the nicest possible way (ahem). She hopes to take control of the wedding and make it a huge expensive affair in Florida. Waverly and Jake, meanwhile, want a small intimate wedding in New York. Jake is besotted with Waverly and pretty much defers to her on every issue, but at the same time he does not help much in dealing with his overbearing mother.

The novel is a fun beach read, good for a rainy day on the porch. There is humor and emotion, as well as many sidebars with Waverly's career and business. Eventually, Waverly finds a retailer who somehow makes her see everything, both professional and personal, in perspective, and the ending is light and satisfying. You probably will not remember this book too long after you read it, but you will be entertained as you read, especially if you are a fan.

text:
Let me say at the outset that I am a great fan of Cook's Magazine, the magazine whose compiled articles compose this superb cookbook. For those unfamiliar with it, each issue of Cook's Magazine takes a few dishes and explores in excruciating detail how to make each one. For example, take beef stew. The magazine would examine every aspect of several recipes, from cuts of beef to cooking time and temperature, to when to add the vegetables, to how and why to brown the beef, and on and on. After experimenting with several, or even dozens of recipes, the magazine gives the "ultimate" beef stew recipe, one that will produce perfect results if followed exactly, even for the novice cook. Along the way, you get a lesson (or several) in the science of food, why some things work and others do not.

This massive cookbook (486 pages) is excellent, but it was a little overwhelming, even for me. I love reading about food and learning what works and why. And I have never tried a Cook's recipe that failed. However, I can see that this cookbook is not for everyone. If you are looking for recipes with 5 ingredients or less, or that use a lot of processed foods, you will be disappointed.

So why five stars? Because this cookbook does a superb job of what it sets out to do. The reader learns the techniques of food preparation, the science (with accompanying diagrams) of why some things work and others do not, and in the end gets a great recipe for whatever it is he or she wants to make. The book covers all sorts of food, from bread and muffins, to meats and poultry, to vegetables, to eggs and cheese, to desserts--be sure to try the recipe for Perfect New York Cheesecake--the ultimate best.

I recommend this book for people who enjoy the "why" of food preparation, not merely the "how." It will take time to go through the article on even one recipe, but you will be rewarded with not only a great recipe, but with greater confidence in your cooking skills.

text:
I must confess at the outset that I am a big fan of Randy Cohen, and I looked forward to his "Ethicist" columns each Sunday in the New York Times Magazine. When he left a couple of years ago, I missed him, even though I thought his successors did a good job. And I like the current Ethicist, Chuck Klosterman, very much. Still, I was happy to see a book of Cohen's columns compiled and categorized.

The columns are loosely grouped into one of several categories. Some of these are Family, Home, Money, Civic Life, Animals, Sports, Work, School, Arts, Love and Sex, Religion, and Community. What I like especially about these ethical dilemmas, and Cohen's comments on them, is that they make the reader think, and in some cases, vehemently disagree. Most of them would make interesting (if not always convivial) dinner conversation. I almost never go away from a response thinking, "Well, obviously!" So much of what we deal with in modern life is not obvious, and the columns Cohen has selected for this book reflect that in spades.

This book goes beyond a simple compilation of columns, however. First, each section is prefaced by a short essay on the topic in general, highlighting both the topic itself and the author's views in general. Second, a few of Cohen's columns generated much response, for example, a column about whether an IT worker is ethically required to report the presence of child pornography on his boss's computer. In these cases, Cohen describes and quotes extensively from the response(s), which are usually objections to what he has written. He evaluates the writer's point of view, sometimes agreeing at least in part, but more often clarifying his own thinking. In reading these, the reader gets a short course, not just in ethics, but in critical thinking.

I enjoyed this book a great deal, and I recommend it to anyone interested in thinking about everyday ethics in today's world.

text:
My husband and I purchased an iPad about a year ago, and it has become one of our favorite electronics ever. To help us become acquainted with all that it had to offer, we bought iPad for Dummies for Seniors, which was very helpful. When iPad for Older and Wiser became available through Amazon Vine, I decided to order it to compare its helpfulness with the Dummies book.

iPad for Older and Wiser is excellent, even better than the Dummies manual. It has extremely clear, step-by-step instructions and numerous helpful illustrations. The summaries and quizzes at the end of each chapter were a good way for the reader to see if he or she had gotten the main points, and if you did not, you could go back to review. It is also an excellent reference for each chapter; a sort of mini-index. The format is appealing, with a lot of subtle color and well-designed paragraphs and bullet points. It is wonderful to have this reference handy when I use my iPad and explore all its capabilities.

I recommend this manual to everyone, not just seniors. Unless you are supremely computer-literate, it will help you with your iPad more than any other reference I have found. I am looking forward to reading other books in this series. Five stars and more!

"Mister Death's Blue-eyed Girls" by Mary Downing Hahn is the somewhat-fictionalized story of a true event, the murder of two teen-aged girls on the last day of school in 1956. In an afterword, the author tells of her distant relationship with these girls and the impact their deaths had on her, her friends, and the entire community. She says that she has been trying to write this book for fifty years, and in my opinion, the resulting novel was well worth the wait.

The story is told from the points of view of various narrators, the main one being Nora, alter-ego of the author. Other narrators include Nora's best friend, Ellie; the boyfriend of one of the victims, Buddy; the murderer himself, Mister Death; and Mister Death's younger brother. There are also diary entries of several of the teenagers, which serve to fill in important background details. This multiple narrator device is useful for a crime novel in which only the murderer knows "who done it"; the terror and confusion experienced by the victims' friends result from the killer never being identified.

The novel is set in the fifties in Edgewood, Maryland, a suburb of Baltimore. The cultural references are perfect: the music, the clothing, the hairstyles, the drinking and smoking in the park, the pre-Vatican II atmosphere in the Catholic Church, the suffocating small-town ambiance, the rigid class divisions among the townspeople and the teenagers as well--all pitch perfect. The murders happen on the last day of school, and they abruptly force Nora and Ellie to realize that bad things can happen to your friends, not just to people on the news that you don't know. One major result of the crime is a crisis of faith in Nora. As she tries to understand how God could let such a thing happen to innocent girls, she is offered only platitudes by the priests of her church. She decides that she cannot believe in a God who can stand by and allow evil to happen.

At the same time, she is exploring her own literary interests, and this adds an important dimension to the book. She begins to read real literature, not the True Confession magazines she passed time reading before the tragedy. Poetry by Wordsworth, Walt Whitman, and T. S. Eliot; French novels, and modern American classics are all referenced, and they reinforce Nora's growing conclusion that the God she was taught about in church does not exist; that when we die, we become part of the earth forever. At one point, she describes herself as a pantheist. I found this part of the book very affecting; most people go through a crisis of faith at one point or another, and this one is well handled.

One other aspect of the book that will resonate with readers is the reaction of the townspeople to the murders and their desire to find and punish the killer. When Buddy is arrested, he is manhandled by the police, who release him after 48 hours because there is no evidence that he committed the crime. But none (or almost none) of the townspeople let a lack of evidence stand in the way of their judgment: they "know" that he did it, no matter what the police say. Buddy's life is made a living hell, and only Nora and one other minor character take his part. Of course, there are no Miranda warnings, and the rush to judgment prevents the police from finding the true killer. Readers may recall Columbine or other school-related shooting of recent years. This aspect is a good cautionary tale about how wrong we can be, even when we are sure we are right.

I give this book my highest recommendation. It is aimed at a teen audience, but I think adults will appreciate it as well, especially those of us who recall the fifties and are happy we have moved on as a society in so many ways. Readers will like Nora, an imperfect but appealing heroine, as she struggles with the unthinkable. Five stars.

"The Professionals" by Owen Laukkanan will appeal to many who enjoy the suspense and crime genres. The novel is almost exclusively plot-driven, with little or no atmosphere, characterization, or psychological insight. However, plot drives many novels of this kind, and they are wildly popular. I predict that this novel will find a wide audience, too.

The story begins as four college friends, unable to find well-paying jobs, turn to low-payout, high volume kidnappings of wealthy businessmen. The kidnappings are solely about money; no violence is ever involved. For two years, they move from "job" to "job' until one day they kidnap a repellent man with mob connections through his wife. For the first time, their carefully planned routine goes awry. At the same time, the law, represented by State Investigator Kirk Stevens and FBI Agent Carla Windermere, is taking an interest in this kidnapping and others that are at first seemingly unrelated. The bulk of the book concerns the efforts of the four young people to evade both the law and the mob.

This novel is easy to read and moves along quickly. At 370 pages, it has 93 chapters, many only two or three pages long. I would classify this book as escapist reading. There are many implausible situations that will leave readers wondering how this or anything even remotely like it could have happened in the real world. The book will not satisfy fans of authors like P. D. James, but James Patterson fans will be happy for another writer to fill their bookshelves. The author's note suggests that the he plans more books featuring Stevens and Windermere, so perhaps their characters will be more developed in later books. They are likable people--maybe a made-for-TV movie is in the offing?

text:
"Emory's Gift" is the story of a 13-year-old boy, Charlie Hall, whose much beloved mother has recently died and who is trying to cope with all the trials of early adolescence, and then some. His father is well-meaning but emotionally absent. His friends desert him when he needs them most, at the start of junior high school. He is small for his age, ungifted athletically, very bright, and awkward with girls--a perfect storm of teenage angst.

Into all this comes Emory, a huge grizzly bear, who is more than he seems to be at first glance. To tell more would be to give away important parts of the story; suffice it to say that Emory becomes a touchstone for Charlie and a litmus test for every human character in the book. If Charlie is the "hero" who must endure trials to find his destiny, Emory is the "animal/helper" whose guidance is essential. Charlie endures all tests and emerges having found a path through junior high school and into adult life.

While the target audience for this book is probably young adult, any animal lover will appreciate it. Well-written and insightful about human nature, it contains some spectacular descriptions of nature, but not so much that a reader would become bored. The author captures perfectly the emotions of a young teenage boy, and teens of either sex will be able to relate to Charlie. Adults will appreciate all over again that they got through this time of life, and will, it is hoped, look upon young teens with more empathy.

I am positively blown away by "The Grief of Others" by Leah Hager Cohen. It has a depth rarely seen in "family in crisis" fiction, and I was impressed from the first page.

The book tells of a year in the life of the Ryrie family, with one extended flashback to eight years previous. The point of view alternates among the family members: Ricky, the mom; John, the dad; Jess, John's young adult daughter by a brief encounter in his college years; Paul, the adolescent son; and Elizabeth (Biscuit), the 11-year-old daughter. Another important character is Gordie, a young man whose father has just died and who is involved at the periphery of the family.

Although many strains are present in this family, the precipitating crisis is Ricky's third pregnancy. In her fifth month, she discovers that she is carrying an anencephalic child who, though he may be born alive, has no chance of survival. Told that most women in her situation choose abortion, and afraid that John will pressure her to do just that, Ricky tells no one about the baby's prognosis, even after he is born and dies 57 hours later. This deception is not revealed for a year, and its consequences form the substance of the book. Each family member suffers in his or her own way as a result, and each expresses grief and anger in different ways. Even before the truth comes out, each family member knows that something is wrong, but no one, even Ricky, knows exactly what.

Two aspects of this book are especially worthy of note. First, the novel is psychologically astute. Each character is fully developed and believable. Thoughts, attitudes, actions--all are authentic. The book has a psychological depth that makes most other characters seem flat. The reader sees each one, even the children, as whole and hurting human beings.

Second, the writing is simply wonderful. I was especially taken with the author's use of metaphor. Each figure of speech was fresh and arresting in its impact. There was not a hackneyed phrase in the entire book.

If you enjoy fiction dealing with families in crisis, you will love and appreciate this book. It is head and shoulders (now THAT is a hackneyed metaphor!) above most other fiction of its type. I recommend this book without reservation.

I hate to give books I review three stars--it seems so wishy-washy. But "This Beautiful Life" by Helen Schulman has forced me to do just that. The cover art for the book is inspired--the "beautiful life" that the Bergamots have worked so hard for is a house of cards, one that comes tumbling down at the first storm.

I will begin with the positives. Schulman has written a devastatingly accurate portrayal of the nouveau riche of New York City. Their obsession with money and status is well drawn, from the boss who deserts Richard Bergamot at the first sign of trouble, to the shallow and gossipy "friends" who do the same to his wife, Lizzy. The reader cannot but despise these people, and one would think that he or she would find him or herself automatically in sympathy with Richard, Lizzy, and their teenaged son, Jake.

However, one would be wrong. Though I wanted to like these characters, I could not. The parents leave a perfectly wonderful upper middle class life in Ithaca, New York, where each has a satisfying career at Cornell University, for the seductive bright lights of New York City, where Richard has been offered a dream job at a university that sounds a lot like Columbia. Lizzy gives up her career to manage her children's lives, "the deal," as Richard characterizes it, which ultimately leads her to frustration and substance abuse. The son, Jake, is typical of his age (15), but not all that likable either. Five-year-old Coco Bergamot, adopted from China, is by far the most attractive member of the family, but the reader wonders how long her irrepressible spirit can last in this land of spiritually dead or dying people.

The book is a fast read, and I think it will find a wide readership, since the central incident, sexting, is much in the news today. There are glimmers of excellent writing, but the overall impression is just appalling. I found the book's end ambiguous, filled with regret over opportunities lost. The book is a cautionary tale of what happens to people who make money their god. They are sorry in the end, but it is too late.

text:
Just in time for beach season comes Sarah Bird's "The Gap Year," a novel tracing the relationship between a mother and daughter during the latter's final year of high school. All the characters in this book are likable: Cam, the young late-boomer mom, and Dori, her aging hippie friend; Aubrey, the uncertain and insecure daughter; Tyler, the new jock boyfriend; Twyla, Dori's daughter and Aubrey's ex-best childhood friend who appears only in memory until well into the last half of the novel; and even Martin, the absent-for-sixteen years dad, whose appearance (coupled with first romance with Tyler) changes the trajectory of Aubrey's life.

The novel shifts in point in view between Cam and Aubrey, and in time between the days just before Aubrey turns 18, and the previous year, at the start of Aubrey's senior year. I found some of the adult dialogue, especially at the beginning, to be somewhat strained, as if the adults were trying too hard to be cool. Aubrey's voice, on the other hand, was perfect, and the author shows a real understanding of late adolescence. I liked the shifts in point of view, though I know from book club discussions that this structure bothers some readers.

The title is superbly chosen, having multiple interpretations. Of course, the gap year is the year that some 18-year-olds take off between high school and college to decide just what direction they wish to pursue. But other gaps are every bit as important: the gap between Cam and Aubrey, between Aubrey and her father, between Cam's expectations for her daughter and Aubrey's own tentative wish for a different way, between the life Cam wanted and the one she actually has--the list goes on.

Mostly, the novel explores the gap between Cam and Aubrey, in a realistic but also good-humored way. Recalling her own conflicted feelings about her mother, Cam tries hard to be a different kind of mom to Aubrey. Inevitably, the mom we need is not always the mom we get, and our efforts to avoid the mistakes of the past create new mistakes--the law of unintended consequences. Still, this novel is not a tragedy. Things do not turn out as anyone expects, but in the end, life is good.

The audience for "The Gap Year" will be almost exclusively women, especially women with teenage daughters who can identify with Cam. This book is not "chick-lit," however; you need not be embarrassed to be seen reading it. While it is not Jane Austen, it is perfect for a summer afternoon on the porch.

I am reading this book for a book club, and if it were not for the upcoming discussion, I would not have read it.  I found the premise--historical fiction about the crusades, interesting, and I thought a woman as lead character would be a welcome change.  However, the writing is repetitive and the pacing of the novel is slow.  A good editor could have cut about 200 of its 500+ pages without sacrificing anything essential.  Something else that would have helped is a comprehensive list of characters and a brief description of each, and a set of maps of places mentioned in the novel.  Especially at the beginning, I found myself in need of both.  In short, this novel was a tough slog, and I recommend it only for fans of Anne Perry's writing.  We have two of those in our club, and they liked the book very much.

This is a good workbook for preschool age children. It requires some adult help to read the instructions. My preference would be for a workbook that has a page or 2 for each day that has both letters, numbers, shapes... all on one page so you don’t have to flip through the book to find different subjects. My almost 5 yr old really enjoys doing it though and likes putting a sticker on each page when she finishes.

The book, by Jack Kelly, describes in great detail how the railroad boom during the Gilded Age, featuring George Pullman, crashed in 1893, with the ensuing labor crisis and depression, resulting in an almost nationwide labor strike, headed by union leader, Eugene Debs. Besides these two men representing unregulated capitalism and the wealth it created for some versus jobs for everyday working people, was President Grover Cleveland who had to contend with possible anarchy.

The book brings relevance to the US economy now, with great income inequality and the strain it’s putting on the working class.

I recommend the book, with a caveat. The book was more detailed than I wanted, so I did a lot of skimming, but the detail does seem valuable for those wanting it.
