text:
If you’ve mastered pie crust, then pie really is “easy as pie”. This book, THE PERFECT PIE: YOUR ULTIMATE GUIDE TO CLASSIC AND MODERN PIES, TARTS, GALLETTES, AND MORE will guide you to pie crust mastery--or you can take the easy way out (as I do) and use commercial pie crust from your grocery store’s cold case (e.g., [[ASIN:B003D1Z2E2 Pillsbury Pie Crusts]]). Whichever approach you use, this book will provide lots of different--but fairly complicated--fillings for you.

This is actually a technical cookbook for moderately advanced pie-makers, rather than a book for cooks who just want to bake pies that will please the family. I really like the explanations of “Why This Recipe Works” that appear at the beginning of many recipes. For example, the book explains that the filling in a “Skillet Apple Pie” (a recipe for a top-crust-only “pie” that begins on the stovetop and finishes in the oven) can be saucier than the filling in a double-crust pie because you don’t have to worry about a bottom crust’s becoming soggy.

I’m NOT a fan of the “Classic” pie recipes, which add steps or non-classic ingredients to basic apple, blueberry, and pumpkin pies. For example, apple pie uses Golden Delicious (horrors!) apples combined with Granny Smith (just okay) apples; and cooks and cools the filling before adding it to the crust. (My Midwestern mother’s apple pie is hard to beat--it’s made with Jonathan, MacIntosh, Gravenstein, or other firm, tart-sweet apples that are tossed with cinnamon and sugar, then added to a double crust, and baked.)

The “Classic” blueberry pie recipe adds a Granny Smith apple; the pumpkin pie recipe adds canned sweet potatoes and maple syrup, and even prebakes the crust. These recipes aren’t really classics, and I question whether they’re worth the extra trouble.

Sadly, there is no recipe at all for plain peach pie, which is one of the world’s great pleasures. Peaches are used in tarts and “Peach Slab Pie”. The “Why This Recipe Works” section for “Peach Slab Pie” says that double-crust peach pies need precooked fruit and multiple thickeners “to prevent a soupy mess”, and this has NOT been true in my experience. Simple peach pie recipes may come out a little soupy, but they’re delicious and definitely NOT a mess.

Of particular value are the many recipes for custard, cream, and curd pies (e.g., banana cream, lemon meringue, black bottom). The section on regional pies (e.g., “New England Mince Pie”, “Southern Praline Pecan Pie”) is also interesting. However, after reading the fruit pie recipes, I wonder whether these recipes will also be overly complicated for family cooks.

Like most specialty pie-making books, this book shows you how to create a variety of crust-edge designs (braided, crimped, cutout, fork-crimped, rope, scalloped-spoon), and top-crust designs (lattice, cutout, braided strips, free-form lattice, free-form shapes, herringbone).

A more unusual feature is the entire section on “Pie and Tart Doughs”. This includes recipes for chocolate, herb, nut, vegan, gluten-free, cookie-crust, and other pie doughs.

The book has many, many beautiful illustrations of finished pies--all of coffee-table-book quality. Many procedures (e.g., removing the seeds from vanilla bean pods, making and rolling slab pie dough) are clearly explained with step-by-step photos. The book also includes a few pages on troubleshooting pie problems (e.g., crust edges brown too quickly), and it is well-indexed.

This book will make a nice gift for a dedicated pie maker or cookbook collector. However, it won’t replace my go-to family-pie-making books, [[ASIN:1118432169 Betty Crocker’s Big Book of Pies and Tarts]], and [[ASIN:0470485531 Pillsbury’s Easy As Pie]].
product: Professional Trade Compliance Certification Study Guide

text:
Like so many independently-authored LEGO building books, HOW TO BUILD BRICK TV AND MOVIE CARS: DETAILED LEGO DESIGNS has beautiful, easy-to-follow building instructions. The book is a delight to page through, if you’re a LEGO fan--especially if you love building LEGO vehicles.

However, to actually build any of the models, you will have to buy an assortment of LEGO elements in the colors specified--and buying individual elements can be an expensive proposition. (There are many online sources of LEGO components in addition to the official online LEGO Store. You’ll have to order your components online, because LEGO retail stores don’t stock a full line of even the currently available elements.)

The author has included an Appendix that lists the build components for each model. The models are: Pontiac Firebird Trans Am (Knight Rider); Ferrari 250 GT SWB California Spider (Ferris Bueller’s Day Off); Ferrari 365 GTS/4 Spyder (Miami Vice); Mini Cooper S (The Italian Job); Volkswagen Beetle (The Love Bug); Ford Mustang (Bullitt); Toyota AE86 Trueno (Initial D); Ferrari 308 GTS (Magnum P.I.); Lotus Esprit (The Spy Who Loved Me); Ford 1932 Coupe Hot Rod (American Graffiti); Ford Falcon V8 Interceptor (Mad Max); and Jaguar E-Type “Shaguar” (Goldmember).

The first three models are presented in minifig scale (1:28); the remainder are designed in the larger Miniland scale (1:20). All of the models are easily identifiable as the cars seen in the movies.

The book has value for LEGO vehicle builders, even if they don’t choose to build the specific models. The instructions are replete with special building techniques and ideas for imitating specific automobile parts.

text:
MARYELLEN: TAKING OFF is the second book written for the American Girl [[ASIN:B01IAQ320U Maryellen Larkin doll]]. Maryellen is one of the historical character dolls (year 1954), so the book is about activities that a 10-year-old girl might have enjoyed in 1954. The first series book, [[ASIN:1683371364 Maryellen: The One and Only]], introduces the character, and is included with a purchase of the doll; it can also be purchased separately.

As usual, the “Taking Off” book illustrations show Maryellen dressed in outfits that can be purchased for the doll (e.g., [[ASIN:B07BGBMF86 Maryellen’s Vacation Playsuit]], on the cover of this book). It doesn’t bother me that the AG doll books are such blatant marketing tools, because the stories are entertaining, and they teach kids about the highlighted historical period. The clothing designs are always accurate, too.

The books, including this one, are goody-two-shoes in the extreme, expressing family-and-friends-first values. Kids today may find them too treacly-sweet to want to read more than one. The nonfiction historical notes (see “Inside Maryellen’s World” at the back), and the scrapbook illustrations (e.g., a Davy Crockett hat in “Maryellen’s Scrapbook”, at the front) are interesting.

The story in this book involves Maryellen’s camping trip, her birthday party planning, her search for a disappeared sister and dog, and her team leadership in building a balloon “flying machine” for a science contest.
product: HTS Code Reference Guide - Harmonized Tariff Schedule

text:
From the book jacket notes, you’d never guess that GALWAY GIRL: A JACK TAYLOR NOVEL is written in a quasi-Joycean style--with cryptic literary quotes separating chapters, frequently-changing stream-of-consciousness viewpoints, unexpected flashbacks that mix up the plot’s chronology, and many references to Irish culture and the Galway locale. There’s a mildly interesting story in there, but the reader must work to find it.

In the novel, a killer trio is out to get private investigator Jack Taylor--the three are Jericho, a psychotic “Galway girl”; Scott, the estranged son of a deceased Guard; and Stapleton (aka Stapes), a burglar. They have their reasons--Taylor killed Jericho’s lover, killed Stapes’ father, and witnessed Scott’s killing of a Guard. Jericho and Scott are evil, feeding off murder and destruction; Stapes mainly wants nice stuff and revenge. Taylor himself is his usual alcoholic-druggie mess, but is apparently worse than usual while recovering from witnessing the murder of his daughter four months earlier.

Much of the plot action involves Guards and civilians being unexpectedly shot in the face. Taylor is needed by his old Garda colleagues to solve the Guards murders, but they also want to bring him down. The climax consists of a particularly brutal and gruesome murder by falcon.

Most of the characters are returning from prior series novels, and from personal experience I can say that GALWAY GIRL does NOT work well as a standalone. (The series begins with [[ASIN:B003J48C1I The Guards]]). From reading reviews of some earlier series books, I gather that nothing good ever happens to, or around, PI Taylor, He just moves foggily along from experience to experience, generally hurting.

The dialogue relies heavily on the f-bomb (hardly noticeable after the first 10 or so uses); also on “Phew”, and the puzzling “Phew-oh”. The prose often morphs into quasi-poetic broken lines, into single words to a line, or into special “down, down, down” visual typesetting involving single words spaced across and down the page. This, I guess, is what constitutes the author’s “lyrical style” (as described by professional critics).

The novel’s genre is probably “literary detective fiction”. Had I known this, I wouldn’t have ordered the book. It isn’t all that hard to read, and the many short lines of type make it a short novel, but it’s neither decent literary fiction nor an exciting detective novel.
product: HTS Code Reference Guide - Harmonized Tariff Schedule

text:
WHEN HELL STRUCK TWELVE is the 14th entry in the “Billy Boyle” series. It’s not my favorite, and it has an annoying cliffhanger ending, but it’s an exciting, entertaining page-turner all the same.

The novel works less well than most of the others as a standalone, because Billy’s close friendship with Kaz often takes center stage. The series as a whole is working its way through the major events of the European Theater of Operations in WWII, starting with the impending invasion of Norway (Operation Jupiter), which is the subject of the first series novel, [[ASIN:1616953551 Billy Boyle]].

In this novel, Billy and Kaz barely escape alive from the Battle of the Falaise Gap (August 1944). On their way back to their “unit”, General Eisenhower’s SHAEF (Supreme Headquarters, Allied Expeditionary Force) at Saint-Gervais, they must literally drive over the tangled German corpses left behind after the conclusion of the horribly bloody Falaise massacre.

At SHAEF, Billy’s job is to interrogate some surviving German officers, and he extracts a bit of key intelligence. There is a traitor (code-named Atlantik), fighting with the French Bolsheviks (FTP), who hates Russia and the Communists so much that he is passing his comrades’ names to the Nazis in occupied France. This intel sets in motion an elaborate Allied deception (the author’s invention) that involves a fake map of the routes that the Allies’ tanks are planning to take to Paris.

In fact, the Allies aren’t going to Paris. When the map is stolen, and two people are murdered, Billy and Kaz know that the deception indeed worked to flush out Atlantik. It’s now their job to track and capture the killer. But when the Allies decide to liberate Paris after all, Billy and Kaz must cross behind German lines, disguised as Frenchmen, and retrieve the map at all costs.

As usual, real WWII events form the background, and Billy experiences many of them firsthand. For example, he encounters General Patton, as well as war correspondent Andy Rooney. But the story is sometimes hard to follow, because there are so many different French resistance factions for the reader to keep straight.

Even though I hate cliffhanger endings, I’m very much looking forward to Billy’s next outing. The Allies have a lot of fighting yet, before they reach VE Day (May 1945).
product: Professional Trade Compliance Certification Study Guide

text:
This ANSEL ADAMS 2020 WALL CALENDAR is the same as the annual Ansel Adams calendar sold in retail bookstores at year's end. I buy this calendar year after year, because of the large size. The wide calendar pages have extra-large boxes for each day, so there is more space than you will normally find on a wall calendar for recording all of my family’s medical appointments, upcoming meetings, activities, and other date-related reminders.

Each month's calendar page includes a mini-calendar for last month and next month, and indicates holidays, moon phases, and important items like daylight savings time changes. Each year’s calendar includes a full page for January of the following year. The black and white photographs by Ansel Adams are beautiful, and every year's selection is different.

I save each used calendar as a quick reference for that entire year's appointments and activities. The old calendars have come in handy many times!

I buy the calendar from Amazon as soon as it is offered (usually in July). That way, I can write in appointments for the upcoming year as soon as they are scheduled, even if they are scheduled beyond January (and the calendar’s bonus monthly page).

text:
Sadly, THAT OLD SCOUNDREL DEATH is the 26th and last of the “Sheriff Dan Rhodes” mysteries: the author passed away in February of 2018. This novel constituted my first meeting with the rural Texas sheriff, and I thoroughly enjoyed it. It’s sort of a cozy mystery, filled with oddball characters and low-key humor. The novel works fine as a standalone; but I’m definitely going back to the first series entry, [[ASIN:B00CWUJUB6 Too Late to Die]], to get started on the 25 earlier novels that I’ve missed.

In the novel, Sheriff Rhodes investigates the murder of a stranger (Cal Stinson? Bruce Wayne?) who is known to give false names to everyone he meets. The body is discovered in an abandoned school building that is the center of a local preserve-it or tear-it-down controversy. The chief suspects are members of three respected local families (Hunleys, Faulkners, Reeses) who are deeply involved in determining the school’s fate, along with a snake-tattooed, gun-toting ne’er-do-well (Kenny Lambert) who threatened to kill the victim for cutting him off in highway traffic.

Rhodes endures his deputies’ long-drawn-out incident reports (a man shot by an armadillo?), and his wife’s healthy cooking (usually something with kale). He gets case-solving help from his private investigator friend (Seepy) and a local busybody (Wanda), and finds good sources of lunchtime burgers (Dairy Queen, Manny Kingston’s grocery). He even finds out who is writing the scurrilous blog, “Digging That Blacklin County Dirt”.

The killer turns out to be a surprise, but the motive is solid, making for a satisfying ending. The characters are well developed, the writing is professional, and the never-flagging plot holds the reader’s interest.
product: HTS Code Reference Guide - Harmonized Tariff Schedule

text:
The best thing about this book, GENIUS LEGO INVENTIONS WITH BRICKS YOU ALREADY HAVE, isn’t that the toys and games use fairly common LEGO elements (including basic Technic gears, beams, and connectors). The best thing about it is the concise explanations of how and why the animated toys (various robots, an animated drummer, a construction crane, a merry-go-round, a basketball shooting game, and many more “inventions”) actually work.

For example, one explanation states: “Ryder the robot is constructed with a cam (a wheel with an off-center axle) driving a foot. If the feet were attached to the center of the gears, they wouldn’t move up and down …. By attaching the feet off-center, the wheel (the gear brick) becomes a cam, causing the feet to move. Circular motion is converted to linear motion. When humans walk, the knee moves in a circle in much the same way ….”

The step-by-step instructions, done with photos, are easy to follow. The photos also make it crystal-clear what the parts that the explanations are talking about (the “cam” in the above-quoted example) actually look like. Thus it’s easy to pick up a good understanding of both the scientific and the model-building principles involved.

This is a terrific book for kids or adults who are new to creating their own animated LEGO constructions. It’s true that you can build anything with LEGO elements--in mid-2019 there is even a working grand piano model, designed by a LEGO fan, that LEGO is considering for possible release as an official LEGO Ideas set. This is the book that tells you how to get started towards making your own complicated models that move.
product: Professional Trade Compliance Certification Study Guide

text:
SOME CHOOSE DARKNESS is a professionally written novel, but it’s just not my cup of tea. It’s kind of a cross between a police procedural and a cosy mystery, in that: (1) the main investigator (Rory Moore) is an expert doll restorer (!), in addition to being a terrific forensic reconstructionist (an independent contractor?); and (2) the story depends heavily on the characters’ personalities and their inter-relationships with other people.

The plot is intricately pieced together out of chapters told from different characters’ viewpoints (e.g., Rory, a woman with hyperthymesia (superior memory), the perp aka “the Thief”), and from different time periods (1979, 1981-1982, and 2019). The slow-moving chapters, packed with description, just dribble out the clues that are supposed to keep the reader reading.

Sadly, I haven’t the patience to slog through a mystery/thriller novel that is constructed like this one. Generally I dislike multiple-viewpoint narration--for escape reading, I prefer police procedurals with fairly straightforward investigations. Also, I really dislike plots where the main investigator (female, of course) gets personally sucked into the crimes being investigated (against women, of course), and ultimately gets nearly killed by the main perp. These plots always require a lot of coincidences that totally destroy my reader’s willing suspension of disbelief.
product: HTS Code Reference Guide - Harmonized Tariff Schedule

text:
ALMOST MIDNIGHT (MIKE BOWDITCH MYSTERIES) is the tenth entry in this entertaining series featuring Maine game warden Mike Bowditch (the first is [[ASIN:1250161657 The Poacher’s Son]]); it works fine as a standalone. The author does a marvelous job of immersing the reader in the remote Maine woods scene. The plot involves administrative corruption within a Maine prison (corruption that causes a few inmate deaths), but it mostly centers on Mike’s search for the person who shot--and probably mortally wounded--his escaped dog/wolf (Shadow) with a crossbow.

In the novel, Mike’s incarcerated best friend (Billy Cronk) urgently summons him to the Maine State Prison, and asks him to investigate the new CO, a sergeant named Dawn Richie. Mike barely gets started before Richie is ambushed and wounded in the prison laundry room. The ambush leaves a prison guard dead, and lands two inmates (one is Billy) in the nearest hospital. More mayhem ensues when one of the injured inmates gets loose and takes a nurse hostage.

When the situation clears, Mike concentrates on identifying Shadow’s attacker. This puts him in touch with a crazy North Woods distiller (Alcohol Mary), a newly established Amish enclave, a young couple dwelling in a yurt, and a wealthy, homicidal landowner (Gorman Peaslee) who is determined to eradicate the Amish. Mike also touches base with many game warden friends, and pursues his relationship with Dani Tate.

The novel illustrates the changes occuring in northern Maine (e.g., the local impact of the closing of a huge paper mill). It paints a very detailed picture of the area, but it never, ever reads like a travelogue. The characters are extremely well-developed. The plot is interesting, though it is driven by Mike’s pursuit of Shadow’s attacker far more than by the murders of the humans, and the Shadow plot gets tiresome over the course of a full-length novel. The action-packed conclusion is both exciting and satisfying.
product: HTS Code Reference Guide - Harmonized Tariff Schedule

text:
Until now, I’ve enjoyed each new entry in the “Ruth Galloway” series. The novels are pleasant “cozy police procedural” mysteries about an archaeologist, now a single mother, who consults with local police whenever bones are discovered in henge sites in England’s North Norfolk salt marshes. However, most of this 11th series entry, THE STONE CIRCLE (RUTH GALLOWAY MYSTERIES), is duplicative of the earlier novels, and the new material about The Great Circle of stones at Stanton Drew is thrown in at the end and almost irrelevant to the solution of the main mystery.

In the novel, a second stone circle is discovered at the marshy henge site explored in the prior novels. This one includes a stone cist (a grave), but Ruth finds, once again, that the bones in it are recent--definitely not Bronze Age like the henge. Ruth’s detective contact (Nelson), her daughter Kate’s father, is once again receiving anonymous letters with literary references that urge him to investigate circumstances related to the child’s bones buried at the henge site.

A prime suspect in the burial is the look-alike son (Leif) of the deceased author of the letters Nelson received in the previous henge investigation. The bones are soon identified as probably belonging to a 12-year-old girl (Margaret) who went missing in 1981. But where were the bones kept, before they were reburied in the newly discovered cist? Who was responsible for Margaret’s death? And who reburied her bones in the cist?

Most of the novel deals with Ruth’s star-crossed relationship with Nelson. Nelson is married with adult daughters, and hasn’t informed his family of Kate’s paternity. He does have a regular visitation arrangement with Kate, so that one wonders why they don’t seem to suspect that he is Kate’s father. The novel also ties up a lot of loose ends from the original henge investigation--it’s basically a reworking of the original henge plot.

The novel does NOT work as a standalone. It’s extremely slow-moving, and the plot and ending are unsatisfying. Read it if you’re interested in following the latest developments in the Ruth/Nelson and Cathbad/Judy relationships.
product: HTS Code Reference Guide - Harmonized Tariff Schedule

text:
DKFINDOUT! CODING is mostly a collection of factoids about early computers (e.g., Babbage’s Difference Engine), programmers and inventors (e.g., Steve Jobs, Bill Gates), elementary coding programs (e.g., Scratch), apps (e.g., smartphones in action), gaming (e.g., virtual reality), and the like. It’s so elementary in scope, I wonder what age group would find this worth reading?

This is probably the first DK book I’ve examined that I haven’t liked. I’m an information junkie, but the presentation here is so shallow, there is very little actual knowledge imparted.

I think the book is pretty useless for increasing a child’s programming know-how, as promised in the advertising. For example, one of the items in the “Learning to code” section (a two-page spread) states: “Be a robot master Robot kits let you build a robot quite easily. You can then tell the robot what to do by writing instructions in code.” Does a child who is interested in coding need to be told this in a book? Would this inspire a child who knows nothing about coding to want to learn to code?
product: Professional Trade Compliance Certification Study Guide

text:
I ordered A SHOT IN THE DARK: A CONSTABLE TWITTEN MYSTERY because a national newspaper reviewer labeled it “the funniest mystery of the year 2018”. Well, it’s written as a farce, and funny at times, but it has an unpleasant darkness underneath that destroys the humor and fun. I shouldn’t have ignored my personal rule, which is to always avoid novels that borrow their titles from other famous literary, dramatic, or comedic works (e.g., [[ASIN:6305308713 Peter Seller’s “A Shot in the Dark”]]).

In the novel, a too-clever-by-half young constable (Twitten) arrives in Brighton, where the police (Steine and Brunswick) are in total denial about the existence of crime in the seaside resort. When crimes are reported, they dismiss them as too baffling to be solved, or accept any faked evidence that will allow them to close a case.

Twitten annoys his superiors and upsets the comfortable status quo by easily solving all the outstanding cases. In the end, he is outsmarted by an equally clever Master Criminal, and the status quo is restored by (1) an unbelievable event that removes a threat to a main character; and (2) the machinations of the Master Criminal.

The novel ends with a mild cliffhanger that suggests there may be more “Inspector Twitten” novels to come. If there are, I won’t be reading them. The novel is professionally written and plotted (with elaborate plot twists), but there isn’t a single character that I really liked.
product: HTS Code Reference Guide - Harmonized Tariff Schedule

text:
What a disappointment--from Interweave Press, of all publishers! THE KNITTER’S DICTIONARY: KNITTING KNOW-HOW FROM A TO Z is a small, handbook-sized dictionary of knitting terms that has mediocre-to-poor definitions. It's spottily illustrated with line drawings, but all it really does is define specialized terms that you may encounter in knitting patterns or magazines. It's far from a solid knitting-bag reference, such as Vicki Square’s classic [[ASIN:1596683147 Knitter’s Companion]] (which defines many of the same terms).

For example, in this book the un-illustrated definition of “combination knitting” reads: “A variation of Continental-style knitting. Yarn is wrapped the other way around for the purl stitch so that the stitches are mounted left leg forward on the needle. Knit stitches are then worked through the back loop. …” There is an illustration for “purling Continental-style”, and another for “knitting through the back loop”, but they don't really help me to visualize combination knitting. I know what “the other way around” means, but I wouldn't know if I needed a definition of this term.

As another example, “needle gauge” is defined--without an illustration--as “Tool for measuring the size of a needle.” (That's really helpful.) Or look at the entry for “hat styles”, which has useful illustrations with useless definitions. “Beanie” is defined as “A style of hat, close-fitted to the head”; “toque” is defined as “Common Canadian usage, a fitted hat like a beanie. May or may not have a pom-pom.” (Is there any difference?)

This un-illustrated definition, for “Cowichan, curling sweater” falls into the “why bother?” category. It reads: “These are both styles of a heavy knit jacket, typically worked in very thick yarn, featuring a shawl collar and intarsia colorwork designs. This style of garment originated in indigenous communities in the Pacific Northwest; the garment known as curling or Mary Maxim sweaters borrow the basic concept for fashion.” I know what a Cowichan sweater looks like, but have never heard of a “Mary Maxim sweater”. I’m familiar with the Mary Maxim crafts catalog, but am consumed by curiosity about what a Cowichan-inspired, fashionable “Mary Maxim sweater” might look like.

This is a knitting book that didn't need to be published. There are many far better knitting references already available.
product: HTS Code Reference Guide - Harmonized Tariff Schedule

text:
MAKE IT, WEAR IT: WEARABLE ELECTRONICS FOR MAKERS, CRAFTERS, AND COSPLAYERS is more valuable for the general ideas than for the specific clothing design projects. You have to download a lot of cutting patterns (e.g., for T-shirt, skirt), 3D printer files (e.g, battery holder from Thingiverse), and how-to videos (e.g., soldering techniques) to be able to make the illuminated fashions as described. You will also need to order specific circuit boards, fiber-optic parts, conductive thread, etc.

Although instructions for procedures like soldering and sewing are fairly general, there are more detailed directions for unusual procedures like 3D printing directly on fabric. (You print a few layers of your 3D embellishments before you lay the fabric onto the printing bed and secure it.) You will need access to a 3D printer or a laser cutter for most of the projects.

The projects are as follows: 3D Embellished T-Shirt; Fiber-Optic Fabric Scarf; Fun Festival Hip Pack; Solar Backpack; Starlight Skirt; Programmable Sewn Circuit Cuff; and LED Matrix Purse. Using them as inspiration, you can go on to make your own fabulous lighted-up costumes and fashion garments.

text:
NIGHTTOWN (A JUNIOR BENDER MYSTERY) is the 7th novel in the amusing “Junior Bender” series, and it works as a standalone. Junior (that's his given name) is a professional burglar--and a very good one, because he was mentored in “the art of the steal” by master burglar Herbie Mott. Thanks to Herbie, Junior lives by an ethical code that's saved him many sleepless nights--the first rule being “never take the best thing they own”.

In the novel, Junior needs money to help out his girlfriend Ronnie, so he warily accepts a job that's offering too much money for the task. The client wants him to enter an empty house, using a supplied house key, and retrieve a doll left there by the recently deceased owner. That's it. For $25,000 now, and $25,000 after delivery of the doll.

Exercising his usual abundance of caution, Junior arrives for the job on the day AFTER the client expected him. He finds another burglar--a friend of his--in the house, and the friend already has the doll. But it's been cut open, and when the friend delivers the doll to the client, she is murdered.

Junior sets out to avenge her, even though it puts the lives of Ronnie, his daughter, his divorced wife, and himself in danger. Junior must cash in all of the accumulated favors owed him by his criminal underworld and Hollywood movie industry contacts, in order to identify and take out all of the people behind the disastrous “easy” job.

Whenever a new Junior Bender novel arrives, I can't wait to read it. Author Timothy Hallinan is a master of the comic mystery, and other reviewers’ comparisons of his novels to those of Raymond Chandler and Donald Westlake are NOT exaggerated.
product: HTS Code Reference Guide - Harmonized Tariff Schedule

text:
RUBIK doesn't’ read like a novel, but like a collection of “experimental” short stories--or, more accurately, like a collection of vignettes prepared for creative writing class assignments. The stories make little sense to me, and leave me feeling sad and mildly depressed, so I didn't enjoy them.

I’m baffled by the Amazon classification of author Elizabeth Tan’s book as “Humor: Satire”. What's funny about the torment of an aging piano teacher that is triggered by a cellphone call that plays the opening notes of a piano piece the teacher memorized long ago? How is the sudden death of a young woman, who is now remembered only by traces left on Internet message boards, either humorous or satirical?

The Amazon classification of the book as “Fantasy: Magical Realism” seems more reasonable. Since I’ve never heard of a “Magical Realism” genre, and have no idea what it is supposed to be, it may explain why I don't understand the stories.

The author does seem to have writing talent, though I don't care for her style. It’s choppy and frequently disregards the usual rules of grammar. It has a certain rhythm and flow that you get used to, as you read, but that doesn't make the prose “lyrical” or “beautiful”.

Here's a brief example, a paragraph describing a doomed character leaving a gas station mini-mart: “Elena, on the diagonal, outside and away from the white glow of diesel and LPG, the faint croon of the radio playing infinitely for refueling motorists.” Or another example, a sentence that opens a chapter: “Kish, unarticulated today, without bar lines, spine creaking like a hymn book; soft, old Kish, having dreamed last night of being an engulfed cathedral, ascending to consciousness in perfect fourths.”

Perhaps there is a story to be found among the disconnected chapters. However, although I like fantasy and science fiction generally, I’m too literal-minded to want to spend time trying to figure out a “novel” like this one.
product: HTS Code Reference Guide - Harmonized Tariff Schedule

text:
When I order a book marketed as a mystery/thriller, I expect a relatively easy-to-read, entertaining book--a novel to be read for the story, and then given away or recycled. What I do NOT want is a book with a plot that dribbles out information about what actually happened or is happening to the characters, or that makes the reader’s job difficult by jumping around in timelines, and/or shifting abruptly from one first-person viewpoint character to another. Why should anyone bother to read a throwaway mass-market novel that transforms pleasure-reading into a demanding job?

THIS IS HOW IT ENDS is one of the worst examples I’ve yet encountered of a mass-market “mystery/thriller” that (1) makes reading the story as difficult as possible for the unsuspecting reader/purchaser, and (2) delivers next to nothing as a reward for the reader's effort in tracking the jumbled-up events.

This novel includes a couple of murders, and two female, angst-ridden, first-person narrators (Ella, Molly). Perhaps the following partial list of early chapter titles will serve to alert casual mystery/thriller readers as to what they are in for, if they want to attempt this book.

Ella Now--6th March
Ella Then--6th March--evening
Molly Now--6th March
Ella Then--6th March
Molly Now--7th March
Ella Then-- 1st March
Molly Now--10th March
Ella Then--15th February

The chapters go on in the same fashion, with skipping-around “Then” dates for Ella and forward-going “Now” dates for Molly.

In my opinion, a novel writer has utterly failed at his/her basic storytelling task, when the reader must read the chapter labels to be able to tell which character is speaking, and the times when the plot events are happening.
product: Professional Trade Compliance Certification Study Guide

text:
DEATH IN PROVENCE: A NOVEL (PENELOPE KITE) is a slow-moving cozy mystery with a drab, boring amateur detective (Penelope) and an uninteresting plot. Especially at the beginning, the narrative is weighed down by many untranslated French phrases that are presumably inserted to establish the Provence setting. I guess they’re needed, because other details regarding Penelope’s run-down stone cottage and its environs are fairly generic.

As for Penelope, she’s a middle-aged, divorced British ex-pat, with unappreciative step-children and horrible, insulting step-grandchildren back in England. She used to work for a medical examiner, which gives her limited forensic knowledge (e.g., the number of hours required for rigor mortis to wear off). She’s rather unsure of herself, which probably explains why local authorities don't take her very seriously when she shares her forensic theories about the murder of a local drunk found floating face down in her swimming pool. There's a love interest for Penelope, in the form of a dishy middle-aged mayor who seems to find her attractive.

The plot turns around some problems regarding the title to Penelope’s newly acquired dream home. Ownership became murky after WWII, when the property was first occupied by a French family suspected of collaborating with the Germans during the occupation. Penelope leans very heavily on the real estate agent (Clemence) who sold her the cottage. Clemence provides support far beyond what might be expected, including offering Penelope temporary shelter after the body is discovered, lining up repairmen, translating to supplement Penelope’s faltering French, and providing introductions to all the important local people.

Nothing about this novel makes me want to read any more of the series. Generally I love any and all cozy mysteries, but this one just falls flat.
product: Professional Trade Compliance Certification Study Guide

text:
Usually I dislike “literary” mysteries, but THE FRENCH GIRL grabbed and held my attention. It’s basically a first-person narrative, but it tells the story without dwelling too much on the personal angst of the narrator (Kate). The plot kept me guessing as to the murderer’s identity until near the end; but I never felt that the author was artificially doling out facts merely to keep the reader in the dark.

In the novel, a group of English college friends learn that a French detective is reopening an investigation into the disappearance of a young woman (Severine) who spent time with them during their 1-week vacation at a French farmhouse some 10 years previously. Severine’s body has been discovered in a farmhouse well that was filled in on the day before or after they departed.

The five friends, now pursuing careers in London, meet multiple times to share their recollections of the ill-starred vacation, and to share the details of their individual interviews with the detective. Suspicion falls heaviest on narrator Kate, a spurned lover who was replaced by Severine. But could Kate have done such a thing, when she protests that she never knew until now what Severine and her lover (Seb) had been up to?

The novel is an excellent example of how first-person narratives that involve recounting of long-ago events should be handled. The plot remains firmly grounded in present-day events, and the author never resorts to the currently trendy device of telling the story in chapters that alternate between different viewpoint characters and between “now” and “then” timelines.

Everything about this well-written, complicated novel hangs together beautifully. Not only does the novel contains a genuine, intriguing murder mystery, but the ending is “as it should be”--quite satisfying. Reading this novel was a pleasure.
product: Professional Trade Compliance Certification Study Guide

text:
THE MISSING HOURS is a slow-moving, confusing novel about kidnap-and-ransom consultants (experts who are hired to negotiate for the safe return of kidnap victims). It’s a hide-the-ball mystery, told in chapters that shift viewpoint characters (mostly alternating between sister-and-brother detectives Leah and Finn), and timelines (ongoing investigations and “disappearance” flashbacks). Interspersed among the mystery/thriller plot chapters are (1) fictional “case histories” of kidnapping cases supposedly handled by the main-character consultants, and (2) fictional “published professional articles” about the same.

I guess that the author knows a lot about real-life kidnap-and-ransom negotiations, and decided to educate readers about the field by incorporating her knowledge into a novel. However, the case histories and articles don’t add to the plot; and the rather unbelievable plot is thin, boring, and in its own way predictable.

Why anyone would choose to present a mystery/thriller intended for a mass market audience in such a complicated format is beyond me. As for the kidnap-and-ransom-negotiator content, I prefer nonfiction when I need to be introduced to, or educated about, such a specialized topic.
product: HTS Code Reference Guide - Harmonized Tariff Schedule

text:
MURDER IN GREENWICH VILLAGE (A LOUISE FAULK MYSTERY) is a decent but run-of-the-mill cozy mystery. It’s supposedly set in Greenwich Village in 1913, but there isn’t much of an historical atmosphere. References to new buildings (e.g., the electrified Woolworth Building) are dropped in; and male figures (especially the police detectives) are predictably patronizing of the female amateur detective (Louise). However, the story doesn’t have much to do with the time period chosen for the setting.

In the novel, Louise (from Altoona, Pa.) is working for a NYC publisher, having landed the job with the help of her wealthy aunt (who writes popular fiction). Louise shares a Greenwich Village apartment with another girl (Callie), and with Callie’s unwelcome, sponging cousin (Ethel). Louise and Callie return home one night and discover Ethel’s body with a butcher knife buried in it.

Callie and Louise lie to the police, by withholding information about admirers who happen to match the description of a man seen on the apartment stairs shortly after Ethel arrived home. Louise ends up spending lots of time at the police station, talking to Detective Muldoon, for various reasons. Eventually Louise figures out who the killer is, forms her own (highly illegal) plan for trapping the killer, and ends up in mortal danger herself when the plan doesn’t succeed as expected.

The novel is well-written, though rather wordy. The plot is nothing special. Amateur detective Louise isn’t very interesting, and is likely to become even less interesting if she pursues her announced desire to become the first female homicide detective in NYC.
product: HTS Code Reference Guide - Harmonized Tariff Schedule

text:
When I ordered FIRST PERSON: A NOVEL, I thought that the book-publishing aspect sounded interesting, and that it was a mystery/thriller. Whatever book list I found it on, it was badly misclassified, because it appears to be a serious attempt at writing literature.

It’s a first-person narrative, written in long, rambling sentences. There is minimal punctuation--for example, conversations are reported on without all the bother of including quotation marks. The story is something about a ghost writer who’s failing at extracting information for an upcoming memoir from an infamous con man. In all honesty, the book makes as much sense to me as James Joyce’s [[ASIN:0394743121 Ulysses]]. I cannot make head nor tail of it.

Neither the narrator nor the prose is interesting enough to justify the effort it would take for me to figure out this book--what it’s about, or what the author’s intentions were in writing it. I leave this much-praised novel to all the students of English literature who particularly enjoy reading “difficult” novels with expansive, all-encompassing literary themes.
product: Professional Trade Compliance Certification Study Guide

text:
SPOILER ALERT: If I understand EXHIBIT ALEXANDRA: A NOVEL at all, it is a novel about a mentally ill performance artist (Alexandra/Amelia) who transforms her own life into the ultimate performance art installation, an installation that is intended to be exhibited in a New York art gallery. Why anyone would be interested in viewing the resulting exhibition is beyond me, but then I've never cared for or understood performance art anyway.

This debut novel is told in first-person narrative, largely in flashbacks, in alternating “now” and “then” chapters. (Sadly, this seems to be the current fashion in debut-novel-writing.) There is enough interior angst to fill a dozen novels. The angst is mostly in the form of the artist’s vicarious imaginings of how her abandoned husband and daughters are reacting and feeling after her disappearance and apparent murder.

To the extent that this novel purports to examine the conflicts faced by an ambitious “career woman” who finds herself trapped in the role of “stay-at-home mother”, it’s stale and trite. It really has nothing new to offer on the subject, and the unlikeable “unreliable narrator” isn’t to be excused for escaping from suburbia in the extreme, selfish, and delusional manner that she chooses. Her justifications at the end of the book fall totally flat.

This novel is definitely NOT what one expects from a novel classified as a mystery/thriller. It isn’t a thriller, because there’s no excitement; it isn’t a mystery because there’s a disappearance without a crime to be solved. Maybe the novel is properly classified as “literary fiction”, but however you classify it, it’s a boring, repetitive, confusing novel that’s a total waste of the reader’s time.
product: Professional Trade Compliance Certification Study Guide

text:
I’m kind of hooked on the DK popular culture books (Star Wars, LEGO, etc.), but I think that DK has really outdone itself with SOLO: A STAR WARS STORY. It has beautiful, large color photos of all the characters, so you can examine the finest details of their makeup, costumes, and gear.

It also has factual explanations of the special features of the items seen in the photos (e.g., emigration officer Sharest’s lipstick shade violates Imperial regulations; Enfys Nest’s armor plates fan out along an articulated servo-joint; Dryden’s mummified hand wears a ring locket containing royal cell samples).

The book has a great write up on the planet Kessel and its mining operations. There are movie design development drawings of the Millenium Falcon, and a big spread detailing the refurbished freighter’s features (including the original auxiliary ship at the front that is lost by the time Solo meets Princess Leia). There are many photos showing special items designed to furnish the interior movie sets (e.g., the irradiator illumination bank that eliminates mildew at Camp Forward).

In short, the Solo movie is covered in great detail. The book also has a good index, making it an invaluable reference for Solo movie Star Wars trivia.
product: HTS Code Reference Guide - Harmonized Tariff Schedule

text:
PROVIDENCE: A NOVEL is professionally written, and it’s probably a decent novel, but it isn’t what I expect from a fantasy mystery/thriller. It’s written as a first-person narrative, in chapters that cycle through three different viewpoint characters (Jon, Chloe, Eggs). The chapters are further grouped into parts involving different times and locales (e.g., Four Years Later; Six Years Later; Lynn, Lynn, City of Sin, etc.), and there are big gaps in the basic timeline.

This style of novel-writing leaves me cold. It’s far too much work keeping the characters and story straight. I also hate sifting through all the inner thoughts, insecurities, ditherings, and general angsts of multiple first-person narrators.

With this book, I couldn’t suspend my disbelief regarding someone’s placing a kidnapped middle-school boy (not a very bright one) into a medically induced coma for four years, and then releasing him as a more powerful person. Nor could I believe in a deep, abiding, lifelong love established between two middle-school students. Also, the opening paragraph really grated, with Jon’s statement that “I brung Pedro home for Thanksgiving break ….” (Pedro is a classroom hamster.)

I ordered this book because the publisher’s description promised a character endowed with unusual powers (after being kidnapped), and a detective that encounters a whodunit involving a serial killer. I should have focused, instead, on the part of the publisher’s description that touts the author’s “keen and powerful observations about human connection and how love and identity can dangerously blur together,” because that’s what this book is really about.
product: HTS Code Reference Guide - Harmonized Tariff Schedule

text:
FRIEND REQUEST is yet another novel about something terrible that happened when the main character was in high school; that comes to light again years later; that causes serious trauma or detriment to the adult character. In this example, the main character (Louise) did things that contributed to the death of a schoolmate (Maria) some 25 years earlier. Louise is now a single, working mother of a young son (Henry), and she’s struggling to cope with daily life. What she doesn’t need is a Facebook “friend request” from the long-dead Maria.

The story is told in Louise’s first-person narrative, mostly in chapters alternating between things as they are now (2016) and things as they were then (1989). It’s all about teenage relationships (cruel as only these can be), and toxic adult relationships (a high school reunion, domestic violence). Through it all, Louise comes across consistently as fearful, ineffectual, insecure, and woefully eager to please.

The plot moves slowly, through flashbacks, and when the mystery is finally revealed, it isn’t very satisfying. The writing is okay, but there are no likeable or particularly memorable characters. The basic idea (what would you do if you received a “friend request” from a dead person) is intriguing, but the novel itself is extremely disappointing.
product: Professional Trade Compliance Certification Study Guide

text:
BABY’S FIRST FELONY (A CECIL YOUNGER INVESTIGATION) is a delightful blend of the private investigator and cozy mystery genres. It’s really an extended shaggy dog story (along the lines of “How I Met Your Mother”), presented as an allocution by criminal defense investigator Cecil Younger to a 3-person panel of sentencing judges. (Yes, Cecil is in deep, deep trouble.) This is the 7th novel in the Cecil Younger series, but the first for me; it works fine as a standalone.

In the novel, Cecil discovers a box containing $50,000 in cash when investigating the facts behind the disappearance and murder of a teenage mother he’d helped. Ignoring the advice of his public defender boss (David Ryder), he doesn’t turn the money in right away. He hides it in his attic, which violates one of the “rules” included in David’s handout for first-time felons (“Baby’s First Felony: A Guide to Avoiding a Life in Crime”). Specifically, in the chapter on physical evidence, BFF suggests hiding incriminating evidence in public areas where they’re not easily traced to you.

Soon, Cecil’s investigation gets him involved with a Mexican drug dealer, who is murdered with a shotgun blast to the head--with Cecil standing near him in a hotel room. After that, it’s all downhill for Cecil, as events conspire to entangle him more and more in felonious criminal activities.

In a concluding “Author’s Note”, author John Straley states that the novel ”is a hallucinatory dream of everything that could have gone wrong for me while working thirty years as a criminal defense investigator in Alaska.” No doubt his real-life experience spawned quite a bit of the pithy, amusing-to-the-reader advice in David’s handout (for example, “When talking on the jail phone, Pig Latin is not an unbreakable code”).

Although I fear that this novel may be the last in the Cecil Younger series, I am looking forward, at the very least, to reading the previous six novels (starting with [[ASIN:B004HW7DWU The Woman Who Married a Bear]]). BABY’S FIRST FELONY is well-written, has great characters, and is highly entertaining.
product: Professional Trade Compliance Certification Study Guide

text:
THE ANOMALY is a rather pedestrian ancient-cave-exploration thriller about a fifth-rate-documentary-filming team’s discovery of actual buried secrets. The irony of it all is that afterwards the team can’t tell anyone about the discovery. Again and again, the author absolutely nails the phony on-screen dialogue spewed by the on-camera “explorer” (Nolan). For example, “Will we find the cavern? I don’t know. But I do know this: in the search for truth, it matters not whether we find. It matters only that we continue to seek.”

I hate those empty reality documentaries that air all the time on cable channels, so I didn’t enjoy reading Nolan’s glib pronouncements one bit. However, the Nolan character himself isn’t bad--Nolan and his producer (Ken) are both cynically aware of the emptiness of the documentaries that they make to sell to a gullible public.

The plot takes off from a real April 1909 newspaper story (reproduced in the novel) about a cavern supposedly discovered in the Grand Canyon by explorer G.E. Kincaid. The story has all the stock elements of an archaeological thriller: a huge stone rolling down a passageway, explorers trapped by blockage of the only exit, discovery of mysterious writings, emergence of frightening creatures, secret society conspiracy, and so on. Some of the film team members live, some don’t.

This is a rather slow-moving novel, and I just couldn’t warm to it. It’s okay if you like any and all thrillers belonging to the “Indiana Jones” genre.
product: Professional Trade Compliance Certification Study Guide

text:
THE POINT is is a supernatural thriller about the West Point of the future--a West Point that includes a specially-recruited, secret group of “posthuman” cadets. The posthumans each have a special power (telekinesis, mind control, mind-healing, superhero strength/speed, etc.). They are being trained--just like the ordinary human West Pointers--to become tomorrow’s military leaders, but they’re also becoming superweapons to be used as needed in the ongoing global arms race.

“Duty, honor, country” (the West Point credo) is being drilled into the posthumans, by means of all the traditional West Point training methods. But the group’s paramount, top-secret objective is to stop a group of rogue posthumans who are launching bloody, terrifying attacks on civilian crowds, and who are also threatening to take over the U.S. government itself.

In the novel, a lifelong screwup (posthuman Scarlett Winter) enrolls at West Point to avoid going to prison. Her superpower is the ability to temporarily absorb huge amounts of energy (as released by an exploding bomb, for example)--but she can’t control her special gift. West Point intends to teach her to control it, and then to use her as a superweapon that will blast power at anyone or anything on command.

Scarlett hates authority, and resists her teachers and trainers. She’s bullied and pummeled by cruel telekinetic upperclassmen/women, and she’s isolated because of her unique, never-before-seen superpower. Eventually Scarlett finds a guy (Seamus) who’s a kindred spirit, and a few friends, and she’s starting to like West Point. But everything blows up for her in the mind-boggling finale, when the school is overtaken by the rogue posthumans, who are led by an evil, sadistic, charismatic posthuman that no ordinary human and few posthumans can resist liking, wanting to please, and blindly obeying.

The plot summary above doesn’t do justice to this fast-moving, page-turner of a thriller. Scarlett and Seamus are solid, well-developed, memorable characters (as is the dangerous dream-invading Dalia). The plot is twisty, filled with surprises, and solidly pulled together at the end. This is a terrifying, intoxicating, innovative, and memorable novel.

.
product: Professional Trade Compliance Certification Study Guide

text:
THE DEVIL’S WIND: A SPIDER JOHN MYSTERY is a clumsily-written novel about a reluctant pirate (Spider John). Spider John is a ship’s carpenter who was forced into 8 years of piratehood after the ship he was on was captured by pirates.

In the novel, Spider John has barely escaped hanging in Port Royal, Jamaica. He is totally ashamed and appalled by his pirating career, and wants nothing more than to sail home to England to his wife Em, as the ordinary (non-pirate) ship’s carpenter on the HMS Redemption (get it?).

The novel pretty much lost me in the very first chapter, when Spider John and his sidekick Hob are seen in a crowd and recognized as pirates. They get away by galloping off on two stolen horses--even though neither one knows how to stop a galloping horse. They dismount by simply jumping off and rolling into the weeds alongside the road, losing their pursuers in the process. (Clearly the author knows nothing about horseback riding.)

As the novel continues, Spider John’s voyage home is complicated by the presence of Sam Smoke. As described by Spider John’s incorrigible also-former-pirate friend Odin (who also narrowly escaped hanging in Jamaica), Sam Smoke is an evil sadist, the devil incarnate. Odin treats John and the readers to a detailed account of Sam’s torture and murder of a lazy sailor. Naturally the voyage is further complicated later by the death of the captain, which will result in formal inquiries when they reach port.

The characters in this novel are cardboard caricatures of pirates. I kept expecting someone to say, “Avast, ye swabs”, or “Aaaargh!”; or to appear with a parrot on the shoulder. I also did not appreciate the gratuitous torture scenes. The writing itself is clunky, lacking flow, in part because it keeps returning to John’s internal thoughts of hanging, being recognized as a pirate, and remorse.

text:
WE ARE THE NERDS: THE BIRTH AND TUMULTUOUS LIFE OF REDDIT, THE INTERNET’S CULTURE LABORATORY is a very long and detailed account of the Reddit-related activities of Alexis Ohanian and Steve Huffman, the original founders and current leaders of Reddit, the so-called “front page of the Internet”. It’s a book about people, based on many personal interviews.

The book doesn’t provide much, if any, insight into what Reddit is or why it became so wildly popular. Read it if you enjoy biographies of individuals who made millions from Silicon Valley/Internet startups.

text:
As the title promises, this LEGO STAR WARS IDEAS BOOK: MORE THAN 200 IDEAS, GAMES, ACTIVITIES, AND BUILDING IDEAS has pages and pages of ideas for “Star Wars” things you can make with LEGO components. It is NOT a book of step-by-step building instructions, but there are photographs of each project with accompanying text giving you enough information to build each project.

What I really love about the book, is the “Radical Rebuild” portion that shows you interesting things to make from specific LEGO “Star Wars” sets that you may already own and be ready to disassemble. These “Rebuild” segments make up only a small portion of the book’s ideas, but here’s a list:

***Obi-Wan’s Jedi Interceptor (2016) rebuilds to a Sail Barge and a Mini Slave I
***TIE Striker (2016) rebuilds to a micro-scale Citadel Tower and Imperial shuttles
***Millennium Falcon (2015) rebuilds to a Wookie Walker and a Kanjiklub Stronghold
***Yoda’s Jedi Starfighter (2017) rebuilds to a Throne, a JEDI Tank, and R2-D2 Gear
***Rebel U-Wing Fighter (2016) rebuilds to a Supercar, a Drill Platform, or a Speeder
***Millennium Falcon (2015) rebuilds to a Scavenger Shuttle with Refueling Station
***Poe’s X-Wing Fighter (2015) rebuilds to a Walker, a Lifter, and a Missile Loader

Builder’s tips are scattered throughout, and there are lots of suggestions for creative designing. Many things can be made from regular LEGO bricks that didn’t originate in the special “Star Wars” line of LEGO sets. There are even suggestions for “Star Wars”-themed practical items like a pencil case, or a storage box that locks with a LEGO key.

The book is fun to browse through even if you don’t want to try your hand at building the models, useful items, or games.

text:
THERE ARE NO DEAD HERE: A STORY OF MURDER AND DENIAL IN COLOMBIA is a readable, but highly detailed account of interconnections between murderous Colombian paramilitary units and the Colombian government and military forces in the period from 1996 to 2010. It’s an impressive contemporary history of an extremely dark time for those living in the South American nation.

However, the book isn’t really something that a history buff or a true crime aficionado (like myself) will enjoy. It’s mostly about Colombian politics and law, and thus it’s a book for political science students and other individuals who have a special interest in the history and government of Colombia.
product: HTS Code Reference Guide - Harmonized Tariff Schedule

text:
THE ROBOTS OF GOTHAM has an interesting setting--a near future (2083) world populated by humans and sentient robots (some friendly to humans, most at war with humans). The writing is superficially okay, but it’s sorely in need of editing. The novel is 675 pages long!

The wordiness makes the story confusing, and extremely slow-moving. In the first 50 pages, there is a big human-robot battle (we aren’t told what’s behind it); the main character (Barry Simcoe) improbably survives a missile hit; he helps treat wounded soldiers and robots despite having no specific training for this; he avoids a firing squad; and he negotiates the repair of a robot (Nineteen Black Winter) that he’s befriended. This may sound like a lot of action, but the book is set in fairly small type, and there is a lot of meaningless back-and-forth conversation between Barry and the other characters in between those actual happenings.

Also, Barry’s character lacks believable motivation for his actions. For example, in the middle of the human-robot battle, himself in mortal danger, he tries to save the lives of two mortally wounded soldiers. Or, as another example, when asked why he’s offering $10,000 to save Nineteen Black Winter’s life, he says that he doesn’t want anyone else to die that day.

Worst of all, the plot requires tracking an incredibly complicated political situation, and the story is told through Barry’s blog posts and through robot Thought Machine Paul the Pirate’s dispatches. The political situation, as described by Paul: “America is now permanently divided, including the Eastern seaboard and much of the Midwest, under foreign occupation. Under the terms of the Memphis Ceasefire the SCC formally withdrew, leaving the occupied zones administered by the AGRT, a peacekeeping force made up of volunteers from over thirty countries. Manhattan has been annexed by a weird robot monarchy, while in Tennessee a more permanent peace is being delicately negotiated between the battered remnants of the US government and an envoy of implacable machines.”

In case you didn’t quite get that, there is a US map at the beginning of the novel that shows the political boundaries. There is also a 2-page chart of “The 2083 Sovereignty Matrix” that gives the names of the leaders of world nations and their classifications (human or machine). Canada is left completely blank on the map, even though protagonist Barry is Canadian; the chart tells us that the Prime Minister is an elected machine.

A novel like this is just too much work for me. It may be the start of some sweeping saga that will eventually gain a huge following (like Lord of the Rings or Game of Thrones), but I’m going to wait for the movie or TV series.
product: HTS Code Reference Guide - Harmonized Tariff Schedule

text:
SUPERFANS: INTO THE HEART OF OBSESSIVE SPORTS FANDOM is a highly readable, often amusing book that takes a moderately serious look at obsessive sports fan behavior and what motivates that behavior. There are interviews with some famous fans (e.g., Nevets of the Timbers Army; Michael Hopson for the Colts; banana-shirted Teddy Kervin for the Brewers) that show they’re basically normal people, except for when they’re doing their fan thing.

As an obsessive Golden State Warriors fan (scoring “extreme identification” on a quiz in chapter 3), I particularly enjoyed the chapter 11 discussion of Oklahoma City Thunder fans’ reaction to the departure of Kevin Durant in July 2016. Apparently the negative Yelp reviews of Kd’s restaurant, and the modification of Durant’s number 35 jersey to read “Cupcake” on the back, were examples of what some psychologists call “blasting”. Blasting--publicly stating negative things about specific players or rival teams-- simply makes disappointed fans feel better.

One anecdote that particularly amused me came at the end of that same chapter, which also discusses negative fan reactions to reporters who write articles perceived by the fans as “biased”. As the author writes, Fox announcer Joe Buck “has been accused of bias by so many fans of so many teams that his Twitter bio playfully states: ‘I love all teams EXCEPT yours’.”
product: Professional Trade Compliance Certification Study Guide

text:
In the Introduction, the authors of BLOCKCHAIN: A PRACTICAL GUIDE TO DEVELOPING BUSINESS, LAW, AND TECHNOLOGY SOLUTIONS explain that the robust blockchain technology “eliminates the risks that come with data being held centrally,” by storing identical blocks of information across an entire network. Also, blockchain uses encryption technology, and allows data and information to be widely distributed. Thus, “blockchain has created the backbone of the new Internet, web3,” and “will change the business models in all types of industries” in the next five to ten years.

That said, the authors state that this book is intended for anyone interested in blockchain technology, as well as for developers of blockchain applications and web applications, and for tech-savvy businesspeople and attorneys who want to stay current with technology. The key word, in my opinion, is “tech-savvy”, because the book is quite technical in nature--it covers “blockchain definition, use cases, distributed technology, and especially blockchain development, with a good deal of code snippets and best practices.”

There is considerable discussion of “smart contracts”, which guarantee that the terms of a contract written to the blockchain will be executed. Smart contracts work when conditions are written in code, then initiated with funds to make good by either party. As directed by the code, the contract will automatically confirm that conditions have been met, and distribute currency and/or goods to the parties accordingly.

The book also discusses solving the problem of public blockchain scalability--the fact that networks like Bitcoin and Ethereum will grind to a halt if they acquire as many users as Visa or Instagram, because every transaction is currently performed by every node on those networks. Some suggested solutions for the scalability problem include building intermediate network layers, not recording every transaction on every node, and sharding.

The authors describe the book's contents as follows:

Chapter 1. An overview of everything blockchain.

Chapter 2. Gives depth to real-world use cases in the financial services industry, with sections on cryptocurrency and digital tokens. Discusses smart property and smart contracts, as well as how blockchain may help security for the Internet of Things.

Chapter 3. Introduces some components of blockchain architecture.

Chapter 4. Discusses blockchain and the law, including the implications of smart contracts, and the current state of blockchain regulation.

Chapter 5. Covers terminology, concepts, technology stack, blockchain development platforms, APIs, the Ethereum Virtual Machine and Ethereum dapps, DAOs, and autonomous smart contracts.

Chapter 6. Introduces Solidity smart contract programming language.

Chapter 7. Introduces more complicated tools and techniques that support “a workflow to handle more complex solutions”.

Chapter 8. Looks into use cases for private and consortium blockchain solutions.

Chapter 9. Reviews the unique challenges faced by blockchains.

Chapter 10. Introduces the development lifecycle of a fully functioning betting application built on Ethereum.

Chapter 11. Shows how to deploy the application built in Chapter 10.

text:
INTO THE STORM: TWO SHIPS, A DEADLY HURRICANE, AND AN EPIC BATTLE FOR SURVIVAL reads like a novel, even though it’s intended as a nonfiction account of the loss of two ships (El Faro and Minouche) during Hurricane Joaquin in September 2015. The U.S. Coast Guard rescued the 12 men on Minouche (by helicopter); the 33 men aboard El Faro perished.

The story has a lot of detail, and moves very slowly. For example, the description of El Faro’s sailing into bad weather doesn’t begin until Chapter 7 (of 17 chapters); the description of Minouche’s encountering difficulties doesn’t begin until the middle of Chapter 8.

To make the account readable, the author uses the “fly on the wall” technique, and recounts the happenings on the two doomed ships by means of invented conversations. (The shipwreck stories are mostly told through quoted dialogue involving crew members. However, the statements attributed to the captains and sailors aren’t really exact quotations, but are the author’s reconstruction of what the people probably said to each other.)

The author informs us that the crew member quotes from Chapter 7 came from the El Faro’s voyage data recorder, NHC’s archives, and the Coast Guard’s MBI report. Information about crew conversations aboard the Minouche are apparently based on interviews with survivors.

In my opinion, what is known about the tragic loss of the two ships is insufficient to warrant a full-length nonfiction book. It seems that the author has worked hard at stretching out the narrative to fill the pages, with the result that the story as a whole is boring. Also, as a novelized nonfiction work, the book doesn’t really qualify as a solid history of the events.
product: Professional Trade Compliance Certification Study Guide

text:
FALLOUT: DISASTERS, LIES, AND THE LEGACY OF THE NUCLEAR AGE is a highly readable account of the damage inflicted on humans by nuclear explosions and disasters, starting with the atomic bomb dropped on Hiroshima, and continuing through the year 2017. It offers a short but complete history of the Atomic Age.

It’s filled with surprising-to-me facts. For example, everyone on the planet received nuclear fallout from the above-ground nuclear tests conducted from 1954-1963--the average human received 0.15 millisieverts (a tiny dose) in 1963 alone. Or, as another example, the best way to dispose of nuclear waste is to reprocess it to extract the plutonium--something that was routinely done when plutonium was used to produce atomic weapons, but that is NOT usually done now that plutonium is used to generate power.

This book is a good choice for anyone who wants to get quickly up to speed on the past, present, and future of nuclear energy and nuclear weapons.
product: Professional Trade Compliance Certification Study Guide

text:
MASTERING AUTOCAD 2019 AND AUTOCAD LT 2019 is a massive guide to using the powerful but highly complex AutoCAD design and drafting software. (The book weighs 3 lb. 3.5 oz., and measures 2.5” thick.) The approach is tutorial rather than encyclopedic--it teaches the use of commands as you work on an actual project.

My primary interest lies in 3D modeling and imaging (Part 4, pp. 665-862), and I find the detailed instructions and exercises to be extremely helpful. In particular, I appreciate the boxed comments that contain comments and tips relevant to various stages of the project being created in the tutorial (e.g., why the AutoCAD screen looks a bit different from the screenshot in the guide; what to watch out for when editing 3D objects).

This guide makes the task of learning to use AutoCAD a little less daunting.

text:
THE FEMINIST REVOLUTION: THE STRUGGLE FOR WOMEN’S LIBERATION is a contemporary history of feminism, commencing with the year 1966, the year that the National Organization for Women (NOW) was founded. To the extent that it’s a serious history, it merely describes what feminists and anti-feminists were doing and saying in the years from 1966 through the 1980s. The text portions are based for the most part on newspaper, magazine, and journal articles.

To my mind, this volume is a picture book, primarily valuable for the scanned, politically-charged images of protesters, advertisements for feminist events, political cartoons, newspaper stories, magazine covers, and the like. Indeed, the images overpower the accompanying text, which is set in small type and often displayed on colored backgrounds (as if no one is really expected to actually read it). The oversized, square book format also detracts from the readability of the text.

The subtitles of the chapters are generally a better guide to the topics than the actual, politicized titles (e.g., ch. 1 “‘Sisterhood is powerful’”, subtitled “How the movement mobilized”). The subtitles are as follows: (1) How the movement mobilized; (2) The struggle for liberation; (3) Civil rights and liberation; (4) Reclaiming the physical and mental treatment of women; (5) Sexuality and lesbian feminism; (6) Reinventing culture in and out of the workplace; (7) Publishing and the media; (8) Music and the arts; (9) The anti-war and anti-nuclear debate; and (10) Radicalization, fragmentation, and educating the next generation.

The history is very fragmented--for example, Ms. Magazine, which caused many 1970s women to wake up and become feminists (remember the “clicks”?) doesn’t appear until ch. 7 (page 154 out of 220-plus pages). Also, the choice of mostly politically-charged images makes the whole book seem biased and “shrill”, even though the text itself is written with a balanced, neutral tone.
product: Professional Trade Compliance Certification Study Guide

text:
The title of this book is highly misleading. THE HIGH ROLLER EXPERIENCE: HOW CAESARS AND OTHER WORLD-CLASS COMPANIES ARE USING DATA TO CREATE AN UNFORGETTABLE CUSTOMER EXPERIENCE contains almost nothing about the special perks (high-roller experience) provided to VIP customers at “world-class” companies.

Instead, the book is all about data analytics, and how big companies can use data to design the most effective customer loyalty rewards programs. The main message is that rewards programs should be tied to the profitability of types of customer behavior, rather than to the dollar-amounts spent by individual customers. For example, slot machines are more or less profitable (by the casino’s share, or “hold”), depending on the customers’ machine choices. Thus the casino’s loyalty program should be designed to reward the customers who use the profitable types of machines, rather than to reward the customers who spend the most dollars.

The book appears to be sound in its analytics, and will no doubt be of considerable interest to business people with customer rewards programs, and to data analysts in general. However, it’s of no interest at all to casual readers who would like to know more about the perks actually extended to Las Vegas’ regular high rollers.
product: Professional Trade Compliance Certification Study Guide

text:
The “Mary Russell” novels are the only modern Sherlock-Holmes-inspired novels that I enjoy. In contrast to the other modern “Holmes” novels, they do NOT attempt to write new adventures for Sir Arthur Conan Doyle’s famous detective. Instead, they chronicle the adventures of Holmes’ wife (Mary Russell), a character created entirely by author Laurie R. King. Holmes himself only appears as a secondary character, and his investigations never drive the main plot.

In this 15th “Mary Russell” novel, THE ISLAND OF THE MAD, Mary and Sherlock travel to Venice in search of her oldest friend’s aunt (Vivian Beaconsfield). Aunt Vivian, diagnosed as mentally ill, has long resided in Bethlehem General Hospital (Bedlam). However, after recently being granted a week’s home leave, she and her nurse have failed to return to Bedlam. They have vanished without a trace.

Tracking down the madwoman requires Mary to check into Bedlam herself, to be able to interview “residents” who knew Vivian. Having obtained the information she needs, Mary pulls off a next-to-impossible escape from Bedlam. She and Holmes then proceed to Venice, where Mary enlists a pair of gondoliers to aid in her continuing search.

The novel does a wonderful job of evoking the look and feel of Venice in 1922. The plot, which examines the plight of high-born, unmarried, abused females in the period between the wars, has many twists and turns.

Although a few of the “Mary Russell” novels are a little bit better (in my opinion), this one definitely ranks among the very best. The novel works reasonably well as a standalone, but to understand how and why Sherlock Holmes came to acquire a wife, you must begin with [[ASIN:0749008520 The Beekeeper’s Apprentice]].
product: Professional Trade Compliance Certification Study Guide

text:
THE FRAMERS’ COUP: THE MAKING OF THE UNITED STATES CONSTITUTION offers an impressive, detailed analysis of the Constitutional Convention, including the recounting of events preceding and following it that impacted the framing and ratification of the final U.S. Constitution and the Bill of Rights. It’s a highly readable history that should be of interest to American history buffs and scholars, as well as to Constitutional law students, professors, and lawyers.

Throughout the volume, the author draws on original source materials. The volume is footnoted, has an extensive Bibliography, and a complete Index.

text:
[[ASIN:0345333926 Ringworld]] will always be my favorite “space station” novel, but I still love to read the new books in this narrow science fiction subgenre. PLACES IN THE DARKNESS is a very good space-station novel, because it paints a complete, detailed picture of a self-contained, heavily populated, off-world “city in the sky” (Ciudad de Cielo).

In the novel, Ciudad de Cielo is an enormous space station, still under construction, that is dedicated to building a super-spaceship (the Arca Estrella) that will take explorers across the galaxy to seek new planets that humankind can relocate to (in the far future when the sun expands and gobbles up the Earth). The main characters are Dr. Alice Blake, sent from Earth to investigate the city’s increased criminal activity (contraband smuggling, not crimes against persons); and Nikki Fixx, a lesbian police officer, usually drunk, who provides “protection” for illegal businesses, mediates personal disputes (as when someone welshes on a deal), and finds solutions to major personal problems (like a prohibited on-station pregnancy).

The novel’s Prologue sets the plot in motion--a pair of technicians reach their usual test chamber assignment, in the Axle connecting the sky city’s two Wheels, and discover another technician’s deconstructed body and organs floating inside the zero-gravity chamber. The technicians are shaken, not only by the gruesome sight, but by the unimaginable nature of the crime. Serious crime simply doesn’t happen at Ciudad de Cielo. The task of unmasking the perpetrator and the politically charged motives underlying the murder will ultimately fall to both Alice and Nikki, and will nearly cost them their lives.

This is an extremely well-written, well-plotted novel. The characters are fully developed and memorable. The descriptions of life in the sky city are detailed, and believable--it comes as no surprise that the jobs are boring, and that drinking, sex, and gambling occupy most of the inhabitants’ free hours. An interesting plot gimmick is “the Mesh”, an optional brain implant for humans that provides instant encyclopedic learning.

text:
CAREERS IN CRIMINAL JUSTICE is worthwhile reading for anyone who’s thinking about a career as an attorney or judge, police officer, forensic investigator, prison guard, private investigator or security guard, or provider of victim services.

The book does much more than merely say what the educational training and/or degree requirements are for entry into these differing criminal justice fields. The book discusses, in some depth, the personality and physical traits that cause a person to be suited to the different jobs; it describes the jobs themselves; it lists current salaries for the jobs; and it includes “guest speaker” pages where individuals currently employed in different criminal justice jobs talk candidly about what the jobs are like.

The book has information about how to apply for the jobs, as well as how to fill out application forms and pass screening tests. At the end, there is an excellent chapter on preparing seriously for job interviews.

Finally, the book has a superb Index that makes it an excellent general reference for information about any and all types of current criminal justice jobs. Some of the lesser-known included jobs are city jailer, forensic entomologist, police dispatcher, health care fraud investigator, toxicologist, sexual assault victim advocate, TSA airport security officer, ATF investigator, and law court security officer.
