text:
The ANSEL ADAMS 2019 WALL CALENDAR is the same as the Ansel Adams calendar sold in retail bookstores at year's end. I buy the annual calendar every year from Amazon as soon as it is offered (usually in July). That way, I can write in appointments from the upcoming year as soon as they are scheduled. (Each year’s calendar includes a full page for January of the following year, but my advance appointments usually extend beyond January.)

I buy this calendar year after year because the extra-large boxes for each day provide ample space for recording all of my family's medical appointments, upcoming meetings, activities, and other date-related reminders. I save each used calendar as a quick reference for that entire year's appointments and activities. The old calendars have come in handy many times!

Each month's calendar page includes a mini-calendar for last month and next month, and indicates holidays, moon phases, and important items like daylight savings time changes. The black and white photographs by Ansel Adams are beautiful, and every year's selection is different.

text:
“Peculiar” is the word that comes to mind when describing A TALE OF TWO MURDERS (A DICKENS OF A CRIME). The novel is another predictably bad example of the “avoid-having-to-imagine-your-own-character-by-choosing-a-historical-personage-for-your-protagonist” genre of mystery-thriller writing.

In this novel, the detective is a young Charles Dickens, who goes around asking people questions--ever so politely and discreetly--in an effort to determine whether two recently deceased young women were poisoned, and if so, who poisoned them. The unconvincing Charles Dickens constantly reminds us of his high regard for Miss Kate Hogarth, the daughter of George Hogarth, his publisher/employer. (In life, Dickens married Kate, and she bore him 10 children, though he eventually fell out of love with her and took a mistress.)

At first it seems that author Heather Redmond has a good handle on the setting and atmosphere of London in the 1830s, but Dickens’ activities soon become repetitive and tedious. Dickens attends parties, watches plays, dines frequently at the Hogarths’ home, follows up leads from newspaper clippings, interviews members of the nobility--but the descriptions are superficial and mechanical. The reader is never immersed in the Dickensian time period.

The plot winds up the complicated murder mystery in too few pages, and requires at least one certifiably crazy person to supply motives for the murders. The ending is unsatisfying. As an appendix, the novel includes a two-page Book Club Reading Guide, with 12 discussion topics, but I can’t imagine why any book club would want to select this novel.

text:
THE NEW YORK TIMES DISUNION: A HISTORY OF THE CIVIL WAR is a curated collection of a series of essays published in the New York Times between 2011 and 2015, in commemoration of various anniversaries of Civil War events. The essays make for fascinating reading, even if you’re NOT a serious Civil War buff and possess only a casual interest in American history.

The essays are short--each is only a few pages long--but many present new takes on Civil War “facts” that we learned in school and thought we knew. For example, Sherman’s burning of Columbia, South Carolina was an accident of war, not an intentional act by either the Union army or the residents of the city (as Sherman always claimed). Or, as another example, General Lee’s home on Arlington Hill--still standing on the site of Arlington National Cemetery--was never owned by the general, but was the property of his wife, Mary Anna Custis Lee, whose great-grandmother was Martha Washington.

The book has black-and-white illustrations, and is well-indexed.
product: Professional Trade Compliance Certification Study Guide

text:
The BBC micro:bit is a printed circuit board developed by the British Broadcasting Company for children to use in learning to write computer software and to build things (e.g., robots). It’s about half the size of a credit card, and is designed to work with other systems (e.g., Raspberry Pi). This book, THE OFFICIAL BBC MICRO: BIT USER GUIDE, tells you everything you need to know to work with the BBC micro:bit--and you don’t need pre-existing knowledge of computers, electronics, or programming to get started.

The book has clear photographs of everything on the circuit board, and screenshots of computer browser windows that you’ll see when you connect the micro:bit to your computer and begin to write programming code. There are separate chapters that discuss programming the micro:bit with (1) Javascript Blocks, (2) JavaScript, and (3) MicroPython. There are many examples of working code.

In addition, there are advanced chapters that discuss using the micro:bit’s built-in radio; using the micro-bit with Raspberry Pi; using the micro:bit to power electronic circuits; using the micro:bit with breakout boards; and using the micro-bit with conductive thread for wearable projects.

The text is readable and always easy to understand. The guide also clearly lists and describes all of the additional hardware (e.g., cables, banana plugs) that you may need to make full use of your micro:bit.
product: Professional Trade Compliance Certification Study Guide

text:
BIOINFORMATION (RESOURCES SERIES) is a scholarly monograph about “bioinformation”--what it is, where it comes from, who owns it, and who consumes it. The monograph also discusses the Big Data revolution, and some of the concerns that arise from “the datafication of everything”. For example, Big Data analytics may be used in diagnosing illnesses, recommending treatments, or identifying criminals, but may also result in “algorithmic branding” of certain individuals, to their detriment (as in charging them more for medical insurance).

The work is readable, but scholarly in tone. Its purpose, according to the authors, is as follows: “(O)ur intention here is to elucidate … how bioinformation is abstracted (its messy corporeal existence) and rendered as notated sequences, photographic images, x-rays, slides, or written or digitalized clinical, criminal or credit records--in short, as highly accessible, readable and manipulable distillations of bioinformation (data and aggregated big datasets) that now have tremendous commercial and economic value.”

This book isn’t intended for the casual reader who is interested primarily in the ethical and privacy concerns surrounding the unauthorized use of biological samples and data. I’m not sure who the intended audience is, but am guessing it’s primarily of interest to people involved with the actual buying and selling of bioinformation.
product: Professional Trade Compliance Certification Study Guide

text:
THE LEGAL GUIDE FOR WRITERS, ARTISTS AND OTHER CREATIVE PEOPLE: PROTECT YOUR WORK AND UNDERSTAND THE LAW provides a pretty good “plain English” summary of current intellectual property law. However, the area of law is not only technical, but it’s largely determined by individual case rulings (which are based on specific legal factors and facts). Thus the book is hard to understand unless you have some legal training (e.g., paralegal, law student, lawyer); and the book won’t give you answers to your specific questions about your own intellectual property rights, unless your questions have already been ruled on by the courts.

The subject matter is presented in Q&A format, which makes the book difficult to use as a legal “nutshell” reference. (For instance, the four “fair use” factors of copyright law are listed under the question, “Did Congress Provide Any Guidance?”.)

The summarized areas of law (see chapters 2-6) are as follows: (1) Copyright and other legal rights; (2) Libel, Privacy, Obscenity, and other possible legal risks; (3) creator’s rights in Cyberspace; (4) Contracts involving creative people; and (5) Business and Tax matters affecting creative people.

Each book in the McGraw-Hill “5 Steps” series is organized around the following five steps: (1) set up a study program using the online site; (2) take a diagnostic test printed in the book to determine your test readiness; (3) develop successful strategies for answering multiple-choice, short-answer, and essay questions; (4) review the specific subject-matter material you are expected to know for the particular AP exam; and (5) take full-length practice tests with questions similar to actual exam questions to build your confidence and testing skills. The books are issued annually, so be sure to check for the latest edition before you buy--the AP exams may have some format changes from year to year.

The 2018 AP Physics 1 exam prep guide includes three full-length practice exams (2 in the book, 1 online). The exams are followed by detailed explanations of the correct answers to the multiple-choice questions, and by point-by-point explanations of sample answers to the free-response questions. A new section of this exam prep guide is a collection of 180 daily practice problems (with fully-explained answers).

By studying the expanded explanations of the correct answers, you can learn what kinds of answers are expected from you. Armed with this knowledge of the exam itself, you can fully utilize your personal knowledge of physics, give the examiners the answers they want to see, and score high on the actual exam.

text:
IS SCIENCE RACIST (DEBATING RACE) is a thoughtful, persuasive essay about the futility of attempting to scientifically identify and classify racial characteristics or behaviors, when “race” is as much a cultural determination as it is a fact of nature. To start a scientific study by dividing the study subjects into groups by “race” simply results in the accumulated data being imposed upon the groups.

Author Jonathan Marks notes that scientists may get away with expressing racist conclusions (e.g., James Watson’s claims that IQ is genetically determined) because they are respected for their valuable scientific contributions. Marks does an excellent job of poking holes in various scientific and “common-sense” theories about race and heredity that sound plausible and correct, but that fall apart when the “scientific” underpinnings are examined.

There is a good discussion (in chapter 4) of how to write top-scoring free-response answers.

If you are taking the AP Microeconomics exam to secure college credit, then this guide should give you everything you need to get a high score. However, this guide contains less review material (fewer pages) than other guides in the series, and seems to give less detail in the multiple-choice question answers, so there may be better prep guides available for this particular exam.
product: HTS Code Reference Guide - Harmonized Tariff Schedule

text:
This 5 STEPS TO A 5: AP BIOLOGY 2018 exam prep guide is organized around the four “big ideas”, or concepts, of biology as identified by the College Board. These are evolution, cellular processes, genetics and informational transfer, and interactions. All of the questions on the AP Biology exam are linked to at least one of these big ideas.

Because this is an exam that awards college course credit, the review material (pages 37-260, taking up most of the guide) is quite daunting. It includes technical terms you must know (defined in the guide), and requires math. Of course, the guide is intended for use alongside a year- or semester-long high school course in AP Biology.

Each book in the McGraw-Hill “5 Steps” series is organized around the following five steps: (1) set up a study program using the online site; (2) take a diagnostic test printed in the book to determine your test readiness; (3) develop successful strategies for answering multiple-choice, short-answer, and essay questions; (4) review the specific subject-matter material you are expected to know for the particular AP exam; and (5) take full-length practice tests with questions similar to actual exam questions to build your confidence and testing skills. The books are issued annually, so be sure to check for the latest edition before you buy--the AP exams may have some format changes from year to year.

The 2018 AP Biology guide includes five practice exams (2 in the book, 3 online). A valuable feature is the online dashboard that tracks your progress and performance. It shows your strengths and weaknesses by question categories, and your scores and percentile ranking compared to other students. The dashboard figures are based on your answers to practice questions and exams.

Another extremely valuable feature is found in the practice exams. The answers contain detailed explanations of the correct answers to the multiple-choice questions, and provide grading outlines for the free-response questions that tell you how many points you’ll receive for mentioning specific things.

If you are taking the AP exam to secure college credit, then this guide should give you everything you need to get a high score.
product: HTS Code Reference Guide - Harmonized Tariff Schedule

text:
Author Zing Tsjeng’s background is in broadcast and online journalism (see the “Broadly.” Channel on Vice), which probably explains the weird typography in FORGOTTEN WOMEN: THE LEADERS. Throughout the text, short factual passages (maybe you could call them “sound bites”) are printed in type that is far larger than the type used for the text as a whole. Presumably this is done to make the best “points” stand out, but in a printed book, it’s like reading a book that someone else has heavily highlighted. It just doesn’t work.

The book also gives the impression that the mini-biographies are researched and compiled almost entirely from online sources, which may or may not be true. (Certainly the Bibliography supports that notion.) Online research is all well and good, but not as accurate or scholarly as one would like to see in a book meant to educate and inspire young adult readers.

I love the concept of this book--short biographies of little-known women who made significant contributions to the politics of their times. I also like the graphic illustrations very much. But in the end, I wouldn’t choose this book as a gift for a young reader, female or male.

The list of included women is useful for anyone interested in finding out more about “forgotten” female rebels, warriors, rulers, activists, and reformers (check the Table of Contents in “Look Inside This Book” for the list). Apparently the list was created with help from Gina Luria Walker, Professor of Women’s Studies at the New School of New York City, and Director of The New Historia.
product: HTS Code Reference Guide - Harmonized Tariff Schedule

text:
The five steps in 5 STEPS TO A 5: AP U.S. HISTORY 2018 are (1) set up a study program, (2) take a diagnostic test to determine your test readiness, (3) develop successful strategies for answering multiple-choice, short-answer, and essay questions, (4) review the specific material you are expected to know for the United States History Advanced Placement test, and (5) take two full-length practice tests to build confidence and test skills.

There is a daunting amount of substantive review material included in this book, but of course the book is intended for use alongside a year-long high school class in AP U.S. History. The included items that strike me as most valuable, in no particular order, are:

(1) The online dashboard that tracks your progress and performance (showing strengths and weaknesses by question categories, and your scores and percentile ranking compared to other students--all of this based on your answers in practice questions and exams);

(2) The practice exams (3 in the printed book--one diagnostic and two to be taken under simulated test conditions; 3 in the online platform) with questions similar to the ones in the actual test;

(3) The copious review material (pages 71-354, organized by major historical periods in U.S. history (Settling of the Western Hemisphere; Colonial America; British Empire in America; Resistance Rebellion, and Revolution; American Revolution and the New Nation; Establishment of New Political Systems; Jeffersonian Revolution; Rise of Manufacturing and the Age of Jackson; Union Expanded and Challenged; Union Divided; Era of Reconstruction; Western Expansion and Its Impact on the American Character; America Transformed into the Industrial Giant of the World; Rise of American Imperialism; Progressive Era; United States and World War I; Beginning of Modern America: the 1920s; Great Depression and the New Deal; World War II; Origins of the Cold War; Prosperity and Anxiety: the 1950s; America in an Era of Turmoil; Decline and Rebirth; Prosperity and a New World Order; Threat of Terrorism, Increase of Presidential Power, and Economic Crisis; Contemporary America: Evaluating the “Big Themes”); and

(4) The many multiple choice review questions with the correct answers explained in detail.

If you are taking an AP exam to secure college credit for knowledge acquired in high school, then it’s extremely important to study the exam itself. As is well known, the people who write exams have personal biases (biases that they may not even be aware of) that creep into the exams and affect the determination of “correct” answers.

By studying the explanations of the correct answers in this AP world history exam prep guide, you can learn what kinds of answers are expected from you. Armed with this knowledge of the exam itself, you can fully utilize your personal knowledge of U.S. history, give the examiners the answers they want to see, and score high on the actual exam.
product: Professional Trade Compliance Certification Study Guide

text:
The biggest mystery in A MEASURE OF DARKNESS: A NOVEL is what Jonathan Kellerman’s name is doing on the cover. This novel is clearly written by Jesse Kellerman, with little help from his famous father, because the writing and plotting aren’t very good. If Jonathan’s name weren’t there, the novel probably wouldn’t sell at all. (Oh wait—I guess that solves the mystery!)

I was very disappointed by this novel, because I really loved the Detective Jacob Lev novels previously published by the father-son writing team (see [[ASIN:B00SI0B9MM The Golem of Paris]] and [[ASIN:B019L4N80E The Golem of Hollywood]]). I suppose that part of the blame should go to the unambitious, working-stiff main character (Clay Edison), because his job with the coroner’s office sounds very dull indeed.

In the novel, a loud, paid-admission party at a renovated Victorian house located in a poor but gentrifying Oakland neighborhood ends with a shootout and some dead and injured attendees. Coroner-worker Clay is tasked with identifying a Jane Doe corpse found in the backyard storage shed, and with notifying the next-of-kin of a transvestite pedestrian who was run down by an attendee who was fleeing the gunfire. (Apparently it’s the coroner’s job to do these identifications and notifications.)

Tracing the victims’ backgrounds is a daunting task, but Clay is up to the challenge. Among other things, his inquiries involve interviews with the head of a “safe house” for transsexuals and transvestites in Oakland, and with the head of a “free school” in a hidden, forested corner of Marin County. Naturally Clay is in trouble all the time with his boss, because he spends too much time investigating.

The descriptions of the San Francisco Bay Area locales are reasonably accurate. The plot has various threads that get tied up in the end, but the storyline is boring. The book Is a fast read because the writing is careless and simplistic. One high point is a cameo appearance by Alex Delaware, father Jonathan’s famous psychiatrist/investigator, who pulls some strings to help Clay out.
product: HTS Code Reference Guide - Harmonized Tariff Schedule

text:
VOGUE KNITTING: THE ULTIMATE KNITTING BOOK (completely revised and updated, 2018 edition) is one of three truly excellent, comprehensive, how-to-knit references. The other two are [[ASIN:0762102489 Montse Stanley’s KNITTER’S HANDBOOK]] and [[ASIN:B006YDG100 June Hemmons Hiatt’s PRINCIPLES OF KNITTING]]. VOGUE KNITTING is probably the best choice for a new knitter, because of the modern photos, large-sized drawings of techniques (everything from the basic knit stitch to changing yarn colors in intarsia), and up-to-date discussions of yarns, charts, and designing garments (scarves, hats, sweaters, etc.).

The Stanley book is the knitting reference that I’d want to have with me on a desert island, because it’s compact and has every imaginable knitting technique in it somewhere (e.g., “twice-knit” knitting). But the illustrations are line drawings, so it’s old-fashioned.

The Hiatt book is an opinionated, “thinking knitters’” reference that discusses the pros and cons of alternative knitting techniques (e.g., is it best to knit one color at a time in each row of stranded knitting). It’s much admired by many intermediate and advanced knitters, and it’s a good choice for any serious knitter who already owns VOGUE KNITTING.

This completely revised and updated edition of VOGUE KNITTING is larger than the original book (published in 1989, revised and updated in 2002). Much of the technical material (text and drawings describing specific knitting techniques) is unchanged from the original book (except for the layout and some bolding added to the text). However, there is an entirely rewritten and expanded section on “Yarn and Supplies”, and there are many additional new pages (352 pages now, instead of the original 280 pages). There is also an improved Index.

When the 2002 updated edition of VOGUE KNITTING was published, it wasn’t enough different to justify a purchase if you already owned the 1989 edition. With this 2018 updated edition, there are enough improvements in presentation and enough new pages to warrant repurchasing the title for your knitting reference library. The book also continues to be a must-have general reference for knitters of all skill levels.
product: HTS Code Reference Guide - Harmonized Tariff Schedule

text:
ARM KNITTING: 30 NO-NEEDLE PROJECTS FOR YOU AND YOUR HOME is a perfectly decent technical knitting book, but it’s just plain silly. Why would you want to knit with big loops of thick yarn on your arms rather than on big knitting needles? And what do you do if nature calls while you’re still all wrapped up—quite literally—in your knitting? (The book is silent about removing loops to stitch holders.)

The looping techniques (cast on, knit, purl, bind off) for arm knitting are the same as for ordinary two-needle knitting. The techniques are clearly explained, and illustrated with step-by-step photos. There are 30 well-thought-out projects, ranging from scarves (of course) to hats (ditto) to pillow covers (natch) to throws (yes) to rugs (practical). There’s even a sweater and a vest.

Try this niche knitting technique for fun, but don’t take it very seriously. It’s NOT easier than knitting with needles—it’s just different and a lot less efficient.
product: Professional Trade Compliance Certification Study Guide

text:
You should read [[ASIN:0544947304 Bannerless]] before attempting this second “Bannerless” novel, THE WILD DEAD, because you need the backstory to understand what the brown-uniformed investigators (Enid & Teeg) are tasked with. Both novels are set in a near-future coastal California, some 100 years after a worldwide plague wiped out most of humanity. Without enough people to keep the infrastructure going, most human civilizations (and their cities) simply collapsed under their own weight (“the Fall”).

Survivors from the Fall saved what technology they could, and established new self-sustaining households along the Coast Road. But these households are governed by strict rules designed to preserve natural resources. All women receive contraceptive implants, and no household can have a child unless the household has been awarded a “banner”. Banners are in short supply. They are only awarded when a local governing committee determines that a household can support the additional person without straining existing resources.

After the Fall, there is little serious crime, except for households that break the Coast Road rules in attempts to illegally win banners. Whenever a rule breach is suspected or complained of, a two-person team of brown-uniformed investigators is dispatched (usually on foot, because only solar-powered vehicles still exist) to resolve the problem. The investigators are “the law”, and they are much feared for their authority to impose penalties and to even break up misbehaving households.

In both Bannerless novels, the main character is Enid, one of the investigators. In this novel, Enid is dispatched to the Estuary, several days’ hike up the Coast Road, to investigate a complaint about a local community’s refusal to help rebuild a structure belonging to one of its households. When Enid arrives, it is clear that the structure is in appalling condition, so deteriorated that it would be a waste of precious resources to rebuild it.

But before she can turn for home, a body shows up on a muddy beach. It’s a young woman with a grievous wound to her throat—clearly a murder victim. But the victim isn’t a local; she’s from “the wild”, the interior where groups of people are still living amidst the cities’ ruins. People from the wild don’t follow the Coast Road rules, so the crime is outside Enid’s jurisdiction—unless the murderer happens to be someone from the Estuary and the Coast Road.

Estuary people recognize the victim as someone they traded with, but won’t volunteer any information to Enid or Teeg, “the brown bullies”. Enid’s headstrong partner wants to hang the crime on the obvious suspect, without evidence. But Enid persists in seeking out the truth, even though it requires a dangerous solo trek into the wild.

I liked THE WILD DEAD, though not quite as much as I liked the earlier novel. Author Carrie Vaughn has done a terrific job in establishing a believable post-apocalypse society, and in developing the character of the thoughtful, likeable Enid. This is a police procedural carried out in a highly unusual setting.
product: Professional Trade Compliance Certification Study Guide

text:
SOUTHERN FROM SCRATCH: PANTRY ESSENTIALS AND DOWN-HOME RECIPES is my kind of cookbook, because there are so many recipes for jams, pickles, canned sauces, vinegars, relishes, applesauce, sauerkraut (fermented cabbage), and other “homely” foods. (I love to preserve fresh fruits and vegetables from my local farmer’s market.)

This is not at all like Paula Deen’s stylish Southern cooking. The cornbread recipe, for example, uses buttermilk and bacon grease—which is darned authentic. The recipe for southern-style pinto beans calls for pork fatback, which is similar to my dried beans recipe from Kentucky that uses salt pork (added after the beans are tender).

I’ll skip the recipes that incorporate homemade lard (my digestive system can’t handle lard), but I’m eager to try “Blackberry Lemon Sonker”, which is a sort of deep-dish cobbler. (I get fresh blackberries at the farmer’s market.)

Even if you don’t want to get into canning, or to sample the countrified recipes, you will like this book if you enjoy reading cookbooks or reading about food. There are many nice photographs of the prepared food, and the recipes are accompanied by a well-written text that explains how the dishes and their ingredients came to be adopted by Southern cooks and to become ubiquitous all over the South.
product: Professional Trade Compliance Certification Study Guide

text:
FASHION AND LIFESTYLE PHOTOGRAPHY: SUCCEED IN THE COMMERCIAL WORLD AND BECOME A STAND-OUT PHOTOGRAPHER delivers everything you want in a photography book—it’s gorgeous to look at, and it’s full of really valuable advice and tips for improving your own photographs.

The illustrations all have descriptive captions that explain how the photo was set up (e.g., with a silver reflector to show the model’s olive skin tone). This means you can compare the photos and really see the differences between the setups and tricks the photographer used to capture different looks or moods.

In addition to discussions of gear, technique, and lighting, there are extensive discussions of the fashion photography industry, building your career, the commercial photo shoot from start to finish, and building your brand.

This book should be extremely helpful to anyone desiring to market clothing designs online (e.g., patterns for knitted sweaters, scarves, hats). A professional-looking photo of a model wearing a garment can make a huge difference in how well a pattern sells. This book provides the necessary insights for taking those great photos!
product: HTS Code Reference Guide - Harmonized Tariff Schedule

text:
If you have a sewing machine that you use for mending or for making clothes, pillows, etc., then THE SEWING BOOK: OVER 300 STEP-BY-STEP TECHNIQUES is an excellent reference book to have on hand. It consists mostly of large photographs that show you exactly how to accomplish everything from the most basic tasks (e.g., a plain seam, applying top-stitching) to much more sophisticated couturier tasks (e.g., a triangular buttonhole, a boned bodice). Each illustrated technique is accompanied by a difficulty level rating (1 to 5 stars).

There is a highly informative, illustrated section on Tools (sewing equipment; fabrics; patterns). This is followed by the Techniques section (sewing essentials; darts, tucks, pleats, and gathers; facings and necklines; collars; waistlines, belts, and tie-backs; sleeves and sleeve finishes; pockets; hems and edges; fasteners; linings and interfacings; professional techniques; mending). Finally, the book includes complete instructions and patterns for several projects (two-tone tote bag; A-line shift dress; wrap skirt; unisex dressing gown; child’s reversible jacket; toiletries bag; envelope cushion; storage baskets; oven mitt; Roman blind).

I can’t recommend the projects (except for sewing practice), because they are so simple and lacking in style as to scream “homemade”. But I can enthusiastically recommend the rest of the book, which includes all of the techniques (yea, zippers!) that a sewer is ever likely to need.
product: HTS Code Reference Guide - Harmonized Tariff Schedule

text:
A STONE’S THROW: AN ELLIE STONE MYSTERY is a competently written mystery/thriller—your basic PI procedural. The PI is “girl reporter” Ellie Stone, a surprisingly thin-skinned Jewish lass (she’s outraged by fairly mild anti-Semitic jokes, and disapproving of any and all bad language). Her thin skin is surprising, because she drinks like a fish (there’s always a bottle of Dewars handy), and she considers herself “a modern girl” (she’ll sleep with a strange guy she met only hours before).

In the novel, newspaper reporter Ellie is drawn into the world of horse racing, as it supposedly existed around Saratoga Springs circa 1962. When covering a fire at an abandoned horse barn, she stumbles upon two burnt bodies. A fragment of unburnt racing silks on the jockey-sized male, and bits of red hair and fur on the normal-sized female, are just enough to launch Ellie’s quest to identify them. Autopsies reveal that the pair were murdered. Ellie uses her contacts with local police, gangsters, racehorse owners, track habitues, and addicted bettors to stay one step ahead of everyone else in getting the facts and publishing her stories ahead of the competition.

The writing is fine, but the plot is just barely interesting enough to keep the reader reading. Ellie herself isn’t particularly likeable (the drinking doesn’t help). Her behavior is often reckless (for example, she goes alone into the basement of a remote, derelict cottage), yet she’s constantly fearful that a stranger may break into her home. Ellie does find a racehorse (the handsome Purgatorio) that takes a shine to her; but this isn’t very believable, given that Ellie knows next to nothing about horses or thoroughbred racing.

Perhaps the other novels in the Ellie Stone series are better, but this one’s a snooze. It works fine as a standalone.
product: Professional Trade Compliance Certification Study Guide

text:
WITH MY HANDS: POEMS ABOUT MAKING THINGS is a pleasant, beautifully illustrated children’s picture book. It has short poems about “making” and “being a maker”, as well as about individual crafts (e.g., soap carving, building a cardboard spaceship, origami, baking cookies, etc.). The poems emphasize making things that are uniquely one’s own, and describe the feelings associated with creative craft activity.

The poems usually include rhyming elements, though they aren’t slavishly rhyming. The metre is awkward in spots in many of the poems. (I would like this book much more if it weren’t for the metre issues.)

The poems about specific crafts vary in quality. For example, I think that “Knitting” misses the mark. (It would be fine if it didn’t include the trite reference to “clicking-clacking” of needles. My knitting needles don’t click, so I wonder whether the poet has tried knitting.)

Overall, the book deserves good marks for encouraging creativity in young children.
product: HTS Code Reference Guide - Harmonized Tariff Schedule

text:
I don’t completely understand SIDE LIFE, but what a ride! Imagine a technology that transports you to an alternate universe (one of an infinite number), and drops you into the mind of someone facing an emotional crisis (e.g., Churchill planning the Gallipoli campaign). You experience that world first-hand, then automatically return to your own world when your preset travel time expires.

But there’s a catch. Because of entropy (the inevitable randomness of, or mistakes that occur in, any system), the highly advanced technology never returns you to quite the same world where you started. Your world may have “changed” a little (new furniture) or a lot (your best friend may no longer exist).

In the novel, you ride along with Vin, a tech entrepreneur who has been forced out of his wildly successful startup company, Sigmoto. Suddenly unemployed, Vin accepts an offer to house-sit a mansion built by a mysterious tech genius, Nerdean.

Owner Nerdean isn’t expected to return soon, if at all, but has left detailed maintenance instructions for her property manager—including a strict prohibition against turning off the power. Vin is exactly the right person to figure out what is responsible for the empty mansion’s high demand for electricity. His curiosity leads him into a series of personal triumphs, disasters, and narrow escapes from death.

The plot leaves some loose ends, and the technological “explanations” regarding travel between parallel universes can be confusing. But who cares, when the writing is professional; the characterization is solid; and the story is entertaining, with myriad unexpected twists and turns?
product: HTS Code Reference Guide - Harmonized Tariff Schedule

text:
CLEAN SWEEP: A CRIME NOVEL is a superb debut mystery/thriller—who’d have thought that an award-winning Canadian automobile journalist would have such a talent for fiction? The novel isn’t really a page-turner, but it’s entertaining and engrossing, and it has a rip-roaring ending. The characters are well-developed and memorable. I hope that we’ll see more of Pastor Tommy (and that his future illicit transport activities will continue to serve good purposes).

In the novel, Pastor Tommy Bosco, a reformed smuggler, runs The Guiding Light Mission, which is located in Winnipeg’s very worst skid row area. Most of his time is spent (1) preaching a hard line to his down-and-out followers, and (2) providing food (some of it “pre-owned” in local restaurants) and shelter (well outside the building codes) to the same. But Tommy has a sideline—when someone (anyone who asks for Tommy’s help) has angered the vicious local gang (the Heaven’s Rejects) so that they cannot stay in Winnipeg and stay alive, then Tommy extracts them and gets them safely “dead” and across the Canada/US border.

But extracting a local prostitute (Claire-Bear), who has killed an HR higher-up and stolen an encoded HR ledger, turns out to be Tommy’s most dangerous extraction of all. And it leads him into the midst of a conspiracy that not only endangers the lives of Winnipeg’s citizenry, but threatens to expand far beyond Canada’s borders and endanger the world’s population.

Author Clark deserves kudos for including a vehicle as a character of sorts (Freddie the Ford) without getting carried away with automotive detail. Whenever vehicles matter in the well-constructed plot, they are handled exactly right. As for the human characters, I definitely cared about what happened to them, and felt bad about the ones that didn’t survive.

Note to U.S. readers: the novel includes a fair amount of Canadian slang (e.g., Bordens, toonies, Peter Jacksons), but it is generally clear from the context what the word means. If it isn’t clear, then there’s aways Google.
product: HTS Code Reference Guide - Harmonized Tariff Schedule

text:
Just looking at sample LSAT questions can be extremely discouraging, but if you want to go to a good law school, you need an excellent score on the exam. You need to prepare as rigorously for the LSAT as for your future State Bar Exam. This book, LSAT UNLOCKED 2018-2019: PROVEN STRATEGIES FOR EVERY QUESTION TYPE + ONLINE (Kaplan Test Prep), along with the included online materials (one complete prep test, videos, workshops) should provide everything you need to do extremely well on your LSAT.

The book itself does a superb job of explaining the methods to use to quickly find the correct answers to each general type of exam question. It also explains in detail how the correct answer to each of the many included sample questions (taken from previous LSATs) was arrived at. Once you know how to approach each type of exam question, and how to recognize the questions to skip and return to if time permits, the odds that you will find the right answers within the allotted time increase astronomically.

Something that’s easy to overlook are the excellent exam preparation guides at the front of the book. These guides tell you which online materials and book sections to concentrate on, depending on how many months or weeks you have available before your actual test date. If your time is short, so that you don’t have time to read the whole book or watch many online videos, then you follow the prep guide for the time you actually have available—it tells you how to make the best use of your study/practice time.

To access the complete prep test and the other online materials, you must register your book at the Kaplan web site. (Be sure that you’re registering your book, NOT registering for a Kaplan course.) The site will ask you a question that can be answered by referring to the LSAT UNLOCKED 2018-2019 book—once you answer correctly, you will be able to complete your registration and see all the online materials.
product: Professional Trade Compliance Certification Study Guide

text:
Because I haven’t yet seen the latest “Star Wars” movie, I wanted this book, STAR WARS: ABSOLUTELY EVERYTHING YOU NEED TO KNOW, UPDATED AND EXPANDED (Journey to Star Wars: the Last Jedi), to get up to speed with popular culture. Well, the “cartoon graphic factoid” format doesn’t help me with the movie plots and overall story arc, but the book is visually appealing and fun to browse through. It’s a terrific source of “Star Wars” trivia.

The book actually works well as a “Star Wars” reference, because it has an excellent Index. I think it delivers excellent value for the money (hardcover about $12 on Amazon).
product: HTS Code Reference Guide - Harmonized Tariff Schedule

text:
A YEAR IN THE GARDEN: 365 INSPIRATIONAL GARDENS AND GARDENING TIPS is a gardener’s perpetual daily calendar (months and dates only, no days of the weeks or years) with beautiful full-color garden photos. It’s very heavy (4 lbs. 8.8 oz.) for such a small hardcover book (8” x 8”), and somewhat clumsy to handle.

Gardening enthusiasts will enjoy reading the “tips”, which are actually short, opinionated essays about garden topics rather than serious how-to advice. For example, the June 1 entry says, in part: “Rose archways not only provide support for the plants but are also eye-catching and can be used to partition space, if they’re the appropriate size, material and style for the garden. ...Choose as wide an arch as possible. Roses bloom more abundantly when their tendrils are not vertical but are guided horizontally in gentle spirals. …”

The accompanying photo for June 1 shows a lovely archway of light and dark pink roses that frames a rustic woven willow bench. The pages all have some space allotted for the owner’s “Notes/Birthdays”. The book also has a Table of Contents (in back) that identifies the subjects of the daily essays by date, so that the owner can use the book as a reference.

text:
THE ECHO KILLING: A MYSTERY is a competently, professionally written private investigator mystery, but it’s just plain boring. Also, the basic premise, that two murders committed 15 years apart must be linked because the crime scenes are almost identical, is weak and unconvincing from the get go.

In the novel, a journalist (Harper) investigates a career woman’s murder. The woman’s nude body was discovered on her kitchen floor by her 12-year-old daughter who had arrived home from school. Harper can relate to the girl, because the exact same thing happened to her when she was 12 (she discovered her mother’s nude body on the kitchen floor after school). The crime scenes are eerily similar—from the body’s unposed position on the floor to the fact that all of the evidence (clothing, fingerprints, blood, residue under fingernails) was removed.

Harper, hoping to finally identify her mother’s killer, is driven to investigate, and uses her special police reporter privileges to access evidence boxes, old and new police reports, etc. The investigation is a slog, as Harper manages to slowly recover various significant photos and documents. The only relief is provided by Harper’s illicit, white-hot relationship with an undercover police detective (Luke), who saves her life from time to time.

POSSIBLE SPOILER ALERT: The perp is guessable, though the perp’s motivation isn’t particularly believable—it’s wildly inconsistent with the perp’s character. There are a lot of reasonably well-drawn characters in the novel, but none of them stand out as memorable—not even Harper.
product: HTS Code Reference Guide - Harmonized Tariff Schedule

text:
Vigilante justice is seldom admirable, and I really dislike the “Sisterhood” vigilantes. NEED TO KNOW is like “The First Wives Club” (which I happen to love) on steroids. The sisters have a dark viciousness about them—laughing and giggling about all the bad things they are going to do to their chosen target—that cancels out for me any sense that they are working for good. The “Lady Justice” that they all salute whenever they enter their underground “war room” is ill-served by these women.

WARNING: POSSIBLE SPOILERS IN THIS PARAGRAPH: The plot is cynical in the extreme—the unlimited funds available to the sisters permit them to break any and all laws, including those against spy surveillance, kidnapping, blackmail, and torture, without being concerned about possible consequences. For example, to get privileged information about their target (an evil, money-grubbing man), one sister who is a lawyer visits another lawyer’s office and lies about her “client’s” intentions—a major violation of her profession’s ethical rules against pretexting/dissembling, fraud, dishonesty, and deceit by lawyers. As another example, while obtaining signatures on court documents by duress, two of the most respectable, loaded-with-money sisters actually sign their real names to the documents as witnesses without worrying that their identities can be traced to the criminal circumstances surrounding the signings. Their money insulates them, and provides the answer to everything.

This series novel (which works fine as a standalone) is pure extreme-feminist fantasy. The sisters and their contacts are women of great power and influence—a countess who has almost all the money in the world; a lawyer who has recently been named “Lawyer of the Year”; a former major newspaper editor-in-chief; a former Supreme Court justice who has resigned to do good for abused wives and children; a garden nursery owner; a world-famous, widely adored, popular singer. As for the men in the sisters’ lives—they adore the women, do the women’s bidding without question, and perform all the cooking and cleaning tasks. Even the watchdogs are female—a mother and her pups.

If you like this sort of escapist fiction, and don’t mind the amorality of the characters, then you will like this book. It’s not my cup of tea, but I recognize that the writing and plotting are professional.
product: HTS Code Reference Guide - Harmonized Tariff Schedule

text:
WRITTEN IN BLOOD introduces Joe “Preach” Everson, a damaged cop tasked with solving a series of murders staged like famous literary murders (Dostoevsky, Poe, Christie, Nabokov). That’s two current mystery/thriller cliches out of the way! Everson is probably healed by the end, but he isn’t all that interesting while damaged—his troubled mind is mostly useful for showcasing introspective reflections on the inhumanity of man. (Everson is to be forgiven, perhaps, because he’s a former preacher.)

The novel is reasonably well-plotted, but NOT so well written. Important details about the characters get lost in the verbiage of the early chapters, so that even the reader attentive for clues may find him/herself asking, “Where did I read before about that character’s background?” as specific items are referenced later. The narrative also includes a number of unnecessary, jarring similes (e.g., “He wished he was inside the lounge, … eating a bone-in rib eye as rare as a blue diamond” (ch. 34; uses two different meanings of “rare”)).

Also, there’s a heck of a lot of literary criticism, fed to Joe by his love interest, a bookstore clerk/comparative lit major/law student named Ari. As nearly as I can tell, the analysis of the context of the literary murders added nothing to the actual solution of the murders.

Layton Green is the author of the Dominic Grey series (beginning with [[ASIN:1477805087 The Summoner]], which I liked a little better.
product: HTS Code Reference Guide - Harmonized Tariff Schedule

text:
It’s a mystery, why THE DISAPPEARANCE OF ADELE BEDEAU is subtitled “A Historical Thriller”. It’s NOT historical (unless you consider a novel first published in 1982 to be “historical”), and it’s NOT a thriller (it’s slow-moving and extremely boring). It’s not even an “unreliable narrator” mystery, because the narrators’ accounts are totally accurate (allowing for the fact that one character is paranoid). What it is, is a psychological study of a loner personality (Manfred Baumann), and of the lasting effects of guilt (over a murder) on the two central characters (Baumann; and Georges Gorski, the policeman who’s investigating the disappearance).

In the novel, a waitress disappears from a bistro in Saint-Louis (a backwater town on the Swiss-French border). Gorski investigates, and interviews the bistro’s regulars (including Baumann). This routine inquiry triggers paranoia in Baumann, who is a perpetual outsider. He constantly observes the people around him without understanding or connecting with them; and the people respond with polite tolerance of Baumann’s presence. Baumann becomes obsessed with maintaining his daily routine and the appearance of normalcy.

In Gorski, the inquiry triggers guilt over a murder he investigated and “solved” many years earlier. Gorski believes the convicted murderer was innocent. Gorski also harbors guilt over his inability to make progress in solving the mystery of the waitress’ disappearance.

About a third of the way into the novel, there is an unexpected revelation about Baumann, which explains his retreat from society. However, the bulk of the novel records Baumann’s inner thoughts about appearances, and about his paranoid belief that Gorski is constantly watching him.

The novel is well-written, but boring. It’s okay as a literary mystery, but it’s a waste of time if you’re expecting a psychological thriller.
product: HTS Code Reference Guide - Harmonized Tariff Schedule

text:
The world probably doesn’t need another biography of Mark Twain, but this one is well-written, apparently solidly researched, and humorous. The focus of this book, HOW NOT TO GET RICH: THE FINANCIAL MISADVENTURES OF MARK TWAIN, is upon Twain’s lifelong obsession with money and the accumulation of lots of it. Luckily for readers of American literature, Twain’s moneymaking schemes and investments generally came to naught, so he was forced to continue his sideline occupation—writing.

The biography is particularly amusing for its tongue-in-cheek parallels between Twain’s financial efforts and modern “financial advisor speak”. For example, it notes that Twain was an “outlier”, who quit school early to pursue a lucrative real-world career. Twain proved to be something of a visionary, formulating the BHAG (Big Hairy Audacious Goal) of cornering the worldwide cocaine market in the year 1857. But he was forced to abandon that goal, when he arrived in New Orleans, on his way to the Amazon River, only to discover there were no ships sailing for Brazil (and there never had been any).

There’s much more, in the same vein, as this book chronicles Twain’s life and adventures.
product: Professional Trade Compliance Certification Study Guide

text:
RETROGRADE has a great concept, but it’s poorly executed. The concept: The international (U.S., China, Russia, Eurasia) colony on Mars is prepared for absolutely everything that can go wrong on Mars, but is totally unprepared for anything to go wrong on Earth. So when nuclear war breaks out, with no one knowing who fired the first missile, the colonists are left in an impossible situation. They must cooperate to survive—their resupply ship has just sailed right on past Mars (failure of automated braking rockets)—but they don’t trust one another.

This is a wonderful plot idea, but the story gets bogged down by far too much detailed description of the layout, construction, and environment of the colony itself. Plus the characters talk, talk, talk about what alternative actions are available, and why each alternative won’t work (e.g., the Russians won’t go for it), but are very slow to start doing anything. When the characters aren’t discussing matters, and worrying about the fate of loved ones back on Earth, the lead character (Liz) is thinking about everything around her, and describing whatever is before her eyes in minute detail (e.g., the wheat growing in the common area shared by all the colonists).

To my mind, there simply isn’t enough action to drive the plot. I can believe that the detailed description of what a Mars colony will be like is technically and scientifically accurate (or as much as it can be in 2017), but it’s just too much information piled into a thin story. Also, I think the novel would be far better with a male lead, because Liz doesn’t ring true to me as a female researcher/adventurer.

In the novel, when it becomes clear what started Earth’s nuclear war, the plot becomes more interesting. However, the whole story has a hurried finish that needs an Epilogue to tie up the many loose ends, and in one important aspect the story has been done before.
product: HTS Code Reference Guide - Harmonized Tariff Schedule

text:
MAKING THINGS TALK: USING SENSORS, NETWORKS, AND ARDUINO TO SEE, HEAR, AND FEEL YOUR WORLD is one of those books that make me wish for unlimited hobby time. It has so many projects that I’d like to try—from the Catcam to the physical object locators to the NFC two-factor authentication.

But this is a book for the serious maker/inventor (not the casual electronics hobbyist), because the projects require a lot of work—understanding theory, gathering and connecting parts, planning, coding, testing, etc. If you have the time, everything you need to know to complete the projects is fully explained, and there are wonderful step-by-step instructions (illustrated with photos, diagrams, and code examples).

This is NOT a book about adding audio to things, or about the Internet of Things—it is a book about networking things. As the author explains in the Preface, this book is “for people who want to make things talk to other things. Maybe you’re a science teacher who wants to show your students how to monitor weather conditions at several locations around your school district simultaneously, or a sculptor who wants to make a whole room of choreographed mechanical sculptures….This book is a primer for people with little technical training and a lot of interest. This book is for people who want to get projects done.”

To use this book, you need some basic knowledge of electronics and programming microcontrollers, and access to the Internet. You will also need to purchase parts (e.g., an Arduino breadboard), but the book includes suggestions for online sources of parts. Two books are recommended for background reading before you start the projects: [[ASIN:159200346X Physical Computing:Sensing and Controlling the Physical World With Computers]], and [[ASIN:1449363334 Getting Started With Arduino]].
product: Professional Trade Compliance Certification Study Guide

text:
I’m a big fan of Timothy Hallinan’s comic mysteries (e.g., [[ASIN:B0120AUB3I King Maybe]]); and this mystery, FOOL’S RIVER (A POKE RAFFERTY NOVEL), has totally sold me on the author’s “Poke Rafferty” series. The unfamiliar Bangkok setting and Thai characters were a little off-putting at first, but the complex, engrossing portrayal of Bangkok’s free-wheeling night life and ladyboy bar scene quickly became a real page-turner.

In the novel, the adopted daughter (Miaow) of a Bangkok writer (Poke) introduces Poke to a school friend (Edward) whose wealthy, dissolute, expat father (Buddy) has gone missing. When Poke makes inquiries, he uncovers a series of suspicious deaths of wealthy expats—all drowned in canals, their assets drained in the few days after they were last seen alive, and their deaths never fully investigated by possibly corrupt Thai police.

Poke involves his incorruptible Thai policeman friend (Arthit), whose investigation puts himself, his staff, and Poke in mortal danger, as they all strive to find Buddy before his assets and time on Earth run out. There are equally interesting subplots, involving the problem pregnancy and backstory of Poke’s wife (Rose); and the difficult life and lifestyle of Maiow’s transgender bar girl friend (Lutanh).

As the author informs us in an “Afterword”, he thought “it might be fun to bring together three or four simultaneous stories and see whether I could tell them all in a very compressed span of time, perhaps 48 hours.” In this novel, he has done exactly that, and woven everything together seamlessly in a continuous narrative, with rounded characters and an exotic, richly detailed background.

The author has homes in both California and Thailand, which is no doubt why the Bangkok setting is so convincing. He is also an extremely skilled writer.
product: HTS Code Reference Guide - Harmonized Tariff Schedule

text:
DESERT REMAINS: A GUS PARKER AND ALEX MILLS NOVEL has some bumpy writing (not-quite-consistent viewpoints), and a muddled, slightly disappointing ending, but overall it’s a page-turner of a mystery/thriller. Parker (a psychic who’s the real deal) and Mills (a veteran homicide detective nearing burnout) make an effective team, though Parker does all of the investigative heavy lifting. His visions of murders past and future are what provide new leads and evidence, and what ultimately drives the plot.

In the novel, a young female hiker is discovered dead in a cave outside Phoenix, Arizona. She was stabbed to death, and the cave wall displays a newly carved “petroglyph” depicting her murder. When Parker sees a TV report of the murder, he gets a disturbing vibe—a woman he sees standing behind detective Mills is being watched by the killer. Now. In real time.

Parker knows that the woman is in danger, and may be the killer’s next victim. So he calls Mills, who has used Parker’s services successfully in the past. The call sets off another joint investigation, as the hiker murder turns out to be the first in a series of similar brutal murders (bodies found in caves near petroglyphs left by indigenous peoples, along with the killer’s new rock carvings showing the murders).

A refreshing element of this novel, which is basically a police procedural, is that the investigation requires no lucky coincidences or dream revelations to move the plot forward. Once the reader accepts that Parker is a genuine psychic whose visions are solidly grounded in fact, the story moves along rapidly without requiring further suspension of disbelief.

The plot also includes some debunking of fake psychics, some forensic profiling by a detective (Chase) who may have left the FBI under a cloud, and some parental angst over Mills’ teenage son who is caught selling pot to an undercover FBI agent. Parker has a few too many visions, in my opinion—if he focuses, he can see far-distant people and their current surroundings (right down to the clothes they are wearing), and he can see future events (for example, that the fish a character will eat at dinner is bad).

Still, I enjoyed reading this novel.
product: Professional Trade Compliance Certification Study Guide

text:
DESCENT: MY EPIC FALL FROM CYCLING SUPERSTARDOM TO DOPING DEAD END is an “as told to” confessional--unlike many of these, it's not overly self-serving, and it's a decent, fast read. Former superstar cyclist Thomas Dekker takes responsibility for his illicit use of Dynepo and blood-doping, and doesn't whine “why me?” over getting caught.

Even though everyone (including Dekker and author Thijs Zonneveld) agrees that Dekker was an arrogant, self-centered guy with delusions of cycling grandeur, the reader can't help feeling sorry that such a promising young cycling talent was lost to the sport. When Dekker became a professional, nearly all of the top cyclists (Armstrong, Ullrich, etc.) were doping, and their teams simply looked the other way. Dekker’s team doctors at Rabobank picked up early on his doping, and merely offered to monitor him closely so he wouldn't get caught and reflect badly on the team.

Why did Dekker cheat? Because he wanted more--more wins, more money--and because the doping made him feel on top of the world during events. Did he know the doping was wrong? Absolutely.

In the end, this book doesn't plow any new ground re the doping problems in professional cycling, even though it names some names. It's mildly interesting for the details of Dekker’s travels to store and receive blood. There's nothing in it regarding the physical dangers of EPO and cortisone shots, so it's hardly a cautionary tale for aspiring pro cyclists.

Much of the book describes Dekker’s repeated self-destructive behavior, which includes skipping secondary school final exams, engaging in dissolute high-living (drinking, brothel visits, etc.), and letting family and cycling teammates down.

text:
BANNERLESS (THE BANNERLESS SAGA) is an entertaining “police procedural” set on a future Earth where civilization as we know it has collapsed under its own weight.

In the novel, a worldwide plague killed so many people, there weren't enough left to sustain the existing national and local infrastructures (e.g., transit systems), and everything just halted (causing “the Fall”). Plague survivors salvaged what scientific knowledge they could (e.g., basic drug formulas and books on farming procedures), but most advanced technical knowledge (e.g., computers) was lost.

People now live mostly in small, self-sustaining towns comprised of extended family groups. Population is strictly controlled, and only a family that demonstrates sufficient wealth to support another person can have a “banner” that authorizes a pregnancy. Competition for banners (children) is intense; “bannerless” pregnancies are terminated or severely penalized.

The novel’s main character, Enid, is a trained “investigator” who looks into, and sets punishments/penalties for, all sorts of social infractions (thefts and frauds, bannerless pregnancies, secret babies, cheating on grain trades, etc.). When Enid dons her investigator’s uniform, she is universally feared.

Enid is called to the town of Haven to investigate one of the rarest of post-Fall crimes--a suspicious death that is a possible murder. The victim is a handyman who lived alone, who was a social outcast because he was generally known to be “bannerless” (someone’s secret baby). Following good investigative procedures, Enid uncovers the facts surrounding his death, despite lukewarm help from Haven’s city leaders. When her investigation is complete, Haven’s hidden secrets are revealed, and the town is left shattered.

This thought-provoking novel creates a fascinating future society with elaborate social rules, has highly memorable characters, and includes a good mystery to boot. I hope that author Carrie Vaughn will be writing more books in this series.
product: Professional Trade Compliance Certification Study Guide

text:
This is the third Audrey Harte mystery (the first is [[ASIN:0316302503 It takes One]]). It works fine as a standalone, with just the right amount of teaser backstory that doesn't give away the earlier plots. Harte is certainly an oddball heroine--a licensed psychologist specializing in troubled teenagers, who is also a convicted murderer! The murderer part is okay, though, because her hometown (Edgeport, Maine) has lots of murderers who haven't been caught (a dysfunctional small town?); and Harte’s murder was a “good” murder. Her victim (Clint) was an evil man.

In the novel, Harte sets out to solve a domestic mystery--who is the biological father of Mackenzie Bell, the daughter of Harte’s now-deceased former best friend Maggie? Maggie was sexually abused by her father Clint, and as a troubled teen, she slept around. Mackenzie, when she learns who the Edgeport possibilities are, has second thoughts about the project to find out “where she came from”. Edgeport is an inbred backwater of a town.

But there's more to the story than a paternity search. Harte’s beloved Jake is threatened by a pair of blackmailers (can’t you trust a hired hit man to take your money and be done with it?). Both end up murdered, though it's clear that Jake didn't do them, because he wouldn't have left any mess. The killers’ identities are ultimately revealed in a last-minute surprise ending.

This is almost a “cozy” mystery, because it's leisurely paced, and mostly focused on people and relationships. But it's also a decent PI procedural. I enjoyed it, but am not sure that I want to read any more “Audrey Harte” series books. I liked Jake a lot, but didn't care much for Harte, even though she is an independent professional woman with believable investigative skills.

text:
HOW NOT TO KILL YOUR HOUSEPLANT: SURVIVAL TIPS FOR THE HORTICULTURALLY CHALLENGED is one of the best plant care books that I've come across. All of the instructions are extremely concise--exactly what busy people need. The basic selection, potting, and watering instructions are truly excellent.

Better yet, the book has two-page spreads devoted to specific plants (e.g., begonia, Boston fern, moth orchid, etc.) that describe general care needs (location, light, watering and feeding, care); and sick plant symptoms (e.g., wilting leaves, brown tips on leaves, yellowing leaves, dropping leaves, shriveled leaves, leggy plant) with specific instructions on what to do about the symptoms. There are specific care instructions for 119 different plants (covering nearly every plant sold in supermarkets, hardware stores, and big box stores).

There are also short sections on the commonest indoor plant diseases and pests. The Table of Contents has drawings of the covered plants, and there is a terrific index. This is the only indoor plant care reference that you should ever need.
product: HTS Code Reference Guide - Harmonized Tariff Schedule

text:
FOCUS IN PHOTOGRAPHY: MASTER THE ADVANCED TECHNIQUES THAT WILL CHANGE YOUR PHOTOGRAPHY FOREVER is a gorgeous “coffee table” book that is also an advanced photography reference book.

It explains how focus works in simple language that is accompanied by diagrams, in a way that even an amateur digital “point and shoot” photographer like myself can understand. It also explains things like how to make DIY lenses (e.g., a tilt lens) out of ordinary stuff (e.g., an enlarger lens plus accordion extension tubes).

The broad topics covered are: Introduction (The Zen of Focus); Light; Bokeh; Optics & Equipment; Closeup; Focus Stacking; Panoramas; and Software. There is a glossary that defines the technical terms, and a good index. This book would make a welcome gift for any casual or serious photographer.
product: HTS Code Reference Guide - Harmonized Tariff Schedule

text:
THE SORBONNE AFFAIR: A HUGO MARSTON NOVEL is the 7th police procedural novel featuring former FBI profiler Hugo Marston, who is currently the head of security for the U.S. Embassy in Paris. It's extremely slow-moving, despite the added subplot that perhaps explains why Hugo (and his best friend Tom Green) left the FBI. The “Sorbonne” in the title isn't the landmark university building, but a fictional luxury hotel.

In the novel, a best-selling romance writer, an American, seeks Hugo’s help after discovering a spy camera hidden in a painting in her hotel room. The camera is aimed at the desk of the writer (Helen Hancock), so she’s convinced that someone is trying to plagiarize her novel-in-progress. Multiple murders follow, including a hotel staffer and one of Helen’s writing workshop students. Hugo's investigation is severely hampered by the characters’ lies regarding the true nature of their relationships with Helen and one another.

In the subplot, Hugo and Tom are threatened by news of the early release of a bank robber who has vowed to kill them both. The story of the robbery-gone-sideways is told in flashback chapters sprinkled throughout the main narrative.

The writing is good, but the main plot is boring and filled with too many red herrings. Also, there are poorly-integrated references to Sherlock Holmes and Agatha Christie novels that don't really advance Hugo’s investigation. The person behind the murders is guessable, and the ultimate resolution of the subplot is both silly and a letdown. I really liked the previous Hugo Marston novel, [[ASIN:1633881776 The Paris Librarian]], but was disappointed by this one.
product: HTS Code Reference Guide - Harmonized Tariff Schedule

text:
The BIG BOOK OF WHY ACTIVITY BOOK (A TIME For Kids Book) is exactly the sort of activity book that I loved as a kid--and still enjoy reading through. This one has lots of facts sprinkled in among a variety of easy puzzles (connect the dots, crosswords, copy the drawing, fill in words to finish the story, etc.).

As a fact example, who knew that laundry “surfactants” have molecules that combine with dirt at one end and water at the other end, so that they pull dirt out of clothes? My favorite page is a cool timeline of the Internet. The general fact topics are: Animals, Earth, Space, Humans, Around the World, History, Science, Technology, Sports.

I’m not sure what grade level or age is appropriate for this book--it doesn't say--but I’d guess third grade or higher, because some reading ability is required. Kids should have fun with the book, if they're willing to set aside their computers and/or phones for a while. (The activities may seem awfully tame for a child who plays even simple computer games like the JumpStart games.)
product: Professional Trade Compliance Certification Study Guide

text:
THE WALLS is a rather slow-moving novel about a severely abused wife's inner turmoil regarding staying with her husband (if she leaves, will he carry out his threats to her teenage son and elderly father?), and plotting her ultimate escape (can she use her prison-insider knowledge to plot a murder, and get away with it?).

The “perfect crime” she envisions is mundane, with an imperfect alibi (watching a movie by herself?). The crime also takes a decidedly back seat to her constant thoughts about protecting the two close family members who mean the world to her. They, of course, think the husband is a terrific guy, and are unaware of the abuse.

As a mystery/thriller, the novel falls short. However, it seems accurate enough in portraying a woman who is trapped in an abusive marriage. It isn't always so easy for a woman to call the cops on a husband, or simply to divorce him. In my opinion, the value of this novel lies in its food for thought: Does the abuse inflicted on the heroine by her controlling, abusive husband justify the heroine's choice of escape route? Could she have done anything else? Should she be forgiven?

I ordered this book as a mystery/thriller, and was disappointed when (1) it turned out to be women's literary fiction, not general escape fiction; and (2) the prison setting seemed incidental to the overall plot.

text:
THE DOWNSIDE is a crime caper novel with all the right stuff: an impossible-to-access, heavily guarded, electronically monitored vault; a ragtag heist team with awesome excavation and building-trades skills; a likeable, “honorable” chief thief; conspiracies and double-crosses; and humorous, unexpected plot twists. As an added plus, there is monster equipment (a gigantic mine excavator, a heavy-haul locomotive) that figures into the plot!

In the novel, pro thief Finn, who only steals big and heavy stuff, is out of prison and planning to go straight. But the bank where his $60,000 nest egg was stored is out of business, and his safe deposit box is in government hands. So Finn accepts a new job from his previous employer. It's simple enough--just shift around some counterfeit bars of rhodium inside a private, ultra-high-security, buried vault.

Finn quickly reassembles his team from his previous job--the job (theft of train cars full of ore) that went totally south and sent him to prison. He figures that working with the same guys in close proximity on a new heist will help him finger the traitor who sold out the failed job. Finn adds a highly skilled computer expert to supplement the team’s trade skills, and the vault caper is on!

This is a fast-moving story, with vivid characterization, and the plot is believable. The plan should have worked--and almost does, until something unexpected and unplanned happens at the last minute. Some readers may guess the caper’s ending, but I didn't, and there are still more twists to come, up to the very last page.
product: Professional Trade Compliance Certification Study Guide

text:
THE PARIS SPY: A MAGGIE HOPE MYSTERY finds Maggie in WWII occupied Paris, seeking to learn the fate of a Special Operations Executive (SOE) spy who stopped including security checks in her transmissions to England. The 7th “Maggie Hope” novel, this one continues from where [[ASIN:0804178720 The Queen’s Accomplice]] left off. It starts slowly, and it doesn't work well as a standalone.

This is a thoroughly researched historical novel that is based on all the facts presently available about the sadly compromised SOE project that lost--or intentionally sacrificed--many brave young female spies during WWII. At times it reads almost like nonfiction, and I found it very hard to separate the likely historical truths from the novelist’s fictional speculations about (1) the motives and loyalties of the crucial characters, and (2) what was really going on inside the SOE and the war effort.

For example, the novel includes imaginary “fly-on-the-wall” scenes from Churchill’s top-secret war council meetings, at which the PM personally chooses the invasion site (Normandy) and says that deception to keep the secret is England’s highest priority. The scene establishes the importance of Maggie’s extremely dangerous mission, and rings more or less true, but of course we cannot know what anyone said at those meetings or what England’s priorities were. There are also fly-on-the-wall scenes featuring Hitler at Wolf’s Lair.

Maggie’s foolhardiness in jumping into her situation (as established in the previous novel) undermined my sympathy for her fears and her general plight. It also wasn't very believable that the French Resistance could supply her with a trunk full of perfectly fitting designer clothes to support her cover persona.

The novel is well-written, with strong characters, and it is a solid addition to the “Maggie Hope” series. Maggie’s fans won't want to miss it.
product: Professional Trade Compliance Certification Study Guide

text:
I love popular reference books, like SUPER CLEAN SUPER FOODS, which have photos, drawings, factoids, recipes, and descriptions of specific foods (e.g., quinoa, cashews, peas, moringa powder). In this book, each 2-page layout for a supposedly-healthy-to-eat food is “eye candy” for the food lover in me.

I also love the descriptions of why each food is supposed to be “healthy” (e.g., quinoa is high in iron that promotes blood health, in manganese that promotes nerve health, and in zinc that promotes immune system health). I prefer books that explain the reasons behind the ingredients choices in their “healthy” recipes.

If I care about the accuracy of any specific “healthy” claim, I can research the issue on the Internet. Mostly I’m content to accept the general accuracy of the claims in this book, because the received wisdom on what foods are good or bad for us is constantly changing. For every study that claims a food or supplement is healthy, there seems to be another that says the opposite!

My favorite parts of this book are the one-day-eating guides with menu suggestions for specific health goals: Eating for Gut Health; Eating for Sleep; Eating for Brain Health; Eating for Immunity; Eating for Skin Health; Eating for Energy. For example, Eating for Gut Health advises drinking mint-infused water; breakfasting on whole grains; snacking on a banana or yogurt; lunching on pasta salad; and eating lean meat, healthy fats, and vegetables at dinner.

There are lots of interesting recipes (e.g., Grilled Salmon With Kale Pesto); and many easy-to-fix suggestions for using specific foods (e.g., mix cooked quinoa with ground cumin and cilantro, chopped parsley and breadcrumbs; bind with egg; shape into patties and pan-fry until golden brown).
