text:
INTRODUCTION TO INFORMATION LITERACY FOR STUDENTS is a textbook for computer research courses. It has decent flowcharts that describe the basic concepts of Internet and library research. However, the text is incredibly wordy, and it talks down to the reader (especially true if the text is intended for college students or adults, as it seems to be).

For example, from the first and second paragraphs of chapter 9, “Books”: “Books spark the imagination. They take us to worlds that no longer exist and others that are far, far away.… If books can broaden our minds, touch our hearts, and inspire our souls, they also can help us reach more immediate ends by informing our research.” Much more generalization in the same vein follows, until the reader finally reaches the actual first topic (how to search a library's online catalog) at the bottom of the next page.

I can't imagine assigning this textbook, expecting students to actually read the basic text. Over and over, my attention wanders off as I try to read it. There are some valuable observations here (e.g., avoiding reliance on Wikipedia if you're doing academic research (see chapter 13, “Web Pages”)), but digging those worthwhile nuggets out is far too much work.

I was hoping for a good chapter on distinguishing between reliable and unreliable sources, but couldn't find anything useful. In chapter 5, “Evaluate Sources”, the key questions it suggests you ask are: “Is it relevant?” “Is it reliable?” “Is it recent?” This is supplemented by suggestions for critical evaluation of books, periodicals, and web pages, which conclude that scholarly sources, long-established book publishers, and library holdings are the most reliable sources. “Fake news” isn't even in the glossary or index.
product: Professional Trade Compliance Certification Study Guide

text:
When I was in grade school, I enjoyed Scholastic’s WEEKLY READER, except that the teacher’s hated pop quizzes took much of the learning pleasure away. This WEEKLY READER: SUMMER EXPRESS (Between Grades 2 & 3) workbook shows that the Scholastic folks haven't lost their touch. It's filled with a lot of interesting puzzle activity pages (70 in all) for kids to complete (1 per day for 10 weeks) in the summer between 2nd and 3rd grade.

There's a complete answer key for parents, and a page of star-shaped stickers for “grading” and rewarding the child's work. The puzzles help the child review 2nd grade skills, and get a head start on 3rd grade skills, in math, reading, writing, grammar, and vocabulary.

I find these fun to do, so parents and kids shouldn't be totally bored. For example, one Week 7 page shows a cartoon octopus in front of a school supplies store, and asks the question, “Why is the octopus crossing the road?” To get the answer, the child works 12 subtraction problems to get a number that represents a letter; then the child writes the letter over a corresponding numbered blank to decode a sentence that reads, “To get a pen for its ink.”
product: Professional Trade Compliance Certification Study Guide

text:
MURDER IN SAINT-GERMAIN is my first “Aimee Leduc” novel, and I suspect that it's NOT among the best in this long-running series. (It's the 17th “Aimee Leduc Investigation”; the first is [[ASIN:1616957301 Murder in the Marais]].) Aimee comes across as a dismayingly disorganized private investigator (surely she's not just inept)--her transportation is an unreliable scooter, she can't ever reach her business partner when she needs to, she takes on complex new assignments when she's already strapped for time, and she's constantly scrambling to find trustworthy caregivers for her infant daughter (Chloe).

In the novel, Aimee runs two concurrent extra investigations atop her contracted computer systems security work at the prestigious Ecole des Beaux-Arts. The first investigation begins as simple scanning of the art school’s emails on behalf of a famous professor (Jules Dechard); it eventually uncovers clandestine sexual liaisons and blackmail threats that threaten the school’s very existence. The second investigation requires locating a presumed-dead Bosnian warlord who may or may not still exist; the warlord or someone else may be plotting to murder Aimee’s extremely busy counterterrorism investigator friend (Suzanne).

Does the plot sound complicated? You bet it is. Plus Aimee herself becomes a murder suspect, gets imprisoned, constantly fends off colleague’s entreaties to visit her dying former mentor (Morbier), and tries to handle her former husband's suspicious insistence on babysitting Chloe.

The investigations are resolved by novel’s end, but the story nevertheless ends with a significant cliffhanger involving Aimee’s back story. This was annoying, even for someone like myself who was ignorant of the back story. The Paris setting is described with considerable detail, and is probably accurate. However, I found the plot to be impossibly cluttered, and I never could warm to Aimee (who carries wigs and other silly disguises around for use at a moment’s notice).

Series fans will of course want to read this book for the information about Aimee's ongoing personal and work relationships.
product: HTS Code Reference Guide - Harmonized Tariff Schedule

text:
Originally published in 1980, THE TWENTIETH DAY OF JANUARY provides a detailed blueprint for a foreign power’s successful intervention in a U.S. Presidential election. Back then, the story surely seemed plausible, but probably somewhat farfetched. Fast forward to 2017, and the novel is disquieting to read, as it illustrates how easy it might be for a foreign power to co-opt an election to get the President of its choice.

In the novel, it's 1980, and British intelligence has discovered that a top aide (Dempsey) to the likely U.S. President-Elect (Powell) had belonged to the Communist Party some 22 years earlier. Acting on the assumption that the FBI may be ignorant of Dempsey’s past affiliation, Special Branch dispatches an agent (MacKay) as an off-the-books liaison to the CIA. When Powell, an “outsider” grassroots candidate, is easily elected, the CIA agent (Nolan) placed in charge of this hot potato has only 66 days (until the 20th of January when Powell is inaugurated) to investigate whether the candidate is controlled by the Soviet Union.

The investigation must be off-the-books, to avoid alerting Powell, Dempsey, and Powell’s supporters to the CIA’s suspicions. The investigation is also a no-win situation for the CIA, because it will appear to be an illegal effort to unseat an elected President, even if the CIA determines that Powell was elected due to Soviet efforts. And it also presents the problem of what to do with Powell, if he is in fact beholden to the Soviets.

This is a classic Cold War spy procedural, somewhat intellectual, without high-intensity action scenes. However, there are lots of bodies, the investigators are placed in serious danger, and the plot is complex and believable. And it shows--as one important character observes--that stealing a Presidential election can be surprisingly simple, if you're playing a long game.
product: HTS Code Reference Guide - Harmonized Tariff Schedule

text:
THE FORGOTTEN GIRLS is the sixth “Stevens and Windermere” novel (the first is [[ASIN:B005GSZJ9I The Professionals]]), and it works fine as a standalone. It's inspired by the all-too-real Vancouver murders of some 49 “throwaway” women (mostly prostitutes and women of Native descent). It was known that women were vanishing from the Downtown Eastside, but the police and city ignored the problem for far too many years.

In the novel, FBI agents Kirk Stevens and Carla Windermere are assigned to investigate an oddball murder--a photo of a brutally beaten woman has shown up without warning on a tractor salesman’s cellphone (a replacement for a stolen one--tech-savvy folks will know where the photo could have come from). The woman's body had already been recovered from an Idaho snowdrift near the Northwestern Railroad’s “High Line”--a stretch of track known to itinerant “train hoppers” as one where female drifters riding alone tended to disappear.

The agents end up in Butcher’s Creek, Idaho, a tiny stop for railroad crews, where they encounter the local rumors about all the missing women (mostly waitresses and train hoppers) who are believed to be victims of a man who rides the freight trains on the High Line. While Stevens and Windermere are in Idaho, they investigate a new murder, and then the disappearance of another woman--that they may possibly be in time to save.

The story's setting is along the railroad tracks that run from Chicago to Spokane, near the U.S.-Canadian border. The book is slow-moving--and even a little confusing--at times, but the plot has unexpected twists, and the exploration of why multiple deaths of certain classes of women may go unnoticed and never investigated is interesting in its own right.

Stevens and Windermere are a likeable pair, and the series books are always entertaining. This one had a bonus element for me, because I love trains, and particularly enjoyed the details about train hopping, and the mystery of how the murderous rider was able to hop trains in the winter without freezing to death.
product: HTS Code Reference Guide - Harmonized Tariff Schedule

text:
CRUEL IS THE NIGHT tells an ugly story. Two couples, presumably old friends, meet in an apartment in London’s fabulous Shard skyscraper, but one husband arrives with a packet of strychnine intended for the other. In the course of a single evening, all four unfaithful spouses drink heavily, engage in clandestine liaisons, and maneuver with murderous intent. Eventually the elegant reunion dinner deteriorates into total madness, leaving three dead and one on the run from the crime scene.

The story is told from multiple viewpoints, with the characters’ thoughts set out in rotating chapters. The storytelling is well done--the developments as the evening unfolds are easy to follow, and there are no annoying chapter-ending cliffhangers. The thoughts of the husbands (a greedy, wealthy banker, and a crusading-for-the-people journalist) and wives (a beautiful trophy wife, and a professional woman) reveal conflicting, flawed philosophies; and also that three of the spouses hushed up a friend’s murder long ago.

The translation from the Finnish is excellent, and all the many diverse plot threads are tied up successfully at the end. However, I did NOT enjoy the long, cruel night that I spent (as a reader) with these four extremely unpleasant people.
product: HTS Code Reference Guide - Harmonized Tariff Schedule

text:
If you're a wine connoisseur, the “Verlaque and Bonnet” mystery series has an extra bonus, because of the name-dropping of all the vintage wines enjoyed by the sophisticated Aix-en-Provence investigators. If you're not a wine connoisseur (I’m certainly not!), there's still a lot to like about this pleasant, well-plotted series--for example, the wonderful depiction of real life in the present-day French countryside. Each of the series books works fine as a standalone; this is the sixth in the series. (The first is [[ASIN:0143119524 Death at the Chateau Bremont]].)

In THE CURSE OF LA FONTAINE, Antoine (a ”juge d’instruction”, more or less equivalent to a detective) and Marine (a law professor) are newlyweds. They're fond of a new local restaurant, La Fontaine, which takes its name from an adjoining private garden containing a fountain with a dark history. When something evil happens in the garden (e.g., a hanging; a Nazi execution of resisters), the fountain stops flowing until the evil perpetrators are brought to justice.

The restaurant, already in trouble with its powerful, aristocratic neighbors for adding outdoor seating in the garden, may be forced to close altogether when the garden becomes a crime scene. A body is found, buried in a shallow grave--and the fountain has once again stopped flowing. It's up to Antoine, with help from Marine, to identify the mysterious corpse, to find the killer, and to see that justice is done, so that the fountain’s curse will be lifted.

This novel, like the others in the series, proceeds at a fairly leisurely pace, and affords the U.S. reader a glimpse into the fact-oriented criminal justice system used in France, that is quite different from the adversarial system used in the U.S. and Great Britain. The plot is interesting, the characterization is excellent, and both Antoine and Marine are highly likeable.
product: Professional Trade Compliance Certification Study Guide

text:
THE STANDARD GRAND: A NOVEL is an impressive example of what I see as moderately “experimental” fiction. There is a story, but the vivid, descriptive words just pile up, and pile up, until the reader is nearly exhausted by the weight of all the language. The prose makes me think of Proust or Joyce--it's actually too rich for me.

The story is a fantasy about U.S. war veterans, both male and female, who are coping with post traumatic stress syndrome after completing tours in Afghanistan, Iraq, etc. The “Standard Grand” of the title is a private rehab (survival) camp in the Catskills, where some extremely damaged vets go to find a structured existence.

It's an interesting book, and the prose is a treat for lovers of the English language. The novel is serious literature, and I suspect that it will find its way onto many a college class reading list.
product: Professional Trade Compliance Certification Study Guide

text:
MURDER ON THE SERPENTINE is the 32nd (!) Charlotte and Thomas Pitt novel, and the last before a new generation of Pitts takes over the series. It's my first Anne Perry novel, and now I know what I’ve been missing. This novel works fine as a standalone, but I’ll be going back to the beginning ([[ASIN:0345513568 The Cater Street Hangman]]) for more of these.

In the novel, Queen Victoria asks Thomas, the newly-promoted head of Special Branch, to investigate the death of her trusted friend, Sir John Halberd. Sir John’s death--at night in a rowboat on the recreational Serpentine lake in London’s Hyde Park--was hushed up, because Sir John seemed to be there for an assignation. The Queen further tasks Thomas with completing Sir John's secret assignment, which involved examining the affairs of Alan Kendrick, a close friend of the Prince of Wales.

Thomas feels inadequate for the task, especially because he cannot discuss the case with Charlotte. The affairs of Special Branch are off-limits even to spouses, and he has always relied on Charlotte’s help in interpreting the behavior of the privileged upper classes. But Thomas’ police detective background serves him well, in quickly establishing that Sir John was murdered. But what secret did Sir John possess that was worth killing him to silence him? Is it related to the possibility of a second Boer War? Of course, Charlotte guesses that Thomas needs her help, and independently gathers important information even without knowing the case details.

This is a well-written, well-plotted, entertaining historical mystery, set in London near the end of Queen Victoria’s long reign. Unlike many novels that come at the end of a long-standing series, this is a carefully crafted work, full of rich background detail. It gives the reader full value for her/his time and money.
product: Professional Trade Compliance Certification Study Guide

text:
THE ONE-EYED JUDGE: A NOVEL is the second entry in this unusual legal procedural series, which ls written from the perspective of a federal criminal judge who is hearing the fictional cases in the books. (The first novel is [[ASIN:1480441945 The Hanging Judge]], but so far the series novels work as standalones.) The procedural law--including references to things that a judge may and may not do (in chambers, in the courtroom, and outside the courtroom) when hearing a particular case--seems very sound. But you would expect that, when the author is in fact a sitting US federal district court judge (United States District Court for the District of Massachusetts)!

In the novel, Judge David Norcross has one bad eye (injured in the previous novel), and a tricky child pornography case that he probably should have recused himself from. The FBI is prosecuting an open-and-shut child pornography case against a local literature professor (Sidney), who specializes in Lewis Carroll's work, and who is a colleague of David's girlfriend (Claire). Sidney was caught in an FBI sting--he accepted delivery of a pornographic DVD that he can't remember ordering--and Claire springs to his defense. This puts David and Claire's romance on hold during what looks to be a long trial, and creates a conflict-of-interest minefield for David to navigate.

The trial's subject matter is unpleasant, and Sidney's case isn't helped by his heated defense of Carroll's practice of photographing nude children (with their parents' consent). To make matters worse, childless David is unexpectedly saddled with caretaking of his two young nieces, when their mother is killed, and their father gravely injured, in an airplane crash.

Judge Norcross is a complex, well-drawn character; and the plot has many surprising twists. But perhaps the best thing about this novel is that it painlessly teaches the reader so much about how the legal system really works. For example, there is a huge difference in the sentences handed down, depending on whether the FBI prosecutor decides to charge a defendant with "possession" of child pornography (no mandatory minimum sentence), or with "receipt" of child pornography (mandatory minimum 5 years).

I liked the first "Judge Norcross" novel a lot, and I think that THE ONE-EYED JUDGE is even better. Judge Michael Ponsor seems to have become comfortable with writing fiction, because the narrative in this novel lacks the stiffness occasionally found in the earlier book.
product: HTS Code Reference Guide - Harmonized Tariff Schedule

text:
ROCKS BEAT PAPER: A WILSON NOVEL is purely a caper novel--Wilson is after a fortune in uncut diamonds and diamond jewelry stored in a location with all-but-impossible-to-breach security. The plot starts slowly (too much detailed description of the security measures), but if you stick around, it moves fast and has many, many unexpected twists up to the final two pages.

Wilson is a stone killer, who will shoot or sacrifice anyone who gets in the way of a successful heist--and "anyone" includes members of his own criminal caper team. But Wilson doesn't kill or abandon people unless he has to, so he isn't all bad. He's just utterly focused on the job at hand (getting the loot), on his own survival, and on not leaving anyone alive who may rat him out.

In the novel, Wilson is hired by a jewelry store employee, as part of a 10-person team, to clean out the massively-secured store's diamond stock. When the employee dies unexpectedly, Wilson goes after the rocks with only part of the original team. But his operation goes seriously sideways, because the left-out members of the team are also after the rocks. Naturally Wilson makes another attempt, because, after all, this is a caper novel.

After the slow start, I enjoyed ROCKS BEAT PAPER a lot. Wilson and his con man friend Miles are memorable characters, and there's plenty of action involving safecrackers (the two Diegos), the musclemen (Johnny and Tony), the computer whiz (Elliott), the female wheelman (Monica), the facilitator (Jake), and the two jewelers (unscrupulous Donny and demented Saul).
product: HTS Code Reference Guide - Harmonized Tariff Schedule

text:
For an O'Reilly book, DESIGNING FOR WEARABLES: EFFECTIVE UX FOR CURRENT AND FUTURE DEVICES seems a little short on technical content. About half of it is a fairly comprehensive, descriptive catalog of existing wearable devices (e.g., FitBit, Jawbone, Pebble smartwatch, Google Glass, Lumo Lift, GoPro, Pokemon Go, Thync). The remaining half is devoted to to actual designer's thinking, tools, problems, and methods (e.g., service design and machine learning, embodiment and perception, prototyping and prototyping tools, selling the invisible).

The author points out that wearable devices offer "services", so that designers must focus on UX (user experience), and create interfaces that translate the data gathered by the devices into things that are helpful and meaningful to the users. For example, an activity tracker that records steps is a mere pedometer, unless the tracker does extras, such as tabulating the wearer's daily, weekly, and monthly activity, heart rate, calories burned, sleep time, etc.

For a wearables designer, an important consideration is figuring out what sort of technology is available to gather the data that can be reported in ways that the user will find helpful and motivating. For example, the Fitbit Charge HR uses a photoplethysmogram (PPG) sensor to measure heart activity, and a gyroscope and accelerometer to count steps.

If you're not an actual designer, but happen to own one (or more) of these devices, it's interesting just to read up on what it's intended to do (from a designer's perspective), and how it actually works.
product: Professional Trade Compliance Certification Study Guide

text:
THE DEVIL'S TRIANGLE is my first Catherine Coulter thriller, and it doesn't inspire me to read more of her novels. Fourth in the "Brit in the FBI" series (the first is [[ASIN:B00C5R7H70 The Final Cut]]), it works fine as a standalone. However, it's a plodding, run-of-the-mill thriller in which a team of nearly immortal (you-can't-kill-'em-but-maybe-you-can-wing-'em-if-you're-really-lucky) covert FBI operatives save the U.S. Government and the world once again. Neither the exotic settings (Venice, Rome, the Bermuda Triangle), nor the fantasy elements (the evildoers' ability to control the weather, thanks to notes from Leonardo da Vinci; the evildoers' mad, obsessive quest for the Ark of the Covenant) are sufficient to lift the novel above the ordinary.

In the novel, a professional thief (Kitsune aka "the Fox") delivers to anonymous clients in Venice an artifact (Aaron's rod) that she stole from the military-guarded Topkapi Palace. Instead of paying
Kitsune, the clients send death squads after her. Kitsune enlists the aid of her FBI enemies from prior series novels (the "Covert Eyes" team of Michaela "Mike" Caine and British-raised Nicholas Drummond), by persuading them that a massive Gobi Desert sandstorm--currently burying Beijing and killing thousands--is not a natural weather phenomenon, but is a man-made event.

After Mike and Nicholas arrive in Venice, their meeting with Kitsune is thwarted by a shootout in the Piazza di San Marcos that leaves seven bad guys dead and one FBI team member (Lia) seriously wounded. Eventually Kitsune successfully contacts Mike and Nicholas. With her help and with help from the FBI team's incredibly skilled teenage computer expert (Adam), Mike and Nicholas identify Kitsune's clients as the evil, "identical" Korath twins (Ajax and Cassandra). They also figure out that the mad twins are seeking the Ark of the Covenant. Mike and Nicholas and Kitsune head off to beard the twins in the Koraths' castle stronghold near Rome, and kill a bunch more bad guys on their way. The novel goes on from there, with more killings and close escapes--it's a very long thriller at 512pp--and eventually the team winds up on an island in the Bermuda Triangle.

The well-written novel has lots of action scenes, even though it's not really a page-turner. It's a decent read, as thrillers go, but nothing to shout home about. I expected much more from an established, popular writer like Coulter.
product: HTS Code Reference Guide - Harmonized Tariff Schedule

text:
MURDER IN PLAIN ENGLISH: FROM MANIFESTOS TO MEMES is a serious academic work, co-authored by a literary criminology professor and an anthropology professor. The book is an examination of "murder" and murderers, using a "literary criminology" approach. This approach is defined as "the study of crime through the lens of literature and language"; it is an approach that includes "the study of half-baked literary attempts by the murderers themselves."

The word "half-baked" clues the reader that this is an opinionated study. Personally, I don't find the authors' analyses of the murderers' writings at all persuasive. For example, in analyzing O.J. Simpson's "suicide letter" (from the time of the infamous "slow speed chase"), they see O.J.'s statement, "First, everyone understand that I have nothing to do with Nicole's murder", as "a weak denial or avoidance strategy" that fails to "assert that he did not kill Nicole". To me, it seems to assert flatly that he did not kill Nicole--but the authors obviously read more into the words and context than I would.

Another example of the authors' opinionated approach is their stated view that "Jack the Ripper" never existed--that he was a figure created by unscrupulous, slanted newspaper coverage that was designed purely to increase newspaper sales. They explain their reasons for adopting this view, and they could be right--I'm not enough of Ripper scholar to be able to evaluate it.

This book amounts to an impressive catalog of murderers--the famous and the not so famous (not just Zodiac and Bundy, but also Hennard and Birnie). The murderers are classified by general types (e.g., the family annihilator, the set-and-run killer), and also by psychopathic types (e.g., the spineless psychopath, the unprincipled psychopath).

The book is probably assigned as a text in some of the authors' college courses. I'm glad that I'm not required to study it closely, because it seems to me that the authors are imposing their preconceived ideas on the facts, instead of reasoning from facts to conclusions. Despite all the facts and analysis included in this book, at the end I didn't feel that I had any greater understanding of murder and murderers than when I started.
product: Professional Trade Compliance Certification Study Guide

text:
SUCCULENTS: THE ULTIMATE GUIDE TO CHOOSING, DESIGNING, AND GROWING 200 EASY CARE PLANTS is a typical Sunset book, with the first 125 pages (of 288) devoted to "Inspirations"--photographs of mature succulent gardens located in the U.S. West. The next 56 pages show "Easy Projects"--photographs and directions for using succulents in Christmas ornaments, table dressings, hair ornaments, bird houses, patio containers, and more.

The next 64 pages are a catalog of "My Favorite Plants"--photographs of 203 easy-care succulents with a single-paragraph description of the plant and its growth behavior. The final 22 pages contain "Planting and Care" information, and a good index (including both Latin and common names of the plants). (See my customer photos for a sample 2-page spread from each main section.)

This is a book filled with "eye candy" for lovers of succulent plants, and it contains a lot of useful information for succulent gardeners. However, it is NOT the comprehensive individual plant reference book that I'd hoped for. Still, it's probably a more complete guide for selection and growing of succulents than either my previous favorite references, [[ASIN:1604693932 Succulents Simplified]], or [[ASIN:0789416573 The Complete Book of Cacti & Succulents]].

It's a useful book for gardeners like myself who are responding to the recent California drought (and the possibility of future droughts) by replacing traditional grass, ground cover, and ornamental plants with water-saving succulent gardens.
product: HTS Code Reference Guide - Harmonized Tariff Schedule

text:
GUNMETAL GRAY is a well-written but formulaic thriller--professional assassin (Courtland Gentry, aka "the Gray Man") accepts a "mission impossible", but carries it off, because he's the best gol-darned assassin ever hired by the CIA or by any other world-class spy organization. It's a long book (over 450 pages), with plenty of exciting, moderately believable, fight-to-the-death scenes.

The trouble is, when a main character is constantly fighting his way out of mortal peril, a novel can become tiresome. Especially when, as with this novel, a gazillion lucky coincidences are needed to both extricate the character and to point him toward the location of his next big fight scene. (No spoilers, but I'm thinking now about Courtland's second exit from the "dive bar", and about the "informer" he ran into while escaping--this happens about a third of the way through the book.)

In the novel, a young, top-level Chinese computer intelligence expert (Fan Jiang) goes on the run. The Chinese, British, Americans, Russians, and every other world spy organization that hears about him is after him--the Chinese to kill him, the rest to capture him and mine his knowledge of Chinese intelligence operations.

Starting in Hong Kong, it's Courtland's job to capture Fan Jiang for the CIA, and also to extract Courtland's former British employer (Sir Donald Fitzroy) from the clutches of the murderous Chinese colonel (Dai Longhai) who is pursuing Fan Jiang. The ongoing casualties are huge--Courtland personally contributes quite a few bodies, and the pursuers contribute many more. There is a sprinkling of mercilous torture.

As thrillers go, this one is decent enough. The plot has a lot of twists, and there is fairly constant action, plus liberal use of modern weapons and electronic surveillance equipment.
product: HTS Code Reference Guide - Harmonized Tariff Schedule

text:
EXPENSIVE SENTENCES: DEBUNKING THE COMMON MYTHS THAT DERAIL DECISIONS AND SABOTAGE SUCCESS is a rarity--a business management book with extremely valuable content. Anyone--not just business managers--can profit from reading this book, which explains why "expensive sentences" (generally accepted truisms) should be carefully analyzed to see whether they in fact apply to a real-life situation--because blindly following a truism can cost a lot of money.

There are three types of expensive sentences--sentences about "stuck", "special", and "scarce" situations. Examples are, respectively, "It's too late to turn back now"; "It would be nice if we could do that, but we can't because of ..."; "Nobody else can do what they do".

Author Quarles explains why it can be far more costly to continue on the same path than to "cut bait" and do something else. A persuasive example is Napoleon's decision to continue the French army's march to Moscow during the War of 1812--despite his losing men to disease and freezing temperatures. That decision ultimately brought down Napoleon's entire Empire.

Another easy to understand analysis of an expensive sentence is the one for "the customer is always right". There are many things to consider here, including the fact that customers don't always know what they want--they may say they want a product with a gazillion features, but actually buy and use a simpler product with only a few essential features.

It should be clear that an expensive sentences analysis can apply to a life situation as well as a business situation. There is a lot of food for thought in this excellent book.
product: Professional Trade Compliance Certification Study Guide

text:
DRONES: TEACH AN ARDUINO TO FLY is one of the best "Make" books ever! It contains complete instructions for building three different drones--small, medium, and large (large enough to require FAA registration). The drones are built from parts kits available online, supplemented with flight controllers (Hubsan X4Wii; Syma X5 with Arduino Teensy 3.2; S500 plus Pixhawk Lite controller and ArduCopter flight control software).

The book explains in detail how drones fly, and includes an early chapter on testing a small drone to understand how changed batteries and propellers can affect performance. Later chapters deal with how changes in the flight controller affect performance, and with how a modular controller can further enhance performance.

The book is profusely illustrated with circuit diagrams, important stages of actual drone construction showing wired connections, drone parts, key programming software screenshots, diagrams of aerodynamics principles, flowcharts, and more. The included code can be used in the builder's programs and documentation without the publisher's permission (unless the builder wants to reproduce significant amounts of the code for commercial purposes).
product: HTS Code Reference Guide - Harmonized Tariff Schedule

text:
You don't have to be a software engineer to understand and appreciate the lessons to be learned from DESIGNING FOR SUSTAINABILITY: A GUIDE TO BUILDING GREENER DIGITAL PRODUCTS AND SERVICES. Internet users like myself tend to think of digital computing as "green"--after all, doesn't paperless banking save trees and paper-manufacturing costs; doesn't online shopping save personal time and gasoline, and reduce auto emissions?

But, as this book points out, digital computing consumes enormous amounts of power, and generating that power contributes to global air pollution and climate change. Furthermore, the new Internet of Things threatens to overwhelm us all with gadgets, gadgets that are reporting data in real time (and consuming even more power). Even a single Tweet adds the CO2 equivalent of a human fart to the atmosphere!

This book doesn't advocate doing away with digital computing, but it does discuss ways that digital gadgets and systems can be designed to have less impact on the environment. For example, digital thermostats can have their real-time reporting combined with other gadgets' reporting, through "smart home" software, to reduce the total power needs of all the gadgets.

This book is a real eye-opener, for pointing out the many hidden ways that digital computing is decidedly NOT "green".
product: Professional Trade Compliance Certification Study Guide

text: As a casual WWII history buff, I can't judge the validity of historian Hornfischer's interpretation and presentation of the events in THE FLEET AT FLOOD TIDE as compared to those of other historians.  However, the facts seem accurate to me, and Hornfischer marshals them expertly.  Focusing on individuals and wartime strategies, he weaves the actual events into a narrative fabric that genuinely reads like a novel.  Once I started reading, I didn't want to put this book down!

text:
BECOMING A U.S. CITIZEN: A GUIDE TO THE LAW, EXAM & INTERVIEW is another excellent Nolo Press legal guide for nonlawyers. In easy-to-understand language, it explains the pros and cons of applying for U.S. citizenship, and advises the prospective applicant of situations where he or she really needs to consult a lawyer.

The book contains sample applications, interview notices, etc., and tells how to fill them out or respond to them. There is a sample U.S. Government and History Exam, with correct answers. There is a section on applying to retake an exam or appealing an application denial.

In sum, this is an excellent book for any prospective applicant for U.S. Citizenship to start out with. It covers the entire application process and fully informs the applicant about what is involved, and what can be expected, at every stage.
product: Professional Trade Compliance Certification Study Guide

text:
Wow! It's hard to believe that NINTH CITY BURNING is a first novel--author J. Patrick Black has created a future-Earth, post-apocalypse novel so rich in detail, you feel the world actually exists. The well-rounded, memorable characters demonstrate formidable survival skills in Earth's countryside (now populated by horseback-riding, nomadic clans), and in Earth's cities (powered by "thelemity", a mysterious electricity-like force; now populated by educated citizens organized in a rigid hierarchical structure).

The novel has many exciting, believable action scenes (both "flight for life" and armed combat), interesting high tech weapons, and a realistically described setting. The detailed plot makes sense, and the pacing creates a genuine page-turner. I can't wait to read the next novel in this new series!

text: In my opinion, Dan Gookin writes the very best "for Dummies" manuals.  WORD 2016 FOR PROFESSIONALS FOR DUMMIES is no exception.  If you're an ordinary end-user like myself, already using a previous version of Word, this reference manual will tell you everything you need to know to fully use the Word 2016 software, and will supply a lot of tips that you might not otherwise discover without seriously studying a power-user's manual.

text:
THE TROUBLE WITH WOMEN is an occasionally amusing, but wholly inconsequential cartoon book for adult women. It's a riff on historical misconceptions about females, as propounded by famous men such as Darwin, Ruskin, Freud, Picasso, and Rousseau.

The humor is marred by the hyperbole and generally bitter tone of the text. For example: "Marie Curie is the only female scientist since women began. Girls weren't allowed to study science because their reproductive organs made them irrational, and abstract thought doesn't get the curtains hung, They were allowed to collect insects as you can do that in your own garden."

For a book that takes about 20 minutes to read, it's pricey (hardcover, $12.99). Probably quite a few feminists will find this in their Christmas stockings. However, the reaction of this feminist to the catalog of erroneous historical assertions is: "So what? People know better now, and it's a waste of energy to harangue a bunch of dead men."

For the above reasons, I rate THE TROUBLE WITH WOMEN at 2 stars ("I don't like it" on the official Amazon scale).
product: Professional Trade Compliance Certification Study Guide

text:
THE SPY WHO COULDN'T SPELL: A DYSLEXIC TRAITOR, AN UNBREAKABLE CODE, AND THE FBI'S HUNT FOR AMERICA'S STOLEN SECRETS is thoroughly researched nonfiction (either biography or "true crime"), but it genuinely reads like a spy thriller. Even more amazing, this highly readable and entertaining book is author Yudhijit Bhattacharjee's first book (see the Acknowledgements).

The book tells the story of how Air Force Master Sergeant Brian Regan stole thousands of pages of highly classified U.S. military documents--including some that could easily have started a war--by walking them out of his National Reconnaissance Office workplace in a gym bag. It also tells the story of exactly how an FBI team identified Regan as the unknown who had attempted to sell military secrets to the Libyan embassy in late 2000; and how the team surveilled Regan until they had the evidence needed for his arrest. But the story doesn't end with Regan's arrest--because the FBI still had to recover the multitude of physical documents that Regan buried on federal lands, even after Regan himself had forgotten the key that unlocked his coded list of GPS coordinates for the cache sites.

If you're interested in codes and code-breaking, you'll particularly enjoy the easy-to-understand chapters on how Regan constructed the unbreakable codes that he used for transcribing the documents he offered for sale to foreign governments. And once you've finished this book, you'll really understand how vulnerable the U.S. is, to the theft of classified military secrets by any "homegrown" traitor who decides, for whatever reason, to profit from a high-level security clearance.

text:
Written by two school librarians, THE BIG BOOK OF MAKERSPACE PROJECTS: INSPIRING MAKERS TO EXPERIMENT, CREATE, AND LEARN seems like an ideal book to add to any public "makerspace". (A "makerspace" is an area in a library or community center that is stocked with crafts supplies, computers, sewing machines, 3D printers, and other items that can be used for making, tinkering with, or inventing things.)

The book includes complete, step-by-step, illustrated (with photographs) instructions for 51 projects, ranging from motorized cleaning-brush "bot warriors" to a "Littlebits Sphero smart track". Each project starts with a summary that gives the cost, the make time, and a list of supplies that includes where to obtain them (for example, an electric toothbrush can be found at Dollar Tree).

The instructions include "classroom tips", with suggestions for setting up group projects, or for what to teach to a group. In the first "bot warriors" project, one classroom tip notes: "... it is good to teach ... about battery safety. ... In the robots you are creating, ... electrons are used to power motors when the circuit is complete. This means that you should never hook up the positive side of a battery to the negative side of a battery with an alligator clip because this will create a short circuit. ..."

The project instructions also include "challenges" for the makers. These are questions that encourage learning through tinkering with the finished project. For example, in the "bot warriors" project, the first challenge question asks: "What happens if you mount two motors to your brush bot powered by one battery? Now try two batteries."

The projects teach concepts of physics, electricity, coding, and more. However, the book would be improved if the projects had better general descriptions--at the beginning of each project--of what the maker is going to be making. For example, the "bot warriors" project has no photograph of the assembled robot until the project is finished. Even then, it's unclear what the cleaning-brush bot is supposed to do, until the maker sees that Project 2 has instructions for making a cardboard arena for brush bot battles.

Clearer general project descriptions would help makers choose which projects interest them most. Also, the book would be improved if there were photographs of the supplies (for example, a photograph of the "alligator clip" that consists of a pair of clips connected with an electrical wire).

Because of the less-than-ideal project descriptions, and the missing photographs or drawings of supplies, I rate this otherwise excellent makerspace project book at 4 stars ("I like it" on the official Amazon scale).
product: Professional Trade Compliance Certification Study Guide

text:
RT Book Reviews got it exactly right, in describing THE SMOKE HUNTER as "Indiana Jones Meets Lara Croft". It's an historical action/adventure novel, with lots of detailed description of the historical setting and times; and with a plot that is over-the-top outlandish. There's no history in it--it's just a made-up account of a bold female heroine's 1898 adventure--her search for the lost city of El Dorado.

In the novel, the heroine's search begins with her finding an Inca artifact secreted in an old Psalter that she steals--with appropriate self-justification--from a desk in the British Office of Public Records. The artifact is accompanied by a map of the New World, as it was when the Mission of San Pedro de Flores was founded. The Psalter also contains letters saying that a half-crazed friar brought the artifact out of the jungle, and died soon after. So the heroine sets out to find the city that the friar babbled about.

Author Jacquelyn Benson is to be commended for her writing skill and mastery of this genre. It's hard to believe that her polished work is a first novel! However, it's not my kind of book--all of that rich, detailed description adds up to a 436-page novel, and I strongly prefer mystery/thriller to action/adventure anyway. Basically I ordered this book by mistake, so I rate THE SMOKE HUNTER at a neutral 3 stars ("It's okay" on the official Amazon scale).
product: HTS Code Reference Guide - Harmonized Tariff Schedule

text:
FRACTURED is a very good novel, but the plot is the type that I always lose patience with. At the beginning, a life-altering, tragic event is hinted at. Then the actual details are dribbled out in the remainder of the novel, through one or more viewpoint characters' flashbacks that recount the events that produced the tragic one. This type of plot must be extremely difficult to write well--and this skillfully written novel is an example of how to pull it off--but as a reader I prefer a more straightforward plot, without flashbacks.

In the novel, the author of a best-selling first novel (Julie) relocates her family from Tacoma to Cincinnati, because she needs to escape the attentions of a particularly troublesome, threatening stalker (Heather). Julie and her unemployed, married neighbor (John) are attracted to each other, and the attraction sets in motion the events that result in tragedy for all.

John, Julie, Julie's attack dog (Sandy), and Julie's twin children all run afoul of the ever-changing "neighborhood rules" written (and emailed) by an officious neighbor (Cindy). Cindy, the self-appointed neighborhood leader and organizer of compulsory monthly block parties, is both amusing and frightening. John's teenage son (Chris) and Cindy's teenage daughter (Ashley) also figure in the complex plot. Ultimately, neighbors end up confronting neighbors both in civil court and in grand jury proceedings.

The Cincinnati setting, in Mount Adams and Eden Park, is incidental to the plot. (This was a disappointment to me, because I was interested in reading about the Cincinnati locale.) The characters are well-drawn--Cindy is particularly memorable, as the type of well-meaning but overbearing person who always seems to turn up in neighborhood groups. Because FRACTURED is well-done, but the kind of novel that I find tedious, I rate it at 3 stars ("It's okay" on the official Amazon scale).
product: Professional Trade Compliance Certification Study Guide

text:
I love LEGO books, but GEEKY LEGO CRAFTS: 21 FUN AND QUIRKY PROJECTS is kind of hard to love. The building instructions are clear enough, but the projects don't cry out, "Build me!"

The basic designs resemble low-resolution screen displays reduced to individual pixels. You have to sort of squint to see what they're supposed to represent--for example, the zombie face on the "Zombie Pencil Holder"; the cassette on the "Cassette Letter Holder"; the trophy deer head in the "Mounted Deer Head" (shown on the book's cover).

Assuming that you want items that generally look like resemble vintage floppy disks, Tetris squares, or pixellated Santas and skulls-and-crossbones, the actual projects are impractical. Typical examples are the "Hazmat Place Mats" (a large studs-up platform with a flame or a skull-and-crossbones design) and the "Space Invaders Space Savers" (upright invader-shaped blocks with basins for paperclips or highlighter pens). Food is going to get stuck on the placemats, and the footprint of the space savers is large compared to their capacity.

Many projects (for example, the "Cassette Letter Holder" require a lot of LEGO blocks, so they represent a small investment in money as well as time. The "Fridge Magnets" fail to specify what sort of magnets to use, or where to purchase them.

The projects in the book are as follows: Space Invader Space Savers; Cursor Bookends; Rubber Ducky; Flying Ducks; Christmas Decorations; Flame Toast Rack; Zombie Pencil Holder; Hazmat Place Mats; Hanging Ghost; Floppy Disk Coasters; Cassette Letter Holder; Sword-in-the-Stone Paperweight; Retro Controller Phone Station; Lady Liberty Fish Tank; "Fuzzy Dice"; Giant Key Holder; Mounted Deer; Fridge Magnets; Bearskin Rug; Grenade Planter; and Robot Wine Bottle Holder.

Because the book is mildly interesting to look through, even though I wouldn't want to build any of the designs, I rate it at a very generous 3 stars ("It's okay" on the official Amazon scale).
product: Professional Trade Compliance Certification Study Guide

text:
SAMSUNG GEAR S2 FOR DUMMIES does an excellent job of explaining how to set up and use the Gear S2 smartwatch. The book describes in detail every preinstalled app and setting. It gets you quickly up to speed on handling telephone calls, text messages, and emails. It has good chapters on troubleshooting, and on finding online help and support. Surprisingly, it even has a chapter on designing, developing, and selling your own Gear S2 apps.

However, I question whether any new owner of a Gear S2 really needs this book. The Gear S2 is a rather simple device, and the interface is quite intuitive to use. I figured out the watch features fairly quickly (all by myself, without benefit of any detailed manual) just by rotating the bezel and tapping on the icons to see what they did.

It did take me a while to realize that the Gear S2 does NOT run wholly independently of an Android smartphone (e.g., an interactive NBA watch face on my Gear S2 stopped updating game scores whenever my phone went to standby mode). This book would have saved me some troubleshooting time in that area--it tells you on page 2 that you need a smartphone running to make full use of the watch.

Switching the watchbands is very tricky, and this book has photos and step-by-step instructions that show you how. However, there is a Samsung video available online that demonstrates the method. There is also a complete Gear 2 User's Manual available online from Samsung that explains all the apps, settings, and capabilities of the device, just as this book does.

Without a doubt, author Eric Butow does a fine job of covering the S2 Gear in this "Dummies" book. It's not his fault that the Gear S2 is limited in things it can do, so that there isn't a whole lot for him to write about. However, at its current Amazon selling price (about $23 in paperback format), I don't think that this book is enough of an improvement over the Samsung User's Manual to be worth the money. I rate it at 3 stars ("It's okay" on the official Amazon scale).

text:
Author Cathy O'Neil is a professional mathematician who also possesses considerable language skills. In WEAPONS OF MATH DESTRUCTION: HOW BIG DATA INCREASES INEQUALITY AND THREATENS DEMOCRACY, she explains with the utmost clarity how the many "WMD" algorithms currently being used in U.S. schools, colleges, workplaces, banks, courts, and elections operate to distort things like "good teacher" retention, student admissions, job offers, loan approvals, sentences for incarceration, and voter turnout.

The basic problem with the WMD algorithms is that they're based on faulty assumptions--the assumptions incorporated into their mathematical models whenever there is insufficient real data available for accurate analysis of their targets. One example is a school system algorithm that supposedly weeds out "bad" teachers, by identifying teachers whose students test worse at the end of a school year in the teachers' classes than they did at the end of the previous school year. The concept seems sound--except that the algorithm can falsely identify "good" teachers as "bad" teachers, if the students' test results for the previous year have been tampered with, and inflated by, genuinely bad and unscrupulous teachers.

O'Neil makes a convincing case for how WMD algorithms particularly discriminate against the poor. For example, there are hiring algorithms that red-light job applicants on the basis of the applicants' home addresses and their credit ratings. This red-lighting only serves to continue the applicants' cycle of poverty, because honest people with little money may live in impoverished neighborhoods and have trouble paying their bills on time.

This book is perhaps a little short on solutions for fixing the WMD algorithms now used in seemingly every facet of modern society. However, to understand the ubiquitous WMD algorithm problem is a first step toward inventing and implementing workable solutions.

This is a significant book, if only because of the highly readable, easy-to-understand descriptions of so many currently-used WMD algorithms (and of the damage these algorithms can do, and are doing, to millions of real people). I'm particularly grateful to O'Neil for her understandable explanation of how WMD algorithms directly contributed to the 2008 U.S. housing market crash and subsequent recession. The disaster happened in part because lenders, investment bankers, and others in the financial industry relied on algorithms that weren't really tracking data, but were only defining and reinforcing their own reality.

text:
SO SAY THE FALLEN is a police procedural, but it's a rare beast--a police procedural with some literary chops. Through two main characters (Detective Chief Inspector Flanagan and Reverend Mr. McKay), it examines the question: What's left, if you lose your faith, your job, your family? The novel makes a case for "the power of prayer"--though it's NOT a heavy novel, not at all. It's a fast-reading mystery/thriller with a complex plot.

In the novel, DCI Flanagan is called to the scene of an apparent suicide (Henry Garrick). Mr. Garrick, the wealthy owner of a car dealership, had lost his legs in a recent auto accident. A deeply religious man, he had been bearing up well, but he was bedridden and in a lot of pain. The evidence points to suicide by morphine overdose, but Flanagan's gut tells her otherwise. Something is off about the suicide scene: the deceased lined up photos of his loved ones on a nearby table, but the photos are facing away from him, not towards him so he could see them as he departed his life.

DCI Flanagan investigates further, even though her soon-to-retire boss (Purdy) tells her to let it go, and even though it lands her in hot water with the higher-ups. One of the higher-ups (Allison) is a personal friend of Mr. Garrick's widow (Roberta Garrick), and he derides Flanagan publicly for harassing the bereaved widow. Mrs. Garrick has experienced more than her share of tragedy, he says, after losing her baby in a swimming accident, and now losing her disabled husband.

As Flanagan looks into Mrs. Garrick's background, she discovers that the woman didn't exist anywhere in public records until 7 years earlier, when she met and married Mr. Garrick. So who IS Mrs. Garrick? And was that swimming accident really an accident? Did Mrs. Garrick kill her baby?

In the meantime, Flanagan is fighting to hold onto her family--her job has taken her away from them so often, they have learned to leave her out of their plans, and even their family conversations at the rare meals where Flanagan joins them. She is helped by Reverend Mr. McKay, the Garricks' religious advisor, but he is fighting his own demons. He is drawn to Mrs. Garrick, and he no longer believes in God.

SO SAY THE FALLEN is extremely well-written, tells an interesting story, and has multiple well-developed characters. I was impressed with this book and its author, and I definitely want to read more of Stuart Neville's "Belfast Novels".
product: Professional Trade Compliance Certification Study Guide

text:
In CLOSED CASKET: THE NEW HERCULE POIROT MYSTERY, author Sophie Hannah does Hercule Poirot very well--almost as well as Agatha Christie herself. But Christie died in 1976, so of course Christie did NOT share in the writing of this excellent (and apparently authorized) "new Hercule Poirot mystery".

It's too bad, I think, that the publisher (or Christie's literary estate) added Christie's well-known signature to the book's cover, and lists the book as "by Sophie Hannah and Agatha Christie", presumably as a marketing ploy. In fact, author Hannah has invented her own quirky plot--for a murder carried out by "the least likely person", per the Christie tradition--and thus she deserves the full byline.

In this novel, a wealthy author (Lady Athelinda "Athie" Playford) of a children's mystery series (Shrimp Seddon and the Lady in the Suit, etc.) invites a group of guests (including famous private detective Hercule Poirot and Scotland Yard detective Edward Catchpool) to a weekend at her secluded estate (Lillieoak). At dinner, Athie announces that she has changed her will to leave everything to her secretary (Joseph Scotcher), who is dying of kidney disease and thought to have only months to live.

The news is not well received by her ineffectual son (Harry) and his wife (Dorro), or by her vicious-tongued daughter (Claudia), or even by Scotcher himself. Athie's proclaimed motive for the bequest is unconvincing, and everyone speculates about her state of mind. But son and daughter needn't have worried--their inheritance proves to be secure when Scotcher is murdered--apparently killed twice, in fact--and it's up to Catchpool and Poirot to unravel the puzzle.

The plot has many unexpected twists and turns, and several quite memorable characters that are wholly invented by Hannah (e.g., nurse Sophie, the vainglorious Kimpton, Scotcher, Claudia, the almost-silent butler Hatton). The book is probably a little too long, and it's certainly NOT a page-turner. But the clues and conflicting bits of evidence fall neatly into place at the end, when Poirot gathers all of the guests together for one of his trademark "reveals". Because CLOSED CASKET does a very good job of writing in the Hercule Poirot tradition, I rate CLOSED CASKET at 4 stars ("I like it" on the official Amazon scale).
product: Professional Trade Compliance Certification Study Guide

text:
THE ORPHAN MASTER'S SON: A NOVEL won the 2013 Pulitzer Prize for fiction, for good reason. The story grabs you, and holds you, even as you're horrified by the fact-based depiction of certain aspects of life in North Korea. Yes, there was a famine (the Arduous March (1994-1998)); yes, there were kidnappings of Japanese citizens (1977-1983); yes, there are stiff penalties for mishandling the mandatory portraits of North Korean leaders.

How could anyone choose life in North Korea, you ask yourself, as, for example, you witness Pak Jun Do's returning, repeatedly and unquestioningly, to North Korea from Japan. But then you realize that North Korea is Jun Do's country, the place where he was born, the totalitarian state with the only customs he knows and is totally familiar with.

This book is indeed a literary masterpiece. It's a classic that belongs on school, college, and "best books" reading lists everywhere. So much has been written about this book, there is little that I can add, except to say, "Read it!" if you haven't already done so.

text:
THE ART OF RIVALRY consists of four short, essay-like, dual biographies of eight major modern artists. In life, the paired artists were friends and rivals--so the focus of each biography is the influence that the two artists had on each other's paintings. The artists are:

+ Lucian Freud and Francis Bacon
+ Edouard Manet and Edgar Degas
+ Henri Matisse and Pablo Picasso
+ Jackson Pollock and Willem de Kooning

The biographies are authoritative, but nevertheless highly readable. They're suitable for a mass audience, and certainly amount to interesting and enjoyable reading for art lovers, art historians, and museum goers alike.

Pulitzer-Prize-winning art critic Sebastian Smee knows art, and knows how to write about a painting in a way that not only enlivens it, but makes it easy for anyone to understand it. Smee's analysis of Freud's tiny portrait of Bacon (stolen from a Berlin art exhibition in 1988, and still missing) is a wonderful example of how meaningful art criticism is done.
product: Professional Trade Compliance Certification Study Guide

text:
THE SECRETS OF WISHTIDE has everything you want in an historical cozy mystery. There's an intelligent, likable female investigator (Mrs. Laetitia "Letty" Rodd); an interesting series of country murders to solve; a believable (but not overly detailed) period setting (Hampstead & Lincolnshire, England); reasonably well-developed secondary characters (brother Fred, landlady Mrs. Bentley, members of client Calderstone's family); leisurely but steady pacing; and good but undemanding writing. This is the first novel in a new series, and I hope there will be many more.

In the novel, the middle-aged and unpretentious Mrs. Rodd is living in much-reduced circumstances, following the fairly recent death of her archdeacon husband (Matt). She's getting by (avoiding the ignominious fate of many widows--becoming a relative's live-in nursemaid), by accepting undercover domestic-investigation assignments from her little brother Fred, a highly successful criminal barrister. So far, Fred's clients have been impressed and satisfied with Letty's work.

Letty travels to Lincolnshire in connection with a blackmail letter received by a rich coal-mine owner, Sir James Calderstone, who has a great deal of influence in Parliament. The letter threatens to reveal sordid details about the woman Sir James' son wants to marry (Mrs. Orme). She's beautiful, but a huge chunk of her background is missing. Operating undercover as the governess hired to "finish" Sir James' two teenaged daughters (rebellious Blanche and eager-to-please Elizabeth), Letty interviews Mrs. Orme. Letty likes her, but knows Mrs. Orme is lying when she says she is the daughter of a curate from a tiny, obscure coastal parish. Because Letty has many friends in the religious community, she quickly teases out the real, highly scandalous story of Mrs. Orme--and the plot thickens from there, as the murders start in and keep coming. Letty also learns many sordid details about the Calderstone family itself.

This sort of British cozy is "my cup of tea", and I can't think of anything bad to say about this novel. I particularly liked the portrayal of Letty's close, big sister-little brother relationship with Fred. He's grown up now, and an important personage, but the two still play "snapdragon" at Christmas--with Fred's children keeping score--and they singe their cuffs same as always.
product: HTS Code Reference Guide - Harmonized Tariff Schedule

text:
FRIENDLY FIRE (A JONATHAN GRAVE THRILLER) is a run-of-the-mill mass market thriller, but it’s perfect for summer reading. The plot moves along quickly, the subplots are easy to keep straight, and the main characters (lawbreakers who kill not solely for money, but for a good cause) are intelligent and reasonably well developed. This is my first “Jonathan Grave” thriller, but the eighth in the series (the first series book is [[ASIN:0786037075 No Mercy]]). This one works fine as a standalone.

In the novel, a Starbucks barista (Ethan) recognizes a customer as the monster who kidnapped and tortured him 11 years earlier, when he was only a child. Ethan chases the customer and knifes him to death in a parking lot, before many witnesses. Ethan tells authorities that he was kidnapped by the monster, and rescued by two men (“Scorpion” and “Big Guy”). However, there is no record of the kidnapping.

But Ethan isn’t lying. In fact, Ethan’s rescue was performed by Jonathan’s Security Solutions team (consisting of Jonathan aka Scorpion; Big Guy; and Mother Hen, a woman with awesome hacking and electronic data collection skills). All of the Security Solutions operations are strictly off the books, because Scorpion and Big Guy tend to leave a lot of bodies behind. That was true in Jonathan’s just-completed successful “extraction”, the rescue of a congressman’s kidnapped daughter.

That operation turns out to be connected to a U.S.-based terrorist group (the fictional al-Amin), which is not only hiring skilled professional kidnappers to snatch high-profile targets (e.g., children of senators and representatives), but is also plotting a major terrorist attack on U.S. soil. Ethan’s monster/murder victim is one of the al-Amin kidnappers, so Jonathan’s team has a very slim chance of helping Ethan stay alive in prison and escape execution by lethal injection, by secretly confirming his unbelievable kidnapping story to the FBI and local police.

The book is well-written, but formulaic. The plot is believable, and has a lot of interesting twists, but the good guys ultimately win out because they have the right government contacts and surveillance skills to be able to get the information they need just in time to take down the bad guys (while leaving lots of bodies). There are a lot of practical details left out, many things skipped over, as the action of the story advances.

Because it’s entertaining and a fast read (though not always a page-turner), I rate FRIENDLY FIRE at 4 stars (“I like it” on the official Amazon scale).
product: HTS Code Reference Guide - Harmonized Tariff Schedule

text:
MELISSA LEAPMAN’S INDISPENSABLE STITCH COLLECTION FOR CROCHETERS is a basic crochet stitch dictionary containing about 200 patterns grouped into six sections: (1) Simple Solid Patterns, (2) Shell and Fan Stitch Patterns, (3) Openwork and Lace Patterns, (4) Textured Patterns, (5) Colorwork Patterns, and (6) Edgings. For each pattern, there are written-out instructions, charted instructions, and a color photo showing a crocheted sample of the pattern.

Because I’m a big Melissa Leapman fan, I was looking forward to this collection of her personal favorite crochet patterns. However, Melissa does NOT include any personal commentary on the individual patterns (e.g., why she likes the pattern, how or where she has used the pattern, etc.). In a brief introduction, Leapman states: “This collection includes 200 of my favorite crochet stitch patterns gathered from nearly thirty years designing for the yarn and fashion industry. Some patterns are long-standing favorites, such as Solid Shell Stitch on page 18; others, like the Saxon Braid Panel on page 99 are original to this collection.” As far as I can tell, Leapman does not otherwise indicate which patterns are old, and which are new.

The book provides a good selection of stitches for new crocheters, and is a good standby reference for experienced crocheters, but it doesn’t go very far beyond the stitch collections generally found in how-to books on crocheting (e.g., [[ASIN:156477998X A to Z of Crochet: The Ultimate Guide for the Beginner to Advanced Crocheter]]). If you’re looking for a more complete crochet stitch dictionary, you might consider [[ASIN:1454701374 The Complete Book of Crochet Stitch Designs: 500 Classic & Original Patterns]]), and the other two titles in the Linda Schapper series.

Because the book has only 200 patterns, and lacks commentary by the author regarding their selection for inclusion, I rate MELISSA LEAPMAN’S INDISPENSABLE STITCH COLLECTION FOR CROCHETERS at 4 stars ("I like it" on the official Amazon scale).
product: HTS Code Reference Guide - Harmonized Tariff Schedule

text:
MURDER AND THE MAKING OF ENGLISH CSI reads like someone’s Ph.D. thesis (and for all I know, that’s exactly what it is). It’s clearly an academic work, not something written to appeal to a mass audience made up of “CSI” television fans, true crime fans, or forensic documentaries fans.

This slender volume (230 pages, including footnotes) is indeed a history of forensics, but the focus is on how early ideas about close observation of crime scenes came to be applied in practice to crime scenes. Thus there is a lot of recounting of exactly how pioneering crime scene investigators conducted themselves. For example, one investigator (Thorndyke) used a knife blade to scrape a sample of sand from a murder victim’s pillow into a seed packet to preserve it for laboratory analysis; another investigator (Spilsbury) would walk through rooms with hands in pockets to avoid disturbing anything.

The book includes many excerpts and diagrams reprinted from groundbreaking handbooks for criminal investigators. For example, there are descriptions of how to walk a crime scene (on a sort of grid) to avoid missing tiny details; and there are passages that emphasize the importance of, and methods for, securing blood-stained items or clothing to avoid subsequent contamination.

The methodology of the actual, lengthy forensic investigations of a couple of famous English crimes (the 1924 murder and dismemberment of Emily Kaye; and the 1953 discovery of John Christie’s “murder house”) are discussed in considerable detail. The chapter on the “murder house” includes photographs of forensic investigators digging up bones. Other chapters have photos of bone fragments, skulls, hair, murder victims’ bodies in situ, and other evidence uncovered by close forensic examination.

The book is of mild interest to forensics buffs, but it would be misleading to describe it as anything other than boring. The authors say that the book is intended to show how the modern forensics view of a homicide scene “as a highly choreographed space of investigation” came into being. I guess it succeeds at that, and so I rate it at 3 stars (“It’s okay” on the official Amazon scale).
product: Professional Trade Compliance Certification Study Guide

text:
“You never quit.” In RED DOG, redneck detective Slim hears that a lot, because he always has something snarkily funny to say, whether he’s tied to table being tortured, mulling over his next move, or just playing Scrabble with his 13-year-old daughter/sidekick Anci. (Anci says, “I’m the brains of this operation,” and Slim doesn’t necessarily disagree.) Given the graphic brutality of some scenes, it’s astonishing that the reader’s overall impression of the book is that it’s an absolute hoot.

In the novel, Slim is hired by an extremely weird father and son to recover their stolen dog Shelby Ann from a man who claims to have her. They offer $65, which won’t fix Slim’s air conditioner, but Anci says take the case. This leads Slim to investigating an underground dog-fighting operation, and into a world of trouble with local deputies, homicidal bad guys, tough women, federal agents, and treacherous friends. He is nearly killed at least twice, but survives thanks to his fearsome friend Jeep Mabry, and to his weaselly lawyer.

To say that the book is well-written is an understatement. I’m in awe of author Jason Miller’s ability to maintain a consistent tone of snarky humor throughout a full-length novel. (A typical example, from a passage about Slim’s encounter with a bad guy inside the guy’s trailer: “. .. he was armed with a sword. Saber, I guess it was. . . . The saber whistled through the air. It . . . lodged in the metal flashing around the doorframe. (The bad guy) grunted and tugged, but the blade was stubborn. I didn’t blame it; I didn’t want to work for (the bad guy), either.”)

Slim speaks with a consistent redneck/hillbilly dialect. The story is set in the “Little Egypt” area of Southern Illinois, so called because many towns there have Egyptian names (e.g., Cairo, Karnak, Thebes). It’s a depressed rural area, with many worked-out coal mines, and thinly populated. The descriptions of the local countryside seem very accurate, and are consistent with the Wikipedia writeups about specific locales that I looked up.

I’m not only looking forward to the next “Slim in Little Egypt mystery”, I’ve already gone and purchased the first one, [[ASIN:0062362194 Down Don't Bother Me)]]. This book, RED DOG, also has a bonus short story, which explains the “thing about the chickens” that is referenced in the main plot that Slim is too embarrassed to talk about.

text:
COLLECTING THE DEAD: A NOVEL introduces a fascinating new “detective”—Magnus Craig, aka “Steps”, of the fictional FBI Special Tracking Unit. Steps is renowned as a “man-tracker”, and has been dubbed “the Human Bloodhound” by the national news media. He is so successful at tracking down missing persons and murderers, he and his partner (FBI Special Agent Jimmy Donovan) are in constant demand from law enforcement. At a moment’s notice, they fly all over the country in their private jet, to visit crime scenes that range from apparently undisturbed to incredibly bloody.

But Steps is a fraud. He isn’t a man-tracker at all--though he plays the part most convincingly by examining imaginary bent leaves, minute traces of blood, and the like, whenever he’s hunting a person. What Steps really does, is hunt with his unique, extraordinary gift. Because of the gift, he sees the “shine” left behind by each and every human who has been physically present at a crime scene. The “shine” is a glowing, colored aura—and he can separate the “shine” left by his target from the “shine” of other people at the scene, because the colors of “shine” vary in accordance with the good or evil nature of individual human beings.

In the novel, Steps is called out to track a serial killer (the sad-faced killer) who kidnaps young brunette women and leaves their bodies posed along park trails near Redding, California. The crime scenes are all covered with the killer’s “shine”, in brilliant amaranth and textured rust. The story is about how Steps manages to find and assemble the clues--through tracking the killer’s “shine”--in order to capture the killer and bring him to justice. The novel’s title, “Collecting the Dead”, is taken from Steps’ habit of keeping one photograph of each murder victim in a black-covered photo album, so that he’ll never forget that true evil exists.

I’ve read quite a few novels that used characters who could see other people’s colored auras, but COLLECTING THE DEAD is the first one that has gotten this concept right. The plot is believable, and the tracking concept is worked out so that it isn’t just a gimmick, but constitutes a genuine part of the plot. The characters of Steps, Jimmy, and their assistant Diane are fairly well-developed, too.

I loved this book, and can’t wait for the next one—which I believe will happen, because Steps hasn’t yet nailed his evil nemesis “Leonardo”!

text:
The “Alice in Wonderland” references in A MOST CURIOUS MURDER: A LITTLE LIBRARY MYSTERY are so forced, they eventually spoil what might otherwise be a reasonably good cozy mystery.

A major character, Zoe Zola, is a highly intelligent “little person” who is writing a nonfiction book about Lewis Carroll’s famous work. Zoe generally talks in riddles sprinkled with quotes from “Alice”. She also loves fairies and has a gorgeous flower garden ornamented with “fairy houses”. Yes, she’s an eccentric character—the sort of character you generally expect, and appreciate, in a cozy mystery. However, Zoe’s disjointed speech patterns don’t make her loveable—they’re just confusing, and they slow down the story. Worse, Zoe’s “Alice” references are dropped in so frequently, and with so little connection to anything in the plot, they ultimately come off as a mere book sales gimmick.

The other major character, Jenny Weston, is reeling from a recent divorce, and has returned to her mother’s place in Bear Falls, Michigan. Jenny is all wrapped up in how she’s been dumped by two men--her former husband and her high school sweetheart. The former sweetheart is married, still living in Bear Falls, and obviously a train wreck that Jenny was lucky to avoid. Still, Jenny spends much of her time brooding about her romantic misfortunes and her lingering feelings for the sweetheart. Otherwise, she renews a lot of her old acquaintances with long-time Bear Falls residents.

In the novel, there are two mysteries to be solved: “Who murdered the spiteful old curmudgeon found dead in Zoe’s fairy garden?” and “Who hacked apart the ‘Little Library’ book exchange box that stood for years in the front yard of Jenny’s childhood home?” Rather inexplicably, the local police chief focuses on Zoe as the major suspect in the curmudgeon’s murder. No one has any idea who murdered the book box. Both mysteries are eventually solved.

The murder mystery plot was fairly interesting until the ending. But the ending is marred because all of the plot threads are wrapped up too quickly in the final few chapters. There isn’t enough preparation for the “reveal” of the murderer; the book-box-destroyer’s motivation is extremely weak; and the incredible, timely return to Bear Falls of a long-missing resident conveniently solves a mystery from Jenny’s past that didn’t even need to be solved.

Because of the gimmicky "Alice" aspects, and the disappointing ending, I rate A MOST CURIOUS MURDER at 3 stars ("It's okay" on the official Amazon scale).

text:
FIELD OF GRAVES is J.T. Ellison’s lightly-revised, previously-unpublished, first novel (and my very first J.T. Ellison novel). FIELD OF GRAVES also happens to be the first “Taylor Jackson” novel; although, strictly speaking, the first published novel, [[ASIN:B00UFIH4X8 All the Pretty Girls]], is the real beginning of the series. Some of the writing is awkward (especially at the beginning), but the plot drew me in, and I ended up enjoying the book.

In the novel, the gorgeous Lieutenant Taylor Jackson leads an investigation into a series of baffling Nashville murders. The body of a young woman is found posed on the steps of the Nashville Parthenon; the body of another young woman is pulled from the river near Riverfront Park; a priest and his student are burned to death in a confessional; a pregnant Vanderbilt student disappears and may be another victim. Early in the investigation, medical examiner Samantha Owens (who has her own J.T. Ellison series) finds signs that the dead victims were all poisoned, and that they may have been sacrificed by a ritual killer.

Taylor is recovering from having shot a fellow police officer to death. She’s suffering from recurring nightmares that feature a field of graves. Luckily, she joins forces with Dr. John Baldwin, an FBI profiler who has been severely damaged by losing three Behavioral Science teammates in a recent BSIS operation. The two damaged detectives turn out to be just what the doctor ordered--for solving the case, and for each other.

The amateurish parts of the book include the heavy use of Taylor’s nightmare dreams for characterization and filler, and the insertion of a poem authored by one of the perpetrator’s targets. These passages exist mostly to show off the author’s creative writing skills, and aren’t really needed to advance the plot. Also, Taylor works the investigation over several days without getting any meaningful sleep, and that sort of “superwoman” stuff strains my willing suspension of disbelief as a reader.

However, despite the novice writer elements, the plot is highly intriguing and has lots of unexpected twists and turns. Also, the characterization is pretty good. I rate FIELD OF GRAVES at 4 stars ("I like it on the official Amazon scale).
