text:
ARDUINO: A TECHNICAL REFERENCE is intended for advanced Arduino programmers and prototypers--for "people who need or want to know the technical details." Like so many of the excellent O'Reilly books, this book is a thoroughly indexed technical handbook and reference book.

"Ideally," the author says, the reader will have "a basic knowledge of C or C++, some idea of how electrons move around in a circuit, and some experience building electronic devices." The book is NOT a tutorial, or an introduction to Arduino, or an Arduino-sanctioned guide to the products produced by Arduino. What it is, is a compilation of information taken from many sources scattered across the Internet. This information is supplemented by the author's own notes and comments, based on his experiences with Arduino.

If you're an Arduino beginner like me, you'll probably want to start with something simpler (e.g., [[ASIN:1449363334 Getting Started with Arduino: The Open Source Electronics Prototyping Platform (Make)]]; or [[ASIN:1593274483 Arduino Workshop: A Hands-On Introduction with 65 Projects]]).

Here's a brief table of contents for the book. For detailed chapter-by-chapter contents, check out the "Look Inside This Book" feature at the top of the product page.

Preface
1. The Arduino Family (e.g., "What Can You Do With Arduino?")
2. The AVR Microcontroller (e.g., "Peripheral Functions")
3. Arduino-Specific Microcontrollers (e.g., "ATmega32U4")
4. Arduino Technical Details (e.g., "Arduino Pinout Configurations")
5. Programming the Arduino and AVR Microcontrollers (e.g., "Bootloaders")
6. Life Without the Arduino IDE (e.g., "AVR Assembly Language")
7. Arduino Libraries
8. Shields (e.g.,"Uncommon Arduino Shields")
9. Modules and I/O Components (e.g.,"Sensors")
10. Creating Custom Components (e.g.,"The Switchinator")
11. Project: A Programmable Signal Generator
12. Project: Smart Thermostat
13. Model Rocker Launcher: A Design Study
A. Tools and Accessories
B. AVT ATmega Control Registers
C. Arduino and Compatible Products Vendors
D. Recommended Reading
E. Arduino and AVR Software Development Tools
Index

text:
BOUNTY is a superb cyberthriller, with a plot so plausible, I kept thinking the events had really happened, whenever I put down the book. It's extremely well-written, and author Michael Byrnes is clearly very knowledgable about the state of current government surveillance capabilities. This is a scary book, definitely not just another your-government-is-watching-you conspiracy book.

In the novel, a "Bounty4Justice" website suddenly surfaces, offering bounties in the hundreds of thousands of dollars to vigilantes who upload video proof of killing website-targeted individuals who have defrauded or molested or otherwise harmed large numbers of people, but who have escaped punishment by normal courtroom/legal system methods. The promised bounties are supposedly paid in cash or Ncryptocash (fictional Bitcoin). Website visitors vote on whether an individual is innocent or guilty, and bounties are paid only if a target is found guilty by a majority popular vote.

The web site goes viral, and it's chillingly lethal. Targets are executed soon after they're added to the website, and the gruesome execution videos are posted on the site. Targets cannot hide, because the website also posts detailed information about their homes, families, hobbies, and activities; and even posts incriminating documents that were suppressed by the courts, or that previously existed only on private networks. The website seems to know everything. Governments cannot take the website down, because it exists in the dark web with an "impossible" software bridge to the Internet that makes its location untraceable--not to mention the fact that really bad things happen when someone tries to block the website.

The story is about international (but mostly U.S.) government investigators' efforts to find the website's source, discover the persons who are operating and funding it, and take it down before too many more people in high places (e.g., senators, world leaders, bankers, businessmen, you name them) are murdered. The story is brought to life by "dialog boxes" that quote email exchanges, news releases, and other accounts of events that are happening as the story unfolds.

I really loved this absolutely believeable novel. It describes a near future that I hope never comes to pass.

text:
AN INVISIBLE CLIENT, written by a successful Utah trial attorney, portrays practical trial strategy, tactics, and legal procedure so accurately that the book is almost a must-read for anyone planning to enter law school (especially for prospective criminal lawyers). It tells the story of a case involving an "invisible client"-- the name for a client with a winnable personal injury case that high-powered PI lawyers won't touch, because the client's lifetime expected earnings won't justify a big damages award at the end of the case. (To be "visible" a client needs an Ivy League education, a six-figure income, an inherited family family business, or the equivalent.)

In the novel, a big-time PI lawyer (Noah Byron) decides to accept an invisible client (Joel Whiting). Twelve-year-old Joel is hospitalized and dying, having been poisoned by tainted cold medicine that irreparably damaged his kidneys and other internal organs. The manufacturer of the cold medicine (Pharma-K) claims the product was poisoned and replaced on store shelves by an unhinged serial killer. When Noah accepts the case, he does so because he thinks Pharma-K's executives and lawyers are hiding something, NOT because of any strong desire to right the wrong done to Joel and his mother (Rebecca Whiting).

The rest of the novel details how Nathan and his mega-firm's newest hire (Olivia Polley) gather the witness testimony and hard evidence they need to prove at trial that Pharma-K caused Joel's injuries. Olivia is a whip-smart Brigham Young University Law grad, naive but pragmatic, and with a desire to work for ACLU-type causes.

AN INVISIBLE CLIENT is a standard legal procedural novel, but it's well-written, well-plotted, and fast-reading. Not only are the main characters well-developed, the author makes you care about them. The novel is a great choice for summer vacation reading. Because it's entertaining and accurate, but is fairly standard legal fiction, I rate it at 4 stars ("I like it" on the official Amazon scale).
product: HTS Code Reference Guide - Harmonized Tariff Schedule

text:
RAILHEAD is a delight for train lovers. In the far future, sleek and sentient locomotives pull carriages full of humans almost instantaneously between star systems that are thousands of light years apart. Each loco has a distinct, highly independent personality. The locos choose their own names, taking them from obscure references found in Old Earth records (e.g., one train is called "Gentlemen Take Polaroids"). The locos answer to themselves alone, but willingly ferry people across the universe because they live for the adrenaline rush they get when traversing the K-gates that connect the star systems.

This novel is intended for teens, but it's highly entertaining for adult "railheads" as well. The main character is a teen (Zen), who loves the trains and knows the celestial network like the back of his hand even though he hasn't yet traveled the entire network. Zen is desperately poor, with a mentally ill mother and a stern older sister. To survive, he shoplifts and sells cheap items from the bazaar shops that surround the interstellar train stations. Then Zen is recruited--without much choice in the matter--to board the Emperor's private train and steal a small item for a weird adult (Raven). No one, not even Zen, can tell what Raven is (a freedom fighter? a heartless criminal?), until the very end of the novel.

Zen is assisted by various friends, principally by a motorik girl (Nova), a charming robot who's almost human. She has the technological equipment that allows her to communicate directly with the locos (and the locos' fearsome battle droids and maintenance spiders) on Zen's behalf. She's also highly intelligent, and a terrific strategic planner.

There are lots of humorous bits, and the whole far future railroad network is vividly described, with a convincing back story on how it came to exist. I thoroughly enjoyed this book, and would love to read a sequel that chronicles Zen and Nova's further adventures.

text:
THE SUDDEN APPEARANCE OF HOPE is an excellent mystery/thriller with a highly unusual concept. Viewpoint character Hope Arden has a gift (and a curse)--she is totally forgettable. Not forgettable because of her drab personality or unremarkable physical features (in fact she is strikingly beautiful), but forgettable because no one can ever remember seeing her, speaking to her, or interacting with her, once they stop looking directly at her.

For Hope, this means she is always alone. She has no one to share life experiences with, because no one remembers being with her. Of course Hope remembers people--her parents, for example--but they retain no memory of her. Whenever Hope meets her parents or anyone else, they see a stranger that they are meeting for the first time.

This guarantees that Hope can always make a great first impression, because she has an unlimited number of do-overs available. But it also means she can't get medical care (doctors and nurses forget to administer prescribed drugs and intended treatments), credit cards (applications are forgotten before they're filed), or a myriad of other things needed in today's daily life. So what does Hope do to survive? She becomes a thief, takes whatever she needs or wants (including a college education), and fills her daily thoughts with learned facts and the counting of everything around her.

In the novel, Hope makes the big mistake of stealing a famous diamond necklace from an Arab princess who is attending a party for the elite members of "Perfection". Perfection is an app that tracks individuals' shopping, exercising, eating, telephone contacts, and everything else, with the goal of advising each individual how to become his or her perfect self. As a person does the things that the app recommends, he or she receives points, and whenever the person accumulates enough points, he or she receives an amazing reward (for example, a weekend at an expensive resort). The Perfection elite--those who have accumulated a million Perfection points--are all beautiful people (even when they are NOT movie stars).

Hope's big mistake isn't that she stole some hard-to-fence diamonds--it's that she disrupted a Perfection party announcing the expansion of Perfection into a new area of the world. The Perfection CEO is incensed, and sends his security people after Hope to kill her. Naturally the pursuers are handicapped by Hope's forgettability, but they're good enough to pick up her trail over and over again, and Hope is hard-pressed to stay alive.

This is only the first part of the plot--it becomes even more complex as the novel continues. In my opinion, this is an extremely well-written book, although perhaps a little repetitive and about 50-100 pages too long. It has memorable characters (Hope, Gauguin, Evard, Byron, Reina, Gracie), and presents an intriguing view of the power of social networking apps to influence crowds, and the power of public and private security and law enforcement agencies to track people through today's surveillance cameras and interconnected databases. The ending is satisfying, and the bloody scenes are tastefully handled.

Some readers may be put off by the facts that Hope is constantly reciting, but these are an integral part of who she is and how she survives being constantly alone. To my mind, the concept of individual survival despite being truly forgettable is imperfectly worked out, but I still think this novel is brilliant.

text:
DOUGLAS MACARTHUR: AMERICAN WARRIOR isn't a good choice for someone like me, who merely wants a readable, fairly complete biography of the famous soldier. The book is 834 pages long, without counting the footnotes, bibliography, index, or other extraneous material!

The text IS readable, but it's simply too much unless you have a serious amateur historian's interest in the life of the late General MacArthur. I haven't read any other MacArthur biographies, so I can't attest to this one's accuracy, or to whether it contains new material, or to whether the author's take on MacArthur's persona is intriguing. However, in looking at the book as a historian might, I'm bothered by the author's decision to present so many real-life conversations as if the reader were "a fly on the wall" at the actual event.

The conversations sound like they were taken verbatim from a Movietone newsreel, or a tape, or a transcript of the actual event--but the passages are NOT footnoted with specific references to the source material. Presenting the conversations in this manner makes the book feel more alive and current (and certainly adds to the overall readability), but it seems to play fast and loose with actual facts. I never feel that I can trust a biography as a reference, when it includes conversations with words in quotation marks, without nailing down the factual sources.

A random example of what I'm talking about is this (from Chapter 15): "The general drew himself upright. 'Don't you know,' he said in his sonorous voice, 'the Japanese haven't yet made the bomb with my name on it?" This sounds like the biographer is describing something seen in actual photographic footage. However, the footnote (#22 in my Advance Reading Copy) says "Quezon, Manchester, 255"--which suggests that the conversation is not necessarily verbatim, because it is based on something this biographer found in some other biographer's book (in this case, a biography by Manchester). Whether the actual words were ever spoken depends on where Manchester got them from, and what he cites as his source.

It may well be that the footnotes will be fleshed out in the final version of this biography (to be released on June 14, 2016), so that my problem with the citations to the source material will be corrected. Nevertheless, I believe that the usefulness of this biography as a reference book is substantially limited by the author's decision to present so many conversations as if they were "overheard". For this reason, I rate DOUGLAS MACARTHUR: AMERICAN WARRIOR at 3 stars ("It's okay" on the official Amazon scale).

text:
KING MAYBE, the fifth "Junior Bender" mystery, is a total delight for fans of the snarkily comic mystery genre. Timothy Hallinan is every bit as skilled at the genre as was the late Donald Westlake (author of [[ASIN:0446677035 The Hot Rock]] and [[ASIN:B005UK7U8A The Busy Body]]). I'm in awe of both writers, because writing a mystery that works perfectly as a mystery, while remaining genuinely humorous in tone, has got to be incredibly difficult.

KING MAYBE works fine as a standalone--it's my first "Junior Bender", and I loved everything about it (except for the clunky title). Junior is a professional burglar, and it's a tribute to his skills that he's never been caught. He's loveable as a boyfriend, as a divorced family man who's devoted to his daughter, as an honorable businessman who lives up to his contracts, and as a consummate pro who willingly shares his best burglar-trade secrets.

In the novel, Junior's fence (Stinky) hires him to steal a rare stamp. The job goes sideways when the stamp's owner, a fearsome gambling-debt collector (The Slugger), returns home unexpectedly. Junior gets away with the stamp, but only with unexpected help from his mysterious girl friend (Ronnie). Then he can't fence the stamp, because Stinky has gone into hiding after his houseboy was murdered.

Junior desperately needs money from the job, to pay off an old movie producer friend (Jake) who has hired a contract killer to do Junior. Then more and more problems surface. "Bad luck arrives on the wind," as Junior's mentor and surrogate father (Herbie) used to say; and the San Fernando Valley winds just won't stop blowing.

This is a fast read, a comic mystery novel with many unguessable plot twists, all neatly wrapped up and tied with a bow at the end. There are also many fun, interesting characters, including a couple of skilled hacker kids, a hit woman, a call girl, and a wheelchair-bound artist. I enjoyed every minute, and can't wait to read the remaining four Junior Bender mysteries.

text:
DIPLOMA MILL$: HOW FOR-PROFIT COLLEGES STIFFED STUDENTS, TAXPAYERS, AND THE AMERICAN DREAM is a scholarly, footnoted history (from colonial times to the present day) of the rise and expansion of for-profit colleges and universities (FPCUs) in the United States. It focuses on "the long history of interplay between national policy and predatory behavior critical to understanding current debates over the for-profit industry." The book's goal, the author tells us, is "producing a substantive history with an eye toward the long view that could help us put recent events within their broader context."

The book achieves this goal, but it's a dry-as-dust, boring history. I was expecting to learn details about how specific fraudulent schools operated (e.g., how they recruited students, how they manipulated financial reports to keep government funds coming, what kind of lasting damage they did to their failing students, whether they helped any students). Unfortunately, there is little anecdotal material about the exploited students themselves.

Information about the FPCUs' actual fraudulent practices is usually presented in a detached, generalized fashion--for example, "Along with canvassing, FPCUs recruited students by printing false or misleading advertising in local papers and periodicals. Proprietary schools often marketed themselves under the employment sections of newspapers." There is a lot of description of government programs, such as the G.I. Bill and the Health Education Act of 1965, though not much explanation of how the FPCUs managed to take advantage of particular legislation.

As a scholarly history, DIPLOMA MILL$ is probably a worthwhile book. However, because it pays so little attention to the impact of FPCUs on the exploited students, I rate it at 3 stars ("It's okay" on the official Amazon scale).
product: Professional Trade Compliance Certification Study Guide

text:
THE DISNEY WAY: HARNESSING THE MANAGEMENT SECRETS OF DISNEY IN YOUR COMPANY (Third Edition) is an "inspirational" business management book. It extracts a basic mantra (Dream Believe Dare Do) from a Walt Disney quote, then recounts Disney company anecdotes that supposedly illustrate how Walt himself used those guiding principles to manage and build his immensely successful family entertainment business.

The basic Walt quote--"as legend has it," the authors say--is: "I dream, I test my dreams against my beliefs, I dare to take risks, and I execute my vision to make those dreams come true." Stated that way, the ideas don't strike me as a specific formula for business success, nor do they provide any real explanation for Walt's individual success or for the Disney corporation's continued success.

Likewise, an anecdote about how Walt ordered construction workers to test out new rides such as "Pirates of the Caribbean"--and ordered one man to keep riding until he figured out what it was that he sensed was wrong with it (no fireflies)--doesn't really fit into the mantra format. (Maybe it illustrates Believe (in your ideas)?). To me, it merely shows that Walt was a perfectionist who paid attention to detail, and could afford to indulge his perfectionism. I think that Walt's success is primarily explained by his amazing artistic creativity, and by his clear understanding of what to put into family entertainment to give it the widest possible appeal--and these are things that cannot be taught in business management seminars.

Probably each included anecdote is capable of inspiring an "aha" moment in someone. However, the presentation--the forcing of disparate anecdotes into the Dream Believe Dare Do structure--severely detracts from the readability and enjoyment of the stories. Also, the anecdotes and principles (e.g., "never a customer, always a guest") seem very dated as examples of how to manage and develop a successful business. For these reasons, I rate THE DISNEY WAY at 2 stars ("I don't like it" on the official Amazon scale).
product: Professional Trade Compliance Certification Study Guide

text:
AUNTY LEE'S CHILLED REVENGE, the third "Aunty Lee" cozy mystery, is NOT as delightfully funny and charming as the first one ([[ASIN:0062227157 Aunty Lee's Delights]]), but is much, much better than the second one ([[ASIN:0062338323 Aunty Lee's Deadly Specials]]). Once you've been properly introduced to Aunty Lee (a tenacious Singaporean busybody and gifted amateur detective), you'll want to read the whole series.

"Chilled Revenge" works as a standalone, because of the solid mystery plot that's independent of the events in the earlier books. However, it has a LOT of continuing characters, because Aunty Lee has such a large extended family (comprised of relatives, friends, domestic helpers, good customers, and local police). For this reason, to get the full Aunty Lee experience, it's a good idea to start with the first book ("Delights").

In "Chilled Revenge", Aunty Lee herself is "on ice", recovering from a sprained ankle caused by a fall--from a stool balanced on an upside-down pail placed on a coffee table. Aunty Lee needed to get something, and had to improvise, because her famous Singapore café lacked a stepladder. Now Aunty Lee's café is being run by her new partner (Cherril), her foreign domestic helper/confidante (Nina), and her annoying stepdaughter-in-law (Selina aka Silly-Nah). Aunty Lee is feeling bored and useless.

So when a visitor (Allison) who was threatening to sue three of Aunty Lee's friends (Cherril, Josephine, and Brian) is murdered in a local hotel, Aunty Lee invites the victim's frightened, distraught sister (Vallerie) to stay in her home (and be nurtured) until the police can solve the crime. Before long, a lady veterinarian (Dr. Kang) is murdered and the modus operandi connects the two crimes. Both seem to relate to Allison's threatened lawsuit over vicious Internet harassment that had been set in motion a few years before by Aunty Lee's animal-loving friends. Allison had hired Dr. Kang to put down an engaging shelter puppy (Lola), instead of returning Lola to the shelter as promised when Lola's adoption didn't work out. The ensuing national scandal had disgraced Allison, and caused her to become well-known in Singapore as "the puppy killer".

The distraught Vallerie turns out to be a handful--insulting Singapore and its cuisine, hurling unfounded accusations at possible suspects (including Aunty Lee's friends), and helping herself liberally to Aunty Lee's food and hospitality without expressing any gratitude. Aunty Lee's usual careful attention to Vallerie's nutritional needs has little effect. Although hobbled by her ankle injury and forced to rely on others to help her solve the murders, Aunty Lee of course puts all the tangled clues together at the end, and in the final scene serves up her fabulous "chilled revenge".

"Chilled Revenge" has vivid descriptions of Aunty Lee's varied Singaporean delicacies, and the author works many Singaporean customs seamlessly into the narrative. Aunty Lee's depth of character is disclosed through her interesting ruminations on the importance of food, babies, and everyday relationships in shaping people's lives and ultimate happiness. The plot is full of twists, and all of the threads are tied up satisfactorily. Once again, I'm looking forward to reading the next book in the series, because I've become a major fan of the quirky but wise Aunty Lee. I rate "Chilled Revenge" at 4 stars ("I like it" on the official Amazon scale.)

text:
If you love baseball, and cozy mystery/thrillers, you are pretty much guaranteed to like DOUBLE SWITCH. This is the second novel featuring Johnny Adcock, the likeable, bored, left-handed relief pitcher for the San Jose Bay Dogs (a Major League team). Author T.T. Monday (a pseudonym for novelist Nick Taylor) really understands baseball, and he creates a convincing "inside baseball" atmosphere that doesn't sacrifice the mystery plot.

After all, reliever Adcock has established his reputation among major league baseball players as a private investigator who will handle their domestic problems confidentially and free of charge--even though his moonlighting work may land him in dangerous situations. DOUBLE SWITCH works as a stand-alone, but those new to the series might want to start with the first book in the series, [[ASIN:0804169829 The Setup Man]].

In the novel, a professional image-shaper (Tiff Tate) seeks Johnny's help in solving a problem related to her newest client (Yonel Ruiz), a rookie hitting phenom with the Colorado Rockies. Yonel, a Cuban defector who escaped Cuba by swimming through shark-infested waters, is being blackmailed by the Venezuelan cartel that helped to smuggle him out of Cuba. Johnny begins by contacting Ruiz' hitting coach (Erik Magnusson) to get some background on the kid. Erik meets Johnny for a drink, and reveals that he's been warned by an anonymous phone call not to talk about Yonel if he wants to live. That said, Erik agrees to meet with Johnny again at Coors Field the next morning. But Erik never shows, because he's been murdered in the Rockies' video room.

Now Johnny is fully committed to finding out who murdered his friend Erik, and to learning the truth about Yonel. Unfortunately, his inquiries are constantly hampered by Major League Baseball's new Director of Personnel Security (Jim Feldspar), who is officially in charge of helping players in their dealings with the law. Feldspar blocks all of Johnny's efforts to get information, always reminding him that the Commissioner of Baseball takes a really, really dim view of Johnny's private investigations.

Naturally Johnny sees Feldspar as merely an obstacle to go around, but eventually he needs Feldspar's services himself--when someone craftily sets Johnny up as the fall guy in a double homicide. As if he didn't have enough on his plate, Johnny must juggle the attentions of various attractive women, even as he works at keeping his one serious romance, with librarian Connie O'Connell, on an even keel and going forward.

Like the first one, this Johnny Adcock novel is well-written, well-plotted, has surprising plot twists, and is a lot of fun. Johnny is a bullpen pitcher who only works a few minutes per game (he usually faces a single batter), so he has a lot of time on his hands. Events at the conclusion of DOUBLE SWITCH suggest that Johnny's PI days may be over, but I sincerely hope that ain't so. It isn't like Johnny to abide strictly by the rules, so I think he'll find a workaround--and I'm looking forward to reading the next Johnny Adcock mystery.

text:
This delightful book, FOOD AND THE CITY, reminds me of the excellent interview-based books by Studs Terkel (e.g.,[[ASIN:1565843428 WORKING]]; THE GOOD WAR). Author Ina Yalof has interviewed all manner of New York City's "food people" (chefs, cooks, bakers, restaurateurs, street vendors, food purveyors), and she presents their individual stories in short essays that really bring the people to life. Because the stories have a consistent first-person voice, the book is easy on the reader--the narrative is not burdened by direct quotes, extraneous facts, or numbered footnotes.

Yalof catches the essence of what each interviewee has to share. I ordered this book because it promised an interview with the inventor of the Cronut (which I became addicted to in a local bakery's copy called the CroDough). Not only did the interview deliver on the Cronut, it provided this delightful snippet about another of the baker's inventions, the DKA, a caramelized croissant. "It's a highly addictive pastry. Seriously, somebody buys a DKA, starts eating it on the way out …, gets halfway down the block, makes a U-Turn, and comes back to get another one. It makes me laugh to see this."

The stories are organized into chapters, more or less by type of food business. For example, "Starting From Scratch" has eight narrators who started and built successful food businesses from absolutely nothing; "Duck and Other Dynasties" has eight narrators who inherited family food businesses and kept them going.

Here is the complete Table of Contents, along with the names of the individuals (and their businesses) that appear in the book. If there is any one name that interests you, you will probably not regret having purchased this book!

STARTING FROM SCRATCH: Dominique Ansel (Dominique Ansel Bakery); Noe Baltazar (Buena Vista Tortillas); Sam Solasz (Master Purveyors); Jelena Pasic (Harlem Shake); Sammy Anastasiou (City Diner); Lauren Clark (Sucre Mort Pralines); Alvin Lee Smalls (Lee Lee's Baked Goods); Mohamed Abouelenein (The Halal Guys).

DUCK AND OTHER DYNASTIES: Wilson Tang (Nom Wah Tea Parlor); Douglas Corwin (Crescent Duck Farm); Palma Denino (Denino's Pizzeria and Tavern); Michael Burke (Denino's Pizzeria and Tavern); Bobby Weiss (Blue Ribbon Fish Company); Amy Rubenstein (Peter Luger Steak House); David Fox (Fox's U-bet); Alexander Poulos (Papaya King).

TAKING THE HEAT: Mackenzie Arrington (Momofuku Ma Peche); Luis "The Mexican Menace" Iglesias (Grand Central Oyster Bar); Jesus Albino "Albi" Chauca (The Four Seasons); Carmen Melendez (Tom Cat Bakery); Guyo Pinyo Ketavanan (The Four Seasons); Justo Thomas (Le Bernardin).

ROMANS A CHEF: Patrick Collins (The Dutch); Sandy Ingber (Grand Central Oyster Bar); Ghaya Oliveiria (Daniel); John Greeley (The "21" Club); Toni Robertson (Mandarin Oriental); Jeb Burke (News Corporation); Luisa Fernandes (Robert).

THE PARTY LINE: Chris Edmonds (The Pierre Hotel); Lulu Powers (Bicoastal "Entertainologist"); Burt Leventhal (Newman & Leventhal Kosher Caterers); Sylvia Weinstock (Sylvia Weinstock Cakes);

FRONT OF THE HOUSE: Ed Schoenfeld (Red Farm); Miriam Tsionov (Cheburechnaya); Jonathan Parrilla (Cafeteria); David McQueen (Gramercy Tavern); Alexander Smalls (The Cecil); Nino Esposito (Sette Mezzo).

PAIRINGS: Alessandro Borgognone (Sushi Nakazawa), Desiuke Nakazawa (Sushi Nakazawa); Connie McDonald (Levain bakery), Pam Weekes (Levain bakery); Bryce Shuman (Betony), Eamon Rockey (Betony).

COUNTER CULTURE: Lenny Burke (Zabar's); Chris Borgotti (Borgotti's Ravioli and Egg Noodles); Tony Schatz (Schatzie the Butcher); Rob Kaufelt (Murray's Cheese); Charlie Sahadi (Sahadi's).

text:
THE LETTER WRITER spends so much time re-creating the historical setting--New York City as it existed in 1942, right after the luxury liner Normandie caught fire and sank in the harbor--that it becomes a long, slow-moving, literary mystery. This is true even though the rather well-developed characters (detective Woodrow Cain, Jewish letter-writer Danziger, girl friend Beryl) engage in a substantial amount of evidence-gathering and crime-solving in the course of the novel.

In the novel, detective Cain (newly arrived in NYC) is assigned to investigate a "floater" pulled from the Hudson River. Against protocol, he examines the body, and discovers a ticket stub from Yorkville (an area of the city that is populated by immigrants who sympathize with Germany's ongoing cause in Europe); a tattoo reading "Sabine"; and several cigarette burns suggesting torture. Mistaking a newspaper reporter for the coroner's representative, Cain gives these details to the reporter, who publishes them in a news story the next day. When letter writer/information specialist Danziger sees the story, he realizes that publication of the details has placed both Cain's and his own life in danger, and contacts Cain..

The intricate plot proceeds--through alternating chapters told from Danziger's and Cain's separate viewpoints--to reveal a corrupt NYC police department that "loses" crimes as political favors; a secret spying collaboration between U.S. government and military officers and the NYC underworld; and many sordid details of both Cain's and Danziger's past lives. Cain's detective efforts are hampered by Danziger's unwillingness to share information except in tiny "need to know" bits, by a hostile police captain who gives him punitive make-work assignments, by his influential father-in-law's spying, and by his own troubled past (in which he shot and killed his partner, who was his wife's lover).

The novel is extremely well-written, and the plot is successful at providing a convincing imagined explanation for certain very real historical events. (The author's Afterword explains clearly what is real, and what is the author's invention.) However, because the novel is too literary and slow-moving for my taste, I rate it at 3 stars ("It's okay" on the official Amazon scale).

text:
BOYS AMONG MEN is a must-read for high school basketball players who are thinking of entering the NBA draft as soon as possible (one year removed from high school under the rule instituted in 2005). By telling the stories of the trailblazing NBA stars who successfully skipped college basketball (e.g., Kevin Garnett, Kobe Bryant), author Jonathan Abrams makes it clear how hard it is for any athlete to make the transition directly from high school player to professional player.

What the NBA prep-to-pro successes had in common was an incredible work ethic and devotion to improving their basketball skills, as well as coaches and teams that were committed to allowing their immature high-schoolers time to develop and to grow into their bodies. What the NBA prep-to-pro failures had in common were raw basketball skills that suffered from lack of college coaching, too much money to spend, and too little personal development before their short-term contracts expired.

The book is also a must-read for serious NBA history fans, because of the detailed accounts of the intense scouting and recruiting of the early prep-to-pro successes, and of the behind-the-scenes draft-pick talks among college and pro coaches, owners, managers, and players' agents. (There is one particularly interesting to me segment on how Nike came to acquire Michael Jordan for the wildly successful "Air Jordan" line of shoes.)

The book is surprisingly easy to read, considering the wealth of factual detail it contains (gathered from many, many firsthand interviews) about the prep-to-pro players, the history of the NBA, and the development of certain NBA franchises from about 1995 to the present day. However, the text is often weighed down by the sheer quantity of names. An enormous number of people were involved in the decision-making that swirled around the key NBA players featured in the book, and it's difficult to keep track of who all the people are, unless you're already familiar with a lot of NBA history. For this reason, BOYS AMONG MEN isn't a good choice for a casual NBA fan like myself, and I rate it at 4 stars ("I like it" on the official Amazon scale).

text:
SWITCHEROO, the 18th "skeleton detective" novel, is mostly a travelogue set in Jersey (in the Channel Islands). The mystery plot is thin, and the story is padded out with a lot of descriptions of archeological sites, present-day buildings, unusual geographical features (e.g., tar pits), and restaurant meals enjoyed by the characters. The mystery itself has an historical background, loosely tied to the German occupation of Jersey during World War II.

In the historical part of the novel, two Jersey families agree to swap their infant sons, so that the more sickly child can be evacuated to hoped-for safety in England until the coming German occupation is over. Only the families and their lawyer know about this arrangement. After the war, the evacuated parents return with the child to Jersey, and the parents who remained behind make good on their promise to deed over Jersey property in return for their child's safety. The children grow up, but both are murdered (the bones of one are recovered from a tar pit), and their murders remain unsolved.

In the present-day bulk of the novel, a bored Gideon Oliver is recruited by a high-ranking Jersey politician (Rafe Carlisle) to examine the tar pit bones (which are purported to include some of his father's bones). Rafe is a wealthy forensics enthusiast, and is curious to know what bone expert Oliver will have to say. From the outset, Oliver is suspicious of the official findings--which determined that the tar pit bones were the bones from two persons of specific ages--because specific ages cannot be determined from the examination of bones alone.

As always happens in mystery novels, it would have been best if Rafe had let sleeping dogs lie. Oliver's investigation naturally leads to another murder. In the course of his investigation, he sees a lot of interesting Jersey buildings, eats some excellent Jersey food, and learns a lot about the history and political structure of Jersey. Eventually Oliver solves the old, cold case, and the guilty present-day murderer is brought to justice.

I have read all, or nearly all, of the Gideon Oliver books, and found all of them, including this one, to be enjoyable. However, SWITCHEROO is definitely NOT one of the best of the series. Because it is slow-moving, has a thin mystery plot, and is somewhat lacking in Gideon Oliver's trademark skeleton analysis, I rate it at 3 stars ("It's okay" on the official Amazon scale).
product: Professional Trade Compliance Certification Study Guide

text:
What a book! SHAKER tells a brutal story--generally without graphic torture scenes, but brutal nonetheless--about the making of a professional killer who is almost, but not quite, a stone killer. The prologue describing the Los Angeles earthquake damage was so over-the-top, I expected a snarkily humorous thriller. But no, this novel is darkly serious. It's the story of a well-executed professional hit, followed by a gang mugging that went wrong and then made everything that followed after go wrong, too.

At the end, I couldn't say that I LIKED this book--it's too dark for that. But it kept me riveted on hitman Roy's story, and to a lesser extent on damaged female detective Kelly's story, too. At the end, I felt terrible about the way things worked out (as they had to) for several of the more likeable characters, and even felt very sad for Roy.

In the novel, Roy Cooper flies to an earthquake-damaged Los Angeles to carry out a routine hit. The hit goes smoothly, but an aftershock hits and disorients Roy as he is leaving the victim's home. While searching for his rental car, Roy stumbles into a mugging, loses his gun to a gangbanger who uses it to execute the mugging victim, and is himself shot and seriously wounded. When Roy wakes up in a hospital, he sees the mugging on TV--someone filmed the whole thing with a cellphone. As if that weren't bad enough, the mugging victim was about to be elected mayor, so Roy is now part of a national news story. Everyone thinks Roy is a hero for trying to stop the mugging, but Roy knows that the TV exposure has already sent professional killers of the very worst kind after him.

Roy's backstory is told in flashback chapters alternating with the ongoing action. Usually I don't care for novels that incorporate extensive flashbacks, but in this novel Roy's past is every bit as intriguing as his present desperate situation. Also, the back and forth time changes are perfectly done--at no point was I tempted to skip ahead to the continuation of a flashback chapter on account of an end-of-chapter cliffhanger.

The ongoing action focuses on Roy's need to locate and kill the gangbanger who took his gun. The flashback action focuses on the things Roy did to survive a lengthy incarceration in a juvenile facility (caused by his own mother's betrayal), and on how Kelly came to be known (unjustly) as the most racist cop on the planet. SHAKER is a hard-to-describe but truly excellent, truly memorable literary thriller.
product: Professional Trade Compliance Certification Study Guide

text:
OFF THE GRID is a beautifully written and paced, super-exciting, page-turner of a thriller. As I neared the end, I couldn't put the book down--and I ended up losing most of a night's sleep. This was my first "Joe Pickett" novel, but it worked fine as a standalone.

At first I was put off by the opening plot device of using Joe's friend Nate Romanowski's dream to foreshadow the coming action. However, once I learned that Nate has demonstrated paranormal abilities in past books, then the dream device was okay. For sure, C.J. Box is a consummate master of the dream technique, because the dream is interwoven with the plot in a believable fashion.

As the novel opens, Nate, a wanted federal felon, is living totally off the grid on a remote Wyoming ranch. But he is located by a secret group of federal agents (the Wolverines), and then coerced (because of his wanted status) into helping them with an anti-terrorism operation in Wyoming's Red Desert. The Wolverines have satellite, camera, and cellphone surveillance systems capable of tracking every individual in the entire U.S., and Nate doesn't trust them at all. But they insist that they need him to locate an Americanized Arab royal (Ibby), who is suspected of planning a major, crippling attack on the U.S. communications/military surveillance infrastructure. Nate's black ops survival skills are needed for the Red Desert itself, and Nate's master falconer skills are needed to give him an "in" with the royal, who is also a master falconer.

While Nate is being "recruited", it's business as usual for Joe, a Wyoming game warden. He learns that a grizzly bear (collared and tracked with a GPS signal) has apparently met up with--and possibly killed and eaten--an unsuspecting elk hunter in the Bighorn Mountains. But before Joe can track down the highly dangerous grizzly, he gets a call from the Wyoming governor (Rulon), who has heard about federal agents in his state, and wants to know what they're doing in Wyoming without notifying him or asking his permission. The grizzly bear was headed straight for the Red Desert before the batteries on his collar gave out, so Joe heads off to investigate the federal agents' activities, using the grizzly bear tracking as his cover. Naturally, Nate's mission and Joe's mission converge, and there is a truly exciting conclusion with very few men and women from either side left standing after all the dust clears.

Throughout the novel there is a lot of bloodshed. The plot, which centers on overzealous government surveillance, is also memorable and scary because of the underlying truths about the vulnerability of the U.S. communications systems. The plot requires a lot of extraordinary coincidences, but the plot events are well-planned, so that they're believable when the reader encounters them. OFF THE GRID is a very fast read, a superbly written thriller that is a beautiful example of what a thriller novel ought to be.

text:
Like all of the "Teach Yourself Visually" manuals, TEACH YOURSELF VISUALLY IPAD (4th Edition) is an excellent choice for brand-new iPad users. Exact iPad screenshots explain every button and every procedure--showing exactly what a user should be seeing on the screen while following the manual's simple, detailed, step-by-step written instructions. The screenshots are overlaid with lettered and numbered circles that help the user quickly shift back and forth between the written-out steps and the related buttons shown in the screenshots.

With this manual, the fastest way to learn how to do something is to skip the rather brief, inadequate Index, and instead check the Table of Contents for your general area of interest. The TOC lists the chapters by general subject and by detailed subchapters that explain the steps for each covered operation. For example, Chapter 5, Networking and Communicating, includes the following subchapters: Using Airplane Mode; Connect Bluetooth Devices to Your iPad; Control Cellular Data and Background Refresh: Connect Your iPad to a Different Carrier; Specify Your iPad's Cellular Access Point; Share Items via AirDrop; Share Your Cellular iPad's Internet Access; Connect to Wi-Fi Networks; Log In to Wi-Fi Hotspots; Connect to a Network via VPN; Send Instant Messages; Mute a Conversation or Share Your Location; Manage Your Instant Messages; Share Your Updates Using Twitter; Post Updates on Facebook; Chat Face-to-Face Using Facetime.

Experienced iPad users who have recently upgraded to iOS 9 may find some useful tips in this manual. However, it's a manual designed for new users, and focuses on managing the iPad's settings, using the main features, and navigating. Answers to unconventional questions (e.g., how do I set up a POP email account in iOS 9?) are NOT to be found here.

+++CAUTION: this manual covers the newer iPad models (iPad Air, iPad Mini, iPad Pro). Some iOS 9 features that are discussed in detail (e.g., Siri) are not available on older iPads (e.g., iPad 2). However, this manual can be used with any iPad that is running iOS 9.

+++CAUTION: because Apple frequently updates the iPad's operating system, the manual's screen shots may be slightly different from what you see on your particular iPad's screen. However, the basic procedures for using an iPad stay pretty much the same. If a feature you see in the manual's screenshot is NOT in the same position on your iPad display, the feature will probably still be around on a nearby screen. If you can't find the feature, you can usually locate it on your particular iPad model by Googling. This manual covers the iPad iOS through iOS 9 (the version released on September 26, 2015).
product: Professional Trade Compliance Certification Study Guide

text:
Using an iPad or iPad Mini is mostly a matter of understanding the operating system (iOS), which Apple updates regularly for free. This MY iPAD manual is up to date with all the features of iOS 9, which applies to the iPad 2 and later iPads, including the Mini. If you have an earlier iPad, you will need an earlier edition of this manual that expressly covers the original iPad (and its applicable iOS).

I really like the QUE "My" series of manuals (e.g., My iPad, My Samsung Galaxy S4, My Windows 10). These manuals always seem to include exactly the information that I need for dealing with a new, unfamiliar mobile device or an upgrade to a new computer operating system. They come with excellent indexes, and are copiously illustrated with screen shots that make it simple to understand and follow the step-by-step instructions for each thing that you want to accomplish. They explain what the individual settings buttons do, and also tell you where to go for additional help on specialized problems that can't be fully covered in the manuals.

In addition, these QUE manuals include many, many useful tips that point you to features you might otherwise never find for yourself. For example, in this particular manual, there is a "Go further" box that explains how to use your iPad virtual keyboard as a trackpad (a new feature in iOS 9). Another example is the text section that tells how to "Use a Real Password" to unlock your iPad with an alphanumeric code or a custom number that is longer than four digits.

MY IPAD is a great quick reference manual because the presentation makes the material found in Apple's downloadable iPad manual so much more accessible. The "Contents at a Glance" shows you how extensive the coverage is:

Chapter 1 Getting Started
Chapter 2 Customizing Your iPad
Chapter 3 Networking and Syncing
Chapter 4 Playing Music and Video
Chapter 5 Reading Books
Chapter 6 Organizing Your Life
Chapter 7 Surfing the Web
Chapter 8 Communicating With Email and Messaging
Chapter 9 Taking and Editing Photos
Chapter 10 Recording Video
Chapter 11 Writing With Pages
Chapter 12 Spreadsheets With Numbers
Chapter 13 Presentations With Keynote
Chapter 14 Navigating With Maps
Chapter 15 The World of Apps
Chapter 16 Must-Have Apps
Chapter 17 Games and Entertainment
Chapter 18 iPad Accessories
Chapter 19 Maintaining Your iPad and Solving Problems

Of course there is a much more detailed Table of Contents included, as well as the already-mentioned Index. There is no need for a lengthy troubleshooting chapter, because (in my experience at least) iPads are so solid that rebooting will fix nearly any problem. If you do have a serious hardware problem, this book even tells you exactly how to make a repair/appointment at your local Apple store, so you won't have to wait in line or return at a less busy time.
product: Professional Trade Compliance Certification Study Guide

text:
Based on a real scientific prediction, SUNFAIL imagines a world in which all major cities (e.g., New York, London) suddenly lose all electrical power. Only the Hidden, an evil conspiracy, has anticipated the event; it is now seeking to exploit the situation for massive future profits. As the Hidden moves to set its plan into motion, it ruthlessly murders its unsuspecting recruits, to erase all human knowledge of the changes they have put into effect.

Standing against the Hidden's coming world domination are just two individuals, Sophie and Jake. Sophie (in on the conspiracy), is pursued by a relentless assassin. Jake, her former lover, has witnessed some of the Hidden's ruthless murders, and is determined to avenge the deaths. Will these two succeed in thwarting the Hidden and saving the world for its ordinary citizens?

SUNFAIL is a page-turner with an exciting, complex plot. All the story threads are expertly woven together, and the conclusion is a surprise. This is an excellent example of a satisfying, memorable scifi/fantasy thriller.
product: Professional Trade Compliance Certification Study Guide

text:
Like all of the Teach Yourself Visually computer books, TEACH YOURSELF VISUALLY OFFICE 2016 is aimed at novice users. It uses exact screenshots to explain even the simplest procedures (e.g., "Create a New File", found in the opening overview section titled "Office Features").

The screenshots explain every icon, button, tile, screen area, and procedure. They show exactly what the user should be seeing on the display, if the user correctly follows the detailed step-by-step written instructions for the procedure (e.g., "Change Word's Views"; "Create a Pivot Table" (in Excel); "Add and Edit Slide Text" (in PowerPoint); "Create a Database Based on a Template" (in Access); "Create a New Contact" (in Outlook); "Swap Pictures" (in Publisher); and "Navigate One Note"). The screenshots are overlaid with lettered and numbered circles that help the user shift back and forth between the written steps and the appropriate navigational buttons and menus shown in the screenshots.

Although this is a technically sound book, and should be somewhat helpful to rank beginners, it barely scratches the surface of each Office 2016 application (Word, Excel, Powerpoint, Access, Outlook, Publisher, One Note). Thus it is of quite limited value, even to novice Office 2016 users.

I think that beginning Office 2016 users would be better off investing in the individual manuals for the applications they use most, as the manuals become available (e.g., [[ASIN:1119074665 Teach Yourself VISUALLY Word 2016 ]] ). For this reason, I rate TEACH YOURSELF VISUALLY OFFICE 2016 at just 3 stars ("It's okay" on the official Amazon scale).
product: Professional Trade Compliance Certification Study Guide

text:
WINDOWS 10 STEP BY STEP is the official Microsoft guide to learning the Windows 10 operating system. It is intended for "home and business users of desktop and portable computers and devices running Windows 10 Home or Windows 10 Pro"--users who are Windows beginners and users who are already familiar with Windows. However, it is primarily a practical learning book (with practice files covering basic Windows operations, and configuration explanations accompanied by screen shots). It is not a complete Win10 OS reference book.

Specifically, this guide covers the features of the Windows 10 operating system itself. It explains "how you can use the operating system and the included tools, such as File Explorer, to access and manage the apps and data files you use in your work and play". It sometimes mentions the apps that are included with the Win10 software (e.g., Maps, Photos, Mail, Calendar, Groove Music, Windows DVD Player), but it is NOT a guide to using these apps.

WINDOWS STEP BY STEP is a very good practical guide to learning Win10, but it is probably NOT the best choice for experienced Windows users who are upgrading from earlier Windows versions and merely want to get going quickly with Win10. For those people, I would recommend [[ASIN:0789754339 My Windows 10]], which is more of a quick reference book.

The chapter titles give you a good idea of the scope of the WINDOWS 10 STEP BY STEP coverage:

Chapter 1. Get started using Windows 10
Chapter 2. Personalize your working environment
Chapter 3. Manage folders and files
Chapter 4. Work with apps and notifications
Chapter 5. Safely and efficiently browse the Internet
Chapter 6. Manage peripheral devices
Chapter 7. Manage network and storage resources
Chapter 8. Manage user accounts and settings
Chapter 9. Manage computer settings
Chapter 10. Manage power and access options
Chapter 11. Work more efficiently
Chapter 12. Protect your computer and data

Appendix A. Install or upgrade to Windows 10
Appendix B. Keyboard shortcuts and touchscreen tips

This guide also has a glossary of computer terms (e.g., broadband connection, flick, IP address, parallel port), and a rather extensive index.
product: Professional Trade Compliance Certification Study Guide

text:
In the tradition of Henry James' THE TURN OF THE SCREW, Jonathan Moore's THE POISON ARTIST is a literary mystery that keeps the reader guessing as toxicologist Caleb Maddox assists the San Francisco medical examiner in solving a series of diabolical murders. The killer tortures his victims, but only after drugging them so that they will experience maximum pain. Happily, the reader is spared the details of the torture and the victims' final agonies, as the plot focuses instead on the forensics and police procedures involved in identifying and arresting the killer.

Caleb has broken up with his girlfriend Bridget, who signaled the end of their relationship by throwing a heavy glass tumbler at his head. Escaping her wrath, Caleb leaves home with a head wound and his wallet, and books a room at the famous Palace Hotel. In the hotel bar, he meets a fascinating woman in black, and becomes obsessed with her. Thus the plot splits into parallel threads, tracing (1) the murder investigation, and (2) Caleb's romance and clandestine meetings with the mystery woman.

The San Francisco background is authentic, with accurate descriptions of the western end of Golden Gate Park, downtown San Francisco, the Haas-Lilienthal House, and other Bay Area locations. The forensics are terrific, with discussions of mass spectrometry that ring true to this decidedly nonexpert reviewer. However, there is far too much description of the details of Caleb's romantic interludes (especially the woman's perfume).

The romance causes the plot to bog down, though the pace picks up at the end with an astonishing, exciting finish. Because this mystery/thriller is too literary for my admittedly plebian tastes, I rate it at 3 stars ("It's okay" on the official Amazon scale).
product: Professional Trade Compliance Certification Study Guide

text:
Patricia Cornwell is a terrific writer, a writer who really knows how to keep the reader turning the pages. She also knows her forensics, which is why I've always kind of liked Medical Examiner Kay Scarpetta. However, the series has always been flawed by Scarpetta's being targeted for death by whatever serial killer she is currently investigating. In this novel, it happens again.

In DEPRAVED HEART, Scarpetta's incredibly evil archenemy, Carrie Grethen, is texting Scarpetta videos that were recorded 17 years earlier in her niece Lucy's FBI dorm room. The videos implicate Lucy in a gun felony crime, and Scarpetta drops everything to try to protect Lucy. The plot describes a convoluted conspiracy that involves Scarpetta's FBI-connected husband Benton and Lucy's partner Janet, as they try to thwart Grethen's easy manipulation of government databases that threatens to convert those databases to "data fiction". ("Data fiction" is data that has been falsified to present an untrue pictureof whatever the data is supposed to represent.)

Most of the plot goes on inside Scarpetta's head, as she tries to make sense of the videos and of the behavior of her close family members. There is a lot of filler that is pure description of the details of filthy-rich Lucy's private estate and its furnishings. At the end, everything is wrapped up in a few pages, with loose ends left dangling, and with Scarpetta meekly accepting having been lied to by many different people.

To me, Scarpetta comes across as a whiner and a paranoid, and the fantastical plot is wholly unbelieveable. Although DEPRAVED HEART is well-written and mildly entertaining, with the usual forensics instruction, I rate it at 2 stars ("I don't like it" on the official Amazon scale").

text:
[[VIDEOID:512ae0995c3d5f536cd724c6f4460732]] GREAT LEGO SETS: A VISUAL HISTORY is a beautifully designed and written coffee-table book that any adult LEGO fan should love. It doesn't include every LEGO set ever released--there are far too many sets--but it does include a great many of the most popular, most famous retired sets (e.g., Grand Carousel, Taj Mahal, Millennium Falcon) from every line (e.g., Friends, Bionicles, Mindstorm) that have become expensive collectors' items. This book allows the average, not-so-well-heeled LEGO fan to closely examine and enjoy these storied creations!

The book is a slip-cased set with two "volumes"--a hardcover GREAT LEGO SETS book, and a cardboard box that contains an exclusive "Mini Space Cruiser" model to build. There are 102 pieces in the model, enough to generate some satisfying building time. The pieces--which show through a window in the slip-case--are in a factory-sealed bag, and come with a standard booklet of LEGO building instructions. The exclusive "Mini Space Cruiser" model is a small version of the Space Cruiser from the 1979 Space Cruiser and Moon Base set.

As an armchair historian, I really love the 6-page, illustrated timeline that traces the development and expansion of LEGO products from the very beginning when the original brick sets were placed on the market, to the present day (2015). The timeline clearly shows the years when each new type of product was introduced and/or renamed (e.g., Elves, Architecture, Technic).

The pages that describe the individual LEGO sets include the set number, product line, year introduced, number of pieces, and number of minifigures. Often there is a text box with a brief biography and photograph of the set's designer. There are closeups of the significant features of the models (e.g., building interiors, underlying story, unusual or collectible minifigs). Each included set has a "Brick by Brick" text box that gives factoids (e.g., first example of minifig printing, description of dropped design elements such as a stormtroopers' bath in the Star Wars Death Star, inclusion of rare bricks like Harry Potter's invisibility cloak).

I was pleased to be able to look closely at the Grand Carousel, Green Grocer, Taj Mahal, and detailed Eiffel Tower, because all of these great collectible sets are now out of my price range. Also, I was pleased to see that my three current personal favorites, the original Haunted House, the Detective's Office (currently available from LEGO stores), and the Emerald Night Train were all featured with full coverage.

text:
Because of the many, many photographs, DEHYDRATING AT HOME: GETTING THE BEST FROM YOUR DEHYDRATOR, FROM FRUIT LEATHER TO MEAT JERKIES is a near-coffee-table-quality book for food dehydrating! With 100 recipes, it's not as comprehensive as my personal favorite, [[ASIN:0778802132 The Dehydrator Bible]] (over 400 recipes), but it still tells you everything you need to know to get the most out of a food dehydrator. The lack in total number of recipes is totally made up for by the gorgeous, full-color, full-page photographs of the finished food from the recipes.

The photographs are extremely useful for someone who is just starting out with food dehydrating. They show what the food should look like when it's properly dried, and not under-dried or over-dried. The book also explains how to store dehydrated foods, and how to rehydrate them. (A word of caution: not all dehydrated foods can be stored without refrigeration or freezing, particularly foods with high fat content like nuts.)

The book includes instructions for plain dehydrated foods (e.g. sliced fruits dried without any extra spices or processing), for dehydrated foods requiring a food processor (e.g., fruit leather, lemon cookies), and for dehydrated foods requiring other special preparation techniques (e.g., salt cod jerky, pasta sauce). I'm particularly intrigued by the prospect of drying my own home-made pasta--something that I'd never thought of doing in a home dehydrator before.

The dehydrator that I use is the [[ASIN:B000FFVJ3C Nesco FD-75PR Snackmaster Pro Food Dehydrator]]. I like it a lot, because (1) you can add lots of trays for more drying capacity, and (2) the motor and fan are on top. Having the power assembly on top makes the dehydrator easy to clean, because the unavoidable drips from fresh produce or jerky collect in the bottom tray, which doesn't include a motor, fan, or power element.

The book's chapters are: (1) Basics; (2) Fruits; (3) Vegetables; (4) Leathers; (5) Jerky; (6) Nuts and Seeds; (7) Crackers and Flatbreads; (8) Herbs and Seasonings; (9) Breakfast; (10) Entrees; (11) Desserts; (12) Main Meals; (13) Pet Treats; and (14) Other Household Uses. There is also an Index and a Suppliers list.

text:
A BLOSSOM OF BRIGHT LIGHT is a readable police procedural, but more "literary" than "mystery". The focus is on romantic relationships (Jimmy and Adele), and on the plight of vulnerable, undocumented Hispanic workers who risk unjust, family-destroying deportation (Manuel and his children) or sexual abuse by exploitive employers (a live-in nanny and several others).

In this second Jimmy Vega mystery (the first is [[ASIN:149670228X Land of Careful Shadows]]), a newborn baby dies because Jimmy persuaded Adele to ignore a call from La Casa, her community help center, and to spend the night with him as they'd planned. Neither Adele nor Jimmy are blameworthy--the call only reported that Zambo, a local crazy, had experienced another vision of the Virgin Mary in the woods behind La Casa--but Jimmy is obsessed with solving the mystery of the baby's identity. The mystery deepens when the body of a teenager, who had just given birth, is discovered buried in the woods. No one recognizes the girl, who had carried her baby to full term.

Jimmy is taken off the case when his daughter Joy becomes the prime suspect in the two deaths. Of course, he investigates anyway, risking his job and his working relationships with his partner and other cops. Jimmy is distracted a little by concerns that Adele will accept a political candidate's offer of an important job in Washington, D.C., and leave Lake Holly altogether. In the end, Jimmy wraps up the case in an action-packed finish that is interesting despite being rather unbelievable and contrived.

The writing in this book is excellent, and its portrayal of the plight of undocumented immigrants rings true, especially the portions about deportation procedures. Although most of the larger plot developments are predictable and easily guessed, there are many detailed plot twists that suffice to keep the reader reading. However, because so much of the story is about relationships and the sadness surrounding the unnecessary death of a newborn baby, I rate A BLOSSOM OF BRIGHT LIGHT at 4 stars ("I like it" on the official Amazon scale).

text:
TEACH YOURSELF VISUALLY WINDOWS 10 is a thorough, complete guide for getting started with using Windows 10 on a PC (start with Ch. 1, "Explore the PC Screen") or on a touchscreen PC or tablet (start with Ch. 1, "Explore the Tablet Screen"). Because this guide uses exact screenshots to explain even the simplest procedure (e.g., "Put Windows to Sleep"), it is probably most useful for relatively new and inexperienced computer users.

However, I needed a basic, moderately-priced (about $20) manual for Windows 10, because I wanted to upgrade a Windows 8.1 laptop to Windows 10 without spending hours learning where everything was in the new operating system. I've never gotten comfortable with Windows 8.1, so upgrading without having a manual available was a near-certain recipe for disaster.

In this guide, exact screenshots explain every icon, button, tile, screen area, and procedure. The screenshots show exactly what a user should be seeing on the display while following detailed, written, step-by-step instructions for each procedure (e.g., "Connect to Your Wireless Network", "Navigate Web Pages", "Share a Document or Folder"). The screenshots are overlaid with lettered and numbered circles that help the user shift back and forth between the written steps and the appropriate navigational buttons and menus shown in the screenshots.

With this guide, the fastest way to learn how to do something is to skip the rather brief, inadequate Index, and to check the Table of Contents for the general area of interest. The TOC lists the chapters by general subject and by detailed subchapters that explain the steps for each covered operation. The basic chapter titles are: "Getting Started With Windows", "Customizing Windows", "Surfing the Web", "Sending and Receiving Email", "Getting Social With Windows", "Performing Day-to-Day Tasks with Apps", "Working with Images", "Working with Multimedia", "Editing Documents", "Working with Files", "Sharing Your Computer", "Getting More from a Tablet PC", "Implementing Security", and "Maintaining Windows".

From the TOC chapter titles, you can see that this manual covers only the basic end-user operations of Windows 10, and that the manual's emphasis is on PC tablet computing. However, that's partly because Windows 8.1 and Windows 10 are operating systems that are themselves designed to be oriented more toward mobile computing than desktop computing.

text:
Although I haven't read everything that Jonathan Kellerman has written, I have never read a novel of his that I didn't like. THE MURDERER'S DAUGHTER is no exception--it's another excellent thriller that demonstrates Kellerman's mastery of the genre--and as an added bonus it features a truly strong female protagonist. Clinical psychologist Grace Blades is a self-sufficient woman, not exactly a sociopath (though she can be as ruthless as Carol O'Connell's great Mallory character). Grace Blades is a female individual who is willing--and able--to do whatever it takes to survive.

After witnessing the murder-suicide of her parents, 5-year-old Grace became a foster child. Eventually adopted by an academic couple, she thrived under their nurturing care. Always a loner who was dedicated to reading books, Grace completed school in record time, and obtained a Ph.D. in clinical psychology before she was even twenty-seven years old. As a professional psychologist possessed of unusual empathy, she developed a specialized practice, counseling only "the Haunted" who could benefit from her one-on-one approach and afford her high-end fees.

But Grace's comfortable life changes when a man she met in a casual sexual encounter shows up as a patient--and is murdered before their uneasy doctor-patient relationship can even commence. Grace soon discovers that her deceased patient gave her a false name. Then a thug with a knife turns up at her secluded beach home, and Grace's troubles really begin. Still the loner, she solves the problems on her own, utilizing her high intelligence and cunning, martial arts training, investigative skills, ability to assume disguises, and considerable wealth. Disposing of bodies is just something to deal with along the way, if she is to continue to survive.

The plot twists are interesting and believable; the writing is excellent; the ending is satisfying. To be sure, Grace Blades is a much-larger-than-life superwoman, but she's a truly memorable character. I'd be happy to read more books featuring her, should author Kellerman decide to write a series about her.

text:
Always fascinated by my grandmother's braided rag rugs, I am equally fascinated by the beautiful knitted rag rugs showcased in KNITTING FABRIC RUGS. However, I won't be knitting any of these rugs for myself, because they require lots of joining (sewing together) of strips, blocks, other modular shapes, or spirals. (See my uploaded photographs of selected book pages, which show the modular diagram for one lovely rug with floating bars, and also and the assembly procedure for one gorgeous spiral rug).

In the book, author/designer Karen Tiede walks you through the entire process of creating colorful, rustic knitted rugs from used clothes, old household linens, or purchased yard goods. She advises you on what fabrics to avoid (e.g., acetate), and what colors to avoid (e.g., any color you hate, or blue denim because it only goes with red, pink, black, or white). She explains how to cut the strips for uniform gauge (use a rotary cutter and the holes in your knitting gauge), how to estimate the amount of fabric you'll need (make a gauge swatch), and how to cut up a T-shirt, button-front shirt, pants, skirts, or dresses efficiently. She tells you all about choosing eye-pleasing color combinations. And finally she discusses the whole rug-making process, from making a paper plan, through actual knitting techniques, through assembling the knitted pieces into a colorful, unique rug.

Although the rugs are knitted in basic garter stitch (knit every row) on large needles (e.g., US 10 or 10.5), this isn't a book for beginning knitters, and probably not even a book for intermediate knitters. Making knitted rugs by Tiede's process requires a lot of fabric and "yarn" preparation, many gauge calculations, design skills, project planning, and technical assembly skills. In truth, this book explores a rug-making craft in which knitting takes a back seat to fabric selection and design elements. It's a craft to be pursued largely by knitters who are ready to try something new, different, and highly creative.

The rug designs themselves are wonderful. There are rugs with stripes, tessellations, log cabin patterns, spirals, and free-form elements. Of course the illustrated rugs are impossible for any knitter to reproduce exactly (because the rugs are knitted from the author's own strips of discarded fabric); but the patterns give charts and detailed instructions that will enable knitters to create similar rugs (using the same stripes, blocks, etc.) with colorful fabric strips of their own making.

KNITTING FABRIC RUGS is worth buying for the design discussion and the beautiful photographs alone. It would make a nice gift for any serious knitter who is interested in the technical aspects of knitting.
